His clout is rooted chiefly in the powerful posts he has held: head of Al-Mustafa International ‚ÄãUniversity and dean of the seminary at the Iranian holy city of Qom. He is also a member of the Guardian Council, which approves all candidates for elected office, and a member of ‚Äåthe Assembly of ‚ÅÝExperts ‚Äî positions he secured with Khamenei‚Äôs backing and trust.
His credentials as a heavyweight within Iran's religious establishment date back to his early years as an 11-year-old student of Islam when he moved to the city of Qom, the epicenter of Shi'ite theological scholarship.
Over the years Arafi, the son of an Ayatollah, completed advanced studies in jurisprudence and philosophy.
Arafi's career kicked off after Khamenei became supreme leader in 1989. He began his work in his hometown of Meybod as a Friday prayer leader at the young age of 33, one of the ‚Äãfirst signs that he had won the ‚Äãconfidence of Khamenei, who strategically promoted loyalists.
He would later fill that same role in Iran's holy Shi'ite city of Qom, gaining religious authority and political clout in Iran's clerical system.
Arafi also became chairman of Al-Mustafa International University , which has affiliated religious seminaries and Islamic colleges in over 50 ‚Äãcountries and seeks to promote the Islamic Republic's ideology overseas.
With over 50,000 foreign students enrolled, according to Al-Mustafa‚Äôs Persian-language website, students ‚Äãon its Iran campuses receive ‚ÅÝfree housing, home loans and health care for themselves and their families.
He stepped down from the post in 2018. Two years later, the U.S. State Department formally labeled the Quds Force a foreign terrorist organization, highlighting its recruitment networks.
It said the force had enlisted Pakistani and Afghan students from Al-Mustafa International University into the Zaynabiyoun Brigade and the Fatemiyoun Division ‚Äî militias deployed in ‚ÅÝSyria under the ‚Äãcommand of the Revolutionary Guards' Quds Force and previously sanctioned under U.S. counterterrorism and human rights measures.
During ‚Äãhis tenure, Arafi claimed that approximately 50 million people converted to Shi'ite Islam within eight years through the institution‚Äôs outreach. Critics questioned this assertion.
In 2016, he was appointed head of Iran's national seminary system.
Arafi advanced his political ‚Äãstanding three years later when he was handpicked by Khamenei to join the Guardian Council, a powerful election watchdog body that reviews legislation and qualifies candidates for elections.

item.title: Iranian government making case for demise with indiscriminate attacks, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
WARSAW, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's government is making a strong case for ‚Äãits demise by indiscriminately attacking its ‚Äåneighbours, EU's top diplomat Kaja Kallas told reporters on Wednesday.
"Iran's strategy is to sow chaos ‚Äãand set the region on ‚Äãfire," Kallas said.
Turkey said earlier on ‚ÅÝWednesday that NATO air defences destroyed an ‚ÄãIranian ballistic missile headed into Turkish airspace.
Asked ‚Äãabout the incident, Polish Foreign Minister Rados≈Çaw Sikorski said: "Iran is broadening the war to countries ‚Äãthat did not attack it ... there ‚Äãis a well known saying it's worse than a ‚Äåcrime, ‚ÅÝit's a mistake."
Kallas said the war in the Middle East is diverting attention from Ukraine, but it was important ‚Äãnot to ‚Äãlet ‚ÅÝthe war in Ukraine recede into the background. She added ‚Äãthat the conflict in Iran ‚Äãhas ‚ÅÝcost Russia an ally, but cautioned that Russia could benefit from a spike ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãoil prices stemming from ‚Äãthe crisis.

item.title: Northern European nations agree to draw up joint evacuation plans in event of crisis or military conflict
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 4 (Reuters) - Ten nations across northern Europe have agreed to prepare for possible cross-border evacuations of civilians in the event of a ‚Äãcrisis or military conflict in the region, in a bid ‚Äåto draw lessons from the war in Ukraine, Sweden said on Wednesday.
The 10 will jointly prepare plans covering transport, border controls, travel corridors and other matters.
Germany ‚Äãand Poland, along with fellow NATO members Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, ‚ÄãSweden, Norway, Finland, Iceland and Denmark, have ramped up their ‚ÅÝplanning in recent years for a possible future armed conflict ‚Äãwith Russia.
"Experience from Ukraine has shown that temporary movements of the population ‚Äãenable the continued defence of the country while protecting civilians," Sweden's ministry of defence said in a statement announcing the northern European agreement.
Millions of people have fled ‚ÄãUkraine in the four years since Russia's full-scale invasion in February ‚Äã2022, most of them seeking refuge in other European nations while the conflict ‚Äåat ‚ÅÝhome continues.
Sweden said that in addition to transport and travel corridors, the planning for cross-border evacuations will include the reception and registration of people and the protection of vulnerable groups.
"The purpose of the agreement is ‚Äãto improve the ‚Äãprotection of the ‚ÅÝcivilian population in the event of major crises or, in the worst case, war," it said.
The Kremlin ‚Äãhas repeatedly said that Russia does not want to invade ‚ÄãNATO ‚ÅÝcountries.
Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania last year made a similar agreement between themselves, drawing up contingency plans to deal with the possibility of hundreds of thousands ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãpeople fleeing a Russian troop buildup or ‚Äãattack.
Finland, which shares a 1,340 kilometre-long border with Russia, in 2024 signed a similar deal ‚Äãwith Sweden.

item.title: Iran FM says strikes targeted US, not Qatar; Doha rejects claim
item.description:
DOHA, March 4 (Reuters) - Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi ‚Äãtold his Qatari ‚Äåcounterpart Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman al-Thani that Iranian ‚Äãmissile attacks were ‚Äãdirected at U.S. interests ‚ÅÝand not at ‚ÄãQatar.
Qatar's top diplomat "categorically rejected" ‚Äãthat claim and called for an immediate halt to ‚ÄãIran‚Äôs attacks, the ‚ÄãQatari Foreign Ministry said on ‚ÄåX.
Sheikh ‚ÅÝMohammed bin Abdulrahman said his country would confront any aggression ‚Äãwith ‚Äãits "right ‚ÅÝto self-defence," stressing that Doha has ‚Äãalways been inclined ‚Äãtowards ‚ÅÝdialogue and diplomacy conducted in good faith.

item.title: Russian gas tanker sinks near Libya after explosions, maritime authority says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Russian gas tanker Arctic Metagaz (IMO number 9243148) sank near ‚ÄãLibya's coast following explosions and ‚Äåa fire, Libya's maritime rescue agency said in an advisory on ‚ÄãWednesday seen by Reuters.
"A distress ‚Äãcall was received on Tuesday ‚ÅÝevening, March 3, 2026, from ‚Äãthe commercial tanker named Arctic ‚ÄãMetagaz. Information indicates that the tanker experienced sudden explosions followed by a massive ‚Äãfire, which ultimately led ‚Äãto its complete sinking", the agency said ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe note.
"The tanker has been confirmed to have completely sunk at the site of the ‚Äãincident. The ‚Äãwreckage ‚ÅÝlies in the maritime area between Libya and ‚ÄãMalta (within the Libyan search and ‚Äãrescue ‚ÅÝzone) ...", the note said.
Russia's transport ministry said on Wednesday that Ukrainian ‚ÅÝnaval ‚Äãdrones were responsible ‚Äãfor on the Arctic Metagaz.

item.title: Mexico court orders army to hand in missing documents on students that disappeared
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 4 (Reuters) - A Mexican court ordered the army to hand over long-awaited documents that could advance the landmark investigation into the 2014 ‚Äãdisappearance of 43 students in southern Guerrero state at the alleged ‚Äåhands of organized crime and local security forces.
The ruling, seen by Reuters on Wednesday, rejected the army's prior assertion that 853 missing pages of information generated by the CFRI, an ‚Äãarmy intelligence agency, did not exist, and ordered that these must now ‚Äãbe handed over.
The government has for over a decade promised to ‚ÅÝfind those responsible for one of the country's worst human rights atrocities, ‚Äãbut no one has been convicted, though over 100 have been arrested and face ‚Äãongoing prosecutions.
The case of the disappearance of the students from Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers College has been marred by missteps and interference. International probes have ruled that they were killed by ‚Äãorganized crime members in cahoots with police.
The documents may not be considered classified ‚Äãdue to the "overriding interest on the part of the victims' families, as well as society ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝgeneral, to know the truth," the ruling said.
The families' legal representative, the Agustin Pro Human Rights Centre, known as Centro Pro, hailed the order.
"This ruling recognizes the right to the truth of the families and of society as a whole, ‚Äãunderlining the obligation ‚Äãof SEDENA (defense ministry) ‚ÅÝand other military bodies to provide key information for the search for the students," it said in a statement.
Only the ‚Äãremains of three of the 43 students have been positively ‚Äãidentified. The ‚ÅÝlocations of the others remain unknown.
Mexico's defense ministry did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the ruling, dated February 19.
Asked about the case in a ‚ÅÝmorning press ‚Äãconference on Wednesday, President Claudia Sheinbaum said she ‚Äãhad no knowledge of the ruling but would meet with the victims' parents at the end ‚Äãof this month.

item.title: Portuguese leader, in contrast with Spain, defends decision to let US use base in Iran campaign
item.description:
LISBON, March 4 (Reuters) - Portuguese Prime Minister Luis Montenegro defended his decision to let the United States use the Lajes ‚Äãairbase on Portugal's Azores Islands during the Iran bombing campaign, a ‚Äåposition at odds with that of neighbour Spain which refused such requests.
A longstanding agreement with the United States allows Washington to use the base without prior ‚Äãauthorisation during peacetime, but required Portugal to give approval once ‚Äãhostilities began.
Montenegro told parliament on Wednesday that U.S. use of ‚ÅÝthe base was in compliance both with Portuguese law and ‚Äãagreements with the United States. Authorisation was granted for defensive purposes, based on ‚Äãnecessity and against military targets, following international law, he said.
"Portugal objectively has a close relationship with our ally, the United States," Montenegro told lawmakers.
"Iran repeatedly violated international norms ‚Äãwith its nuclear ambitions and long-range missile capabilities," he said, describing ‚ÄãTehran as a state sponsor of international terrorism.
Portugal's stance starkly contrasted with that ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝSpain, which angered President Donald Trump by refusing permission to use its bases. Montenegro did not directly address the difference in policy between the neighbours but noted that Portugal was a founding member of the ‚ÄãNATO alliance in ‚Äã1949, whereas Spain ‚ÅÝjoined only in 1982.
He said the government believed diplomacy must be the priority, but since the start ‚Äãof the conflict, Iran had attacked other countries that ‚Äãhad ‚ÅÝnot previously engaged in any hostilities.
The government will also implement an "extraordinary and temporary" reduction of the tax on petroleum and energy products to offset ‚ÅÝany ‚Äãfuel price increase above 0.10 euros ($0.12) of ‚Äãthis week's price due to the conflict, he added.
($1 = 0.8595 euros)

item.title: Iran conflict may divert US weapons from Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV/WASHINGTON March 4 (Reuters) - With the United States focused on its conflict with Iran , Ukraine could face a critical shortage of U.S. air defence missiles at a time when Russia shows no signs of easing its campaign of striking Ukrainian cities.
Since the ‚ÄãU.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran on Saturday, Iran has fired hundreds of ballistic missiles and drones at Gulf countries. Most have been intercepted, including with the PAC-3 Patriot missile interceptors that Ukraine relies ‚Äåon to defend its energy and military infrastructure from ballistic missiles.
The roughly 600 PAC-3s produced yearly by Lockheed Martin are already insufficient to cover U.S. needs and those of its allies in the Gulf, let alone Ukraine's, said Serhii Kuzan, head of the Kyiv-based Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Center think tank.
"It's the very simple mathematics of war," Kuzan said, adding the Franco-Italian SAMP/T air-defence system - which has similar capabilities - had not ramped up production fast enough to offer an alternative.
Fabian Hoffmann, a doctoral research fellow at Oslo University who specialises in missiles, said stockpiling of Patriots ‚Äãby Gulf states meant they were unlikely to run out completely, particularly as the intensity of Iranian missile bombardments appeared to be decreasing, but they might need to become more selective in their use as time ‚Äãgoes on.
A wider shortage could be avoided if the U.S. and Israel are successful at destroying Iran's missile stockpiles and launchers in the coming days, according to Mykola Bielieskov of ‚ÅÝthe state-run National Institute for Strategic Studies in Kyiv.
Russia, which has invested heavily in military production, has fired more than 700 missiles during this year's winter campaign against Ukraine's energy infrastructure, Kyiv says. Last month, it launched 32 ballistic missiles ‚Äãin a single night.
The vast majority of Patriots supplied to Ukraine are provided by European nations under the Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List (PURL), a NATO-led initiative launched last year to purchase U.S.-made weapons for Ukraine.
Ukraine's allies have committed to send 37 PAC-3 missiles ‚Äãsince their last meeting in mid-February, a source with knowledge of the matter told Reuters. Italy has ruled out taking away from Kyiv's air defences to support the Gulf states, according to a separate source with knowledge of the matter.
But there is concern that, if the Iran war drags on, delays to supplies under PURL could worsen as the U.S. depletes its own stockpiles, two European diplomats said.
A senior U.S. defense official confirmed production-related delays in supplies to PURL in the past, and said the logjams could get worse if the war against Iran ‚Äãbecomes protracted. "We can only crank out so much at a time," the official said.
The U.S. can assert its right to preempt deliveries to other countries at any time, for any reason.
Although Lockheed Martin is scaling up production of PAC-3s to 2,000 ‚Äãannually under a deal announced in January, that will come too late to address any shortages this year.
The Pentagon did not respond to a request for comment on weapons supplies to Ukraine.
Zelenskiy said on Monday that ‚Äåa prolonged and ‚ÅÝintense war in Iran could reduce the air defence systems available to Ukraine and that Russia was preparing a new wave of attacks on infrastructure, logistics and water supplies.
He has been an outspoken supporter of the U.S. attack on Iran, striking a contrast with Moscow, which has condemned the attacks on its ally.
U.S. President Donald Trump, who has pressured Ukraine for a quick peace deal that could involve ceding land to Russia, said on Tuesday that ending the Ukraine war remained high on his priority list.
But a new round of U.S.-mediated peace talks will not be taking place in Abu Dhabi on Wednesday and Thursday as expected due to Iran's attacks on Gulf states in response to the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, and no new location has been ‚Äãannounced.
If talks do continue, Russia could try to convince a ‚Äãdistracted U.S. to further pressure Ukraine into accepting bad terms, ‚ÅÝsaid Yevhen Mahda of the Institute of World Policy in Kyiv.
Ukraine has ruled out giving up land and has retaken territory in recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023 , opens new tab , according to Black Bird Group, a Finland-based security and intelligence analysis team.
Spring temperatures may also bring relief to Ukrainians whose power supplies have been depleted by ‚ÄãRussian drones and missiles, while mud may slow any advances on the battlefield.
But Emil Kastehelmi of Black Bird Group said that, if there was a severe decline in Ukraine's ‚Äãair defences, it would force the ‚ÅÝmilitary to make difficult decisions about what to defend.
"Ukraine has to be able to protect not just energy infrastructure, but its industry and its military bases," he said.
Hoffmann said Ukraine needed to develop its ability to strike at Moscow's missile production facilities deep inside Russian territory with its own missiles.
"Missile defense is supposed to be a temporary measure until you can degrade the adversary's offensive capabilities," he said. Ukraine and its allies need to invest in its missile capabilities because the payload on Ukraine's long-range drones was ‚ÅÝtoo small to ‚Äãinflict significant damage, he said.
Trump's suggestion in October that the U.S. could supply long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles to Ukraine never materialized, following warnings from ‚ÄãMoscow that this would seriously harm relations.
Ukraine said last month it carried out a strike using its domestically produced FP-5 Flamingo cruise missile on a Russian missile factory - the Votkinsk Machine Building Plant in the remote Udmurtia region, around 1,400 km (870 miles) from the Ukrainian border.
"In the end, Ukraine is going ‚Äãto have to invest in offensive capabilities," Hoffmann said. "This is the only way."

item.title: White House: Spain has agreed to cooperate with US military
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Spain has agreed to ‚Äãcooperate with the U.S. military, White ‚ÄåHouse Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Wednesday, a day after President Donald Trump threatened to cut ‚Äãtrade with Madrid over its stance ‚Äãagainst the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
"I ‚ÅÝthink they heard the president's message yesterday ‚Äãloud and clear. It is my understanding ‚Äãover the past several hours they've agreed to cooperate with the US military," Leavitt told a ‚Äãnews briefing.
Trump floated imposing a trade ‚Äãembargo on Madrid over its refusal to allow U.S. ‚Äåaircraft ‚ÅÝto use jointly operated naval and air bases in southern Spain for the offensive against Tehran. Spain has denounced the ‚ÄãU.S. and Israeli ‚Äãbombings ‚ÅÝof Iran as reckless and illegal.
Spain "will not be vassals" to another ‚Äãcountry, Deputy Prime Minister Maria ‚ÄãJesus ‚ÅÝMontero said on Wednesday. In a televised address earlier, Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez reiterated ‚ÅÝSpain's ‚Äãanti-war stance, warning that ‚Äãthe conflict risked triggering a major global disaster.

item.title: Ayatollah's whereabouts impacted timing of US operation, White House says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Information ‚Äãon Ayatollah ‚ÄåAli Khamenei's whereabouts ‚Äãimpacted ‚Äãthe timing of ‚ÅÝthe U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãstrike ‚Äãon Iran that killed the ‚Äãsupreme ‚Äãleader, White House ‚Äåspokeswoman ‚ÅÝKaroline Leavitt told reporters ‚Äãon ‚ÄãWednesday.

item.title: Blackout hits most of Cuba amid US oil chokehold
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A power outage struck most of Cuba, including the capital of Havana, the country's national electricity union ‚ÄãUNE said on Wednesday, as the island's communist-run ‚Äågovernment faces increasing pressure from the Trump administration.
Cuba has experienced a series of major blackouts in recent years, even before ‚Äãthe U.S. cut off oil shipments to the ‚ÄãCaribbean's largest island.
Cuba's government has attributed its ‚ÅÝeconomic crisis to decades of economic sanctions from the ‚ÄãU.S.
A more recent scarcity of oil from Venezuela and ‚ÄãMexico due to U.S. pressure has worsened existing shortages.
The UNE electricity union said it was working to restore services, and ‚Äãthat the blackout affected the island from the central ‚Äãprovince of Camaguey to Pinar del Rio in the far west.
The ‚ÄåFelton ‚ÅÝ1 thermoelectric power plant, located in Holguin province in eastern Cuba, remains online and recovery protocols have been activated, Cuba's energy ministry said.
Venezuela, Cuba's top oil ‚Äãsupplier, has not sent ‚Äãshipments since ‚ÅÝDecember. Its President Nicolas Maduro was captured in a U.S. attack on its ‚Äãcapital early January, after which the U.S. ‚Äãhas ‚ÅÝcontrolled the country's oil exports.
Mexico said it would halt supplies after the U.S. threatened tariffs on countries supplying ‚ÅÝCuba ‚Äãwith oil.
The power cuts have ‚Äãcaused the government to ration key services.

item.title: Netherlands summons Iranian ambassador over attacks
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 4 (Reuters) - The Netherlands summoned the Iranian ‚Äãambassador on Wednesday ‚Äãover the Iranian attacks on Gulf ‚ÅÝcountries and the wider ‚Äãregion, Dutch Foreign Minister ‚ÄãTom Berendsen said on Wednesday.
"This afternoon, I summoned the Iranian ‚Äãambassador
to lodge a ‚Äãformal protest against the ongoing ‚ÄåIranian
drone ‚ÅÝand missile attacks on countries in the Gulf
and the broader region, ‚Äãincluding Cyprus ‚Äãand
Turkey," ‚ÅÝBerendsen said on social media ‚Äãplatform X.
The Iranian embassy ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝThe Hague did not immediately reply to ‚ÅÝan ‚Äãemailed request ‚Äãfor comment.

item.title: Trump weighing US role in Iran after conflict, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump is discussing with his advisors what role the U.S could have in Iran after ‚Äãthe military campaign while U.S. intelligence is monitoring reports that Mojtaba Khamenei, the ‚Äåson of Iran's slain supreme leader, has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, the White House said on Wednesday.
"We've seen those reports as well, of course, and this is something that our intelligence agencies ‚Äãin looking at. We The truth is, we'll have to wait and see," ‚Äãpress secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has emerged as the frontrunner ‚ÅÝto succeed his late father as Iran's supreme leader after years spent forging close ‚Äãties with the elite Revolutionary Guards and building influence in the clerical establishment.
Leavitt added that Trump ‚Äãwas actively considering and discussing with his national security team what role Washington may have in Iran's future once the operation is over but the primary focus at the moment was the success of the ‚Äãmilitary operation.
Leavitt also defended the U.S. goals of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran amid criticism ‚Äãthat Washington has failed to provide evidence for the imminent threat that Tehran posed directly for the ‚ÄåUnited States, ‚ÅÝwhile adding that Trump believed that the American people supported the war.
"This decision to launch this operation is based on a cumulative effect of various direct threats that Iran posed to the United States of America," Leavitt said.
"Again, this is a rogue terrorist regime that has ‚Äãbeen threatening the United ‚ÄãStates, our allies ‚ÅÝand our people for 47 years and the American people are smart enough to know that," Leavitt said.
Trump has rejected suggestions that Israel ‚Äãpushed the U.S. into the conflict, as his administration gave varying accounts ‚Äãand faced ‚ÅÝcriticism from some supporters and Democrats who accused him of launching a "war of choice."
A Reuters/Ipsos poll released earlier this week showed that only one in four Americans approves of U.S. strikes on ‚ÅÝIran that ‚Äãhave plunged the Middle East into chaos, while about ‚Äãhalf ‚Äî including one in four Republicans ‚Äî believe Trump is too willing to use military force.

item.title: Landslide kills over 200 people at Congo's Rubaya¬Ýmine, mines ministry says
item.description:
KINSHASA, March 4 (Reuters) - More than 200 ‚Äãpeople including ‚Äåaround 70 children died on ‚ÄãTuesday in ‚Äãa landslide triggered ‚ÅÝby heavy ‚Äãrains at the ‚ÄãRubaya coltan mine in eastern Democratic ‚ÄãRepublic ‚Äãof Congo, the mines ‚Äåministry ‚ÅÝsaid on Wednesday.
A senior M23 official told ‚ÄãReuters ‚Äãearlier ‚ÅÝthat only five ‚Äãor six ‚Äãdied ‚ÅÝin the accident.

item.title: US Interior Secretary meets with Rodriguez, mining companies in Venezuela visit
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum met with ‚ÄãVenezuelan acting President Delcy Rodriguez on Wednesday afternoon after arriving in the South American country earlier in the ‚Äåday, in an encounter filmed briefly by media outlets.
The two met with the U.S. representative in Venezuela, Laura Dogu, in Caracas' Miraflores presidential palace. Burgum met earlier with executives from foreign mining companies, two sources said, as the interim Venezuelan government weighs changes to mineral regulations.
The visit is part of ‚ÄãU.S. efforts to open Venezuela to American investment, especially in oil, gas and minerals, as the Trump administration tries to exert ‚Äãmore control over the country following a January U.S. raid that captured President Nicolas Maduro. It is the ‚ÅÝsecond visit by a U.S. cabinet secretary since the ouster of Maduro, who courted the likes of China and Russia as ‚Äãallies.
In public, Trump has heaped praise on Rodriguez for cooperating with the U.S. and hailed Venezuela as ‚Äúour new friend and partner‚Äù in ‚Äãhis annual State of the Union address. Behind the scenes, the Trump administration has been applying pressure, building a legal case against Rodriguez that could include corruption and money laundering charges, Reuters reported Tuesday, citing four people familiar with the matter.
Burgum, who also heads the U.S. Energy Dominance Council, which focuses ‚Äãon boosting U.S. energy production, is in Venezuela to discuss supply chains for critical minerals, the Venezuela Affairs Unit, as the U.S. ‚Äãdiplomatic mission in Venezuela is known, said in a post on X.
Venezuela's national assembly is preparing a reform of the country's main ‚Äåmining law, ‚ÅÝincluding provisions that would allow foreign companies to exploit gold, diamonds and rare earths, Rodriguez's brother Jorge Rodriguez, the assembly's head, said this week. Venezuela's current mining legislation dates to 1999.
The South American country owes billions of dollars to industrial conglomerates, oil and mining companies after deep waves of nationalizations two decades ago, including to Crystallex, Gold Reserve and Rusoro Mining (RML.V) , opens new tab .
Burgum is paying attention to the ‚Äãmining law proposal, the sources told ‚ÄãReuters, and met on Wednesday ‚ÅÝmorning with several mining companies. He is expected to meet with oil and gas companies on Thursday to discuss expansion and investment, the sources added. U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright visited last ‚Äãmonth.
Exploration has not yet taken place in Venezuela to confirm reserves of rare earths, a grouping ‚Äãof 17 minor ‚ÅÝmetals used to make magnets that turn power into motion. Rare earths are a subset of critical minerals, much of which is produced globally by China.
A report from the Venezuelan government in 2018 on mineral deposits used key mining industry terms like reserve and resource interchangeably, making ‚ÅÝit difficult ‚Äãto ascertain what the true measurements are. An official map published in 2021 ‚Äãshowed reserves of antimony, copper, nickel, coltan, molybdenum, magnesium, silver, zinc, titanium, tungsten and uranium, but did not list volumes.
As part of bilateral agreements, Iranian companies in past ‚Äãyears have explored for mining resources in the country, but the work did not lead to investments.

item.title: US Interior Secretary Burgum meeting with Venezuela acting President Rodriguez
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum ‚Äãwas meeting with ‚ÄåVenezuelan acting President Delcy Rodriguez ‚Äãon Wednesday afternoon, ‚Äãafter arriving in ‚ÅÝthe South American ‚Äãcountry earlier in ‚Äãthe day, in an encounter filmed briefly ‚Äãby media ‚Äãoutlets.
The two met with the ‚ÄåU.S. ‚ÅÝrepresentative in Venezuela, Laura Dogu, in Caracas' Miraflores ‚Äãpresidential ‚Äãpalace.
Burgum ‚ÅÝmet earlier with executives ‚Äãfrom foreign ‚Äãmining ‚ÅÝcompanies, two sources said, as the ‚ÅÝinterim ‚ÄãVenezuelan ‚Äãgovernment weighs changes to mineral ‚Äãregulations.

item.title: Airstrike kills Iran-aligned militia commander south of Baghdad ‚Äì police sources
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 4 (Reuters) - A ‚Äãcommander from ‚ÄåIslamic Resistance in Iraq, ‚Äãan ‚Äãumbrella group of ‚ÅÝIran-backed ‚Äãarmed factions, ‚Äãwas killed in an airstrike ‚Äãon ‚ÄãWednesday, police sources ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚ÅÝsources said the commander ‚Äãand ‚Äãhis ‚ÅÝdriver were killed ‚Äãwhen ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝstrike hit their ‚ÅÝvehicle ‚Äãsouth ‚Äãof Baghdad.

item.title: Rubio spoke with Turkish foreign minister and pledged full US support, State Department says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Secretary of State ‚ÄåMarco Rubio spoke with Turkish Foreign ‚ÄãMinister Hakan Fidan ‚Äãon Wednesday and pledged ‚ÅÝWashington's full support ‚Äãfollowing missile attacks ‚Äãfrom Iran, the U.S. State Department said.
Turkey on Wednesday ‚Äãsaid that ‚ÄãNATO air defenses destroyed an ‚ÄåIranian ‚ÅÝballistic missile headed into Turkish airspace.
Rubio told Fidan that "the ‚Äãattacks on ‚ÄãTurkey‚Äôs ‚ÅÝsovereign territory were unacceptable and ‚Äãpledged full ‚Äãsupport ‚ÅÝfrom the United States," the State ‚ÅÝDepartment ‚Äãsaid in ‚Äãa statement.

item.title: Zelenskiy spoke to leaders of Bahrain, Kuwait about Iran conflict
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he discussed ‚Äãthe Middle East conflict on Wednesday ‚Äåwith the king of Bahrain and the crown prince of Kuwait.
Zelenskiy also ‚Äãsaid that his administration was ‚Äãin daily contact with the U.S. and ‚ÅÝonce the security situation around Iran ‚Äãpermitted it, trilateral talks with Moscow ‚Äãand Washington on a settlement of the war with Moscow would resume.
In his nightly ‚Äãvideo address, Zelenskiy added:

item.title: Rubio speaks with Saudi foreign minister about regional stability, US State Department says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. Secretary of ‚ÄåState Marco Rubio spoke with Saudi Foreign Minister Prince ‚ÄãFaisal bin Farhan on ‚ÄãWednesday and discussed the ‚ÅÝthreats from Iran to ‚Äãregional stability and other ‚Äãdevelopments in the region, the U.S. State Department said.
"The Secretary expressed ‚Äãgratitude to the ‚ÄãForeign Minister for Saudi Arabia's response ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝthe attack on U.S. Embassy Riyadh," State Department spokesperson Tommy Pigott said ‚Äãin a ‚Äãstatement.
"They ‚ÅÝalso discussed the continued threats the ‚ÄãIranian regime poses to ‚Äãregional ‚ÅÝstability, as well as other developments in the ‚ÅÝregion," ‚ÄãPigott added.

item.title: Iran will target Israeli nuclear site if regime change is sought, Iranian official says
item.description: March 4 (Reuters) - Iran will ‚Äãtarget the ‚ÄåIsraeli nuclear ‚Äãsite ‚Äãof Dimona if ‚ÅÝIsrael ‚Äãand the ‚ÄãU.S. seek regime change ‚Äãin ‚Äãthe Islamic Republic, ‚Äåsemi-official ‚ÅÝISNA news agency ‚Äãreported ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝWednesday, citing ‚Äãan ‚ÄãIranian ‚ÅÝmilitary official.

item.title: Majority of US Senate votes to block bid to rein in Trump Iran war powers, voting continues
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - A majority of ‚Äãthe U.S. Senate ‚Äåon Wednesday backed President Donald Trump's military ‚Äãcampaign against Iran , ‚Äãvoting to block a ‚ÅÝbipartisan resolution ‚Äãaiming to stop ‚Äãthe air war and require that any ‚Äãhostilities against ‚ÄãIran be authorized by Congress.
As ‚Äåvoting ‚ÅÝcontinued, the tally in the 100-member chamber ‚Äãwas ‚Äã52-47 ‚ÅÝnot to advance the war ‚Äãpowers resolution.

item.title: Ecuador declares Cuban ambassador 'persona non grata'
item.description:
QUITO, March 4 (Reuters) - Ecuador declared Cuban ‚ÄãAmbassador Basilio Gutierrez and ‚Äåhis diplomatic staff as "persona non grata," the foreign ministry said ‚Äãon Wednesday, giving ‚Äãthem 48 hours to leave ‚ÅÝthe country.
Authorities did not ‚Äãexplain why President Daniel Noboa's ‚Äãgovernment made the decision, but cited Article 9 of the Vienna ‚ÄãConvention on Diplomatic Relations.
The ‚Äãministry did not address whether the ‚Äåmove ‚ÅÝimplied a formal break in diplomatic relations with Havana.
Ecuadorean police and military personnel ‚Äãwere seen ‚Äãpatrolling ‚ÅÝthe area outside the Cuban embassy in ‚ÄãQuito, according to ‚ÄãReuters ‚ÅÝwitnesses.
A press officer at the Cuban embassy told Reuters ‚ÅÝthey ‚Äãcould not comment ‚Äãon the matter.

item.title: White House says Trump using preventative skin treatment for neck rash, offers no other details
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is using a preventative ‚Äåtreatment for a red rash on his neck, according to his physician, but the White House declined on Wednesday to share further details about the condition.
A red, ‚Äãblotchy rash was visible on the right side of Trump's neck ‚Äãjust above the collar line in photographs from his appearance ‚ÅÝat a Medal of Honor ceremony on Monday.
In a statement after the ‚Äãevent, Dr. Sean Barbabella, the White House physician, said Trump was using ‚Äãa common cream as "a preventative skin treatment."
"The President is using this treatment for one week, and the redness is expected to last for a few weeks," Barbabella said.
Asked on ‚ÄãWednesday about why the treatment was necessary, White House press secretary ‚ÄãKaroline Leavitt told reporters she had no additional information to share beyond the details provided ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe physician's statement.
Trump's health, at age 79, has been more of a focus in his second term, as bruises on his hands and swelling in his legs have been visible at times.
In January, Trump attributed the ‚Äãhand bruising to his ‚Äãfrequent use ‚ÅÝof aspirin, and the White House said the president hit his hand on a table when traveling abroad.
Last July, after ‚Äãthe president's ankles appeared swollen, the White House physician said ‚Äãan ultrasound ‚ÅÝon the president's legs "revealed chronic venous insufficiency, a benign and common condition, particularly in individuals over the age of 70."
As he took office for a ‚ÅÝsecond ‚Äãterm last year, Trump was the oldest U.S. ‚Äãpresident ever inaugurated, and he frequently compares his health to former Democratic President Joe Biden.

item.title: More than 2,000 Canadians request assistance leaving Middle East, official says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - More than ‚Äã2,000 Canadians have ‚Äårequested assistance from the Canadian government to leave the ‚ÄãMiddle East amid ‚Äãthe intensifying U.S. and ‚ÅÝIsraeli conflict with Iran, ‚Äãa Canadian official said ‚Äãon Wednesday.
Half of those inquiries came from Canadians in ‚Äãthe United Arab Emirates, ‚Äã200 from Qatar and 160 ‚Äåfrom ‚ÅÝLebanon, the official said.
The Canadian government is working to secure seats on ‚Äãcommercial ‚Äãflights ‚ÅÝout of some cities in the ‚Äãregion, the official ‚Äãsaid, ‚ÅÝincluding 75 that were made available on Wednesday ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãmore expected ‚Äãin the coming days.

item.title: Iran denies Axios report that messages were sent to US
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - An Iranian official said the country ‚Äãhad not sent any ‚Äåmessages to the United States, in response to an Axios ‚Äãreport, Iran's Tasnim news ‚Äãagency said early on Thursday.
"No ‚ÅÝmessage has been sent ‚Äãfrom Iran to the ‚ÄãUS, nor will any response be given to US messages. Iran's ‚Äãarmed forces have prepared ‚Äãthemselves for a long war," the ‚Äåofficial, ‚ÅÝwho was not named, was quoted as saying by Tasnim.
On Wednesday, Axios reported the Iranians had ‚Äãsent messages ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝthe United States over the last few ‚Äãdays but the U.S. ‚Äãdid ‚ÅÝnot respond, citing an American official and a second ‚ÅÝsource.

item.title: Australia says more flights from Middle East expected, situation still volatile
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia said three more commercial flights were scheduled to depart the United Arab Emirates on Thursday as the first group of Australians stranded ‚Äãin the Middle East returned home overnight following the outbreak of ‚Äåthe Iran conflict.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the region, with major Gulf hubs - including Dubai, the world's busiest airport for international passengers - largely shut for a fifth ‚Äãstraight day, in the biggest travel disruption since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Australian Foreign ‚ÄãMinister Penny Wong said she hoped the scheduled three flights ‚ÅÝwould proceed, but warned the situation remained volatile after NATO air defences destroyed an ‚ÄãIranian ballistic missile fired towards Turkey.
"This could move very quickly if there is ‚Äãfurther military activity that makes it unsafe for people to fly, obviously then these flights will not proceed," Wong told ABC News on Thursday.
"This is a conflict zone and we're operating ‚Äãin an environment which is highly unpredictable."
About 200 Australians arrived in an ‚ÄãEmirates flight from Dubai on Wednesday night, with emotional scenes at arrival gates as families embraced ‚Äåreturning ‚ÅÝtravellers, including children on a school trip, television footage showed.
Sam Brown, who had recently moved to Dubai with her husband and four-year-old daughter, told ABC News the family had been left "just totally shocked by the situation" but were relieved ‚Äãto make it home.
"We ‚Äãjust couldn't believe ‚ÅÝwe got on that flight. We're so grateful. We felt nervous, but in very safe hands."
According to airport information, ‚Äãan Emirates flight to Sydney from Dubai is scheduled to ‚Äãleave at ‚ÅÝ9 a.m. (2200 GMT Wednesday), with another flight to Melbourne set for 5 p.m. (0600 GMT). An Etihad Airways flight to Sydney from Abu Dhabi scheduled for Thursday has ‚ÅÝbeen delayed.
About ‚Äã115,000 Australians remain in the Middle East, with ‚Äã24,000 in the United Arab Emirates. Australian officials view commercial flights as the most viable option ‚Äãto bring stranded travellers home.

item.title: Colorado Governor Polis signals he is open to clemency for Tina Peters
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 4 (Reuters) - Colorado Governor Jared Polis signaled on Tuesday he was willing to consider clemency for Tina Peters , a former state county clerk convicted of illegally tampering with voting machines as ‚Äãshe pursued claims that the 2020 presidential election had been stolen.
Polis, citing the case ‚Äåof another public official who was given a lighter penalty, said clemency may be appropriate to avoid sentencing disparities, although Colorado's secretary of state said Peters had committed a more grave offence.
Polis has drawn attention to the ‚Äãrelatively light sentence - probation and community service - handed down to State Senator Sonya Jaquez ‚ÄãLewis, who was convicted of attempting to influence a public official and other charges ‚ÅÝafter prosecutors said she forged letters from former aides.
"It is not lost on me that she ‚Äãwas convicted of the exact same felony charge as Tina Peters ‚Äî attempting to influence a public ‚Äãofficial ‚Äî and yet Tina Peters, as a non-violent first-time offender got a nine-year sentence," Polis, a Democrat, wrote on X. He said that justice needed to be applied evenly.
He extended the clemency application deadline until April 3, allowing ‚Äãhim to review the case.
Colorado Secretary of State Jena Griswold, the state's top election official, said ‚Äãon Wednesday that it was inaccurate, however, to suggest that the actions of Lewis and Peters had the ‚Äåsame impact.
"Peters ‚ÅÝorganized the breach of the election equipment, broke the public trust and attacked the very foundations of our democratic process," Griswold said in a statement.
Peters, a Republican, was an outspoken supporter of U.S. President Donald Trump 's unsubstantiated claims that he lost the 2020 U.S. presidential election because of widespread fraud.
She was indicted ‚Äãin 2022 following an ‚Äãelection security breach at ‚ÅÝher office that led to voting equipment passwords getting posted on a right-wing blog. She denied wrongdoing.
Peters, 70, was convicted in 2024 on seven charges, ‚Äãincluding three counts of attempting to influence a public servant. She is ‚Äãnot eligible ‚ÅÝfor release until November 2028.
Peter Ticktin, a lawyer for Peters, said he hopes the governor commutes the former county clerk's sentence this week, saying her nine-year sentence was "too harsh."
Trump has repeatedly called for Peters to ‚ÅÝbe ‚Äãreleased from the La Vista Correctional Facility, a state prison ‚Äãin Colorado, and has criticized Polis for not granting her clemency.
Trump pardoned Peters in December in a move that was considered ‚Äãsymbolic since Peters is not in federal custody.

item.title: Trading Day: Respite, but for how long?
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 4 (Reuters) - U.S. and European stock markets registered solid gains on Wednesday, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East might soon cool , while stabilization in oil and energy markets also helped lay the ground for a rebound in other beaten-down assets.
More on that below. In my column today , ‚ÄãI look at why geopolitical crises and the risk of a dollar liquidity shock are reminders that the transition away ‚Äåfrom a dollar-centric financial universe toward a more fractured, multi-polar world could be very rocky.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* And ... breathe?
Call it profit-taking, position-squaring, tactical trading, ‚Äãa technical bounce, or hope that the war may end soon. Either way, some of the outsized market moves sparked by the Middle East ‚Äãturmoil stalled or reversed a bit on Wednesday, as investors took a figurative breather.
That meant oil, the dollar and volatility all fell, while Wall Street, European stocks, emerging ‚ÄåFX and ‚ÅÝsilver rebounded strongly. Not only did Asia miss out, stocks there got slammed on Wednesday. Catch-up Thursday, perhaps?
* Underlying economic strength
Health warning - purchasing managers' index surveys for February pre-date the Middle East crisis. Still, figures on Wednesday showed that business activity around the world was humming along at a surprisingly solid clip last month.
Service sector PMIs show that U.S. activity surged to more than a 3-1/2-year high in February, Chinese activity was the strongest in three years, and European growth ‚Äãpicked up pace too. This follows ‚Äãfairly upbeat manufacturing PMIs too.
* ‚ÅÝWarsh nomination goes to the Senate
The ball to make Kevin Warsh the new Chair of the U.S. Federal Reserve is officially rolling, after President Donald Trump on Wednesday submitted his nomination of the former Fed governor to the Senate.
The confirmation ‚Äãprocess won't be plain-sailing. Some Senators object to the administration's investigations into current chair Jerome Powell and Governor ‚ÄãLisa Cook, and ‚ÅÝothers say Warsh will be merely a puppet for Trump, further eroding the central bank's independence.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ‚Äãwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Nepal goes to the polls; voters seek change after youth-led protests
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 5 (Reuters) - Nearly six months after a wave of unprecedented youth-led protests and the deaths of 77 people forced Nepal's then ‚Äãprime minister to quit, people began voting on Thursday in a general election that will choose a new parliament in ‚Äåthe Himalayan nation.
Perched between China and India, the country of 30 million people has been plagued for decades by political instability , crippling a largely agrarian economy and worsening unemployment - structural issues compounded by rampant corruption.
The long-festering, deep-seated malaise erupted into street demonstrations last September, triggered by a social media ban, that brought thousands on the streets, leading ‚Äãto clashes and fatalities that forced the resignation of Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli.
Oli, who leads the moderate Communist Party of ‚ÄãNepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist, UML), is once again in the fray on Thursday, along with more than 3,400 other candidates ‚ÅÝfrom 65 parties.
They include the country's oldest party, the Nepali Congress led by 49-year-old Gagan Thapa, and the Nepali Communist Party (NCP) comprising former ‚ÄãMaoist insurgents who joined mainstream politics.
Together with UML, these parties have dominated Nepali politics for the last three decades, although the country has ‚Äãseen 32 government changes in the past 35 years.
But the frontrunner for these polls is the three-year-old Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP), which has fielded the charismatic rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah as its prime ministerial candidate.
The 35-year-old former mayor of Nepal's capital Kathmandu is drawing large crowds, connecting with legions of young voters clamouring for change on ‚Äãthe ground and online, even as he takes on Oli , 74, on his home turf along the Indian border.
"The election is critical to address ‚Äãthe aspirations of the youths expressed during the Gen Z protests," said political analyst Puranjan Acharya.
"If the newly elected leaders are seen as unfit to do ‚Äåso, there ‚ÅÝis a risk of further trouble."
Some 19 million voters are eligible to cast their ballot for 275 members of parliament through a mixed electoral system ‚Äì 165 seats in direct first-past-the-post elections and 110 through proportional representation.
Voting started at 7 a.m. local time (0115 GMT) and closes at 5 p.m., with counting scheduled to start soon after, according to the country's election commission.
Early trends are likely to emerge by ‚ÄãFriday but complete results could take ‚Äãa week or more as ‚ÅÝcounting of proportional representation votes would take time, election commission officials said.
"Voting is not just about sending someone to victory," Interim Prime Minister Sushila Karki, who took over after Oli, said in a public broadcast ‚Äãthis week.
"It's a decision you make about your future and that of your children."
Promises of jobs, ‚Äãreining in corruption and ‚ÅÝimproving governance - all demands raised during the September protests - have dominated much of the election campaign, which officially ended on Monday ahead of a two-day cooling-off period.
In Kathmandu, many candidates, wearing marigold garlands and vermillion smeared on their forehead walked the streets, rode on trucks and appeared from the ‚ÅÝsunroof of ‚Äãcars, to woo voters one last time before the polls.
But some voters are still ‚Äãon the fence.
"Old parties did nothing except corruption," said Ramkrishna Pandey, 50, discussing politics with half a dozen others at a newspaper kiosk on the outskirts of the ‚Äãcapital.
"New ones don't seem any better. I have not decided who to support."

item.title: China to build 'birth-friendly society', refine social security system
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China said on Thursday that it would build a "childbirth-friendly society" in the next five years as it pledged to ‚Äãaddress concerns over employment, education, medical care, health and income, according to ‚Äåan official government report.
Authorities will improve population services and respond proactively to population ageing, including "promoting high-quality, full employment, improving the income distribution system, and refining the social security system."
The announcement comes after China's ‚Äãpopulation fell for a fourth consecutive year in 2025, as the birth rate ‚Äãplunged to a record low, official data showed in January, with ‚ÅÝexperts warning of further decline.
China's population has been shrinking since 2022 and is ageing rapidly, ‚Äãcomplicating Beijing's plan to boost domestic consumption and rein in debt.
New policies will be ‚Äãintroduced to promote "high quality development of silver economy", targeted at those aged 60 and older, with elderly care services to be increased, particularly in rural areas, the report said.
Authorities will also draw ‚Äãup measures to hone supportive policies designed for seniors including pension finance, wellness and ‚Äãcare, it said.
By 2035, the number of people aged over 60 is set to hit 400 ‚Äåmillion - ‚ÅÝroughly equal to the populations of the United States and Italy combined - meaning hundreds of millions of people are set to leave the workforce at a time when pension budgets are already stretched .
China has already increased retirement ages, with men now expected to ‚Äãwork until they are ‚Äã63 rather than ‚ÅÝ60, and women until they are 58 rather than 55.
China will foster "positive attitudes toward marriage and childbearing," the report said, adding ‚Äãthat it would boost housing support for families with children.
Services for ‚Äãwomen in ‚ÅÝearly stages of pregnancy as well as reproductive health would be improved while authorities aimed to better prevent and treat birth defects.
Authorities will also refine policies on free preschool ‚ÅÝeducation ‚Äãand increase the supply of regular senior secondary school ‚Äãplaces, with government spending on education mandated to be higher than 4% of GDP, the report said.

item.title: Who is contesting Nepal's polls and what is at stake?
item.description:
March 5 - Nepal votes in a general election on Thursday, its first since deadly youth-led anti-graft protests toppled the government ‚Äãof then-Prime Minister K.P. Sharma Oli in September.
Here is a look at ‚Äåthe key contenders, and what is at stake.
Nearly 19 million of Nepal's 30 million people are eligible to vote in the for the 275-member assembly.
About one million of the voters - most ‚Äãof them youth - were added after last year's protests , which killed 77 people ‚Äãand injured more than 2,000.
While direct contests will decide 165 seats, ‚ÅÝwhich means the person who gets the most votes will win, the rest ‚Äãwill be filled through proportional representation, where seats are allocated to parties in proportion ‚Äãto their vote share.
Election authorities say 65 political parties are in the fray.
Apart from corruption, job creation is among the main issues, analysts say, with about a fifth of the population ‚Äãliving in poverty, and high youth unemployment.
Ties with India and China, which border Nepal ‚Äãand are among its major trade partners, will also be a factor in the election as the ‚Äålandlocked ‚ÅÝnation negotiates a balance between the Asian powers.
While India accounts for two-thirds of Nepal's international trade, China accounts for 14% and has also lent the country - among the world's poorest - more than $130 million, according to the World Bank.
Rapper-turned-politician and former Kathmandu ‚Äãmayor Balendra Shah, ‚Äã35, of the centrist ‚ÅÝRastriya Swatantra Party is among the frontrunners for prime minister.
Facing him in the Jhapa 5 constituency is four-time prime minister Oli , 74, of ‚Äãthe Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist‚ÄìLeninist), vying for the top post again ‚Äãbut facing ‚ÅÝan uphill battle to win back young voters who ousted him barely six months ago.
Other contenders include the centrist Nepali Congress party's 49-year-old Gagan Thapa and three-time prime ‚ÅÝminister Pushpa ‚ÄãKamal Dahal, 71, who now leads the Nepali Communist ‚ÄãParty.
Oli has been a liberal communist since the 1990s while Dahal led a bloody Maoist insurgency for a ‚Äãdecade before joining mainstream politics in 2006.

item.title: Australia, Canada sign new deals on critical minerals
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada said on Thursday they had signed new agreements ‚Äåon critical minerals as Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney made a landmark address to the Australian parliament, a sign of the developing bond between the "middle powers".
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan and India. His ‚Äãstop in Australia included the first address to Australia's parliament by a Canadian leader since 2007.
"In ‚Äãa world of great power rivalry, middle powers have a choice: compete for favour ‚ÅÝor combine for strength," he told lawmakers.
Introducing Carney in parliament, Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said his address ‚Äãrepresented the closeness of the ties between the two nations.
"Australia and Canada are middle powers in a world ‚Äãthat is changing. We cannot change it back, but we can back ourselves, back our citizens, and back each other," he said.
Albanese told a press conference that Australia would join Canada's G7 critical minerals production alliance.
"We have agreed to deepen ‚Äãour relationship across several areas, building on our joint declaration of intent on critical minerals that we signed ‚Äãlast year," he told a press conference.
The G7 alliance is a Canada-led initiative to diversify and secure global critical minerals ‚Äåproduction ‚ÅÝand supply.
Canada and Australia together produce about a third of global lithium and uranium, as well as more than 40% of global iron ore.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and processing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
Canada believes ‚Äãthat the best way ‚Äãto address the issue ‚ÅÝof concentrated supply of critical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson ‚Äãtold Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated A$1.2 billion ($850 million) to build a ‚Äãcritical minerals stockpile, ‚ÅÝbeginning with antimony, gallium and rare earths.
That will now be more closely aligned with Canada's defence stockpiling regime that has a similar aim, Albanese said.
"There's a lot Canada and Australia can do together on critical minerals as producer ‚ÅÝnations," ‚ÄãAustralian Resources Minister Madeleine King told Reuters ahead of Carney's visit.
Australia ‚Äãand Canada will also deepen cooperation in areas including defence and maritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, the two leaders said.
($1 = 1.4122 Australian ‚Äãdollars)

item.title: China's defence spending to rise 7% in 2026 vs 7.2% set for 2025
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China's 2026 defence spending will rise 7%, a copy of an official government ‚Äãreport reviewed by Reuters showed on Thursday, slightly ‚Äålower than the 2025 increase of 7.2% but roughly in line with rises over the last decade.
Regional military attaches and security analysts ‚Äãare closely watching the budget as China's military ‚Äãmodernisation effort continues apace and its deployments increase ‚ÅÝacross East Asia amid regional tensions.
The on-going rise in ‚Äãspending also reflects President Xi Jinping's goal of completing ‚Äãthat modernisation by 2035, with China's military developing new advanced missiles, ships, submarines and surveillance technologies.
The budget announcement comes amid an on-going ‚Äãcorruption crackdown that was sparked by the highest-profile ‚Äãpurge of upper military ranks in decades.
China's two most senior generals ‚Äåhave ‚ÅÝbecome ensnared in disciplinary investigations, with Zhang Youxia - a veteran military ally of Xi - placed under investigation in January and He Weidong expelled in October last year.
The International ‚ÄãInstitute of ‚ÄãStrategic Studies said ‚ÅÝin a report last month that rises in Chinese military spending were consistently ‚Äãoutpacing the rest of Asia amid a global ‚Äãsurge ‚ÅÝin defence budgets.
China's share of the total amount spent on the military in Asia grew to almost 44% in ‚ÅÝ2025, ‚Äãup from an average of ‚Äã37% between 2010 and 2020, the report said.

item.title: China to support Hong Kong's integration with country's overall development
item.description:
BEIJING, March 5 (Reuters) - China will ensure ‚ÄãHong Kong ‚Äåis more effectively governed in ‚Äãaccordance with ‚Äãthe law, in ‚ÅÝa copy ‚Äãof an official ‚Äãgovernment report reviewed by Reuters on ‚ÄãThursday.
The ‚Äãreport also said China ‚Äåwould ‚ÅÝsupport Hong Kong and Macau in better ‚Äãintegrating ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝcontributing to the ‚Äãcountry's overall ‚Äãdevelopment.

item.title: Australia to join G7 critical minerals alliance, Canada's Carney says
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 5 (Reuters) - Australia and Canada on Thursday signed a series of new agreements on critical ‚Äãminerals, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said, including Australia ‚Äåjoining the G7 minerals alliance.
Western nations have been attempting to diversify their supply chains away from China, which still controls the majority of production and ‚Äãprocessing of critical minerals, essential for semiconductors and defence applications.
"Earlier ‚Äãtoday, we signed a series of new agreements on ‚ÅÝcritical minerals, including Australia joining the G7 minerals alliance ‚Äì the largest ‚Äãgrouping of trusted democratic mineral reserves in the world," Carney said ‚Äãin a speech to Australia's parliament, on his final day of a three-day visit to the country.
The two countries produce around a third of global ‚Äãlithium and uranium, as well as over 40% of global iron ‚Äãore.
Canada believes that the best way to address the issue of concentrated supply ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝcritical minerals is through a production alliance or a buyers' club rather than just a price floor, Energy and Mining Minister Tim Hodgson told Reuters on Tuesday.
Australia has already allocated funds to build ‚Äãa critical minerals ‚Äãstockpile, beginning ‚ÅÝwith antimony and gallium.
Carney is on a multi-leg trip across the Asia-Pacific region also taking in Japan ‚Äãand India, with his stop in Australia aimed ‚Äãat bolstering ‚ÅÝrelations between the two so-called "middle powers".
As well as critical minerals, Australia and Canada are also expected to deepen cooperation in areas including defence ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãmaritime security, trade and artificial intelligence, ‚ÄãCarney's office said ahead of the visit.
(Reporting by Alasdair Pal in Sydney, editing by Jamie ‚ÄãFreed)
(( Alasdair.Pal@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab ; +61 291 717 228; Reuters Messaging: ))
Keywords: AUSTRALIA CANADA/

item.title: Iran launches wave of missiles at Israel; US Republicans block measure to halt US air campaign
item.description:
DUBAI/JERUSALEM/ANKARA, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran launched a wave of missiles at Israel early on Thursday, sending millions of residents into bomb shelters as the U.S.-Israel war with Iran entered ‚Äãits sixth day and just hours after moves to halt the U.S. air assault were blocked in Washington.
Republican senators in Washington voted against a motion aimed ‚Äåat stopping the air campaign and requiring that military action be authorised by Congress, leaving President Donald Trump's power to direct the war largely unbound, as the conflict continues to widen across the Middle East and beyond.
The U.S. Senate voted 53 to 47 not to advance the resolution, largely along party lines, with all but one Republican voting against the procedural motion and all but one Democrat supporting it.
The U.S.‚ÄìIran war has ‚Äãwidened sharply, with a U.S. submarine sinking an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka on Wednesday, killing at least 80 people, and NATO air defences destroying an Iranian ballistic ‚Äãmissile fired towards Turkey.
The escalation came as the powerful son of Iran's slain supreme leader emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, suggesting Tehran ‚ÅÝwas not about to buckle to pressure from the United States and Israel's military campaign that has killed hundreds and convulsed global markets .
The missile incident is the first time that Turkey ‚Äì ‚Äãwhich borders Iran and has NATO's second-largest military ‚Äì has been drawn into the conflict, but U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said there was no sense that it would trigger the Atlantic ‚Äãalliance's collective-defence clause.
The war continued to paralyse shipping through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, choking off vital Middle East oil and gas flows. Trump has pledged to provide insurance and naval escorts for ships to contain soaring costs, with oil prices rising on Thursday . At least 200 vessels remain anchored off the coast, according to Reuters estimates.
The U.S. Navy will escort oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz "as soon as it ‚Äãcan" but is focused on the conflict for now, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Fox News on Wednesday.
"No, not yet ... We'll do that as soon as we can. ‚ÄãRight now, our Navy, and of course, our military, is focused on other things, which is disarming this Iranian regime," Wright said, when asked if any commercial vessels had requested U.S. Navy assistance in ‚Äåthe Gulf.
Asian ‚ÅÝshares rallied on Thursday after days of sharp losses, while U.S. stocks closed up on Wednesday on hopes that the war might end soon. Some traders said the improved sentiment followed a New York Times report that Iranian intelligence had reached out to the CIA early in the war about a path towards ending it.
A source from the Iranian intelligence ministry rejected the article as "absolute lies and psychological warfare in the midst of war", Iran's semi-official news agency Tasnim reported.
Repatriation flights departed the Middle East on Wednesday as governments rushed to bring home tens of thousands ‚Äãof citizens stranded by the war.
Commercial air traffic ‚Äãremained largely absent across much of ‚ÅÝthe region, with major Gulf hubs including Dubai, the world's busiest airport for international passengers, affected.
Plans were in doubt for a funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei , 86, killed by Israeli forces on Saturday in the first assassination of a nation's top ruler by an airstrike.
The ‚Äãbody had been expected to lie in state in a vast Tehran mosque from Wednesday evening, but Iran announced that three days ‚Äãof farewell ceremonies had ‚ÅÝbeen indefinitely postponed and no funeral date had been announced.
Two Iranian sources, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Reuters that Mojtaba Khamenei , son of Iran's slain supreme leader, was not in Tehran when his father was killed.
Iran said the Assembly of Experts that will select the new leader would announce its decision soon, only the second time it has done so since the Islamic Republic's founding ‚ÅÝin 1979.
Assembly ‚Äãmember Ayatollah Ahmad Khatami told state TV the candidates had already been identified but did not name them.
Israel ‚Äãsaid it would hunt down whoever was chosen.
Other candidates for supreme leader include Hassan Khomeini , grandson of the Islamic Republic's founder and a champion of the reformist faction sidelined in recent decades.

item.title: Pakistani man says Iran forced him into plot to kill Trump, media say
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man accused of planning to kill President Donald Trump told jurors on Wednesday that he did not willingly work with Iran's elite ‚ÄãIslamic Revolutionary Guards Corps to devise the plot, media said.
The Justice ‚ÄåDepartment accused Asif Merchant of trying to recruit people in the United States in the plan targeting Trump and other U.S. politicians in retaliation for Washington's killing of the ‚ÄãCorps' top commander, Qassem Soleimani .
The Corps has a central role in Iran, with ‚Äãits combination of military and economic power and an intelligence ‚ÅÝnetwork.
"I was not wanting to do this so willingly," the New York ‚ÄãTimes quoted Merchant as telling a court during his trial for terrorism and ‚Äãmurder-for-hire charges, adding that he participated to protect his family in Tehran.
Prosecutors rejected Merchant's claim, citing a "lack of evidentiary support for a true duress or coercion," according to a ‚Äãletter sent on Tuesday to the judge in the case dating from ‚Äã2024.
According to the newspaper, Merchant said he had never been ordered to kill a specific ‚Äåperson ‚ÅÝbut that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
In addition to Trump, these were Joe Biden, the president at the time; Nikki Haley, who unsuccessfully sought the Republican nomination for ‚Äãthe 2024 presidential ‚Äãelection.
Lawyers for Merchant ‚ÅÝdid not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately comment.
The trial started last ‚Äãweek, days before Trump ordered strikes on Iran carried out ‚Äãwith Israel ‚ÅÝthat killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and top officials in the Middle Eastern nation.
Trump cited an alleged Iranian plot when he spoke to ABC News on ‚ÅÝSunday about ‚Äãa joint U.S.-Israeli operation that killed Khamenei, ‚Äãsaying, "I got him before he got me."
Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump and other U.S. ‚Äãofficials.

item.title: Iranian sailors recovering in Sri Lankan hospital after US submarine attack, authorities say
item.description:
GALLE, Sri Lanka, March 5 (Reuters) - Iranian sailors who survived a U.S. submarine strike in the Indian Ocean were recovering at ‚Äãa hospital in the Sri Lankan port city of ‚ÄåGalle, authorities said on Thursday, a day after at least 87 were killed in the attack.
Authorities at the National Hospital in Galle and ‚Äãnavy sources said 87 bodies were brought in by ‚Äãmilitary rescuers who responded to an early-morning distress call ‚ÅÝfrom the IRIS Dena on Wednesday.
Search and rescue operations for ‚Äãan estimated 60 people on board who remain unaccounted for would ‚Äãcontinue on Thursday, authorities said.
The 32 rescued sailors were being treated for minor injuries and could be released from hospital on Thursday, authorities said. ‚ÄãTwo policemen guarded the entrance to ward No. 58 of ‚Äãthe hospital as nurses milled about and doctors conducted morning rounds.
The attack, which ‚Äådramatically ‚ÅÝwidens the scope of the war, happened hundreds of miles across the Indian Ocean from the Gulf, where U.S. and Israeli forces are striking Iran and Tehran is retaliating with missile and ‚Äãdrone attacks.
"An American ‚Äãsubmarine sank an ‚ÅÝIranian warship that thought it was safe in international waters," U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äãat the Pentagon. "Instead, it was sunk by a ‚Äãtorpedo. ‚ÅÝQuiet death."
A Pentagon video purporting to have captured the attack showed the warship being hit by a huge explosion, which blew apart ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãrear of the vessel, lifting it from ‚Äãthe water, and causing it to begin sinking from the stern.

item.title: Singapore police announce arrest of 3 linked to alleged scam syndicate Prince Group
item.description: SINGAPORE, March 5 (Reuters) - Singapore police have announced arrests of three Singaporeans as part of investigations into alleged transnational scam ‚Äãsyndicate Prince Group and its founder and chairman, Chen ‚ÄåZhi , who is in Chinese detention.

item.title: Pentagon identifies two soldiers killed in Iran war
item.description:
LOS ANGELES, March 4 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Wednesday identified two more soldiers who were killed in the war ‚Äãagainst Iran.
The two Army Reserve soldiers died ‚Äåon Sunday in a drone attack on a U.S. military facility in Port Shuaiba, Kuwait that also killed four other reservists .
The ‚ÄãPentagon said Major Jeffrey O'Brien, 45, of Iowa, ‚Äãwas killed in the attack and announced ‚ÅÝthe "believed death" of Chief Warrant Officer 3 Robert M. ‚ÄãMarzan, 54, of Sacramento, California. Positive identification of Marzan ‚Äãwill be completed by the medical examiner, the Pentagon said.
Both O'Brien and Marzan served in the 103rd Sustainment Command from ‚ÄãDes Moines, Iowa, part of the Army's global ‚Äãlogistics and supply operation.
The U.S. military on Tuesday identified four soldiers ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe same command who also died in the drone attack, marking the first casualties of the war.
The military identified the soldiers as:
The announcement comes as the conflict intensifies, with U.S. Defense Secretary Pete ‚ÅÝHegseth ‚Äãsaying on Wednesday that the ‚ÄãU.S. "can sustain this fight easily for as long as we need to."

item.title: Iran conflict's impact on energy temporary and a 'small price,' US energy secretary says
item.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - The impact of the Iran conflict on energy markets will ‚Äåbe temporary and a "small price" to pay for U.S. military goals, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright told Fox News on Wednesday.
U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and the subsequent response by Tehran have widened regional tensions and paralyzed ‚Äãshipping through the Strait of Hormuz , disrupting vital Middle East oil and gas flows and sending energy ‚Äãprices higher. Oil prices rose on Thursday in Asia amid growing concern over the prolonged ‚ÅÝclosure of the Strait of Hormuz.
"This will definitely be temporary," Wright told the "Ingraham Angle" program on Fox ‚ÄãNews.
"We have the world just massively well-supplied, abundant oil around the world, and American production at ‚Äãrecord highs. So we'll get through this, it'll be a bump on the road."
U.S. President Donald Trump has pledged to provide insurance and naval escorts for ships exporting energy from the region to contain soaring costs.
Wright said the ‚Äãclosure of the Strait of Hormuz was a temporary event, and that the U.S. Navy would ‚Äãsoon be escorting oil tankers through the waterway that typically carries about a fifth of the world's daily oil ‚Äåconsumption.
"We'll ‚ÅÝdo that as soon as we can. Right now, our navy, and of course, our military, is focused on other things, which is disarming this Iranian regime," Wright said, when asked if any commercial vessels had requested U.S. Navy assistance.
Trump's decision to attack Iran in a campaign that has killed ‚ÄãSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ‚ÄãKhamenei , whose government Trump has ‚ÅÝaccused of developing a nuclear weapon, will likely ratchet up uncertainty for the U.S. economy.
Although the U.S. is more buffered from energy shocks than many ‚Äãother developed countries because of its domestic oil and gas production, the ‚Äãglobal impact on ‚ÅÝtrade, prices and investment could spill back and undermine what had been a developing bullish outlook for 2026.
"Are we going to have a transient bump up as we've seen in gasoline prices? We do ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãlittle bit, but I think a very small price to ‚Äãpay to remove the nation that's killed more American soldiers in the last 20 years than any nation on earth," ‚ÄãWright said on Fox News.

item.title: Exclusive: Ukraine's F-16 jets were starved of US-made missiles for weeks
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine's F-16 fighter jets didn't have enough missiles to shoot down Russian drones and missiles for more than three weeks after supplies from Kyiv's partners dried up just as Moscow was preparing a massive winter air campaign , three sources said.
The acute shortage from late November to mid-December, which has not been previously reported, lays bare the vulnerability of Ukraine's air defences which ‚Äãrely heavily on Western allies for missiles and defence systems to repel frequent Russian strikes .
Kyiv has often complained about a lack of weapons since the full-scale war began more than four years ago, sounding the alarm over critical shortages in recent months ‚Äåwhile seeking not to anger U.S. President Donald Trump's administration.
The need for Western arms is unlikely to diminish any time soon, with no end to the Ukraine conflict in sight, and with the war against Iran raging, competition to secure defensive weapons in the Middle East and beyond is likely to intensify .
The three sources, all with direct knowledge of the situation, said Ukraine only had a handful of U.S.-made AIM-9 "Sidewinder" air-to-air missiles for its entire squadron of F-16s when supplies stopped.
Hundreds of thousands of Ukrainians have survived the worst of a bitter winter without heating, electricity and running water as a result of an intensifying Russian onslaught on the energy system that Ukraine has been unable to ‚Äãcompletely repel.
Despite Ukraine's vocal lobbying, concrete examples of how shortages affect its defence capabilities are normally kept secret. In this case, one of the sources told Reuters that Ukraine had nothing to put on its jets for almost a month.
All three sources asked not ‚Äãto be named to describe sensitive battlefield vulnerabilities caused by interruptions to weapons flows.
Reuters could not establish the cause of the shortages, nor whether the delays were down to U.S. or European foot dragging. The first ‚ÅÝsource said Ukraine's foreign partners had told Kyiv they had no available stocks, without specifying which partners.
In response to a request for White House comment, a U.S. official said Washington was committed to stopping the war and supported Ukraine by selling U.S. weapons through NATO. The official said ‚ÄãTrump's administration had made "tremendous progress" towards a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia.
The U.S. Department of Defense, Ukraine's Air Force and the Ukrainian presidency did not respond to questions.
During the shortage, F-16 pilots flew daytime sorties and tried to hit drones with rotary cannons, a second source said, ‚Äãadding that it was too dangerous to conduct such missions in the dark even though Russian drone attacks usually happen at night.
Pilots also tried using missiles that had failed to fire on earlier missions in the hope they would work after maintenance, the source said. In some cases they were successful.
Ukraine's F-16 pilots have relied heavily on variants of AIM-9 missiles known as Limas and Mikes that were produced in the 1970s and 1980s, the sources said.
Though decades old, those missiles have provided Ukraine with a relatively cheap way to intercept Russian drones and cruise missiles, the three people said.
The shortfall was plugged in December when Ukraine received AIM-9 air-to-air missiles from ‚Äãpartners, the three sources said, shortly before a major Russian attack. They declined to name the country or countries behind the delivery, citing secrecy.
Reuters could not determine the impact of the temporary missile shortfall. The first source said it had not coincided with the largest Russian attacks of the winter.
A ‚Äãfourth source, who also declined to be named, said NATO members Germany and Canada had supplied Sidewinder missiles in recent months and confirmed there had been a "bit of a dip" in supplies before, although they declined to say why.
Germany's defence ministry declined to comment on specific deliveries or weapons for security reasons. Germany ‚Äåhas been one of ‚ÅÝUkraine's biggest military and financial backers since 2022.
Canada's Department of National Defence told Reuters it was in the process of donating AIM-9M-8 missiles from Canadian Armed Forces stocks. "This new donation will complement the past donation of hundreds of Canadian AIM missiles and related components that are being used by Ukraine for its air defence."
Trump introduced a system for supplying U.S. weapons to Ukraine, replacing direct military aid sent under his predecessor Joe Biden.
Under the so-called PURL (Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List) mechanism , the United States sells weapons to NATO allies for delivery to Ukraine.
Asked to comment, a NATO official said PURL was providing crucial U.S. material and, since summer, had supplied about 75% of all missiles for Ukraine's Patriot air defence batteries and 90% of ammunition for other air defence systems.
Nevertheless, Ukraine still faces a challenge to secure enough missiles for its sprawling air defence network.
Russia fires several hundred ‚Äãattack drones and missiles during its large attacks, and Ukraine tries to ‚Äãdown them with gunfire from trucks, electronic jamming, interceptor drones, as ‚ÅÝwell as air-to-air and ground-to-air missiles.
President Volodymyr Zelenskiy issued an urgent plea about the shortage of ammunition in January, especially for the U.S.-made Patriot system that Ukraine relies on to shoot down Russia's Iskander ballistic missiles.
F-16s, which were supplied by Kyiv's European partners in 2024, are part of the airborne component of Ukraine's air defence network, along with helicopters and other warplanes.
F-16s have intercepted 2,000 drones and missiles during sorties in an air defence ‚Äãrole, the second source said.
Ukraine and its partners have not revealed how many F-16s are fighting in Ukraine. The second source said the number was in "the dozens" but declined to elaborate further.
The F-16s can ‚Äãcarry AIM-9s, or more sophisticated AIM-120 missiles. ‚ÅÝThey are produced by Raytheon, a unit of U.S. defence group RTX Corp (RTX.N) , opens new tab .
When asked for comment on the shortages, RTX referred Reuters to the U.S. government.
Each AIM-120 missile costs well over one million dollars, two of the three sources said, meaning they are not typically used at scale to counter cheaply produced Russian attack drones.
Those missiles are also used in Ukraine's Norwegian-made NASAMS surface-to-air systems, meaning that during the supply squeeze their operations were curtailed, the last of the three sources said.
That person also said there had been a shortage of U.S.-made RIM-7 missiles which Ukraine has used in modified Soviet-era air defence systems since ‚ÅÝthe 2022 invasion.
The Norwegian ‚Äãdefence ministry said the government had delivered a "substantial number" of interceptors for NASAMS earlier this winter ... "so that the NASAMS system can continue to protect Ukrainian citizens from deadly air ‚Äãstrikes."

item.title: Chinese military flights around Taiwan fall, Trump-Xi meeting may be factor
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 5 (Reuters) - Chinese air force activity around Taiwan has fallen sharply in recent weeks, with ‚Äåno flights at all in the past week, a sudden drop in what had been daily military manoeuvres that could signal Beijing is recalibrating its pressure on Taipei.
China has dispatched 460 military planes - from fighter jets to drones - into Taiwan's air defence identification zone ‚Äãso far this year, a 46.5% drop compared with a year ago, according to Taiwan government data ‚Äãcompiled by research group Secure Taiwan Associate Corporation (STA).
In February, Taiwan detected 190 such Chinese aircraft, ‚ÅÝthe lowest monthly tally since its defence ministry began publishing detailed daily figures in 2022 on what Taipei ‚Äãsees an ongoing pressure campaign by Beijing which has been honing its ability to attack the island, including with war ‚Äãgames .
Chinese President Xi Jinping may be toning down visible pressure tactics to create a better atmosphere ahead of his anticipated meeting with U.S. President Donald Trump at the end of the month, two Taiwanese officials said.
Facing Trump, "Beijing might be trying to create a false ‚Äãimpression: I am peaceful, I am moving toward peace, so you should stop selling weapons to Taiwan," a senior ‚ÄãTaiwan security official told Reuters, requesting anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter.
Neither China's defence ministry nor its Taiwan Affairs ‚ÄåOffice responded ‚ÅÝto requests for comment.
China, which considers the island its own territory, has not dispatched any military planes near Taiwan since February 27, marking an unprecedented six-day lull in such operations that coincided with the war in Iran, according to Tristan Tang, a researcher at STA.
