Unlike this extended lull, past pauses were brief, usually tied to typhoons or ‚ÄãChinese holidays.
Another interpretation is China's ‚Äãongoing military corruption purge .
Su Tzu-yun, ‚ÅÝa research fellow at Taiwan's top military think tank, the Institute for National Defence and Security Research, said the purge could be the "primary reason", with Chinese forces undergoing changes ‚Äãto their command structure that are likely undermining overall readiness.
A Taiwan defence official, also ‚Äãspeaking on condition ‚ÅÝof anonymity, suggested Beijing is using the lull to digest lessons from recent large drills.
However, China's navy and coast guard remain active, according to Taiwan's defence ministry, showing Beijing had only dialled down the more politically sensitive air activity.
"Just because they're ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãcoming now doesn't mean they won't come back in the future, and ‚Äãwe can't rule out that they may be preparing for an even larger operation," said another senior Taiwan security official.
"We should not project ‚Äãany change in Beijing's intentions based solely on a few days of activity."

item.title: Perishables to plane parts stranded as Middle East conflict hits air cargo
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Shipments ranging from fresh produce to airplane parts are in limbo as ‚Äãan escalating Middle East conflict reduces the world's air cargo capacity by more than one-fifth and pushes up freight rates, with executives bracing ‚Äåfor backlogs of goods.
The U.S. and Israeli air war against Iran has grounded passenger and freighter flights across the region, including in key global air cargo hubs Doha and Dubai.
That has led to a 22% reduction in global air cargo capacity between February 28 and March 3 compared with a four-day period last month before the Chinese New Year holiday, according to data from aviation and logistics consulting ‚Äãfirm Aevean.
"It is an absolute halt of the supply chain to the Middle East," said Abdol Moaberry, CEO of Florida-based GA Telesis, which provides aircraft ‚Äãparts and repair services and is unable to move parts to the region or to receive parts that need repairs.
Air cargo accounts ‚ÅÝfor about one-third of global trade by value, according to airline trade group International Air Transport Association, with goods ranging from Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab products to temperature-controlled pharmaceuticals, fresh fruit ‚Äãand auto parts carried both in the bellies of passenger planes and on dedicated freighters.
Middle Eastern carriers account for about 13% of global air cargo capacity, according ‚Äãto Aevean.
Brian Bourke, chief commercial officer at SEKO Logistics, said Europe and the Asia-Pacific region, which rely more heavily on Middle Eastern hubs, would experience a greater impact.
"Companies in the U.S. should be paying attention," he said. "But it's not as immediate as if you're in Europe or Asia or Australia."
Air cargo capacity on the corridor from Asia to the Middle East to Europe declined 39% since ‚Äãthe start of the conflict, even as direct capacity between China and Europe increased by 26%, Aevean said.
Chinese airlines could hold a competitive edge, said Joshua Ng, director ‚Äãat Alton Aviation Consultancy, as they can fly through Russian airspace where many rivals are banned, giving them a shorter flight time and lower operating costs.
According to consultancy Freightos' air index, rates ‚Äåfrom Southeast ‚ÅÝAsia to Europe have climbed more than 6% to $3.82/kg since Friday, with South Asia rates up 3% to Europe and 5% to the United States.
"Whenever operational disruptions increase costs or reduce capacity, air cargo rates tend to experience upward pressure," Ng said. "In the near term, shippers may begin to see higher spot rates on the Asia‚ÄìEurope corridor, particularly if the disruption persists and capacity constraints continue."
Stefan Paul, CEO of Swiss logistics group Kuehne+Nagel (KNIN.S) , opens new tab , said on Tuesday that by the beginning of next week there could be "certain backlogs arising ‚Äãin Southeast Asia and in China for ‚Äãthe European and the U.S. marketplace."
Critical aircraft parts often move by air, and prolonged disruptions could slow Middle East carriers' efforts to restore flights. In 2025, aerospace air shipments to and from the region made up 6.7% of global aerospace shipments, Kuehne+Nagel said.
"Even if the aircraft is not ‚Äãflying because of the war, the aircraft still has to be airworthy for when the skies open up," said Amyr ‚ÄãQureshi, senior vice president ‚ÅÝat Aventure Aviation.
Aventure supports about 70 airlines, including Etihad Airways, Emirates and Qatar Airways, and stocks key parts such as engine valves and avionics at its U.S. distribution center in Atlanta.
Qureshi said many carriers have been calling urgently for parts, as sometimes a single component needed to release an aircraft from maintenance. "If the part doesn't arrive on time the airplane sits ‚ÅÝin the ‚Äãhangar more," he said, describing it as a "domino effect."
In better news, Qureshi said some parts that were ‚Äãdue to ship from Dubai airport for repair in Atlanta were picked up by a freight forwarder on Wednesday.
"There is a gradual improvement," he said. "But these things are so unpredictable‚Ä¶ tomorrow everything comes to a standstill."

item.title: Charting the widening impact of the Iran crisis on energy markets
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, March 5 (Reuters) - The repercussions of the ongoing U.S.-Iran war are being felt across the world, with U.S. fuel prices, European natural gas costs and Asian tanker freight rates all jumping sharply since strikes began at the weekend.
The transmission of market jitters through the shipping, fuel and power sectors ‚Äãillustrates how interconnected global energy markets remain, despite the push for more home-grown energy supplies and greater energy security in recent years.
Here's a breakdown of key ‚Äåmarkets that have been impacted since the conflagration started over the weekend, and the main data points to keep track of as the skirmishes drag on and the disruptions to energy product flows potentially worsen.
The global tanker fleet has been one of the sectors hardest hit in the aftermath of last weekend's bombing campaign and the subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz .
Around a fifth of global oil, fuel and LNG travels through that well-known ‚Äãmaritime chokepoint, so virtually all carriers of energy liquids have taken a hit from the traffic disruptions and the subsequent scramble to reroute shipments.
Quotes for shipping crude oil from ‚Äãthe Middle East to China have captured the ferocity of the logistics crunch, with the cost of chartering a very large crude carrier (VLCC) jumping ‚ÅÝfrom around $120,000 a day last week to more than $450,000 a day since fighting erupted.
China is the world's largest oil importer and domestic crude oil futures prices have also surged this week, ‚Äãrising by 31% since last Friday compared to 12% increases in Brent and U.S. crude oil futures over the same period.
However, it's not just China that has been impacted by the supply chain ‚Äãbedlam.
Fuel tanker rates from Singapore to Japan and from the U.S. to Europe have also jumped higher this week in response to the growing tightness of global energy supplies and a more panicky mindset among all oil and fuel buyers.
Europe's natural gas markets have also recorded steep price gains this week as several European nations remain heavily reliant on gas for power and industry and yet have depleted local gas stockpiles to multi-year lows ‚Äãin recent months.
Benchmark European natural gas prices have bolted sharply higher in response to the news that Qatar has paused loadings of liquefied natural gas since its main gas liquefaction facilities were hit ‚Äãby Iranian drones over the weekend.
Nearby European gas futures contracts have soared by close to 70% since Friday, while even prices for December 2026 have gained around 40% this week on expectations of an enduring ‚Äåtightness in global ‚ÅÝgas supplies while Qatar remains offline.
Forward prices for the global benchmark for crude oil - Brent - have also lurched higher this week, with nearby May futures rising by around 12% since Friday and year-end prices rising by around 3%.
Growing worries about how long shipping channels from the Middle East will remain clogged have served to boost sentiment across all major oil markets, especially as storage tanks across the Middle East rapidly fill up and force producers to consider cutting output if they can't resume exports soon.
U.S. gasoline futures prices have followed a similar trend, even though the U.S. is ‚Äãa major crude oil producer and exporter.
Nearby U.S. ‚Äãgasoline contracts have climbed by around 10% ‚ÅÝsince late last week and year-end prices are up by 4% as fuel distributors responded to the outlook for tighter global oil supplies and higher logistics costs.
U.S. President Trump has announced steps to restore ship traffic in the Middle East in an effort to turn energy costs lower again ‚Äãfor U.S. consumers, including a proposal for the U.S. Navy to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.
However, with the U.S., Israel and ‚ÄãIran all still stepping up ‚ÅÝtheir bombing efforts and widening the damage done to energy and logistics channels across the Middle East, energy prices are likely to continue stretching higher for the near term at least.
That means even steeper gains in power, fuel and freight prices could emerge in the weeks ahead in all major energy markets.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), ‚ÅÝyour essential ‚Äãnew source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . ‚ÄãSubscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.title: Jet fuel's huge price surge points to coming pain from Iran war
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 5 (Reuters) - The explosion in jet fuel prices in Asia is an early indicator that the economic pain of the war in the Middle East is about to become reality for energy consumers.
Jet fuel prices in Asia's trading hub Singapore rocketed 72% to $225.44 ‚Äãa barrel on Wednesday, a record high, on worries about future supplies given the disruption of about 20 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil ‚Äåand refined product shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.
The spot price of jet kerosene has now gained 140% since the close of $93.45 a barrel on February 27, the day before the United States and Israel launched an aerial bombing campaign against Iran.
While U.S. President Donald Trump is making some belated efforts to assure that tankers will be able to transit the narrow waterway that carries about one-fifth of global oil consumption, ‚Äãthe market remains far from convinced.
Jet fuel is the part of the crude oil barrel most exposed to supply disruptions as it tends to have the lowest ‚Äãlevel of inventories given it needs to be stored in specialised tanks.
The surging spot price means that the profit margin for making ‚ÅÝa barrel of jet kerosene from Dubai crude has jumped to more than $100 a barrel, a level that suggests market participants are expecting a severe shortage in coming weeks.
The blowout ‚Äãin jet fuel prices is probably overdone, with levels far beyond what is justified even by a worst-case scenario of an extended effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
But it's ‚Äãworth noting that there are increasing signs of stress in Asian refiners, with reports of some plants lowering operating rates to preserve crude stockpiles, while others bring forward maintenance.
India's Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab has suspended fuel exports, two sources familiar with the matter said on Wednesday.
The state-run refiner, which runs a 300,000 bpd plant in the southern state of Karnataka, exports about 40% of its refined fuel output.
It's likely that other ‚Äãrefineries will follow MRPL's lead, especially those in India, which sources the bulk of its crude from the Middle East and will battle to find alternative suppliers at short ‚Äãnotice.
China has urged companies to suspend signing new contracts to export refined fuel, and to try and cancel shipments already committed, several industry and trade sources with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.
If confirmed ‚Äåthis would ‚ÅÝdramatically tighten regional product markets as China is one of the few countries with spare refining capacity and a large stockpile of crude oil.
It's also worth noting that much of the oil not getting through the Strait of Hormuz is medium-sour crude, a grade prized for its higher yield of middle distillates such as jet kerosene and diesel.
Even if refiners can source alternative crudes from producers in Africa and South America, these grades tend to be lighter and therefore produce more light distillates such as gasoline and naphtha.
This suggests that ‚Äãeven when refiners can maintain processing rates, ‚Äãthey may be forced to produce more ‚ÅÝlight distillates than needed and not enough middle distillates.
The profit margin for producing a barrel of gasoil, the building block for jet fuel and diesel, in Singapore jumped 30.4% on Wednesday to end at $47.69.
It has more than doubled since February 27, when it ended at $21.90 a ‚Äãbarrel and is now at levels last seen in the aftermath of the February 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.
It won't take ‚Äãlong for these elevated ‚ÅÝmargins to reach retail fuel prices in Asian countries with market pricing for products such as gasoline and diesel.
A sharp increase in fuel prices will drive an inflation spike, dent consumer spending, put a pause on some capital investment and lead central banks to contemplate raising interest rates.
The wider problem is that even if there is a resolution to the Iran conflict within the coming ‚ÅÝweeks that ‚Äãresults in the free movement of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, the damage from the existing disruption ‚Äãis now locked into the supply chain.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster ‚Äãthan ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Russian drone strikes foreign cargo ship near Ukraine Black Sea port
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - A Russian drone damaged a civilian Panama-flagged vessel ‚Äãthat was transporting corn near the Ukrainian ‚Äåport of Chornomorsk in the Black Sea Odesa region, the Ukrainian Sea Ports Authority said late on Wednesday.
The ‚Äãvessel was hit while en route ‚Äãout of the port, the ports authority said ‚ÅÝon the Telegram messaging app, without specifying ‚Äãthe extent of the damage to the ship.
The ‚ÄãUkrainian navy said one crew member had been hurt in the attack on the "BULL" vessel, which had left ‚Äãthe port and was heading towards the Bosphorus ‚ÄãStrait.
The captain refused assistance and evacuation of the injured person ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝcontinued on its designated route, the statement, posted on social media, added.
Ukraine ships about 90% of its exports via the Odesa port hub.
Russia ‚Äãin recent ‚Äãmonths has been ‚ÅÝattacking Ukraine's maritime export arteries, including ports, which are crucial for its ‚Äãforeign trade and the survival of ‚Äãits ‚ÅÝwartime economy.
The attacks on port infrastructure - causing a jump in logistics and freight costs - have hurt ‚ÅÝlocal businesses, ‚Äãforcing them to lower ‚Äãtheir prices to remain competitive on the global market.

item.title: Sri Lanka says trying to 'safeguard lives' on another Iranian ship off its coast
item.description:
GALLE, Sri Lanka, March 5 (Reuters) - Sri ‚ÄãLanka is ‚Äåtrying to "safeguard lives" on another ‚ÄãIranian ship ‚Äãoff its coast, ‚ÅÝthe country's ‚Äãcabinet spokesperson said ‚Äãon Thursday, adding that the vessel ‚Äãwas ‚Äãin the economic zone ‚Äåbeyond ‚ÅÝthe South Asian island nation's territorial waters.
"We ‚Äãare ‚Äãdoing ‚ÅÝour utmost to ‚Äãsafeguard lives," ‚Äãspokesperson Nalinda ‚ÅÝJayatissa said.

item.title: Islamist militants kill 14 Nigerian soldiers in Borno state, sources say
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, March 5 - Islamist militants killed at least 14 Nigerian soldiers and injured several others during ‚Äãtwo separate attacks on army bases in northeastern Borno state ‚Äåon Tuesday night, security sources said, as insurgents intensify attacks on the military.
Suspected Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP) fighters attacked the Nigerian Army's base in Ngoshe ‚Äãtown, Gwoza district, killing at least nine troops and ‚Äãa local imam, three army sources said late on Wednesday.
The ‚ÅÝattackers, who forced the troops to retreat from their base, ‚Äãseized guns and ammunition before abducting an unknown number of women, ‚Äãsaid the sources, who declined to be named because they were not authorised to speak to the media.
At the same time, militants also attacked another military base ‚Äãin Pulka, further north in Gwoza district, killing five soldiers, including ‚Äãa commanding officer, two other sources with knowledge of the attack said.
The injured troops ‚Äåwere ‚ÅÝtaken to Maiduguri city, the Borno state capital, for medical treatment.
Insurgents have been launching coordinated attacks aimed at stretching the military and limiting its ability to quickly bring reinforcements.
The Nigerian Army did not respond ‚Äãto a request ‚Äãfor comment.
Nigeria's military ‚ÅÝhas been into insurgent strongholds in the northeast this year as part of a renewed ‚Äãoffensive against militant groups.
But despite repeated operations, ISWAP and ‚ÄãBoko ‚ÅÝHaram continue to mount large-scale attacks, exploiting difficult terrain, porous borders and a weak state presence across parts of Nigeria's arid northeast.
Borno remains the ‚ÅÝepicentre of ‚Äãthe 17-year Islamist insurgency, which has ‚Äãkilled thousands and displaced 2 million people, according to humanitarian aid groups.

item.title: Europe can absorb this Mideast energy hit - but not much more
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - The energy price jolt from this week's Middle East shock has left Europe fretting about an economic hit akin to the invasion of Ukraine four years ago. But initial fears may be overdone if - and it's a big "if" - futures markets prove accurate.
As the weekend's attacks on Iran marked the start of a full-blown - now region-wide - war that could ‚Äãlast several weeks at least, European markets shivered at the prospect of yet another brutal energy squeeze spurring inflation and sapping demand.
Surging oil prices hit markets of big importing economies ‚Äåaround the world. But Europe's particular exposure to renewed disruption of natural gas supplies - already severed when Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022 - is a potential double whammy.
Although below Tuesday's peaks, crude oil is still up more than 10% from last week and at its highest levels in more than a year. European natural gas futures are still more than 50% up on last Friday and also at levels seen over 12 months ago.
On cue, the euro has dropped almost 2% against the dollar even as money markets wiped out ‚Äãany probability of another European Central Bank interest rate cut this year and have started to price in the small chance of a rate rise. Benchmark 10-year government borrowing rates jumped more than 10 basis points ‚Äãand euro stock indexes lost up to 6%.
Too much, or just the beginning?
A brief glimpse at the reaction to the Ukraine invasion puts all of that in ‚ÅÝperspective.
In the six months that followed the February 2022 attacks, European gas prices trebled, ECB rates rose 100 bps, German bund yields piled on 135 bps, the euro lost 16% against the dollar and euro ‚Äãstocks plunged 22%.
We're not in that boat, not yet at least.
Despite Qatar's halt to liquefied natural gas production on Wednesday after its installations were hit and exports blocked, the gas price surge is clearly nowhere near the initial ‚ÄãUkraine spike.
But one key area of difference between now and then is how markets price changes in long-term inflation expectations.
Using five-year, five-year forward inflation-linked swaps that cover the subsequent decade, those expectations soared by 70 bps in the seven months that followed the Ukraine invasion to 2%. And even though we're only five days after the Iran attacks, they have barely budged so far.
Part of that is down to a dogged assumption among most investors that the conflict and energy supply hiatus in the region will ‚Äãbe limited and allow normalization of pricing through the summer. The Polymarket prediction market, for what it's worth, sees about a 40% chance the current Iranian government will fall by midyear.
But more concretely, energy traders themselves see ‚Äãthis as a very time-limited supply outage. The futures curve for Brent crude oil, for example, sees prices drop more than $10 per barrel again by year-end - and back to pre-attack prices.
Natural gas futures see prices stay higher for longer - pricing year-end rates about $10 ‚Äåhigher than ‚ÅÝthey were a month ago. But the gap closes within a year.
And this is where most economists hover around the potential economic damage, in as much as anyone can see through the fog of war at the moment.
While some ECB hawks are wary of attempting to "see through" any inflation rise in a way they mistakenly did post-pandemic and Ukraine, when many say central banks at large tightened too late to head off steep price rises, there are key differences in the backdrop this time around.
Monetary and fiscal policy is already much tighter now than it was then, for a start. Plus the ECB's main risk before this was, arguably, a significant undershooting of its 2% inflation target ‚Äãover the next year.
Deutsche Bank economists point out that ‚Äãthe relatively limited energy futures moves so ‚ÅÝfar and undisturbed long-term market inflation expectations are "more consistent with removing the risk of undershooting of the 2% inflation target than with clear overshooting."
"Rising energy prices muddy the waters but, broadly speaking, don't yet challenge the ECB baseline of inflation on target in the medium term," they concluded, adding the situation was clearly fraught with uncertainty ‚Äãand that in itself is a drag on the economy.
Using its standard rule-of-thumb models, or "ready reckoners," Deutsche Bank estimates the rise in geopolitical uncertainty overall this year could lop ‚Äãsome 0.25 percentage point off euro ‚ÅÝzone GDP if it persists. The geopolitical shock of 2022, by comparison, knocked about one full point off GDP.
Europe is doubtless still vulnerable to global energy markets, not just in the Middle East but also due to its heavy reliance on U.S. LNG at a time of increasingly fractious trade and diplomatic relations with Washington.
But as it stands, it can likely take this one on the chin.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? ‚ÅÝCheck out Reuters ‚ÄãOpen Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
Plus, sign up for my weekday newsletter, Morning Bid U.S. ‚Äãand listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . item.title: War angst and AI hype mar Seoul's market ambitions
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - When South Korea's President Lee Jae Myung took office in June, many were sceptical of his campaign pledge to double the country's ‚Äãbenchmark KOSPI index (.KS11) , opens new tab to 5,000 within his five-year term. Just eight months later, though, the KOSPI topped ‚Äå6,000, becoming the best-performing major index globally. But this week's rollercoaster ride has exposed just how volatile the country's stocks are. Between war angst and artificial intelligence hype, Lee now faces a new headache: steadying markets.
Thanks to booming demand for AI-related hardware and infrastructure, global investors flocked to ‚ÄãSouth Korean heavyweights such as memory chip giants Samsung Electronics (005930.KS) , opens new tab and SK Hynix (000660.KS) , opens new tab . As of January, foreign holdings ‚Äãof local stocks topped , opens new tab $1.1 trillion, more than double the level from a year earlier and 32% of ‚ÅÝthe total. Shares in AI darling SK Hynix have risen roughly fivefold over the past 12 months, vastly outperforming its key customer ‚ÄãNvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab and rivals.
War in the Middle East has not only sapped that momentum but also injected volatility virtually unseen in a ‚Äãmajor stock market. In just the three trading days following the U.S. and Israeli air strikes in Iran that killed many of the country's top leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the benchmark KOSPI closed down 7%, then another 12% ‚Äì a record daily selloff ‚Äì before rallying as ‚Äãmuch as 12% on Thursday morning. The export-driven economy's sensitivity to rising oil prices, concerns over global stagflation, plus broad-based ‚Äãprofit-taking are all factors, according to Jason Liu, head of APAC equity and derivatives strategy at BNP Paribas.
Having staked much of his political ‚Äåcapital on ‚ÅÝa strong stock market, Lee's priority now is to ease volatility. Officials have been quick to reassure investors that it will deploy its 100 trillion won ($68 billion) market stabilisation fund. The government is also preparing to introduce initiatives to encourage the country's army of retail investors to repatriate their foreign stock holdings.
Yet it's unlikely Seoul can do much to tame hot money flows. ‚ÄãJust before the attacks on ‚ÄãIran, foreign investors sold a ‚ÅÝnet $4.8 billion of shares on Friday ‚Äì the largest one-day outflow on record ‚Äì suggesting many funds had already started to unwind their bets; and in the midst of Wednesday's historic market crash, ‚Äãforeign investors were actually net buyers, per BNP's Liu. War or not, Seoul has its work ‚Äãcut out steadying ‚ÅÝmarkets.
Follow Robyn Mak on X , opens new tab .

item.title: Private credit meets ‚Äòshow-me-the-money‚Äô moment
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - It's time for private credit titans to put their money where their mouths are. Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab , Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab , Ares Management (ARES.N) , opens new tab , KKR (KKR.N) , opens new tab and others grabbed a giant chunk of the leveraged finance market over the past decade. To help fund this growth, they created business development companies (BDCs) to raise vast sums from individual investors. As worries about shaky debts mount, those mom-and-pop backers are now fleeing. Managers could stem the panic by ‚Äãstepping in to buy as others leave. Hesitating risks inflaming the panic.
Now counting half a trillion dollars in assets, according to data gatherer Solve, BDCs allow direct lenders to accumulate funds without a set expiration date. ‚ÄåThese tax-advantaged shell companies, which exist to raise capital and lend it out, come in two forms. Listed versions, like $31 billion Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC.O) , opens new tab , allow investors to trade in and out at a price set by market supply and demand for the shares. Nontraded vehicles, like Blackstone's $82 billion BCRED, allow their wealthy buyers to withdraw at a fund‚Äôs stated net asset value up to a quarterly cap, usually 5%. Both types have hit problems.
The median listed BDC now trades at just 73% of its claimed worth, according to Morningstar data. Meanwhile, fleeing investors have breached ostensible withdrawal limits in private vehicles. Blue Owl, bruised by ‚Äãone attempt to wind down an aging fund, agreed to redeem 15% of assets in another. Behemoth BCRED on Monday reported withdrawal requests equivalent to 7.9% of its shares. Other rival funds have exceeded , opens new tab the typical 5% limit recently.
The first shock came ‚Äãfrom software ‚Äî specifically, fears that artificial intelligence could replace a swathe of existing borrowers' products. That would be no small thing for private credit: Barclays pegs the average BDC‚Äôs software exposure at about ‚ÅÝ20%. Since these are asset-light businesses, lenders risk getting very little of value in future bankruptcies.
Gaming out the pain of any defaults is crucial. The average listed BDC magnifies its firepower by raising slightly more than a dollar of debt for every dollar contributed ‚Äãby individual investors. If default rates reach 10% and recovery rates fall to 50%, a worst-case scenario envisioned by Oppenheimer analysts for first-lien debt, a fund with $1 billion of investments would lose $50 million in principal. Typical leverage would magnify the hit twice over, wiping out ‚Äãa tenth of the fund investors' net asset value.
Broader problems transcend software, and date back to a wildly exuberant period of dealmaking following the pandemic. Fitch Ratings' recorded private credit defaults are led by healthcare providers and consumer products. FS KKR Capital (FSK.N) , opens new tab , a listed BDC with $13 billion of investments as of December, recently said that some borrowers in insurance claims management, veterinary services and dentistry had stopped making payments. BlackRock TCP Capital (TCPC.O) , opens new tab stumbled on loans to firms that buy up sellers of goods on Amazon.com.
And while technical defaults across private credit are subdued, at just 3.2% at the end of 2025 according to ‚ÄãLincoln Financial, the share of loans that switched from paying interest in cash to non-cash rocketed to 6.4%. Meanwhile, managers of unlisted BDCs face a particularly tricky task: convincing investors to hold onto shares at the fund's own stated net asset value, even as listed ‚Äãvehicles with similar investments suffer giant discounts.
The panic among typically flighty retail investors is therefore unsurprising. How these vehicles manage the turbulence is paramount. Nontraded BDCs must keep enough liquidity to honor some withdrawals. BCRED's $8 billion , opens new tab of available firepower at the end of last year could absorb roughly ‚Äåthree quarters of ‚ÅÝredemptions if they remain at recent, extraordinary levels.
Another issue is how to refinance maturing leverage, referring to the borrowings that the funds themselves have taken on. There are three pools to tap for this debt: so-called revolving loans from banks, massive debt vehicles known as collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), or the bond market. The complication is orchestrating them in concert.
Banks require BDCs to pledge portfolio assets as collateral, which can be problematic if the underlying pool of loans is being marked down. Nor is issuing unsecured bonds easy. Fitch in February pegged , opens new tab $12.7 billion of BDC maturities that must be refinanced this year. Blackstone‚Äôs listed Secured Lending Fund, BXSL, (BXSL.N) , opens new tab recently had to pay half a percentage point above its prior issuance, Bloomberg reported , opens new tab . Under-pressure funds may face an even larger refinancing penalty. The yield on outstanding bonds issued by FS KKR, maturing ‚Äãin 2031, has shot up by a full percentage point ‚Äãin 2026.
That leaves CLOs as arguably the most promising route. ‚ÅÝPricing is paramount. BDCs are a spread business, meaning that their profit is determined by the gap between the cost of their own debt and the yield on loans they make. If one side goes up without the other also rising, it will hurt.
There is a more extreme option to raise cash: selling parts of the loan book, as Blue Owl and New Mountain Capital have done. Regulations ‚Äãmake it tricky for managers to sell assets to other vehicles they run. And the underlying borrowers, usually owned by buyout barons, often have a say over whether this can happen. ‚ÄãFew industry insiders see this as a ‚ÅÝlarge-scale fix.
That's why it may fall to the managers themselves to cough up fresh cash, just as Blackstone did in a small way when senior leaders and the firm itself invested this week in BCRED. After all, for all the talk of problems, software companies' revenues are growing and BDC default rates are fairly low. Listed vehicles have traded at even wider discounts in the past and snapped back. Private credit barons who frequently dismiss naysayers now have the opportunity to buy their own loans at 73 cents on the dollar.
Some have announced buybacks or expanded ‚ÅÝprograms, like Blue ‚ÄãOwl, whose listed vehicles upped repurchases to $300 million from $200 million. Anyone refusing to do likewise would seem to signal weakness. Liquidity constraints may prove an obstacle. ‚ÄãBut some of the biggest asset managers in the world like Blackstone, BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab and KKR have hundreds of billions of credit dry powder they could put to work. Add it to a show of force, say by arranging a massive unsecured bond placement with blue-chip institutional investors, and it might help cut the doom loop. ‚ÄãIf nothing else, it would live up to the industry‚Äôs years of bravado.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Anger among Pakistan's Shi'ites underlines its Iran-U.S. tightrope walk
item.description:
LAHORE, March 5 (Reuters) - Pakistan's efforts to preserve close ties with President Donald Trump are being put to the test after protesters stormed the U.S. consulate in Karachi last week and poured onto streets elsewhere over the killing of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in U.S. and Israeli strikes.
Islamabad is looking to balance the anger among its minority Shi'ite Muslim community, the second-largest in ‚Äãthe world after Iran, and its alliance with Washington, underlined by its membership of Trump's Board of Peace this year, where Israel also has a seat at the table.
Complicating ‚Äåthe challenge is Pakistan's worst fighting in years with Afghanistan, the other nation on its western border along with Iran.
Pakistan's relationship with the U.S. has strengthened since Trump returned to the White House last year, ending more than a decade on Washington's blacklist and providing a useful counterbalance to its troubled ties with India. The mercurial Trump has a close relationship with Field Marshal Asim Munir, the most powerful man in Pakistan.
Pakistan also maintains deep military, economic and political ties ‚Äãwith Saudi Arabia and recently signed a strategic defence pact that states aggression against either state should be treated as an attack on both.
Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states have been ‚Äãtargeted by Iran in its counter-strikes following the U.S. and Israel attacks.
"Pakistan is trying to maintain domestic peace by expressing solidarity with Iran, while it ‚ÅÝalso risks being pulled into the orbit of the war by the U.S. and Saudis," said Arsalan Khan, assistant professor at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville.
"If the war progresses, then it may find itself ‚Äãmaking trade-offs between domestic peace and its geopolitical commitments."
The government has not commented on the issue, although a senior security official told Reuters: "Balancing relationships and blowback is the most pressing issue for Pakistan."
At least 26 people were killed as protesters clashed with police following news on Sunday of the death of Khamenei, seen by Pakistan's Shi'ites as their spiritual leader. In Karachi, U.S. Marines fired on protesters who breached the walls of the consulate, two U.S. officials have said. Video footage of the incident showed some of the protesters were armed and fired into the compound.
Senior Shi'ite clerics in Pakistan have announced days of mourning and warned that more protests would follow, ‚Äãwhich could lead to bursts of instability in its main cities, analysts said.
Khamenei's "death has not weakened the Shi'ites but united them with a new spirit of revolution and independence from the slavery of ‚Äãthe U.S. and its allies," Shi'ite cleric Sajid Ali Naqvi told Reuters.
Shi'ites, or Shias in the Urdu language, make up about one-fifth of Pakistan's 240 million people. The vast majority of Pakistan's people are Sunni Muslims.
The minority community ‚Äåhas often ‚ÅÝbeen targeted in sectarian attacks, including by Islamic State and the Sunni Islamist group Tehreek-e-Taliban Pakistan.
A schism between Sunnis and Shi'ites developed after the Prophet Muhammad died in 632 when his followers could not agree on a successor. Emotions over the issue have boiled through to modern times, and have even pushed some countries to the brink of civil war.
Pakistan's own political history, alongside Khamenei's revered status, have shaped the Shi'ite response to the Iranian leader's killing, said Madiha Tahir, an assistant professor at Yale.
While General Zia ul Haq unleashed a brand of Sunni Muslim Islamisation in Pakistan in the 1980s, ‚Äãthe 1979 Iranian revolution created a new source of ‚Äãsupport and theological ties for its Shi'ites.
"Pakistani ‚ÅÝShias found themselves marginalized in an increasingly sectarian state and vulnerable to violence," Tahir said.
"At the same time, the Iranian Revolution meant that they could draw on Iran for aid. It had a profound effect on Shia communities and politics in Pakistan."
For Pakistan's Shi'ites, the Ayatollah in Iran turned into a defender ‚Äãof their identity, said Kamran Bokhari, senior director at the New Lines Institute in Washington.
"Add in anti-Americanism and anti-Israeli sentiment and you can see how ‚Äãthis becomes a powerful potion," ‚ÅÝhe said.
Shi'ite leaders linked to the Millat-e-Jafaria network and other groups, including the cleric Naqvi, have called for protests and investigations into the killings of demonstrators, and demanded a criminal case against the U.S. consul general.
"He was our representative. He was like our pope," said Syed Hussain Ali, who runs the digital platform Hussaini Khayal and organised a vigil to mourn Khamenei.
Officially, Pakistan Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif has condemned Khamenei's ‚ÅÝkilling, calling it ‚Äãa "violation of international law".
But he did not name the U.S. and also said "Pakistan stands in full solidarity with Saudi Arabia ‚Äãand our brotherly Gulf countries in this perilous time".
Analysts say the Shi'ite protests could fade in time but the deaths of demonstrators may keep tensions alive, especially with funerals for those killed drawing large crowds.
"Each one of those deaths is a reminder ‚Äãof the embattled place of Shias within Pakistan," Tahir said. "There is definite potential for this to continue."

item.title: On the French Riviera, a Sarkozy heir seeks to chart his own political path
item.description:
MENTON, France, March 5 (Reuters) - In a French Riviera town nestled between the mountains, the Mediterranean and the Italian border, a disgraced former president's son is hoping to launch his political career with a new, untested brand of conservatism flecked with U.S. ‚Äãinfluence.
Louis Sarkozy, 28, son of ex-President Nicolas Sarkozy , spent his teens and early 20s living in the United States, where he said he forged his political identity during ‚Äåan era dominated by President Donald Trump.
Now back in France, Sarkozy is using this month's mayoral election in the small town of Menton as a testing ground for whether his household name and Trump-inflected brand of right-wing politics can outflank a strong far-right rival rooted in traditional French nationalism.
Sarkozy's political platform is tailored to local themes, such as a housing crisis sparked by an influx of vacation rentals and fighting crime. He is ‚Äãrunning without any party affiliation but is supported by centrist and centre-right rivals, who have backed him with the aim of preventing a far-right National Rally (RN) victory.
Louis Sarkozy hopes he ‚Äãcan follow in the footsteps of his father, who used the mayorship of the wealthy Paris suburb of Neuilly as a catapult to the ‚ÅÝpresidency.
But he is aware the Sarkozy name could also prove a hindrance. His father was briefly jailed last year on corruption charges he denies, and has spent much of his post-presidency life grappling with ‚Äãa series of legal scandals that have tarnished the family name.
"Son of a prisoner," was graffitied on Sarkozy's Menton HQ last month, and locals' reaction to his candidacy has been mixed. The National ‚ÄãRally candidate Alexandra Masson was well ahead of Sarkozy in the only poll to come from Menton, but he is likely to win enough votes to reach a second round, where alliances with rivals provide a possible path to victory.
"People do not vote for you because your name is Sarkozy, they vote for your project," he told Reuters, adding that his father was advising him on the race.
Sarkozy, a well-known figure from the French tabloids who ‚Äãrides a motorcycle and flaunts his arm tattoos and wariness of progressive ideas, said he was a fusion of classic French liberalism, cut through with conservative U.S. ideas on immigration and security. ‚ÄãWhile he is avowedly tough on crime, he also supports the decriminalization of drugs.
Although not explicitly pro-Trump, an unpopular figure in France, Sarkozy said he judged him on a case-by-case basis.
He is critical of the Trump administration's ‚Äåhandling of ‚ÅÝimmigration policy and has previously called for legal immigrants to do military service - a move he says would help keep numbers low.
Sarkozy - who spent four years at a military school in rural Pennsylvania, a perch which he said gave him a good insight into the real America - also criticised Trump's threats against Greenland. But he gave him credit for "doing exactly what he said," adding "he is much more popular than we think".
Sarkozy's support of the U.S. entrepreneurial economic model was much more full-throated, arguing France was hamstrung by restrictive labour laws and a pension system that was no longer fit for purpose.
"We must ...unblock ‚Äãour companies, let the French work. Work longer ‚Äãin life, work longer in the week ‚ÅÝeven if it is not popular," he said. "Either we do it now, or it is an IMF rescue plan that makes us do it in 25 years."
Sarkozy is part of a generational shift, with a small but growing number of political figures seeking to instil Trumpian ideas among the conservative ‚ÄãFrench right. Another is Sarah Knafo , a little-known European Parliament lawmaker running for Paris City Hall.
"This discourse from the United States resonates with a ‚Äãsignificant segment of the population ‚ÅÝfavourable to Trump's 'I say it, I do it' brand of politics," said Stewart Chau, Research Director at Verian.
Sarkozy's reimagining of French conservatism underlines the shifts on the political right, as the centre-right Les Republicains party grapples for an answer to Marine Le Pen's ascending National Rally.
Sarkozy called Masson "a good candidate", but he ruled out any alliance with the RN.
"It is an opposition party, a party made ‚ÅÝto shout," he ‚Äãsaid, adding he believed the far right would struggle with the responsibilities of power if it won on a ‚Äãnational level.
Masson was dismissive of Sarkozy's parachuting into Menton, calling him a "Franco-American boy" who "lacks territorial anchoring."
"People know that I am very invested in local issues," Masson said.
As he was out campaigning in Menton, Sarkozy was joined by his mother C√©cilia Attias, ‚Äãwho had flown in from New York.
"Louis needs to know France and there is nothing better than a municipal mandate for that," she said.

item.title: Missiles, drones coming from Iran fell on airport in Azerbaijan, source says
item.description:
BAKU, March 5 (Reuters) - Missiles and drones flying ‚Äåfrom the direction of Iran fell on the territory of ‚Äãthe airport in ‚Äãthe Azerbaijani exclave of Nakhchivan ‚ÅÝon Thursday, a source ‚Äãclose to the Azerbaijani ‚Äãgovernment told Reuters.
A fire has started as a result of the incident, ‚Äãthe source said, ‚Äãadding that an official statement would ‚Äåsoon ‚ÅÝfollow. It was not immediately clear how many missiles and drones fell in ‚Äãthe ‚Äãarea.
Video ‚ÅÝfootage shared by the source showed black ‚Äãsmoke rising near ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝterminal of Nakhchivan International Airport, which is about 10 km (6 miles) from the ‚ÅÝborder ‚Äãwith Iran.

item.title: Explainer: What you need to know about France's local elections
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - France will hold municipal elections on March 15 and 22, votes seen as a key test ahead of next year's presidential election.
The two-round ballot will measure the strength of the far-right National Rally (RN) and showcase what types of alliances could emerge in an increasingly fragmented landscape.
Heading nearly 35,000 communes - ‚Äãfrom major cities to villages with only a few dozen residents - mayors are France's most trusted elected officials, seen by voters as the political ‚Äåfigures closest to their daily concerns.
Local results can also shape national momentum and reveal which themes resonate with voters, especially when they take place just over a year before the next presidential election.
The anti-immigration, eurosceptic party, which has so far struggled to make meaningful gains at a local level, is treating the March votes as a critical step toward building momentum for the ‚Äã2027 presidential ballot .
It hopes to consolidate towns it already controls and expand into larger urban areas, saying it would field about 650 lists, substantially more than ‚Äãin previous cycles.
Currently, only around a dozen mayors belong to, or are backed by, the RN. The far-right party only runs one ‚ÅÝcity of over 100,000 inhabitants, Perpignan .
One key question ahead of the 2027 vote is what alliances the RN will strike with other parties between the two rounds. Will decades ‚Äãof tradition of shunning the far right hold? Some, especially in mainstream parties on the right, are tempted to do otherwise this time.
In a sign of how important this election ‚Äãis for the RN, 33 out of 119 of its members of parliament are candidates in the municipal elections.
- Marseille is a long-time conservative city which the left won in the last ballot in 2020, and which all main parties, including the RN, are fighting over.
- Paris is another key battleground. For decades a city led by conservatives, it has had a ‚ÄãSocialist mayor since 2001.
- The RN is also targeting Toulon , a city of 180,000 in southern France, which it already ruled under its previous name, the National Front, from ‚Äã1995 to 2001.
- The left did well across France in the last municipal elections in 2020. It is now weakened nationally. Whether it can keep some of the cities it won last time, ‚Äåsuch as ‚ÅÝNantes and Montpellier for the Socialists, or Lyon and Strasbourg for the Greens, will be in focus.
- The hard-left France Unbowed, which like the RN has never been strong in local elections, is also hoping to make gains, including in the Seine-Saint-Denis area where it has several members of parliament.
- The conservative Republicans (LR) have lost heavily in the last national elections but have long been strong in municipal elections.
- President Emmanuel Macron's allies hold relatively few municipalities, limiting the potential for an anti-government vote.
- Menton, a quiet Riviera town ‚Äãpopular with retirees, is bound to attract ‚Äãattention. Former President Nicolas Sarkozy's son Louis , ‚ÅÝopenly inspired by Trump-style rhetoric, is a candidate there.
There are nearly 35,000 separate elections, many of which are focused on local issues and are not reviewed by pollsters, making them hard to predict. Nationwide, security, housing and ‚Äãlocal taxes are among key issues for voters in this election, surveys show.
Another factor making those elections hard to predict is ‚Äãhow they work. A ‚ÅÝlist that wins an absolute majority in the first round takes control of the municipality. Failing that, all lists with 10% or more advance to the second round. Those with at least 5% can merge with larger lists. This system often leads to three- or four-way runoffs, making the second round quite unpredictable.
There could also potentially be an impact from the killing ‚ÅÝof far-right activist Quentin ‚ÄãDeranque , who was beaten to death in February in a fight with alleged hard-left activists. The incident ‚Äãsoured the mood towards France Unbowed, a poll showed.
Voting starts at 8 a.m. (0700 GMT) on March 15 and closes between 1700 GMT and 1900 GMT, depending on the city. In most medium ‚Äãto large cities, there will be a second round on March 22. Voting times are the same. Results will trickle in on those evenings.

item.title: Iran says it hits U.S. oil tanker in Gulf, no immediate confirmation
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards said on Thursday that they ‚Äãhad hit a ‚ÄãU.S. tanker in the northern ‚ÅÝpart of the ‚ÄãGulf and the vessel ‚Äãwas on fire. There was no immediate confirmation of the ‚Äãincident or of a ‚Äãsimilar attack that Iran claimed ‚Äåearlier ‚ÅÝthis week.
The Guards said in their Thursday statement carried by state ‚Äãmedia that, ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝtime of war, passage through ‚Äãthe Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãwill ‚ÅÝbe under the control of the Islamic ‚ÅÝRepublic.

item.title: Turkey says it is closely following actions of Iranian Kurdish PJAK militant group
item.description:
ANKARA, March 5 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Thursday that it was closely following the actions ‚Äãof the Iranian Kurdish PJAK militant group, ‚Äåwhich it said threatened Iran's security and regional stability, amid reports of discussions between Iranian Kurdish militias and Washington about ‚Äãthe U.S. and Israeli war against Iran.
On Tuesday, ‚Äãsources told Reuters that the Iranian Kurdish groups ‚ÅÝhad consulted with the U.S. about whether, and how, ‚Äãto attack Iran's security forces in the western part ‚Äãof the country.
Turkey, a NATO member and Iran's neighbour, is currently working on securing peace with the outlawed Iraq-based Kurdistan ‚ÄãWorkers Party (PKK) militant group. Ankara also backs the ‚Äãintegration of Syrian Kurdish militants into Syria's state systems, and is ‚Äåunlikely ‚ÅÝto support collaboration with Iranian Kurdish militias by NATO ally Washington.
"The activities of groups that fuel ethnic separatism, such as the terrorist organization PJAK, negatively affect ‚Äãnot only Iran's ‚Äãsecurity but ‚ÅÝalso the overall peace and stability of the region," Turkey's defence ministry told ‚Äãa weekly press briefing in Ankara.
It added ‚Äãthat ‚ÅÝTurkey's state institutions were all closely following actions by the Kurdistan Free Life Party (PJAK), an outlawed militant group ‚ÅÝthat ‚Äãhas links to the PKK, ‚Äãwhich Turkey, the U.S. and European Union designate as a terrorist group.

item.title: Ukraine seeks to postpone next round of trilateral talks with Russia amid Middle East events, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine spoke to the United ‚ÄãStates about the ‚Äåpossibility of changing location and ‚Äãpostponing the next ‚Äãround of trilateral ‚ÅÝtalks with Russia ‚Äãdue to events ‚Äãin the Middle East, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äãsaid.
"The next ‚ÄãUkraine‚ÄìU.S.‚ÄìRussia trilateral meeting was planned ‚Äåfor ‚ÅÝthe period from March 5 to 9, depending ‚Äãon ‚Äãdevelopments ‚ÅÝaround the world. Currently, ‚Äãthere is ‚Äãanother ‚ÅÝwar in the Middle East," he ‚ÅÝsaid ‚Äãon ‚ÄãX.

item.title: Syrian man handed 13-year sentence for Berlin Holocaust memorial stabbing
item.description:
BERLIN, March 5 (Reuters) - A Syrian refugee who stabbed a tourist at Berlin's ‚ÄãHolocaust memorial last year has ‚Äåbeen sentenced to 13 years in prison on charges of attempted murder, ‚Äãamong others, said a Berlin ‚Äãcourt spokesperson on Thursday.
The now ‚ÅÝ20-year-old Islamic State supporter used ‚Äãa knife to stab a 30-year-old ‚ÄãSpanish tourist in the neck at the memorial in the heart of the ‚ÄãGerman capital.
He inflicted life-threatening injuries ‚Äãthat required the man to undergo emergency surgery ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝbe placed in an induced coma.
Prosecutors said at the time that the Syrian national appeared to ‚Äãhave been ‚Äãplanning ‚ÅÝto kill Jews for several weeks, apparently motivated ‚Äãby the Middle Eastern conflict, ‚Äãwhich ‚ÅÝis why he chose the location.
The memorial commemorates the murder of ‚ÅÝ6 ‚Äãmillion Jews by ‚ÄãAdolf Hitler's Nazis during World War Two.

item.title: Russia accuses US and Israel of trying to drag Arab countries into a wider Middle East conflict
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia on Thursday accused the United States ‚Äãand Israel of trying to drag Arab ‚Äåcountries into a wider Middle East conflict by provoking Iran into striking targets across the region and said there ‚Äãwas no sign Washington and Tel Aviv ‚Äãwould let up.
Arab states in the Gulf, all ‚ÅÝclose U.S. allies - some of which also ‚Äãhave close ties with Russia - have come under ‚ÄãIranian drone and missile attacks since the United States and Israel launched their air strikes on Iran on Saturday.
Russian President ‚ÄãVladimir Putin spoke by phone to leaders of ‚Äãfour Arab Gulf states on Monday, offering to use Moscow's ‚Äåties ‚ÅÝto Iran to relay concerns about Tehran's strikes on oil infrastructure across the region.
Russia's Foreign Ministry in a statement on Thursday accused the U.S. and Israel of ‚Äãdeliberately trying ‚Äãto draw ‚ÅÝArab Gulf states into a wider conflagration.
"They deliberately provoked Iran into retaliatory ‚Äãstrikes against targets in some Arab countries, ‚Äãwhich ‚ÅÝled to human and material losses, which the Russian side deeply regrets," the ministry said.
"In doing so, they (Washington ‚ÅÝand ‚ÄãTel Aviv) are trying to ‚Äãdrag the Arabs into a war for someone else's interests".

item.title: Russia jails Romanian citizen for 15 years for spying for Ukraine
item.description: MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - A ‚ÄãRomanian man ‚Äåwas convicted of ‚Äãspying ‚Äãfor Ukraine and ‚ÅÝsentenced ‚Äãby a ‚ÄãRussian court on Thursday ‚Äãto ‚Äã15 years in ‚Äåprison, ‚ÅÝthe court in the ‚Äãsouthern ‚Äãregion ‚ÅÝof Krasnodar ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Pakistanis fleeing Iran describe strikes shaking ground under their feet
item.description:
QUETTA, Pakistan, March 5 (Reuters) - Pakistanis fleeing ‚ÄãIran described explosions and missile strikes across Tehran shaking the ground under their feet and engulfing buildings ‚Äåin fire and smoke in a city emptied of many of its residents.
The conflict has widened sharply, with a U.S. submarine sinking an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka on Wednesday and NATO air defences destroying an Iranian missile fired towards Turkey.
Governments have been scrambling to evacuate stranded citizens, ‚Äãwith most of the region's airspace closed due to the risk of missiles hitting passenger planes.
"I was ‚Äãin the classroom when a powerful explosion rocked our university building," Hareem Zahra, 23, a student ‚ÅÝat the Tehran University of Engineering, told Reuters after crossing Pakistan's land border with Iran.
"We saw thick smoke coming ‚Äãfrom many buildings on fire," she said, adding Tehran was under attack until the moment she left.
Nearly 1,000 ‚Äãstudents, businessmen and pilgrims have fled Iran since the war started out of a total 35,000 Pakistanis in the country, Mudassir Tipu, Pakistan's ambassador to Tehran, said.
"There are now serious challenges. As you know there is no internet in most parts of Iran," he said.
Iran has ‚Äãretaliated with a barrage of ballistic missiles targeting Israel and Washington's allies in the Gulf, including Qatar, Kuwait, the UAE, ‚Äãand Saudi Arabia, following U.S. and Israeli air strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Saturday.
Tehran has looked deserted since the ‚Äåconflict began, ‚ÅÝsaid Nadir Abbas, 25, a student of Persian literature at a university in the Iranian capital.
"I saw a drone hit a basketball court where six girl players lost their lives."
Reuters could not verify his account.
Islamabad is walking a diplomatic tightrope as it attempts to maintain warming ties with Washington while expressing solidarity with Iran.
Pakistan is home to the second-largest Shi'ite ‚Äãpopulation in the world after ‚ÄãIran and being drawn into ‚ÅÝthe conflict could lead to instability at home as well as complications evacuating its citizens.
"The first attack happened right next to my hospital," said Sakhi Aun Mohammad, a student at ‚ÄãTehran University of Medical Sciences.
After he reached the border, an Iranian friend called to ‚Äãcheck if he ‚ÅÝwas safe, saying: "'Thank God, you have gone to Pakistan, all of you are safe, but your hostel has been attacked'".
A Pakistani diplomat who is still in Tehran said attacks took place every four or five hours, adding one missile struck a building ‚ÅÝnext to ‚Äãhis office.
"At times you will feel as if something exploded right at ‚Äãyour feet," he said.
"The last time I got out was at night. Buildings had collapsed, some others were on fire. There is destruction everywhere."
He added: "It ‚Äãis almost like a ghost town."
(This story has been refiled to change the dateline to March 5)

item.title: Somali parliament approves constitution change to extend president's term, delay election
item.description:
MOGADISHU, March 5 (Reuters) - Somalia's parliament voted to change its constitution and extend the term in office for lawmakers ‚Äãand the president, the president and the parliament's ‚Äåspeaker said, pushing back planned elections by a year.
Somalia has endured conflict and clan battles with no strong central government since ‚Äãthe fall of autocratic ruler Mohamed Siad Barre ‚Äãin 1991.
While an African Union peacekeeping mission has ‚ÅÝpushed back the al Qaeda-linked al Shabaab group, ‚Äãit still controls vast areas of the countryside and ‚Äãhas the ability to conduct regular strikes on major population centres.
President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud had reached a deal last August with some ‚Äãopposition leaders stipulating that, while lawmakers would be ‚Äãdirectly elected in 2026, the president would still be chosen by ‚Äåparliament. ‚ÅÝA 2024 law restored universal suffrage ahead of the vote.
On Wednesday, 222 lawmakers from the parliament and senate out of a total of 329 voted by acclamation ‚Äãto change the ‚Äãlaw, extending ‚ÅÝtheir term and that of the president to five years, from four years previously.
"Today ‚Äãis a historic day for it is ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝofficial completion of the constitution which had dragged for a long period," the president told a press conference on ‚ÅÝWednesday.
Opposition ‚Äãparty leaders, including former presidents and ‚Äãformer prime ministers, rejected the amendment and called for elections in ‚ÄãMay as planned.

item.title: Gulf countries concerned about risk of Iran civil war, says EU's foreign chief
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - Countries in the Middle East ‚Äãhave told European officials they are concerned about the risk of ‚Äåcivil war in Iran as a result of the conflict between Tehran and the United States and Israel, EU foreign chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
"When we talk to ‚Äãthe countries in the region, they are also worried about civil ‚Äãwars inside Iran because of the regime's leadership and what is going on there," ‚ÅÝshe said ahead of a video conference with EU foreign ministers and ‚Äãrepresentatives of the Gulf Cooperation Council on the situation in Iran and the broader ‚ÄãMiddle East .
The EU wants to push forward a diplomatic solution. "Wars really end in diplomacy and there has to be room for diplomacy here to really get out of this cycle ‚Äãof escalation," she told reporters.
The EU is "extremely worried" about maritime security in the ‚Äãregion and that it is trying to keep routes such as the strait of Hormuz ‚Äåopen, she ‚ÅÝsaid although, since the EU was not dependent on oil from Gulf states, the attacks there did not have a large short-term impact on the security of the supply of oil to the EU.
The conflict has also escalated a dispute ‚Äãbetween the United ‚ÄãStates and Spain, ‚ÅÝwith Washington threatening to cut trade with Madrid over Spain's refusal to allow U.S. aircraft to use jointly operated naval ‚Äãand air bases in southern Spain for the offensive ‚Äãagainst Tehran.
Spain ‚ÅÝhas denounced the U.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless and illegal. The White House said on Wednesday Spain agreed to cooperate, but Madrid has denied ‚ÅÝthis.
Asked ‚Äãabout the spat, Kallas said she hoped Washington ‚Äãwould respect the trade agreement reached last year that applies to all EU member states.

item.title: Iran has not requested Russian arms supplies, Kremlin says
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran has not approached ‚ÄãMoscow with a request for ‚Äåarms supplies, the Kremlin said on Thursday.
Russia has condemned U.S.-Israeli strikes and called for ‚Äãan immediate ceasefire against a ‚Äãcountry it has drawn closer ‚ÅÝto in recent years.
Iran last year ‚Äãsealed a 20-year strategic partnership ‚Äãagreement with Moscow, Russia is building two new nuclear units at Bushehr, the site ‚Äãof Iran's only nuclear power ‚Äãplant, and Iran supplied Russia with shahed drones ‚Äåfor ‚ÅÝuse against Ukraine.
Asked by a reporter on Thursday whether Russia intends to provide material help to Iran, ‚Äãincluding ‚Äãarms deliveries, ‚ÅÝKremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters:
"In this case, ‚Äãthere were no requests from ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝIranian side. Our consistent position is well known to everyone, and there ‚ÅÝhave ‚Äãbeen no changes ‚Äãin this regard."

item.title: Philippine resupply mission data leaked to Chinese intelligence, security official says
item.description:
MANILA, March 5 (Reuters) - A spokesman for the National Security Council (NSC) of the Philippines on Thursday said some information on the country's resupply missions in ‚Äãthe South China Sea was compromised and passed on to Chinese intelligence agents.
His statement came a day ‚Äåafter the Philippine government said it apprehended some of its citizens on suspicion of spying for China in a "serious national security matter."
NSC spokesman Cornelio Valencia called the breach "alarming" but limited in scope, adding that the channels used to transmit the information had been shut down.
"Rotation and resupply data fall under operational ‚Äãsecurity because disclosing it can endanger personnel, and that has been compromised," Valencia said.
Philippine and Chinese vessels have frequent ‚Äãrun-ins during Manila's resupply missions to its occupied features in the South China Sea. And Chinese diplomats, ‚ÅÝPhilippine lawmakers, and a Coast Guard spokesperson have recently engaged in heated public exchanges over the disputed waterway.
Valencia said three Filipinos had ‚Äãbeen apprehended and "there could be more." He declined to say whether charges would be filed.
Reuters spoke with the three accused people last month under ‚Äãan agreement with security sources on the condition that their identities not be revealed.
One of the accused, who obtained information on deployments, resupply runs and personnel rotations through a connection with a Philippine Coast Guard staffer, said he passed the details to his contact using a phone issued to him.
The accused showed ‚ÄãReuters the phone, which contained a Tetris game that, when a code was entered, opened a hidden messaging platform that he said he ‚Äãused to communicate with his handler.
"What I can say is that they're cooperating with our security services," Valencia said. "They have confessed and admitted to it. They ‚Äådisclosed the ‚ÅÝdata that they were able to get and share, the assessments they made and how they were recruited."
China's Foreign Ministry rejected the Philippines' accusations, saying on Thursday: "We object to it, the case is not clear and there is no conclusive evidence."
Another accused person told Reuters that he was initially approached by a Filipina acquaintance when he was a junior staffer at the Department of National Defence with an offer ‚Äãto write opinion articles in ‚Äãreturn for money.
That later expanded to ‚ÅÝproviding information related to the South China Sea and the defence ministry's bilateral engagement with Philippine allies, including the U.S., he said.
He did not immediately realise he was working for Chinese agents and only became suspicious ‚Äãlater, but it was hard to stop as he needed the money, he said, adding that ‚Äãhe did this ‚ÅÝwork between 2023 and 2025.
The recruitment pattern mirrors methods seen in other foreign intelligence operations, where seemingly innocuous offers of consultancy or writing work are used as entry points before escalating into requests for sensitive material.
Last year, Reuters reported that networks linked to Chinese actors have used consulting ‚ÅÝfirms and ‚Äãcovert job offers to lure targets, often exploiting financial vulnerabilities and gradually seeking ‚Äãmore sensitive information, a pattern analysts say follows techniques used by previous Chinese intelligence operations.
Philippine lawmakers from both the ruling party and the opposition are seeking to overhaul ‚Äãdecades-old espionage laws that would expand their traditionally wartime focus to cover peacetime and cyber-enabled threats.

item.title: Crude tanker reports suspected hull breach after blast near Iraq port
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - The Bahamas flagged crude oil tanker Sonangol Namibe's hull ‚Äãwas likely to have been breached after ‚Äåbeing hit by a blast while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services said on ‚ÄãThursday.
The vessel was approached by an ‚Äãunknown small vessel at 0120 local time ‚ÅÝon March 5.
"The small boat approached the port ‚Äãside of the tanker and a short time ‚Äãlater a loud bang was heard," Sonangol Marine Services said in a statement.
"The crew reports that a port ‚Äãballast tank is losing water which suggests ‚Äãsome form of hull breach but the ship remains ‚Äåstable ‚ÅÝand safely afloat."
The vessel was not carrying cargo and there were no reports of pollution, the company added.
The tanker had a contract with ‚ÄãIraq's state ‚Äãoil marketer ‚ÅÝSOMO and was heading to an Iraqi terminal to load 80,000 METRIC ‚Äãtons of Iraqi fuel, an Iraqi ‚Äãenergy ‚ÅÝsource and a shipping agent said.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said on Thursday that they had hit a U.S. ‚ÅÝtanker ‚Äãin the northern part of ‚Äãthe Gulf and the vessel was on fire.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO allies will keep supporting Ukraine despite situation in Iran, Rutte says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO allies will keep supporting ‚ÄãUkraine despite the ‚Äåsituation in Iran, NATO Secretary General Mark ‚ÄãRutte told Reuters ‚Äãon Thursday.
"Many leaders in ‚ÅÝEurope and the ‚ÄãUnited States, Canada ‚Äãsay it has to be 'and, and': make sure that ‚Äãas allies ‚Äãwe enable what the Americans ‚Äåare ‚ÅÝdoing in the Middle East ... and at the same time ‚Äãmake ‚Äãsure ‚ÅÝthat Ukraine has what it ‚Äãneeds to stay ‚Äãstrong ‚ÅÝin the fight", he said.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte does not see need to invoke Art. 5 after missile incident
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - The shooting down of a ballistic missile headed for Turkey by NATO ‚Äãair defences does not provide immediate reason to trigger ‚Äåthe alliance's Article 5 mutual defence clause, its Secretary General Mark Rutte told Reuters on Thursday.
"Nobody's talking about Article 5," Rutte ‚Äãsaid. "The most important thing is that our adversaries have ‚Äãseen yesterday that NATO is so strong and ‚ÅÝso vigilant, and even more vigilant, if possible, since ‚ÄãSaturday."
Turkey said NATO air defence s destroyed an Iranian ballistic missile as it headed into ‚ÄãTurkish airspace, marking the first time the alliance member has been drawn into the Middle East conflict and raising the possibility of an expansion ‚Äãinvolving its bloc allies.
The Iranian Armed Forces General Staff ‚Äãon Thursday denied it had fired missiles at Turkey, saying the Islamic ‚ÄåRepublic ‚ÅÝrespected the sovereignty of "friendly" Turkey, according to a statement carried by Iranian media.
NATO's alliance's Article 5 specifies that an attack on one of the alliance's members is an attack on ‚Äãall the ‚Äãothers.
Rutte said NATO ‚ÅÝsupports the United States in its strikes against Iran as the country was "close to ‚Äãbecoming a threat to Europe as well".
As ‚Äãthe U.S.‚ÄìIran ‚ÅÝwar entered its sixth day , the conflict has widened beyond Gulf states and into Asia, convulsing global markets and prompting thousands ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãstranded tourists and residents to try ‚Äãto flee the Middle East.

item.title: For Mexico's security chief, El Mencho killing was personal
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Mexico‚Äôs security chief, the man who helped lead the operation that killed the drug lord known as ‚ÄúEl Mencho,‚Äù spends his days and nights inside fortified office buildings, including a one-bedroom apartment in the security ministry built for him.
His quarters ‚Äì inside a modern complex beside a busy thoroughfare ‚Äì include a bedroom, gym, kitchen and a conference room that seats 25. From the living room, guests can hear the crack of gunfire ‚Äãfrom a firing range within the building complex, according to a high-ranking government official who has visited the apartment. A red telephone on his desk provides a direct line to the president.
Omar Garcia Harfuch, 44, has lived this way since 2020, when on his ‚Äåcommute to work a truck cut off his armored Suburban and gunmen disguised as road workers sprayed his vehicle with more than 400 bullets. Harfuch returned fire and survived with three gunshot wounds. Two of his bodyguards and a bystander were killed.
The security chief blamed the assassination attempt on Nemesio Oseguera, 59, better known as El Mencho, leader of the brutal Jalisco New Generation Cartel, one of Mexico‚Äôs largest and bloodiest crime groups. Six years later, bringing down the cartel leader was a deeply personal moment for Harfuch, who friends say was devastated by his security guards‚Äô deaths.
Harfuch declined to comment for this story. The account is based on interviews with a dozen friends, colleagues and security analysts.
Those close to Harfuch say that he's unlikely to ‚Äãlet his guard down now that El Mencho is gone. But the death of the kingpin has lifted the profile of Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum‚Äôs Minister of Security and Citizen Protection, who‚Äôs credited with spearheading her more muscular approach to fighting cartels, so much so he‚Äôs considered an ‚Äãearly frontrunner for the presidency when her six-year term ends in 2030.
‚ÄúOmar Garcia Harfuch is the number one presidential candidate today,‚Äù said Armando Vargas, the top security expert at the think tank M√©xico Evalua. ‚ÄúHe is the most visible leader ‚ÅÝof this new strategy.‚Äù
The approach is not without risks: El Mencho‚Äôs death triggered a wave of violence across Mexico that killed 25 National Guard members and could fuel deadly feuds as rival cartel factions fight for control.
It‚Äôs also a marked departure from former President Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador‚Äôs ‚Äúhugs not bullets‚Äù philosophy, under which cartels ‚Äãgrew in power and reach to control vast swaths of territory and diversified from drugs into extortion, human trafficking, and contraband fuel.
Harfuch rose to prominence within the Mexico City government when now-President Sheinbaum was the capital‚Äôs mayor.
Rodrigo Canales, who advised Sheinbaum on her security strategy, said Harfuch helped her navigate a difficult ‚Äãperiod early in her mayoral tenure when high-level police officers were accused of corruption.
‚ÄúHe has Claudia‚Äôs absolute trust and earned it by being extremely loyal and effective in key moments early in her mayorship,‚Äù Canales said.
Sheinbaum promoted Harfuch to head of the city‚Äôs police in 2019 after sacking his predecessor over a money laundering scandal.
Harfuch had been in the job for under a year when assassins made the attempt on his life. After initially returning fire, he scrambled into the back seats of his armored SUV and crouched down until reinforcements could arrive, he recalled in interviews after the attack. Twelve alleged members of the Jalisco cartel were arrested and given life sentences.
Following the ambush, he moved out of his house and into ‚ÄãMexico City‚Äôs police headquarters. His inner circle, already small, became even tighter. He sees his children in fleeting moments.
"He went from someone who could go to a restaurant, meet friends, attend a colleague's birthday party, to being guarded in an office, spending practically 90 percent of his life inside police buildings," said a friend ‚Äãwho has known Harfuch for 20 years.
Like the cartel bosses he hunts, one misstep could cost him his life.
Harfuch comes from a line of Mexican top brass.
His grandfather, Marcelino Garcia Barragan, was defense minister in the 1960s while his father, Javier Garcia Paniagua, was a senator and presidential contender who led a federal security agency ‚Äåin the 1970s.
That mix ‚ÅÝof police and military heritage is rare in Mexico and puts Harfuch in a unique position to lead the country‚Äôs heavily militarized public security structure, two sources who‚Äôve worked with him say.
‚ÄúGarcia Harfuch was sort of destined to follow in his father and grandfather‚Äôs footsteps,‚Äù said Gladys McCormick, a professor and historian of U.S.-Mexican relations at Syracuse University.
But that same legacy is regarded with suspicion in parts of the ruling leftist Morena party. Both his grandfather and father oversaw periods of military abuses and repression of social movements by security forces.
Critics also highlight Harfuch‚Äôs links to the infamous disappearance of 43 students from the Ayotzinapa teaching college in 2014. A 2022 truth commission report cited him‚Äîthen a mid-level federal police officer‚Äîas having attended meetings where officials crafted a version of events that masked the role of security forces in the disappearances.
Harfuch, who was never accused of wrongdoing, has previously said he only attended meetings to help coordinate the search for the missing students. No local or ‚Äãfederal officials have been sentenced in the case.
For the United States, Harfuch has ‚Äãemerged as the linchpin in security collaboration with Mexico at a ‚ÅÝtime when U.S. President Donald Trump is piling pressure on his southern neighbor to aggressively combat cartels, and threatening U.S. military force if Mexico can‚Äôt show results.
Derek Maltz, former acting administrator of the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration, said he met Harfuch last year shortly after Mexico transferred 29 suspected high-level cartel members to the U.S. in what was then the largest such handover in history.
‚ÄúHe looked me in the eyes, and he said, ‚ÄòThis is only the beginning,‚Äù Maltz recalled.
Maltz had his doubts, ‚Äãbut in the coming months Mexico handed over another 63 suspected cartel leaders and took down the country‚Äôs most-wanted drug lord. ‚ÄúI'm very impressed with what I'm seeing,‚Äù he said.
The prisoner transfers underscore what officials from both countries ‚Äãsay is an unprecedented level of cooperation and ‚ÅÝintelligence sharing as they seek to dismantle cartels through military confrontations, money laundering investigations, and drug and firearm seizures.
The hunt for El Mencho gained urgency in November, when the Jalisco cartel kidnapped two of Harfuch‚Äôs investigators in the city of Zapopan, a cartel stronghold, according to the high-ranking Mexican official.
Soldiers raided houses of suspected cartel members and interrogations produced information that helped tighten the net around El Mencho. Reuters is the first outlet to report the role of the kidnappings in the search for El Mencho. The agents were released after a week.
The breakthrough came when authorities tracked one of El Mencho‚Äôs multiple girlfriends to his villa, Mexico‚Äôs Defense Minister Ricardo Trevilla has ‚ÅÝsaid. A new U.S.-military-led ‚Äãtask force confirmed the exact location of the house, Reuters reported.
But the government official said the real slip wasn‚Äôt the romance; it was that the 59-year-old cartel leader wanted to see his ‚Äãtwo children with the woman. Mexican troops swooped in after the girlfriend and El Mencho‚Äôs children left.
After a shootout, El Mencho died on a military helicopter en route to a hospital. Eight of his bodyguards were also killed. Two soldiers died during the raid; another two died later from injuries.
Harfuch received a confirmation text with an image of El Mencho‚Äôs body, still clad in a flak ‚Äãjacket, the Mexican official said.
‚ÄúI spoke with him Sunday morning after El Mencho was killed,‚Äù said Eduardo Clark, a top health official in Mexico who is close to Harfuch. ‚ÄúHe told me, ‚Äòthis is a huge relief.‚Äô‚Äù

item.title: Gulf food strategy tested as Iran war snarls shipping routes
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - Wealthy Gulf states are facing their biggest food security challenge since the 2008 global food crisis, as the Iran conflict threatens ports and disrupts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz , opens new tab .
The war is testing strategies adopted after ‚Äã2008, when soaring food prices prompted Gulf nations to shift toward import-dependent policies reliant on pouring cash into agricultural investments abroad.
That strategy did away with prior expensive programmes that sought to ‚Äåraise domestic production of strategic grains but ran up against the region's brutal climate and lack of water. Saudi Arabia, for example, began to scale back a domestic wheat-growing programme in 2008 to become almost exclusively reliant on imports.
Now with global shipping disrupted and airspace closed in many countries in a region that is 80%-90% dependent on food imports, price surges and scarcity of some goods are expected.
"With over 70% of GCC foodstuffs being imported through the Strait of Hormuz, Gulf states face shortages if the war persists," said Neil ‚ÄãQuilliam, associate fellow at think tank Chatham House.
"While GCC countries have taken steps to diversify suppliers and ensure sufficient stores to withstand disruption, this can only last several months. At this point, price increases ‚Äãand longer lead times will start to hit the markets."
Analysts warn that even temporary blockages in Hormuz that force rerouting from major ports to smaller ones will ‚ÅÝcreate strains.
Most major Gulf ports, including Dubai's Jebel Ali and primary ports in Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar and the Saudi Gulf coast, are located where most incoming traffic would have to pass through the Hormuz waterway.
Iranian attacks struck ‚Äãmany of those lifelines including Jebel Ali, the region's largest container port, this week, suspending operations for hours.
"The biggest immediate effect will be due to the blockade of Jebel Ali, serving about 50 million people," Ishan Bhanu, lead agricultural ‚Äãcommodities analyst at Kpler, said about the Dubai port that also serves as a re-export hub to the region and beyond.
