UAE ports outside the strait have limited capacity. Khorfakkan can handle 5 million twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs) and Fujairah less than 1 million and would be hard-placed to make up for capacity lost at Jebel Ali or Abu Dhabi‚Äôs Khalifa Port.
"Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain and Iraq effectively become landlocked and will depend on overland routes through Saudi Arabia," Bhanu added, warning of costly congestion.
Those bottlenecks are yet to show and the UAE has said its strategic reserves of vital goods cover four to six months of needs. It urged residents to report unjustified price increases through a dedicated hotline.
Supermarket staff ‚Äãtold Reuters shelves remain largely stocked, though suppliers are taking longer to replenish certain products. Dubai this week temporarily relaxed truck-movement restrictions to maintain the flow of goods.
The start of Iran's strikes on the Gulf on Saturday prompted many to hoard and ‚Äåcaused a ‚ÅÝtemporary dip that further fueled panic, a dry run for what could come.
"It is worth noting that perception risk matters and even if stocks are sufficient now, public runs on supermarkets can spook the public," Quilliam said.
A few residents already noticed some price rises.
"Is it just me whose groceries cost three times as of yesterday?" one shopper wrote in a local Facebook group on Tuesday. "Even the bananas cost have gone crazy."
Goods like bananas that spoil quickly are particularly vulnerable to any shipping rerouting that makes journeys longer. Flying in perishable food when airspace opens to cut the journey will raise prices.
"If food is flown in or brought in overland, that is going to be more expensive ‚Äãthan shipping," said Justin Alexander, Gulf analyst at GlobalSource Partners ‚Äãand director at Khalij Economics.
"It may be that ‚ÅÝgovernments choose to absorb some of that cost through subsidizing the food. And they've certainly done that in previous crises."
Apart from investing abroad to secure access to major food production bases, Gulf states have also been constructing modern silos capable of storing hundreds of thousands of tons of strategic grains in the past two decades. These ‚Äãsilos offer a buffer for staples that can be stored for months like wheat, rice and edible oils.
The UAE opened its Fujairah grain silos in 2016 on ‚Äãthe Indian Ocean coast outside ‚ÅÝthe Strait of Hormuz, with roughly 300,000 metric ton capacity. The location was chosen strategically to bypass Hormuz with Iran having already threatened to close the strait whenever tensions rose with the West.
"Fujairah's grain silos act as a strategically important pressure valve providing routing flexibility and risk diversification when the Gulf's maritime environment tightens," said Sudhakar Tomar, president of India Middle East Agri Alliance Ecosystem.
The initiative originally envisioned emergency stocks to be shared by the entire Gulf region. But practical obstacles, ‚ÅÝincluding long distances ‚Äãand limited road or rail links between countries, meant it stayed domestic. Other Gulf states have since built their own storage, including Qatar's ‚ÄãFood Security Terminal at Hamad Port with 51 climate-controlled silos.
Regional cooperation among Gulf Cooperation Council members will be critical to averting food shortages, but the six-nation bloc has long struggled with coordination.
"It will require close cooperation amongst GCC states to manage complex logistics and ensure that ‚Äãall six states and Iraq are sated," Quilliam said.

item.title: Trump undertakes sweeping makeover of White House and Washington
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump is pursuing an ambitious remaking of Washington, from a $400 million White House ballroom to a 250-foot arch and a renovated Kennedy Center.
Trump's sweeping redesign efforts would be arguably the most dramatic by a U.S. president since Theodore Roosevelt championed a structural overhaul of the National Mall in the early 1900s. In the 1950s, Harry Truman gutted and rebuilt the White House.
Here are some of Trump's passion ‚Äãprojects, which have drawn sharp criticism from Americans concerned about pocket-book issues and the preservation of historic landmarks.
Trump has said his planned 90,000-square-foot ballroom will be "the Greatest of ‚Äåits kind ever built," matching the existing White House in height and scale. Estimated to cost $400 million, he said it will be funded by wealthy individuals and corporations.
But the president's enthusiasm about the project, which he says will seat 1,000 guests, has not been widely shared. He has faced public backlash since he demolished the East Wing to make room for the structure, despite earlier assurances that he would not.
A Washington Post-ABC News-Ipsos poll in October found 56% of Americans opposed the project, with 28% supporting it.
Preservationists and opponents of ‚Äãthe project criticized the loss of the East Wing - which housed the offices of the first lady and the White House movie theater - and raised concerns that it could visually overpower the main mansion.
The work ‚Äãon the project provides a daily cacophony of construction noise between the White House and the Treasury Department, a disruption likely to continue for much of Trump's three ‚ÅÝremaining years in office.
The National Capital Planning Commission, whose chairman is a top White House aide, is scheduled to hear public comments on the project and potentially vote on it on Thursday.
Congress authorized the John ‚ÄãF. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts to honor the Democratic president who was assassinated in 1963. That name went unchallenged for decades ‚Äî until a Trump‚Äëappointed board voted last year to rename it the Trump‚ÄìKennedy Center.
The storied cultural center ‚Äãsaw a flurry of show cancellations and slumping ticket sales after Trump's takeover.
On February 1, Trump announced that the property will close for two years beginning July 4 for a major overhaul. Trump insists the structure needs a retool, citing problems with the plumbing and crumbling masonry.
He has said he does not plan to tear down the Kennedy Center but instead will renovate it, a claim that critics note he also made about the East Wing before it was destroyed. He estimated the renovations will cost $200 million.
Across the ‚ÄãPotomac River from the Lincoln Memorial lies a nondescript highway roundabout upon which Trump wants to build what he calls the Independence Arch - an arch reminiscent of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, except much bigger.
The height of the ‚Äãarch, with eagle statues and a Lady Liberty-type figure on top, has been estimated at 250 feet. That is higher than the Lincoln Memorial and not far off the size of the U.S. Capitol, which at 288 feet can be seen across ‚Äåmuch of Washington.
By ‚ÅÝcontrast, the Arc de Triomphe in Paris is 164 feet high.
Trump told reporters on January 31 that he wanted a large arch because "we're the biggest, most powerful nation."
Whether the arch will actually be built with a 250-foot height remains unclear, as there is a possibility it could interfere with the flight path of southbound planes on the final approach to Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport a few miles away.
Trump embarked on his first major redecoration project at the White House when he moved back into the Oval Office in January 2025.
He transformed the storied room with gold accents and statuettes, portraits of famous Americans pulled from storage - including some who are not easily identifiable - ‚Äãand a copy of the Declaration of Independence hanging on ‚Äãthe wall behind a black sheet.
Busts of Abraham ‚ÅÝLincoln and Benjamin Franklin are now situated on tables near his desk. The level of bric-a-brac can give the room a cluttered, old library feel compared to the styles of previous presidents, but Trump is pleased with it and likes to give tours to visitors.
Outside the Oval Office, Trump replaced the Rose Garden's iconic grass ‚Äãlawn , opens new tab with a white stone patio and umbrella-covered tables to give it a patio style much like Trump has by his pool at his Mar-a-Lago Club in Palm Beach, ‚ÄãFlorida. He said the stone ‚ÅÝwas needed because women wearing high heels would sink into the soil.
Along the nearby colonnade walkway, Trump placed portraits of America's 47 presidents on the wall, with a plaque beneath each inscribed with Trump's view of that person.
Bitter at his loss to Democrat Joe Biden in the 2020 election, Trump replaced Biden‚Äôs portrait with an image of an autopen, a device he and other Republicans have criticized Biden for using for signing documents despite it being a fairly standard practice for recent presidents.
Elsewhere ‚ÅÝon the White ‚ÄãHouse grounds, Trump has placed large flagpoles on both the North and South Lawns.
Inside the White House, Trump tore out the green-tiled, retro-styled ‚Äãbathroom in the Lincoln Bedroom and replaced it with a modern version featuring white and black marble.
The green-tiled Lincoln bathroom dated back to Truman's White House renovation in the 1950s, according to a Washington Post article from 2007.
At the time, then-President George W. Bush and first lady ‚ÄãLaura Bush undertook a renovation of the Lincoln Bedroom, which Lincoln used as an office. The Post article describing the Bush renovation said they had left alone the "well-preserved" bathroom.

item.title: The Kurdish struggle for rights and land
item.description:
March 5 - Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the U.S. about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in western Iran, sources say, as the U.S. and Israel pursue their military campaign against Tehran.
The Kurds are an ethnic group that was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the collapsing Ottoman Empire.
Kurdish nationalism stirred in the 1890s ‚Äãwhen the Ottoman Empire was on its last legs. The 1920 Treaty of Sevres, which imposed a colonial carve-up of Turkey after World War One, promised them independence.
Three years later, ‚ÄåTurkish leader Mustafa Kemal Ataturk rendered that accord obsolete by winning the Turkish War of Independence. The Treaty of Lausanne, ratified in 1924, divided the Kurds among the new nations of the Middle East.
They speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous region straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey. Kurds are mostly Sunni Muslims.
Kurds form about 10% of the Syrian population.
Under deposed President Bashar al-Assad, the Baathist state deprived thousands of them of citizenship rights, banned their ‚Äãlanguage and clamped down on Kurdish political activity.
As Syria descended into civil war in 2011, with Assad focused on trying to crush Sunni Arab rebels in western Syria, the Syrian Kurdish PYD group ‚Äãand an affiliated militia, the YPG, established self-rule in Kurdish-majority areas of the north.
The area under Kurdish control expanded as the YPG, under the umbrella of the ‚ÅÝSyrian Democratic Forces, partnered with the United States in the campaign against Islamic State.
Following Assad's ouster by Islamist rebels led by Sharaa in 2024, the Kurdish groups have aimed to preserve their autonomy, concerned that the new ‚Äãrulers in Damascus aim to dominate the new order.
But their hand has been weakened as Washington has built close ties to Sharaa, echoing his calls for the Kurds to integrate with Damascus.
Sharaa, who has vowed to protect ‚Äãthe rights of all Syrian groups, issued a decree on January 16 formally recognizing Kurdish as a national language alongside Arabic, and permitting it to be taught in schools, among other steps.
Since then, government advances have forced the SDF to retreat to Kurdish-majority areas.
The PYD is heavily influenced by the ideology of the Kurdistan Workers Party (PKK), long active in neighbouring Turkey.
Kurds form about 20% of the population of Turkey, mainly living in the southeast.
The PKK took up arms against the state in 1984, waging an ‚Äãinsurgency, initially seeking a separate state but later moderating its goal to autonomy and greater rights for Kurds.
More than 40,000 people have been killed in the conflict.
Turkey has held PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan in prison since capturing him ‚Äãin 1999.
In early 2025, Turkey launched a peace process with the PKK, and Ocalan called on the PKK to lay down arms. The process has, however, somewhat stalled since then.
President Tayyip Erdogan removed restrictions on using the Kurdish language during the ‚Äå2000s and early ‚ÅÝ2010s.
The United States, the European Union and Turkey classify the PKK as a terrorist organization.
Turkey's military has often struck targets in Iraq's Kurdish region near the PKK's stronghold in the Qandil mountains.
Turkey has also sent troops into northern Syria several times to counter the YPG, which Ankara deems an extension of the PKK.
Turkish officials have said recent events in Syria could kick-start the peace process in Turkey.
Kurds form 15-20% of the population, mainly inhabiting three mountainous northern provinces that make up Iraqi Kurdistan.
In the late 1980s, Iraq's authoritarian ruler Saddam Hussein targeted Iraqi Kurds with chemical gas, razed Kurdish villages and forced thousands of Kurds into camps.
After Saddam's forces were driven out ‚Äãof the north in the first Gulf War in ‚Äã1991, Kurdish political parties gained control of Iraqi ‚ÅÝKurdistan.
Since the U.S.-led invasion that toppled Saddam in 2003, Iraq's central government has recognised autonomy for Kurdistan, which is led by a regional government with a budget apportioned from Baghdad under a formula for dividing oil income.
When Islamic State militants swept through much of northern Iraq in 2014, Kurdish fighters exploited the collapse of central authority ‚Äãto expand their territory, including taking control of Kirkuk, an oil city they view as their ancient capital.
In September 2017, Iraq's Kurds held a referendum on ‚Äãindependence, which backfired, eliciting fierce ‚ÅÝopposition from Baghdad and regional powers. The vote prompted military and economic retaliation from Baghdad, which retook Kirkuk and other disputed areas. Ties between the centre and the Kurdish regional government have since improved, though tensions remain over oil exports and revenue-sharing.
Kurds form about 10% of the population.
Rights groups say Kurds, along with other religious and ethnic minorities, face discrimination under the ruling clerical establishment. The Islamic Republic denies persecuting Kurds.
There are three main Iranian Kurdish separatist factions, all ‚ÅÝbased in Iraqi ‚ÄãKurdistan. Iran has demanded authorities in Iraq hand over separatist Kurdish dissidents stationed there and close their bases.
Iran's Kurdish regions have been ‚Äãa focal point of unrest following anti-government protests that began in late December 2025 and led to thousands of people being killed in January 2026. During the unrest Reuters reported that armed Kurdish separatist groups had sought to cross the border into Iran from Iraq.
Kurdish areas ‚Äãwere also a major flashpoint during the previous major wave of domestic unrest in 2022, when nationwide protests were triggered by the death in custody of an Iranian-Kurdish woman.

item.title: Mexico steps up investigations into fuel smuggling by drug cartels
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Mexico has expanded a probe into fuel smuggling at sea ports highlighted in a 2025 Reuters investigation into cartel-linked fuel crimes, according to a government document reviewed by the news agency outlining ‚Äãactions taken by the administration.
The February document says the anti-corruption ministry is overseeing internal investigations ‚Äåwithin the ports of Guaymas, Tampico and Ensenada as well as in the Navy and customs agency.
The government previously had acknowledged probes into suspected fuel smuggling at the Port of Tampico and last year arrested 14 people allegedly involved, including customs agents and ‚ÄãNavy officials.
The government comments on the fuel smuggling investigations came in response to questions from the ‚Äãopposition National Action Party (PAN) about suspected corruption within state agencies that purportedly allowed fuel smuggling ‚ÅÝto flourish in Mexico. The fuel smuggling section was part of a broader 217-page response to questions ‚Äãfrom senators about the administration's performance on its policy priorities.
That section of the document, available on the Senate's website , opens new tab , was ‚Äãfirst reported by local media.
The port administrations of Guaymas, Tampico and Ensenada didn't respond to requests to comment, nor did Mexico's Attorney General's office, Navy or customs agency.
Fuel smuggling in Mexico involves falsifying shipping and customs paperwork on fuel coming mainly from the ‚ÄãUnited States. Smugglers evade a steep Mexican tax on imported diesel and gasoline by declaring it to be ‚Äãsome other type of petroleum product that's exempt from the duty. The savings can amount to more than half a cargo's ‚Äåvalue.
Illicit fuel ‚ÅÝand stolen crude are now the second-largest source of revenue for Mexico's cartels behind narcotics, according to the U.S. Treasury Department.
Emboldened smugglers have graduated from truck and rail shipments to using petroleum tankers, a development that U.S. and Mexican security officials say points to collusion between cartels and crooked port insiders.
In its 2025 investigation, Reuters tracked ‚Äãa vessel that offloaded imported ‚Äãdiesel in the ports ‚ÅÝof Ensenada and Guaymas that was declared in Mexico to be tax-free lubricants, costing the government $7 million in lost duties.
The importer was suspected to be a front company ‚Äãfor the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, according to three Mexican security officials and ‚Äãan undated government ‚ÅÝsecurity document viewed by Reuters.
PAN in its questions urged the federal government to explain how it would investigate, dismantle and punish anyone involved in the fuel smuggling network, including high-ranking government officials that may be part of it.
Mexican President ‚ÅÝClaudia Sheinbaum ‚Äãhas been under intense pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump to ‚Äãcrack down on drug cartels.
PAN and the president's office did not respond to requests for comment.

item.title: US-Israeli strikes on Iran in breach of international law, says Italian minister
item.description:
ROME, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran which sparked the latest Middle East crisis were ‚Äãclearly in breach of international law, Italian ‚ÄåDefence Minister Guido Crosetto said on Thursday.
It was the strongest criticism to date from Italy's right-wing administration, ‚Äãwhich has sought to establish close ‚Äãties with U.S. President Donald Trump since ‚ÅÝhe took office last year.
The decision to ‚Äãlaunch the strikes last weekend "of course fell outside, ‚Äãneedless to say, the rules of international law", Crosetto told the lower house of parliament.
He said Italy ‚Äãwas being forced to respond to a ‚Äãconflict that the United States and Israel had initiated without ‚Äåwarning ‚ÅÝtheir allies and partners.
"It is a war that was started without anyone in the world knowing. One in which we, like the ‚Äãrest of ‚Äãthe world, ‚ÅÝfind ourselves having to manage (the consequences)," said Crosetto, who is a ‚Äãmember of Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's ‚ÄãBrothers ‚ÅÝof Italy party.
Italy has said it will send air defence aid to Gulf countries and naval assets ‚ÅÝto ‚ÄãCyprus to help them ‚Äãprotect against Iranian strikes launched in response to the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãattacks.

item.title: Russia and Ukraine to exchange 500 POWs over coming two days, Moscow says
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia ‚Äåand Ukraine exchanged 200 prisoners of ‚Äãwar on ‚Äãeach side on Thursday ‚ÅÝand will swap another ‚Äã300 each on ‚ÄãFriday, the Russian Foreign Ministry said.
The two ‚Äãsides have ‚Äãperiodically traded POWs and ‚Äåreturned bodies ‚ÅÝof dead soldiers - most recently last week - ‚Äãdespite ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝlack of progress towards ‚Äãa peace ‚Äãagreement ‚ÅÝto end their four-year war.

item.title: French appeals court lowers charges against police officer facing trial for killing of teenager
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - A French police officer initially charged with murder in the deadly shooting of a teenager in 2023 will instead stand trial for ‚Äãthe lesser charge of voluntary assault leading to death, the Versailles ‚Äåappeals court said on Thursday.
Police officer Florian M. had fired at Nahel Merzouk after the latter had failed to comply with an order to stop his car.
The seventeen year old had ‚Äãlater died from his wounds, sparking violent protests across the country.
In 2025, the ‚ÄãNanterre prosecutor's office said one of two officers under investigation, ‚ÅÝidentified as Florian M., would face the Assize Court of Hauts-de-Seine on charges ‚Äãof murder.
Florian M. appealed, and the court lowered the charges on Thursday.
His lawyer ‚ÄãLaurent-Franck Lienard said he was satisfied with that decision but said that his client should not be on trial at all.
"On this point, it is a real disappointment," he said. "We will ‚Äãcontinue our efforts so that his shooting is recognized as legitimate."
Under French ‚Äãlaw, voluntary assault leading to death by a person holding state authority carries a maximum sentence ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝ20 years, compared with up to 30 years for murder.
A charge of complicity in murder against the other officer was dropped in June.
"The court did not follow the investigating judges regarding the homicidal intent, considering that it had not ‚Äãbeen established that Florian ‚ÄãM. intended, at ‚ÅÝthe moment he fired, to take the driver‚Äôs life," the court said in a statement.
A video, widely circulated on ‚Äãsocial media and verified by Reuters, had shown two police ‚Äãofficers beside ‚ÅÝthe car, a Mercedes-AMG, with one firing his weapon as the driver pulled away.
"This is a rather scandalous and shameful decision," said Frank Berton, lawyer for the ‚ÅÝfamily of ‚ÄãNahel M.
"It is not for the Versailles appeals ‚Äãcourt to rule on the intent to kill. With this decision, the court allows the police ‚Äãofficer to avoid appearing before a jury."

item.title: UK‚Äôs slow support for allies in Iran conflict deepens doubts about military effectiveness
item.description:
LONDON, March 4 (Reuters) - Britain's cautious response to the escalating conflict in the Middle East and its hesitancy in defending its allies from attack have renewed doubts among partners about its military effectiveness when the U.S. ‚Äãis demanding widespread rearmament.
Trump lashed out at Prime Minister Keir Starmer on Tuesday, suggesting he had helped to "ruin" the countries' historically close relationship after London blocked the U.S.'s use ‚Äåof British bases to launch pre-emptive strikes on Iran .
Cypriot officials have also criticised London after an Iranian-made drone hit one of Britain's military bases on the island, prompting France, Greece and others to send support . A British destroyer is not expected to sail until next week, and will likely take around a week to get to the region.
Starmer has defended his actions , saying British personnel would only get involved in military action that was legal and well planned. ‚ÄãBritish jets have since brought down Iranian drones, Britain has resupplied allies' air defence systems and its bases are now being used by the U.S. for defensive operations .
But the initial response ‚Äãhas sparked alarm about the state of Britain's military preparedness after years of cuts.
Simon Diggins, a former British defence attach√© who worked with U.S. troops ‚ÅÝin Afghanistan, said if he had to summarise the situation he would say: "The Brits are not serious".
He said Trump was "vulgar, brash, rude, and undiplomatic" but Britain had made itself "operationally and strategically" irrelevant, despite ‚Äãits nuclear weapons and advanced fighter jets such as F-35s.
The latest spat follows months of growing tensions between Washington and its traditional allies.
But the shrinking of Britain's armed ‚Äãforces has undermined London's efforts to be seen as the U.S.'s most reliable military partner in Europe, when mounting geopolitical tensions mean Britain is facing requests to bolster positions in Ukraine, in the Arctic, and now in the Middle East.
With a little over 70,000 full-time trained soldiers, Britain's army is its smallest since the Napoleonic wars.
According to the International Institute for Strategic Studies think-tank, Britain has fallen behind the rearmament of other European countries with gaps in armoured ‚Äãvehicles, ships and ground-based air defence systems.
Starmer, who has blamed previous Conservative governments for a lack of military investment, plans to spend 2.5% of GDP on defence by 2027 and aims for 3% after ‚Äã2029.
But his government has delayed the release of its defence investment plan for the next decade.
Trump has said that Germany and France are now challenging Britain as Washington's most valued partners in Europe.
Starmer's caution is ‚Äåexplained in ‚ÅÝpart by the spectre of the Iraq war, when previous Labour Prime Minister Tony Blair's decision to join the 2003 U.S.-led invasion proved deeply divisive in the country.
Analysts also said that, while Britain could defend its hesitancy on the use of its bases due to legal concerns, the lack of military preparation while the U.S. amassed warships and fighter jets in the region was a strategic failure.
Tom Sharpe, who commanded four British warships during a 27-year naval career, said the failure to have a warship in the region was one of the worst British military decisions he had seen.
"You have the wafer-thinness of our ‚Äãforces and that increases the imperative to make ‚Äãgood operational decisions and we can't seem to ‚ÅÝdo that either," he said.
That lack of readiness has been brought home by the drone attack on the British Royal Air Force's Akrotiri base in Cyprus.
Cypriot officials told Reuters they were infuriated that Starmer had not stated publicly that the U.S. would not use Akrotiri, shortly before it was hit by ‚Äãa drone thought to have come from Hezbollah.
Former diplomats, a current official and analysts said although Trump was clearly ‚Äãangry with Britain, they did ‚ÅÝnot see his latest outburst - when he said Starmer was "no Winston Churchill" - as a fundamental shift in policy.
Michael Martins, who worked at London's U.S. embassy in Trump's first term, said he did not expect the two countries to "decouple meaningfully" when they work closely on intelligence.
A U.S. official said he saw the criticism as "a moment, not a shift", and Starmer himself said the U.S.-British "special relationship" was reflected in their deep levels of cooperation, ‚ÅÝnot in the ‚Äãlatest words of the president.
But the widening conflict in the Middle East means it is not just the U.S. which is ‚Äãlooking for Britain to do more.
Bader Al-Saif, an associate fellow at Chatham House based in Kuwait, said European countries needed to show they could support their Gulf allies when needed.
"I'm not saying that they should join the war effort, but I ‚Äãthink they have vested partners in the region, including the Gulf states, which they can offer support to," he said.

item.title: Iranian warship sunk by US torpedo participated in Indian drill
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 5 - The U.S. attack on an Iranian warship in the Indian Ocean this week was the first time since World War Two that the United States has sunk an enemy vessel ‚Äãwith a torpedo.
The following details are based on accounts from Sri Lankan, U.S. and ‚ÄåIranian officials of the March 4 incident, part of U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and its military assets.
The Sri Lankan Navy launched a search-and-rescue operation after receiving a distress call from the Iranian warship IRIS ‚ÄãDena early on Wednesday.
By the time rescuers reached the area, the ship had already sunk, leaving ‚Äãonly an oil slick on the surface. The vessel had about 130 people ‚ÅÝon board.
Sri Lanka recovered the bodies of 87 sailors and rescued 32, who were being treated for ‚Äãminor injuries and were expected to be released from hospital on Thursday. Search operations were continuing for ‚Äãan estimated 10 crew members who remain unaccounted for.
The ship sank within Sri Lanka‚Äôs exclusive economic zone, about 19 nautical miles off the southern port city of Galle, after coming under ‚Äãattack from a U.S. submarine.
Dena was returning to Iran after taking part in a naval exercise ‚Äãin India from February 16 to February 26, held off the southern Indian city of Visakhapatnam.
The Iranian vessel was among ‚Äå18 ‚ÅÝforeign warships participating in the biennial MILAN exercise, alongside ships from Sri Lanka, Australia, Japan and Russia. Officials from more than 70 countries attended, including representatives from the United States and Britain.
India described the exercise as one of the largest multilateral naval drills in the Indo-Pacific, aimed at strengthening interoperability, maritime domain ‚Äãawareness and collective response ‚Äãcapabilities.
India‚Äôs ‚ÅÝnavy had welcomed Dena‚Äôs arrival as a reflection of the ‚Äúlong-standing cultural links between the two nations‚Äù. Since the sinking, videos have circulated of Iranian sailors marching ‚Äãthrough the streets of Visakhapatnam during exercise events.
Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÅÝX that Dena was ‚Äúa guest of India‚Äôs Navy‚Äù and was struck without warning in international waters. He said the U.S. "will bitterly regret the precedent it has set".
New Delhi has not officially commented on the attack, though ‚ÅÝmany in ‚ÄãIndia have praised southern neighbour Sri Lanka for its role ‚Äãin the rescue. India‚Äôs main opposition Congress party criticised the government‚Äôs silence, saying the conflict had ‚Äúreached our backyard‚Äù and questioning New ‚ÄãDelhi‚Äôs role as a ‚Äúnet security provider‚Äù in the Indian Ocean region.

item.title: Joy of UK's Iranian diaspora turning to fear as conflict rocks homeland
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - In an area of London known ‚Äãas Little Tehran, the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei prompted spontaneous street parties, dancing and fireworks. Shoppers still smile ‚Äåas some stores hand out sweet pastries, traditional in Persian culture when there's cause for celebration.
But as the bombardment of their homeland enters its sixth day, the joy is beginning to fade.
Hundreds of civilians have been killed, major cities are being pummelled and there is growing concern for uncles, ‚Äãaunts and cousins who cannot be reached in Iran.
"On Saturday night, people were happy, it was amazing," said Mostafa ‚ÄãZaryabi, 44, who works in an Iranian bakery in the Little Tehran area in Finchley, north ‚ÅÝLondon, explaining that with the death of Khamenei, who he called "a dictator," he was hopeful there could now be freedom in ‚ÄãIran.
At a gathering of about 200 mostly Iranian British citizens in central London on Wednesday, 38-year-old human rights activist Bita, who did ‚Äãnot want to share her surname, spoke of the "happy mood" of Iranians in Britain.
"There's a sense of justice and I think, above all, there's a sense of hopefulness," she said.
Another activist, Haleh Blake, 39, said she felt much calmer in a world without Khamenei and while she did feel ‚Äãfear about the route to change, she was optimistic about the future.
"We can actually dream about what Iran will be next," ‚Äãshe said.
But Rajad Ali Shahabi, 69, is worried about the destruction of the country. He owns the bakery where the diaspora come to buy ‚Äåtraditional ‚ÅÝhandmade flatbreads sprinkled with sesame seeds, and delicacies like green raisins, saffron ginger and sour cherry snacks.
"It's day and night, they don't stop," Shahabi said of the bombing, which he finds stressful to follow on the news.
Reflecting on the events of recent days, 52-year-old I.T. engineer Kamran Naderi said he felt "happy and sad" at the same time.
"Happy to see the government will hopefully disappear, but ‚Äãsad to see some innocent ‚Äãpeople as collateral damage could ‚ÅÝbe killed."
Naderi is one of 114,000 Iranian-born people living in Britain, according to the 2021/22 census. Between 2015 and 2024, tens of thousands ‚Äãof Iranians applied for asylum in the UK, more than any other nationality, according to the ‚ÄãMigration Observatory at ‚ÅÝthe University of Oxford.
Like dozens of premises in the area, Shahabi's bakery displays the Lion and Sun flag, the symbol of Iran before the Islamic Revolution of 1979. It is often used to show support for the opposition figure Reza Pahlavi , the exiled son of Iran's ‚ÅÝlast shah.
Shahabi, ‚Äãwho would like to see Pahlavi return to lead a secular democratic Iran, ‚Äãsaid while there were apprehensions about the conflict, the majority of his customers have been smiling since Saturday.
"You can see joy on their faces," he said. "This ‚Äãis the beginning of the end."

item.title: Conservative Anglicans set up a council to lead them, not Archbishop of Canterbury
item.description:
ABUJA, March 5 (Reuters) - A group of conservative Anglicans said ‚Äåat a conference in Nigeria on Thursday that they were setting up a council to lead the global Anglican Communion, in a ‚Äãdirect challenge to the authority of the Archbishop ‚Äãof Canterbury.
The council will include bishops, clergy and lay members, ‚ÅÝeach with voting privileges, the group announced.
It unanimously ‚Äãelected Rwandan archbishop Laurent Mbanda as the chairman of the ‚Äãnew council but said he would not be "primus inter pares" (first among equals) but rather share power.
"Believing the current instruments of communion no ‚Äãlonger meet the needs of the majority of ‚ÄãAnglicans around the world, the global Anglican Communion is to be led ‚Äåby ‚ÅÝa conciliar structure," Bishop Paul Donison told the conference.
"I am also pleased to announce that Archbishop Laurent Mbanda was unanimously elected chairman of the Global Anglican Council," ‚ÄãDonison added.
The Global ‚ÄãAnglican Future ‚ÅÝConference (GAFCON) brings together conservative churches mainly in Africa and Asia and claims to now ‚Äãrepresent the majority of Anglicans worldwide.
It opposes ‚Äãliberal ‚ÅÝshifts in parts of the Communion, including the ordination of women and greater inclusion of LGBTQ+ members. The group ‚ÅÝstrongly criticised ‚Äãthe Church of England's appointment ‚Äãlast October of Sarah Mullally as its first female Archbishop of Canterbury.

item.title: Belarus leader Lukashenko pardons 18 prisoners, his administration says
item.description: MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Belarusian ‚ÄãPresident Alexander ‚ÄåLukashenko has pardoned ‚Äã18 prisoners, ‚Äãof whom 15 ‚ÅÝwere ‚Äãserving sentences ‚Äãfor "extremism", a charge often ‚Äãused ‚Äãagainst opponents of ‚Äåhis ‚ÅÝgovernment, a Telegram channel linked ‚Äãto ‚Äãhis ‚ÅÝadministration said ‚Äãon ‚ÄãThursday.

item.title: More than 20,000 Israelis return since start of Iran war, ministry says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - More than 20,000 Israelis have returned to the country since the start of the Iran air war that began on Saturday, the Transportation Ministry said on Thursday, adding ‚Äãthat about 120,000 more Israelis currently abroad are seeking to come home.
Israel began to open ‚Äåits airspace on Thursday and allowed a handful of flights to land at Ben Gurion International Airport near Tel Aviv.
Israel's airspace was closed on Saturday at the start of the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, which have triggered missile barrages ‚Äãfrom Iran against Israel, leaving tens of thousands of passengers stranded abroad.
The ministry said its data ‚Äãshow 120,000 Israelis are currently abroad who wish to return to Israel and ‚ÅÝthat the operation to repatriate them would likely take seven to 10 days.
Israel's Population and Immigration Authority ‚Äãsaid nearly 300,000 Israelis have flown abroad in the last three months and airlines have said tens ‚Äãof thousands of customers seek to return.
It added that it was working to expand options for arriving in and departing from Israel by air, land and sea crossings.
Most Israelis have so far arrived in Israel via land crossings into the ‚Äãsouthern Red Sea resort city of Eilat.
Israeli airlines have been operating repatriation flights from European cities to ‚ÄãTaba, Egypt and Aqaba, Jordan - adjacent to Eilat.
These flights will continue but Israel's four airlines - flag carrier El Al (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab , Israir (ISRG.TA) , opens new tab , ‚ÄåArkia ‚ÅÝand Air Haifa - have begun flights to Tel Aviv. While airspace is being gradually opened, only incoming flights are currently allowed, at a rate of just one landing per hour due to frequent missile fire from Iran.
"We are doing everything to return every Israeli home safely," said Transportation Minister Miri Regev, previously a ‚ÄãBrigadier General in the ‚Äãmilitary.
"With the opening of ‚ÅÝairspace, the return of Israelis to Israel began, and we continue to work around the clock together with all parties to expand the options for returning ‚Äãand leaving the country in accordance with security restrictions," she said.
Earlier, the first ‚Äãplanes to ‚ÅÝarrive at Ben Gurion were Israir and Arkia flights from Rome, and an El Al flight from Athens. Flights from other cities in Europe, as well as from the United States and Asia, are also planned.
Ben Gurion ‚ÅÝsaid ‚Äãoutgoing flights would resume on Sunday, but initially would be ‚Äãlimited to 50 passengers per flight.
Israel's airlines have halted ticket sales between March 15 and March 21 to allow customers whose flights ‚Äãwere cancelled to be accommodated when the airspace opens.

item.title: Israel to attack Iran's underground missile sites in second phase of war, sources say
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel's war in Iran is entering a second phase that will see its fighter jets attacking ballistic missile sites buried ‚Äãdeep underground, two sources familiar with Israel's military campaign said.
The joint air assault with the U.S. in Iran is ‚Äånearing the end of its first week after opening salvos killed the country's leaders and set off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and Iraq, and Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
Israel's military says it has hit hundreds of Iranian missile launchers above ground that could target Israeli cities. The second phase will include ‚Äãbunkers storing ballistic missiles and equipment, said the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the ‚Äãsubject.
One said Israel aimed to neutralise Iran's ability to launch aerial attacks at Israel by the end of the war, ‚ÅÝwhich was also focused on taking out the Islamic Republic's leadership.
A military spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment on its ‚Äãattack plans. The military has previously asserted that it and the U.S. military took control of much of Iran's airspace in the opening days of ‚Äãthe attacks.
In a statement on Thursday, the military said that, overnight, the Air Force struck "an underground infrastructure site used by the Iranian regime to store ballistic missiles and storage sites for missiles intended for use against aircraft."
The military has not previously announced attacks on underground missile facilities, according to a review of its ‚Äãpublic statements since the start of the joint U.S.-Israeli attacks on Saturday.
Estimates of Iran's missile stockpile vary widely, from roughly 2,500 before the ‚Äãwar, according to Israel's military, to around 6,000 according to other analysts. The extent of what remains could prove critical to how the war develops. Tehran ‚Äåhas continued ‚ÅÝto carry out missile attacks on Israel and across the region.
Douglas Barrie of the UK-based International Institute for Strategic Studies said on Wednesday the think tank assesses Iran still possesses some land-attack cruise missiles, precision-guided weapons that fly low to evade radar detection.
Israel's Air Force fighter jets have carried out near-constant sorties since Saturday, accelerating further in pace after Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah militants fired rockets ‚Äãat Israel, drawing heavy Israeli ‚Äãairstrikes as far north as Beirut.
In ‚ÅÝsome cases, the same Israeli warplanes have struck both Iran and Lebanon in a single operation: bombing targets in Tehran or western Iran on the way out, and striking Hezbollah sites on the way ‚Äãback, one of the sources familiar with the plans and an Israeli security source said.
Israeli and U.S. ‚Äãofficials say ballistic ‚ÅÝmissile and drone launches from Iran have declined since Saturday, a decrease that they attribute in part to U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iranian launch sites and related military infrastructure.
The Israeli military has said that the decrease could also reflect an effort by Tehran to preserve its missile stocks as ‚ÅÝit prepares ‚Äãfor a drawn-out war of attrition.
Eran Lerman, a former Israeli deputy national security adviser, ‚Äãsaid the hope from the initial week of strikes was that Iran's ruling system would "begin to disintegrate earlier, more quickly".
"But this has yet to happen and as long ‚Äãas it doesn't, the system needs to be further and further degraded," Lerman said.

item.title: More tankers come under attack as US-Iran conflict spreads in the region
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - More tankers came under attack in Gulf waters on Thursday as ‚Äãthe U.S.‚ÄìIran war escalated , and Iranian drones entered Azerbaijan, threatening to spread the crisis to more oil producers in the region.
A ‚ÄåBahamas-flagged crude oil tanker was targeted by an Iranian remote-controlled boat laden with explosives while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, according to initial assessments. A second tanker at anchor off Kuwait was taking on water and spilling oil after a large explosion on its port side.
Nine vessels have come under attack since the conflict broke out between the ‚ÄãU.S., Israel and Iran on Saturday. Iran launched a wave of missiles at Israel early on Thursday and also sent drones into Azerbaijan , ‚Äãinjuring four people.
The escalation comes after a motion to halt the U.S. attacks was blocked in Washington and as ‚ÅÝthe son of Iran's slain supreme leader emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, suggesting Tehran was not about to buckle under the pressure.
Around ‚Äã200 ships, including oil and liquefied natural gas tankers as well as cargo ships, remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major ‚ÄãGulf producers, according to Reuters estimates based on ship-tracking data from the MarineTraffic platform.
Hundreds of other vessels remained outside the Strait of Hormuz unable to reach ports, shipping data showed. The waterway is a key artery for around a fifth of the world's oil and LNG supply.
U.S. President Donald Trump offered U.S. navy escorts and insurance in ‚Äãa bid to restart shipping flows and curtail energy prices. Insurance market Lloyd's of London said on Thursday it is engaging with the U.S. ‚Äãgovernment on a plan .
BP (BP.L) , opens new tab evacuated foreign staff from Iraq's Rumaila oil field after two unidentified drones landed inside the field, according to Iraqi oil sources. Baghdad has cut its oil ‚Äåproduction by ‚ÅÝnearly 1.5 million barrels a day as the country ran out of storage and was unable to load tankers, officials told Reuters.
A refinery in Kuwait shut down and another reduced its processing rate. A third refinery in Bahrain also cut its output.
Oil prices extended their rally on Thursday, climbing around 2%. They have risen 15% since the war started on Saturday, as U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran disrupted Middle East supplies.
A benchmark European gas price also gained 2% on ‚ÄãThursday. It has surged around 50% ‚Äãthis week so far.
Russia's President ‚ÅÝVladimir Putin said that Russia could halt gas supplies to Europe right now, amid the spike in energy prices from the Iran crisis.
Qatar, which provides 20% of the world's LNG supply, halted gas production earlier this week due to ‚Äãthe conflict. Other major producers such as the U.S. and Australia have little spare capacity to offset this lost ‚Äãsupply, according to ‚ÅÝReuters calculations and industry analysts.
The EU's task of refilling its gas storage in the coming months has become riskier and more expensive due to the Iran conflict and disrupted LNG supplies. It still imports some gas from Russia, planning to end pipeline supply by late 2027 and ban new short-term LNG contracts from late ‚ÅÝApril 2026.
Asian ‚Äãimporters came under further pressure from the disruptions in supply from the Middle East. China ‚Äãasked refiners not to sign new contracts to export fuel and to try to cancel shipments already committed, several people with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.

item.title: Highlights of NATO chief's interview with Reuters
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told Reuters on Thursday the alliance was vigilant and ready to defend every inch of its territory after Iran fired a ballistic missile towards NATO member Turkey.
Rutte also addressed France's shift in nuclear doctrine and responded to criticism from some over the way he lavishes praise on U.S. President Donald ‚ÄãTrump.
Here are highlights from the interview.
"NATO air missile defence was able to take it out. ‚ÄåSo that was very important. It also shows you, I think, clear evidence that we, with a 360-degree approach, will defend every inch of NATO territory. Our military are constantly on it. But of course, this was serious and absolutely condemnable."
"Article 5, I think, is not in order here. Nobody's talking about Article 5. ‚ÄãAnd the most important thing is that our adversaries have seen yesterday that NATO is so strong and so vigilant, and even more vigilant, if ‚Äãpossible, since Saturday because of the situation, of course, in the Middle East."
"We know that Iran ‚ÅÝwas close to getting its hands on a nuclear capability, a missile capability, which would be a threat, not only to the Middle East, and of course, ‚Äãto Israel, and potentially it would be life-threatening to Israel, but also a threat to Europe."
"Clearly, NATO is not itself involved here. NATO allies are providing key enabling support. ‚ÄãWhat we see here is that NATO, in that sense, is also this power protection projection platform for the United States. Because without European allies, the U.S. would have found it very difficult to launch this campaign against Iran."
"I think there is widespread acknowledgement and agreement that at least we have to be sure that going forward, Iran, this ‚Äãrepublic, is not able again, to pose (a) death threat to its neighbours, to Israel, the Middle East, to Europe."
"From my conversations with senior American military and political ‚Äãleaders, it's my absolute sense that they know where they are going."
"Iran was one of the key supporters of the Russian war effort against Ukraine ‚Äî totally unprovoked, this ‚Äåcrazy war ‚ÅÝwhich started in 2014 and then the full-scale invasion in February 2022."
"What we also see now is that Ukraine is already, for some weeks, being able to recapture terrain, territory which the Russians got their hands on before, so that is really good news. We know that the Ukrainians are posing extreme losses on the Russians, months in, months out, many times more than the Russians lost in Afghanistan in the 1980s."
"Obviously I do believe that the United States is the most ‚Äãpowerful and most quintessential ally within ‚Äãthe Alliance. If the president of that ‚ÅÝcountry is able to get the whole of NATO at 2% last year, and then in The Hague, collectively agreeing to the 5% ‚Äî I would doubt it, without President Trump, we would have really reached it. And then him now taking this ‚Äãdecisive action to take out this capability of Iran ... if a president of a country is providing that type of ‚Äãleadership, some praise is ‚ÅÝwarranted."
"Spain is deployed all over NATO territory. Their troops are part of many forward land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want to commend them for that ‚Äî there is a Spanish Patriot system in Turkey, defending key American interests. Yes, there are also discussions on spending. But this is an alliance of ‚ÅÝdemocracies. When ‚Äãthere are debates between allies, I always try to stay a bit muted."
"I ‚Äãreally welcome this. It is leveraging more of what France is doing. However, and we all agree on this, the ultimate, supreme guarantor of our way of life ... is, in the end, the United States nuclear ‚Äãumbrella, and that is key."
"I'm absolutely convinced the U.S. is completely committed to NATO."

item.title: Four medics killed in Iran, ambulances damaged, WHO says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 5 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization chief said on Thursday ‚Äãthat it has verified 13 attacks ‚Äåon health infrastructure in Iran amid a U.S.-Israeli campaign, killing four health care workers and injuring ‚Äã25 others.
"WHO has verified 13 attacks ‚Äãon health care in Iran and one ‚ÅÝin Lebanon," World Health Organization Director-General ‚ÄãTedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus told a press conference, ‚Äãwithout attributing blame.
Dr. Hanan Balkhy added at the same briefing that four ambulances in Iran were ‚Äãalso affected and that hospitals and ‚Äãother health sites suffered minor damage due to strikes ‚Äånearby. ‚ÅÝOne of these hospitals in the capital Tehran was evacuated as a result, the U.N. health agency previously said.
Iran's ambassador to the ‚ÄãU.N. in ‚ÄãGeneva has ‚ÅÝalleged that 10 facilities have been hit by military strikes ‚Äãin a letter to Tedros earlier ‚Äãthis ‚ÅÝweek.
Balkhy says that the WHO logistics hub in Dubai which provides health supplies ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãdozens of countries is temporarily ‚Äãout of service because of transport restrictions in the ‚Äãregion.

item.title: US Senator Warren targets US ammunition sales linked to Mexican cartels
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren will introduce legislation on Thursday to stop a U.S. Army-owned ammunition plant from selling military-grade bullets ‚Äãto civilians, asserting that some are being diverted to arm Mexican drug ‚Äåcartels and have been used in more than a dozen American mass shootings.
The Stop Militarizing Our Streets Act, co-sponsored by Senator Andy Kim and Representatives Robert Garcia and Jamie Raskin, ‚Äãwould prohibit Pentagon contractors from selling military-grade assault weapons and ammunition ‚Äãto civilians.
It would also require that military contractors only sell firearms ‚ÅÝand ammunition to commercial dealers that follow minimum safety practices, such as ‚Äãscreening customers and having a low history of gun sales that are later linked ‚Äãto a crime.
The bill takes aim at the Lake City Army Ammunition Plant in Independence, Missouri, a facility built during World War Two to supply the U.S. military and the ‚Äãlargest manufacturer of rifle ammunition for the U.S. armed forces.
The facility is ‚Äãrun by Olin Winchester, part of Olin Corporation (OLN.N) , opens new tab . The Army has a deal with Winchester that ‚Äåwhatever ‚ÅÝammunition is not purchased by the service can be sold commercially on the civilian market.
High-powered .50-caliber cartridges that Mexican authorities have seized from cartels were traced to the Lake City plant, Warren said.
Neither Olin nor its subsidiary immediately responded to ‚Äãa request for comment.
"Americans' ‚Äãtax dollars ‚ÅÝshould not be used to fuel gun violence," Warren said in a statement. "Congress must step in to keep Americans ‚Äãsafe, and that means stopping the U.S. military and giant ‚Äãdefense contractors ‚ÅÝfrom selling weapons of war to cartels, criminal groups, and mass shooters that terrorize our communities."
A New York Times investigation in 2023 found that AR-15 ammunition produced at ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãLake City plant had been used in at ‚Äãleast a dozen mass shootings since 2012, including the attacks in Aurora, San Bernardino, Las Vegas, Sutherland Springs, ‚ÄãParkland, Buffalo, and Uvalde.

item.title: Exclusive: Hezbollah elite fighters return to south Lebanon to fight Israeli troops
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Hezbollah has deployed elite fighters to confront Israeli forces in southern Lebanon, sending them back into the border ‚Äãregion from which they withdrew after a war in 2024, three Lebanese sources familiar with the deployments said, as the ‚ÄåIran-backed group wades deeper into the conflict in the Middle East .
Members of Hezbollah's Radwan force had received orders to join the battle and block advances by Israeli tanks, the sources said, citing the town of Khiyam - one of several areas where Israeli troops reportedly advanced on Wednesday - as an example.
Hezbollah, a Lebanese Shi'ite Muslim group established by Iran's ‚ÄãRevolutionary Guards in 1982, entered the war on Monday, opening fire to avenge the death of Iran's supreme leader, who was killed in ‚Äãa surprise U.S.-Israeli attack on Tehran.
Israel has carried out heavy strikes in southern Lebanon and more widely since then, sending ‚ÅÝmore troops over the border, and ordering Lebanese to leave a swathe of territory in the frontier region, where the Israeli military had kept soldiers ‚Äãat several positions since the 2024 war.
Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem said in a televised speech on Wednesday that the group would confront what he described ‚Äãas an Israeli plan of "occupation and expansion".
"For us, this is an existential defence," he said.
The Hezbollah elite fighters deployed to the south after the group opened fire with rockets and drones on Monday, according to the sources, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the topic.
They had left that part of Lebanon - specifically ‚Äãthe area between the Litani River and the border with Israel - after a U.S.-brokered ceasefire in the 2024 war, moving north of the river, ‚Äãthe sources said.
The sources did not say how many elite forces had been deployed.
Hezbollah's opening salvo on Monday was fired from north of the Litani River, Lebanese President ‚ÄåJoseph Aoun ‚ÅÝhas said.
An Israeli military spokesperson said they were checking but didn't immediately provide comment on the sources' accounts. The Lebanese army declined to comment. The office of Prime Minister Nawaf Salam didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Israeli military said on Wednesday two soldiers were wounded as a result of anti-tank fire against troops in southern Lebanon, the first reported injuries among Israeli forces since the start ‚Äãof the regional war.
Hezbollah has announced ‚Äãseveral attacks targeting Israeli tanks ‚ÅÝthis week, along with numerous rocket and drone launches targeting Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson said on Wednesday the military was "positioning troops a little farther" into Lebanon than before, "to prevent any attacks against the northern communities" in Israel.
In ‚ÄãMay, 2023, before the Hamas-led October 7 attack on Israel triggered a region-wide conflict, Hezbollah invited media to ‚Äãwatch Radwan fighters ‚ÅÝsimulate an invasion of Israel.
Some 5,000 Hezbollah fighters were killed in the 2024 war with Israel, many of them members of the Radwan force. The war left Hezbollah severely weakened.
Israel has carried out near-daily strikes targeting Hezbollah since that conflict, including an attack in December on what it described as a training compound used ‚ÅÝby Radwan ‚Äãforces in the south.
Following the 2024 truce, which demanded the Lebanese army confiscate all unauthorized ‚Äãarms , beginning in the area south of the Litani, the Lebanese army seized many Hezbollah weapons caches. Hezbollah said it respected the ceasefire south of the Litani, but that it didn't ‚Äãapply to the rest of Lebanon.

item.title: NATO's Rutte backs Macron's nuclear revamp but says US umbrella is ultimate¬Ýguarantee
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte on Thursday welcomed French President Emmanuel Macron's pledge to expand his country's nuclear deterrent but said the U.S. nuclear umbrella would ‚Äãremain the ultimate guarantor of European security.
Macron this week announced France would ‚Äåincrease its nuclear arsenal and potentially allow European partners to host French warplanes on deterrence missions, following concerns among allies about the reliability of the U.S..
Rutte said Macron's update to France's nuclear doctrine would make ‚Äãit harder for Russia to assess European deterrence and defense and that he supported discussions ‚Äãover nuclear cooperation between Paris and several European capitals.
But he said ‚ÅÝEurope could not do without U.S. protection and sought to dismiss concerns over Washington's commitment to ‚Äãdefending Europe.
"The ultimate, supreme guarantor of our way of life ... is, in the end, the United ‚ÄãStates' nuclear umbrella, and that is key," Rutte told Reuters in an interview.
In his speech at a submarine base, Macron said the rethink of French nuclear doctrine had been done in transparency with Washington, and was complementary ‚Äãto NATO's nuclear mission.
France and Germany have established a nuclear steering group to discuss ‚Äãdeterrence issues and will start concrete cooperation this year. Greece, Poland, the Netherlands, Belgium, Denmark and Sweden could also ‚Äåbe ‚ÅÝexpected to take part in French nuclear wargames, Macron said.
The Trump administration has at times over the past 14 months been scathing in its view of Europe's willingness and ability to defend itself.
In mistakenly shared comments by top Trump administration officials ahead of strikes on Yemen's Iran-aligned ‚ÄãHouthis last year, a ‚Äãperson identified as Vice ‚ÅÝPresident JD Vance wrote "I just hate bailing out Europe", prompting a second official identified as U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to lambast European "free-loading" in ‚Äãresponse.
Under pressure from Washington, NATO's European members have raised their military spending ‚Äãcommitments .
A ‚ÅÝstandoff over Greenland earlier this year added to European doubts about U.S. commitments to defending its allies. But Rutte told Reuters he did not doubt the United States' commitment to NATO and the ‚ÅÝsafeguarding of ‚ÄãEuropean security.
"I'm absolutely convinced the U.S. is completely committed ‚Äãto NATO," he said.
"The United States knows that to stay safe itself (on) the U.S. mainland, you need a secure Atlantic, a ‚Äãsecure Europe, a secure Arctic."

item.title: UK starts visa requirements for St. Lucians
item.description:
CASTRIES, March 5 (Reuters) - Britain has introduced a visa requirement for St. Lucians effective from ‚ÄãThursday citing a "notable increase" in nationals from the ‚Äåsmall Caribbean nation claiming asylum, St. Lucia's government said in a statement.
Immigration is one of Britain's most politically sensitive ‚Äãissues, and Prime Minister Keir Starmer's government has ‚Äãsought to show it is tightening the system ‚ÅÝas the populist Reform UK party gains ground ‚Äãin opinion polls.

item.title: UK PM Starmer to address press conference on Middle East conflict at 1400 GMT
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer will address a ‚Äãpress conference on Thursday afternoon ‚Äåto give an update on the escalating conflict in the Middle East , his ‚Äãspokesman said.
Starmer will speak to ‚Äãjournalists at 1400 GMT in ‚ÅÝLondon, his spokesman said.
Britain, which ‚Äãwas not part of the assault ‚Äãon Iran that began at the weekend, has announced it will start moving ‚Äãmilitary resources to the region ‚Äãafter an attack on one of its ‚Äåbases ‚ÅÝin Cyprus.
Starmer's government said this week it would deploy a warship, as well as helicopters with ‚Äãcounter-drone capabilities ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝhelp blunt Iran's retaliatory strikes.
As the U.S.‚ÄìIran war ‚Äãentered its sixth day, the ‚Äãconflict ‚ÅÝhas widened beyond Gulf states and into Asia, prompting thousands of ‚ÅÝstranded ‚Äãtourists and residents to ‚Äãtry to flee the Middle East.

item.title: US jobs data forces gaze back from Mideast mayhem to AI 'doom'
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - With war still raging in the Middle East , investors on Friday will, at least temporarily, turn their attention back to more familiar economic ground: U.S. jobs data .
Events since the joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran last Saturday have dominated market thinking so much that fears of artificial intelligence soon throwing millions of ‚Äãwhite-collar workers onto the scrapheap have been pushed to the back burner.
Friday's U.S. non-farm payrolls and unemployment figures for February will bring those concerns to the forefront ‚Äåof investors' minds once again, and depending on the details, perhaps to the top of policymakers' agendas as well.
The median consensus in a Reuters poll of economists is for a net rise in non-farm payrolls last month of 59,000, less than half of January's rise. The unemployment rate is expected to remain stable at 4.3%.
While it may be too early to see concrete evidence of AI-related labor market disruption, the jobs ‚Äãreport will still be closely scrutinized for warning signs, including weak job growth, or even net job losses, and an unwelcome rise in the unemployment rate.
Indeed, from this ‚Äãpoint on, the monthly payrolls reports and other labor market indicators such as the "JOLTS" job openings, layoff figures and weekly jobless claims are ‚ÅÝlikely to be lightning rods for the "AI doom" debate over whether the technology will end up destroying jobs, demand and economic growth.
Markets last week were awash with talk of ‚Äãthe coming AI "apocalypse." Stocks wobbled, as investors sought to identify AI winners and losers, and bets on multiple Federal Reserve rate cuts this year rose.
Helping to stoke fear was Jack Dorsey, CEO of ‚ÄãBlock Inc (XYZ.N) , opens new tab , who openly cited AI in his announcement on February 26 that he was firing nearly half of his workforce, even though the fintech company is "strong ... and profitability is improving."
There's a school of thought that Dorsey and other CEOs and chief financial officers may blame the expected disruptive power of AI for what are really just cost-cutting efforts ‚Äì especially given the labor hoarding that occurred after the pandemic.
But regardless, Dorsey's ‚Äãstatement spooked investors because it came after a series of long-form research notes and blogs outlining the doomsday AI scenario had gone viral.
However, when assessing the impact of AI on the ‚Äãlabor market, investors and policymakers must separate facts from noise. That means analyzing hard data, which are obviously often backward-looking. The challenge is using that to predict which way the wind is blowing.
So far, ‚Äåthe picture ‚ÅÝseems more balanced than the AI doomers would have you believe.
A recent study co-led by Harvard Business School Professor Suraj Srinivasan analyzed nearly all U.S. job postings from 2019 through March last year. It found that following ChatGPT's launch in November 2022, openings for routine jobs most likely to be replaced by AI fell 13%, but demand for more analytical, technical, and creative roles rose 20%.
Economists at Goldman Sachs estimate that AI currently presents a headwind to job growth of 5,000-10,000 per month. In an economy that creates more than 30 million gross new ‚Äãjobs per year, that's negligible.
Although only 2.5% of ‚Äãworkers are at risk of being replaced ‚ÅÝby current AI use cases, Goldman's economists estimate that 11 million jobs - 6-7% of the workforce - will be displaced by AI going forward. But the technology will create new jobs.
"We therefore do not anticipate a job apocalypse," they wrote last week.
Other research points in the same ‚Äãdirection. A Morgan Stanley survey of U.S. companies in January indicated that firms in industries most prone to AI adoption are more ‚Äãlikely to hire or retrain ‚ÅÝworkers than eliminate or not fill positions.
And a Dallas Fed paper last week found that, so far at least, AI is both aiding and replacing workers.
The monthly U.S. employment reports are usually all about the headline figures. But with AI doomsday fears rife, the details beneath the headlines may now begin to take on much more significance and cut through the fog of war.
(The opinions ‚ÅÝexpressed here are ‚Äãthose of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for ‚Äãglobal financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Morning Bid: Global markets take a breath
item.description:
March 5 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Asian markets snapped back on Thursday, with South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI rising by almost 10%, clawing back a chunk of its drubbing in ‚Äãrecent days, and Japan‚Äôs Nikkei adding nearly 2%.
The apparent respite amid the ongoing Middle East conflict wasn‚Äôt confined to Asia, with U.S. ‚Äåand European stocks also pushing higher on Wednesday as investors took comfort from even the faintest hints that geopolitical tensions might ease.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why Europe may be able to absorb the Middle East energy shock - for now.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe ‚Äãto hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Global markets appear ‚Äãto be catching their breath. The Nasdaq jumped 1.29% on Wednesday in a tech-led rally, while the S&P 500 ‚ÅÝrose 0.78%, helped along by some upbeat macro prints.
A mix of factors seem to be fuelling this market breather. Traders seemed to take heart ‚Äãin the New York Times‚Äô report yesterday that Iranian intelligence had indirectly reached out to the CIA after Saturday‚Äôs attacks to try to reach a resolution. Washington‚Äôs plan ‚Äãto help reopen the embattled Strait of Hormuz via navy escorts and insurance coverage may also have raised some optimism.
There was also some positive news on the labour market front ahead of Friday‚Äôs payrolls release. U.S. private payrolls printed a higher-than-expected rise for February. ISM's non-manufacturing PMI, meantime, hit a more than three-year high.
But uncertainty still reigns as ‚Äãthe Middle East conflagration enters its sixth day and a swift resolution to a clogged-up Strait of Hormuz looks, for now, like a distant prospect.
Indeed, ‚Äãanalysts have warned that Iran can keep up drone harassment in the narrow waterway for months, and it's far from clear that tankers would brave the strait even with ‚ÄåU.S. naval ‚ÅÝand insurance support in place.
And other signs point towards this being more than a days-long conflict, as the U.S. Senate failed to block the administration‚Äôs campaign against Tehran, giving President Trump largely unbounded power to direct the war.
Oil marched higher on Thursday on the prospect of continued disruption. Both U.S. and Brent crude jumped over 2%, with the latter topping $83 per barrel.
Gold also edged up on safe-haven demand, and the dollar strengthened again after retreating from three-month highs.
Meantime, U.S. ‚Äãstock index futures edged lower ahead of ‚Äãthe bell and U.S. Treasuries eased. ‚ÅÝInvestors will have even more to chew on later today with the release of weekly jobless claims, ahead of tomorrow's non-farm payrolls.
Elsewhere, chip designer Broadcom on Wednesday reported a first-quarter revenue rise of 29% to $19.31 billion and projected its ‚ÄãAI chip revenue would top $100 billion next year. It‚Äôs emerging as a strong competitor in the space as ‚Äãit signs deals ‚ÅÝto rival those of its dominant competitor Nvidia, including with Meta.
Chart of the day
Global stocks have rebounded in recent days, recovering some of their 2026 gains lost in the wake of the U.S.-Israel attack on Iran on 28 February.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EST), January import prices (8:30 AM EST), ‚ÅÝQ4 productivity (8:30 ‚ÄãAM EST)
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every ‚Äãweekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect ‚Äãthe views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Iran reveals stretched Trump tariff trades
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Financial markets have swung from fear to hope, pricing in the inflationary impact of the war in the Middle East but also seeming oddly blas√© about how long ‚Äãthe disruption will last. Tellingly, in both bouts of optimism and pessimism this week, the winning trades of the past ‚Äåyear appear to have run their course.
At first glance, financial markets seem to be painting a coherent picture since Donald Trump began his Iran war. Brent hovered around $83 a barrel on Thursday ‚Äî up 21% on the month but far from the $100 plus levels expected under a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The implication ‚Äãis that blocking this vital trade artery for more than a few weeks would be so devastating that the countries involved ‚Äãwon‚Äôt allow it.
Other markets reflect a similar view that the war will push up inflation a bit but ‚ÅÝnot be catastrophic. Equity indexes are slightly lower, energy stocks are higher, and the U.S. dollar has risen 1.3% since the attack on ‚ÄãIran thanks to America‚Äôs status as a net oil exporter. The S&P 500 has held up better than European and emerging market indexes, which ‚Äãrely more on imported energy. Government bonds have edged down on the assumption that central banks will turn a little more hawkish .
But many other current trends make little sense. Gold, a traditional inflation hedge, is down about 3% since the attacks. Consumer staples shares, think food and cleaning products, have been beaten by consumer discretionary ‚Äãones ‚Äî apparel, travel and cars ‚Äî despite being less sensitive to household spending. ‚ÄúGrowth‚Äù stocks, normally hurt most by rising rates, have beaten cheaper ‚Äúvalue‚Äù names, like ‚ÄãUniversal Health Services. This includes software firms such as Adobe (ADBE.O) , opens new tab , which had been battered by AI fears and had no obvious reason to do well. These movements ‚Äåare mostly ‚ÅÝthe opposite of what happened in 2022 after a similar energy shock: Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine.
What ties this together is simple: assets that had been outperforming are now lagging, and vice versa. The year‚Äôs winning trades before the war were extensions of strategies that surged after Donald Trump‚Äôs tariff package last year. Many had become overstretched. So-called dedollarisation wagers were so popular that net short bets on the greenback using derivatives hit ‚Äãa five-year high of $22.8 billion in ‚ÄãFebruary, according to the Commodity ‚ÅÝFutures Trading Commission. Gold seemed to have become an unbeatable all weather asset , which is unsustainable. Fund flows into U.S. staples were at record highs.
Some of those underlying trends could theoretically continue: central banks may keep buying ‚Äãgold, and global investors look for alternatives to the dollar. But it is telling that, even as ‚Äãmarkets rebounded Wednesday, ‚ÅÝthe old winning wagers kept underperforming ‚Äì staples, for instance, fell another 0.5%. Every investment playbook eventually tires, and this crisis has revealed how worn out some pages already were.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia charges ex-deputy defence minister with corruption
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 5 (Reuters) - Russia has charged a former deputy defence minister ‚Äãwith corruption, investigators said on ‚ÄåThursday, in the latest of a series of prosecutions against top ‚Äãfigures in the military ‚Äãestablishment.
The state Investigative Committee said ‚ÅÝRuslan Tsalikov was accused of ‚Äãembezzlement, money laundering and bribe-taking.
Tsalikov ‚Äãserved as first deputy defence minister from 2015 to 2024, when a ‚Äãseries of corruption cases led ‚Äãto the arrest of a string of ‚Äågenerals, ‚ÅÝdeputy ministers and civilian defence civilians.
Another former deputy minister, Timur Ivanov, was jailed for 13 ‚Äãyears in ‚ÄãJuly ‚ÅÝ2025.
Ivanov and Tsalikov served under Sergei Shoigu, a ‚Äãlongtime ally of Russian ‚ÄãPresident ‚ÅÝVladimir Putin. Shoigu was removed as defence minister in 2024, ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãappointed secretary of ‚ÄãRussia's Security Council.

item.title: UK sending four extra Typhoon jets to Qatar, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on Thursday that four additional ‚ÄãTyphoon fighter jets would be sent to ‚ÄåQatar as the conflict in the Middle East escalates, insisting that the UK has the right plan for defence.
Britain's ‚Äãcautious response to the Iran crisis and ‚Äãa drone attack on its key military base ‚ÅÝin Cyprus have led to doubts among ‚Äãpartners about its military effectiveness.
U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãhas also criticised Starmer for failing to provide sufficient support for his strikes on Iran.
Starmer told a press conference Britain ‚Äãhad the right plan and had already ‚Äãbeen pre-deploying military equipment across the region before the start ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe war.
"My focus is providing calm, level headed leadership in the national interest," Starmer said.
"That means deploying our military and diplomatic strength to protect our ‚Äãpeople, and ‚Äãit means ‚ÅÝhaving the strength to stand firm by our values and our principles, ‚Äãno matter the pressure to do otherwise."
He ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝBritain was sending four additional typhoon jets to join the squadron in Qatar to strengthen our defensive operations there and ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãthe region.
"We have the right ‚Äãplan for defence," he said.

item.title: Israel orders residents to leave southern Beirut
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel warned residents to leave Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs on Thursday, sparking panic as people fled a swathe of the Lebanese capital on the fourth day of full-scale ‚Äãhostilities between the Iran-backed group and Israel.
An Israeli military spokesperson, in ‚Äãa post on X, ordered residents of the southern suburbs ‚ÅÝto move east and north, posting a map showing four sprawling districts of ‚Äãthe capital he said they must leave, part of the area is adjacent ‚Äãto Beirut airport.
"Save your lives, evacuate your homes immediately," Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee posted, saying that any movement southwards may endanger their lives.
Roads out of the suburbs were clogged ‚Äãas people fled by car and on foot, television footage showed. Gunfire ‚Äãwas heard in the southern suburbs, warning residents to leave.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in ‚Äåthe Middle ‚ÅÝEast on Monday, when Hezbollah opened fire, sparking intensified Israeli airstrikes largely focused on the southern suburbs, southern Lebanon and eastern Lebanon.
On Wednesday, the Israeli military ordered residents to leave an area of southern Lebanon amounting to about 8% of its ‚Äãterritory.
Israeli bombardment and warnings ‚Äãhave already forced ‚ÅÝtens of thousands of Lebanese to flee homes in the southern suburbs and the south this week. The Lebanese ‚Äãhealth ministry has said 77 people have been killed.
There ‚Äãhave been ‚ÅÝno reports of fatalities in Israel as a result of Hezbollah attacks.
Beirut's predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern suburbs are among the capital's most densely populated areas. The ‚ÅÝarea ‚Äãwas pounded by Israeli airstrikes during a war ‚Äãwith Hezbollah in 2024, and during a previous war with Israel in 2006.

item.title: Algerian military aircraft crashes after takeoff from Boufarik base, two crew killed
item.description: March 5 (Reuters) - A small ‚ÄãAlgerian military ‚Äåtransport aircraft ‚Äãcrashed ‚Äãshortly after taking ‚ÅÝoff ‚Äãfrom Boufarik ‚Äãair base on ‚ÄãThursday, ‚Äãkilling two crew ‚Äåmembers ‚ÅÝand injuring four others, ‚ÄãEnnahar ‚ÄãTV ‚ÅÝreported ‚Äãciting the ‚Äãdefence ‚ÅÝministry.

item.title: US opposes IAEA board resolution condemning attacks on Ukraine's power grid
item.description:
VIENNA, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States joined Russia, China, and ‚ÄåNiger on Thursday in opposing a resolution adopted by the U.N. nuclear watchdog's board denouncing attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure as a threat to nuclear safety, diplomats said.
The resolution, passed by the ‚ÄãInternational Atomic Energy Agency's 35-nation Board of Governors, is the seventh on Ukraine ‚Äãsince Russia invaded its neighbour four years ago.
This is the first time ‚ÅÝthe United States has opposed one.
"While we continue supporting the IAEA's work in-country, ‚Äãwe do not support the Board's current consideration of an unnecessary resolution that does not ‚Äãhelp achieve peace between Ukraine and Russia," the United States said in its statement to the board before the vote.
U.S. President Donald Trump has in the past year pressured Ukraine for a quick peace ‚Äãdeal that could involve ceding land to Russia. Ukraine has ruled out giving up ‚Äãterritory.
The IAEA board passed the resolution with 20 votes in favour, including France, Britain, Australia, Canada, South ‚ÄåAfrica ‚ÅÝand Argentina, 10 abstentions and the four votes against, diplomats at the closed-door meeting said. Brazil, Egypt, Morocco and Saudi Arabia were among the abstentions.
The resolution's wording was not as strong as previous ones.
The text seen by Reuters said the board "reemphasizes that attacks ‚Äãtargeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure ‚Äãfor the off-site power ‚ÅÝsupply of nuclear power plants, including at the ZNPP (Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant), represent a direct threat to nuclear safety and security".
The ‚Äãmove follows the United States' abstention at the U.N. General Assembly on ‚Äãthe anniversary ‚ÅÝof the invasion in February when it passed a resolution supporting Ukraine, backing its international borders and voicing concern over intensifying Russian attacks on civilians and critical energy infrastructure.
That resolution ‚ÅÝpassed ‚Äãwith 107 countries voting in favour, 12 against, and ‚Äã51 abstentions.
The United States said at the time that the General Assembly resolution included language likely to distract ‚Äãfrom ongoing negotiations aimed at ending the conflict.

item.title: Cartels can only be defeated with military power, White House's Miller says
item.description:
MIAMI, March 5 (Reuters) - Cartels operating in the ‚ÄãWestern Hemisphere can ‚Äåonly be defeated with military power, ‚ÄãWhite House ‚ÄãDeputy Chief of Staff ‚ÅÝStephen Miller ‚Äãsaid on Thursday.
