"We ‚Äãhave learned after decades of effort is that ‚Äãthere is ‚Äãnot a criminal justice ‚Äåsolution ‚ÅÝto the cartel problem," Miller told Latin American ‚Äãdefense leaders ‚Äãgathered ‚ÅÝat the U.S. Southern ‚ÄãCommand headquarters ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝa conference.

item.title: Greek seafarers strike over crews stranded in the Gulf by Iran war
item.description:
PIRAEUS, Greece, March 5 (Reuters) - Greek seafarers began a 24-hour strike on Thursday, halting local ferry services, as they protested over vessel crews stranded in the Gulf amid the escalating ‚ÄãMiddle East war, and demanded the area is declared a war risk ‚Äåzone to enable their repatriation.
The Iran conflict threatens Gulf ports and has already disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery accounting for about 20% of global oil and gas supply. ‚ÄãAlthough the Strait is not shut, Iran has warned that it will fire ‚Äãon any ship trying to pass through.
Greece is a dominant force in ‚ÅÝglobal shipping, controlling one of the world‚Äôs largest merchant fleets. More than 325 ships of ‚ÄãGreek interests, their crews including dozens of Greek seafarers, are in the wider Gulf ‚Äãarea.
"We demand that all of our colleagues, currently in the dangerous Gulf area, the Gulf of Oman and the Red Sea, are evacuated and safely repatriated," said Angelos Galanopoulos, head of Greece's lower engine ‚Äãcrews' union Stephenson.
Dozens of protesters rallied outside the shipowners union near the port of ‚ÄãPiraeus and spray-painted: "No sacrifice for profits and wars" on the ground. A motorcycle convoy drove to ‚Äåthe shipping ‚ÅÝministry.
The International Maritime Organization says it is concerned for about roughly 20,000 seafarers in the region. At least nine ships have been damaged in strikes since the conflict began on Saturday.
Seafarers usually have the contractual right to refuse to sail in designated war zones ‚Äãand demand repatriation at ‚Äãthe shipowner's expense.
Dozens ‚ÅÝof ships remained at anchor in open waters off the coast of major Gulf producers and dozens of oil tankers were inside the ‚ÄãStrait, ship tracking data showed.
Risks include not only attacks but also ‚Äãpotential food ‚ÅÝand supply shortages said Apostolis Kypraios, head of the marine engineers' union PEMEN.
"The government and shipownwers are responsible for the people trapped in war areas," Kypraios said.
"We demand that they ‚ÅÝfind ‚Äãa solution for our colleagues to return home. Their ‚Äãfamilies are worried and seafarers don't know if they will return alive, if they will be injured."

item.title: As Iran conflict rages, Pope Leo asks God to help leaders renounce war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 5 (Reuters) - Pope Leo released a video on Thursday praying that God would help world leaders renounce war ‚Äãas a means of resolving conflicts in an unusual ‚Äåappeal as the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran pressed on for the sixth day.
"Lord, enlighten the leaders of the nations, so they may have the courage ‚Äãto abandon projects of death," the pontiff said in the ‚Äãvideo message.
"Today we lift up our prayer for peace ‚ÅÝin the world, asking that nations renounce weapons and choose the ‚Äãpath of dialogue and diplomacy," he said.
Leo releases a video message ‚Äãeach month to announce his prayer intentions for that month. The pope's intention for March is "for disarmament and peace".
It was unclear if Thursday's video was ‚Äãcreated specifically to respond to the U.S.-Israeli military campaign, which has ‚Äãset off a regional war with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and ‚ÄåIraq, ‚ÅÝand Israeli attacks in Lebanon.
The Vatican did not immediately respond to a question about when the video was recorded.
The Vatican's top diplomat warned on Wednesday that the U.S.-Israeli strikes undermined international law and said ‚Äãnations did not ‚Äãhave a ‚ÅÝright to launch "preventive wars", an unusually direct criticism of the military campaign.
"If states were to be recognised ‚Äãas having a right to 'preventive war' ... the entire ‚Äãworld could ‚ÅÝrisk going up in flames," Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's secretary of state, said in an interview with Vatican News.
In his video, the ‚ÅÝpope ‚Äãasked God to help the world ‚Äãunderstand "that true security does not come from control fuelled by fear but from trust, justice ‚Äãand solidarity among peoples."

item.title: Diplomats in Riyadh ordered to shelter in place due to potential threat, sources say
item.description:
RIYADH, March 5 (Reuters) - Diplomats and staff at ‚Äåembassies in Riyadh's Diplomatic Quarter were told to shelter ‚Äãin place on ‚ÄãThursday due to a potential ‚ÅÝbut unspecified threat, ‚Äãfour people with direct ‚Äãknowledge of the matter said.
They did not have further details, ‚Äãand the Saudi government ‚Äãmedia office did not immediately ‚Äårespond ‚ÅÝto a request for comment.
The gates to the Diplomatic Quarter, a neighborhood on ‚Äãthe western ‚Äãedge ‚ÅÝof the Saudi capital that houses ‚Äãmost foreign missions ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe country, were closed as of Thursday afternoon, ‚ÅÝtwo ‚Äãof the people ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Exclusive: NATO's Rutte 'not deaf' to criticism of his Trump praise, offers more
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte said on Thursday he was "not ‚Äãdeaf" to criticism that he goes overboard in praising Donald Trump but argued the U.S. president ‚Äådeserved it.
However, in an interview with Reuters , Rutte also offered praise for Spain - a target of Trump's ire for refusing to allow U.S. military bases on its soil to be used in the American-Israeli assault on Iran .
Rutte, a Dutch former prime minister, was selected to lead NATO partly ‚Äãdue to his strong bond with Trump , which diplomats say has helped keep the military alliance together despite the ‚ÄãU.S. president's criticism of the organisation and its members.
But some officials, analysts and commentators have ‚ÅÝaccused Rutte of going over the top in lavishing praise on Trump, arguing it is demeaning to Europe and ‚Äãdownplays the continent's ability to defend itself.
"I hear the criticism, obviously. I'm not deaf," Rutte said in the interview at ‚ÄãNATO headquarters in Brussels.
Famously, at a NATO summit, Rutte likened Trump to a "daddy" intervening in last year's conflict between Israel and Iran.
Most recently, he has offered strong backing for the new U.S.-Israeli military action against Iran, even though leaders of ‚Äãsome NATO nations such as Spain and Turkey have been critical of the intervention and some others have not endorsed ‚Äãit.
In the interview, Rutte said Trump had played a crucial role in getting NATO allies to agree a new spending target of ‚Äå5% ‚ÅÝof GDP last year, composed of 3.5% on core defence and 1.5% on broader security-related spending.
The previous NATO target was to spend 2% of GDP on core defence.
Rutte also called the U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran "decisive action to take out this capability of Iran to pose a threat as an exporter of terrorism and chaos to the Middle East, to Europe, ‚Äãto the world".
"I ‚ÅÝthink if a president of a country is ... providing that type of leadership, some praise is warranted," he said.
On Tuesday, Trump threatened to impose a full U.S. trade embargo on ‚ÄãSpain due to the row over the military bases and also renewed criticism of ‚ÄãMadrid for not ‚ÅÝsigning up to the 5% defence spending target.
Asked about the spat between Trump and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez , Rutte offered praise for Spain's contribution to NATO.
"Their troops are part of many foreign land forces, many initiatives, many NATO missions. I really want ‚ÅÝto commend ‚Äãthem for that," he said.
In response to a question about whether ‚Äãhe had spoken to Trump and Sanchez to lower the tension between them, Rutte said: "When there are debates between allies, I always try to stay ‚Äãa bit muted, and therefore being able, if necessary, to help a bit."

item.title: Zelenskiy taunts Hungary's Orban for blocking aid to Ukraine
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy taunted Hungarian leader Viktor Orban ‚Äåon Thursday for blocking a 90 billion euro ($104 billion) aid package from the European Union critical to Kyiv's survival as it fends off Russia.
Ukraine relies on financial aid from partners to cover its budgetary needs while it funnels most state funds ‚Äãtoward defence. EU member Hungary, which has maintained cordial ties with Moscow, vetoed the package this ‚Äãmonth amid a dispute over oil supplies.
"We hope a certain person in the EU will ‚ÅÝnot keep blocking the 90 billion... and Ukrainian soldiers will have weapons," Zelenskiy told reporters in Kyiv.
"Otherwise, we ‚Äãwill give the address of this person to our armed forces, our guys. Let them call him, speak ‚Äãwith him in their own language."
The remark will likely heighten the tension between the two leaders during Russia's four-year war on Ukraine, which Orban - who faces elections on April 12 - has been careful not to condemn.
Hungary's veto of the aid package, ‚Äãas well as new EU sanctions on Russia, came in response to what it says was Ukraine deliberately ‚Äãcutting supplies from the Druzhba pipeline carrying Russian crude to Europe.
Kyiv says the oil flows stopped after a Russian attack ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝpipeline infrastructure in January and it is fixing the damage as fast as it can. Zelenskiy said on Thursday that the Soviet-era pipeline could be operational within a month and a half.
"They (the Russians) are killing us, and we're supposed to give poor little Orban oil, because without it he won't win elections?" he said.
Orban has ‚Äãmade the Ukraine war ‚Äãa focal point in his ‚ÅÝcampaign for re-election.
Ukraine's troops are fending off grinding Russian assaults along several parts of the 1,200-kilometer front line, as Kyiv faces U.S. pressure to secure peace while ‚Äãfending off Russian demands to cede land.
Despite being outgunned and outmanned, Kyiv's army ‚Äãhas retaken territory ‚ÅÝin recent weeks, gaining more in February than it lost for the first time since 2023, according to the Finland-based Black Bird Group analysis team.
European foreign ministers had tried unsuccessfully this month to persuade Budapest not to punish ‚ÅÝUkraine ‚Äãfor delays restarting the pipeline, which also supplies Slovakia, another EU ‚Äãneighbour of Ukraine.
Slovakia, whose Prime Minister Robert Fico is also sympathetic to the Kremlin, said it would refuse requests from Kyiv for emergency ‚Äãelectricity supplies until oil flows resume via the Druzhba pipeline.
($1 = 0.8625 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump tells Reuters US should have a role in choosing Iran's next leader, encourages Kurdish opposition
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he wants the United States to play a role in ‚Äãchoosing Iran's next leader and encouraged Kurdish opposition groups to go on the ‚Äåoffensive.
Trump said in a telephone interview it was very early in the process of picking a new leader but that the late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba was an unlikely choice, without elaborating.
"We want ‚Äãto be involved in the process of choosing the person who is going ‚Äãto lead Iran into the future," Trump said. "We don't have to go ‚ÅÝback every five years and do this again and again."
Trump also encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces to go ‚Äãon the offensive.
"I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all ‚Äãfor it," the president said.
When asked if the U.S. would provide or had offered air cover, he responded, "I can't tell you that," but added that the objective for the Kurds would be "to win."
"If they're ‚Äãgoing to do that, that's good," Trump added.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United ‚ÄãStates in recent days about whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based on the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, as the ‚ÄãUnited States and Israel ‚Äãpound Iranian targets ‚ÅÝwith bombs and missiles.
Trump also signaled confidence that the major shipping route near Iran, the Strait of Hormuz, will remain open.
Closing the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz , a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which ‚Äãa fifth of ‚ÅÝthe world‚Äôs crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes, has been one of Iran's main objectives, and shipping through the crucial energy artery has ground to a near halt after Iranian ‚ÅÝhits ‚Äãon six vessels.
"They have no navy, you know the ‚Äãnavy is now at the bottom of the sea," Trump said. "I'm watching Hormuz very closely."

item.title: Isolated and under fire: Iran strikes out as Russia and China stand aside
item.description:
DUBAI, March 5 (Reuters) - With its supreme leader killed and its war machine under relentless U.S. pressure , Iran now stands largely alone - its longtime partners Russia and China offering nothing more than diplomatic condemnations and expressions of concern.
Tehran has responded to the U.S. ‚Äãand Israeli attacks by widening the conflict beyond the Middle East, firing missiles and drones with an impact that is reverberating through global energy markets , rattling capitals from Washington to Beijing, and ‚Äåparalyzing the shipping that carries 20% of the world's oil supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iranian missiles reached as far as Cyprus, Azerbaijan , Turkey and Gulf states, taking the war to their doorstep by targeting critical businesses, energy infrastructure and U.S. bases. Oil facilities, refineries and key supply routes were hit, causing severe disruption to crude and natural gas supplies.
With the Strait of Hormuz already shut, the attacks have sent energy prices soaring, destabilising global markets and forcing major economies to scramble , underscoring the world‚Äôs exposure to the ‚Äãfallout from Tehran‚Äôs response to the war.
Russia and China‚Äôs restraint reflects a cold calculation, analysts say: intervening as Iran faces Israel and the United States would bring high costs, limited gains and unpredictable ‚Äãrisks - burdens neither power appears willing to shoulder.
‚ÄúPutin has other priorities, and chief among them is Ukraine,‚Äù said Anna Borshchevskaya, a Russia expert at the Washington Institute. ‚ÄúIt ‚ÅÝwould be foolish for Russia to go into a direct military confrontation with the United States.‚Äù
A senior Russian source said ‚Äúthe escalation in and around Iran and the Gulf is already diverting attention from the war in Ukraine. That‚Äôs ‚Äãjust a fact. Everything else is just emotion about a ‚Äòfallen ally‚Äô,‚Äù the source said.
Beijing and Moscow have both helped Iran build military capacity to counter U.S. and Israeli pressure, supplying missiles, air‚Äëdefence systems and technology intended to ‚Äãbolster deterrence, complicate U.S. operations and raise the costs of attack. That support, however, now appears capped.
China has spent years embedding itself in Middle Eastern diplomacy, while Russia has cast Iran as a pillar of its anti-Western alignment.
Yet as the conflict flared, both powers were constrained -China by its dependence on Gulf energy and trade and by security priorities in Asia, and Russia by a grinding war in Ukraine that has sapped its capacity to shield partners and sharpened its need to preserve ties with ‚Äãoil-rich Gulf states.
The result is a stark paradox: Iran remains strategically useful to both, but not useful enough to fight for.
With Russia‚Äôs military, diplomatic bandwidth and economic resources still absorbed by the war in Ukraine, President Vladimir ‚ÄãPutin‚Äôs priority is to avoid escalation with Washington and safeguard Russia‚Äôs interests in the Middle East, rather than gamble on Iran‚Äôs battlefield fortunes.
‚ÄúIf Russia had supported Iran directly, it would have alienated the Gulf states and Israel,‚Äù Borshchevskaya said. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs not ‚Äåwhat Putin wants."
Beijing‚Äôs ‚ÅÝrestrained response reflects a longstanding strategy: avoiding binding security commitments far from its core interests.
Unlike the United States, whose alliances rest on mutual defence obligations, China prefers partnerships built on trade, investment and arms sales, ties that stop short of dragging it into costly conflicts beyond East Asia, said Evan A. Feigenbaum from the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
Beijing, one of the world‚Äôs largest trading powers and energy buyers, maintains ties with Iran and Gulf Sunni rivals, and in Latin America, never placed all its bets on Venezuela alone.
"If Beijing wanted to do more, it won‚Äôt redirect strategic attention or military assets from core security," argues Henry Tugendhat ‚Äãat the Washington Institute. "It only cares about its name ‚Äãabroad. It cares about Taiwan, the South China ‚ÅÝSea, and perceived threats from the U.S. and Japan.‚Äù
The conflict may even carry advantages for Beijing. From the sidelines, China can watch as U.S. forces are tied down far from East Asia and military stockpiles are depleted, while gaining a real-time view of American capabilities and operations, insights that could inform its thinking on a future ‚ÄãTaiwan scenario.
China‚Äôs key vulnerability remains energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which carries some 45% of its oil imports. But Beijing has built strategic reserves ‚Äãand substantial volumes of Iranian oil ‚ÅÝalready sitting in tankers or storage, experts say.
They say the crisis has allowed Moscow and Beijing to recast themselves as mediators. China said Foreign Minister Wang Yi has spoken with European and Arab ministers to press for dialogue, while Putin has held similar calls with Gulf leaders and Iranian officials.
Russia also sees concrete benefits: rising oil prices strengthen its war economy, and a U.S. administration tied up in the Middle East has less bandwidth for ‚ÅÝUkraine.
Russia does not ‚Äãbenefit from the collapse of the Iranian regime, but it is also not tying its fate to Tehran‚Äôs survival, said Borshchevskaya. Moscow ‚Äãis hedging, preserving flexibility, regardless of the conflict‚Äôs outcome, and would build ties with any new government, even one aligned with Washington.
The Russian source pointed to Syria as a precedent. Despite backing ousted president Bashar al‚ÄëAssad for years, Moscow retained its Mediterranean bases and quickly ‚Äãbuilt ties with Syria‚Äôs new leader, Ahmed al‚ÄëSharaa, underscoring its willingness to trade loyalty for long-term leverage.

item.title: Trump says he needs to be involved in selecting Iran's next leader, Axios reports
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios ‚Äãon Thursday that he needs to ‚Äåbe personally involved in selecting Iran's next leader.
"Khamenei's son is unacceptable to me. ‚ÄãWe want someone that will ‚Äãbring harmony and peace to Iran," ‚ÅÝAxios quoted Trump as saying ‚Äãin an interview.
"I have to be ‚Äãinvolved in the appointment, like with Delcy (Rodriguez) in Venezuela," Trump said.
Mojtaba Khamenei, son of ‚ÄãIran's late Supreme Leader, has survived ‚Äãthe U.S. and Israeli airstrikes on Iran ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝwhich his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed, Iranian sources told Reuters on Wednesday.
A mid-ranking cleric with close ties ‚Äãto Iran's ‚Äãelite ‚ÅÝRevolutionary Guards, hardliner Mojtaba is one of the most ‚Äãinfluential figures in the Iranian ‚Äãclerical ‚ÅÝestablishment and is seen as a possible successor to his father.
Iran has ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãyet announced a new ‚Äãleader.

item.title: Trump renews Netanyahu pardon call, saying only Iran should be troubling him
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday called again on Israel's president to grant Prime Minister Benjamin ‚ÄãNetanyahu a pardon for corruption charges, saying the premier should ‚Äåhave nothing on his mind but the war with Iran.
KEY CONTEXT
WHAT'S NEXT

item.title: Swiss and EU vow to deepen cooperation on security and defence
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Switzerland and the European Union on Thursday said they planned to deepen ‚Äãcooperation on security and defence, including exchange ‚Äåof information in areas ranging from Ukraine to the Middle East.
The two sides issued a joint ‚Äãdeclaration stating that aim following talks in ‚ÄãZurich between Swiss Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis ‚ÅÝand EU foreign policy chief Kaja ‚ÄãKallas.
A draft of the declaration said the two ‚Äãsides' top diplomats aimed to hold annual dialogue and step up exchanges on issues and places of ‚Äãjoint interest including Ukraine, the Middle East, ‚ÄãAfrica, Asia, Latin America and the United States.
The two sides ‚Äåpledged ‚ÅÝto strengthen cooperation on foreign policy, security and defence, and Swiss representatives would be invited, as appropriate, to meetings of the ‚ÄãEU Foreign Affairs ‚ÄãCouncil and ‚ÅÝrelevant preparatory bodies, it said.
Issues of common interest also encompassed human ‚Äãrights, peace mediation, multilateral affairs, restrictive ‚Äãmeasures, ‚ÅÝconsular affairs, and crisis management abroad.
The declaration said the two planned to boost cooperation between ‚ÅÝEU ‚Äãdelegations and offices and ‚ÄãSwiss embassies and missions in third countries and to international ‚Äãorganisations.

item.title: France ramps up military aid to Lebanon as Macron calls on Hezbollah, Israel to halt attacks
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - France will strengthen its cooperation with the Lebanese Armed ‚ÄãForces and provide armored transport vehicles as well ‚Äåas operational and logistical support, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Thursday, as Lebanon was pulled deeper into the war in ‚Äãthe Middle East earlier this week.
"Everything must be ‚Äãdone to prevent this country, so close to ‚ÅÝFrance, from once again being drawn into war," Macron ‚Äãsaid in a post on X.
"At this moment of ‚Äãgreat danger, I call on the Israeli prime minister not to expand the war to Lebanon. I call on Iranian leaders not ‚Äãto further draw Lebanon into a war that ‚Äãis not its own," the French leader added.
After meeting with the ‚Äåchief ‚ÅÝof staff of the French military in Beirut, Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun asked Macron to intervene to "prevent the targeting of the southern suburbs following threats by the Israeli ‚Äãarmy against ‚Äãits residents," the ‚ÅÝLebanese presidency said in a separate statement.
France has said that it aims to ‚Äãprevent escalation across the region and has taken steps ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝprotect its own positions amid the wider conflict involving the United States, Israel and Iran .
"Hezbollah must immediately cease its fire ‚ÅÝtoward ‚ÄãIsrael. Israel must refrain from ‚Äãany ground intervention or large-scale operation on Lebanese territory," Macron said.

item.title: Flights for US citizens stuck in Middle East ramping up, State Dept says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. State Department said on Thursday it was ramping up the provision of charter flights from the Middle East ‚Äãafter criticism over the Trump administration's planning and initial assistance to ‚ÄåU.S. citizens trying to leave the region since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began.
The strikes that began on Saturday and Iran's retaliatory attacks on its neighbors have led to widespread ‚Äãairspace closures, clogging busy aviation routes throughout the region and leaving ‚Äãthousands stranded.
The department, headed by Secretary of State Marco Rubio, announced ‚ÅÝon Wednesday the first U.S. government charter flight departed the region bound ‚Äãfor the U.S., giving few details.
"At the direction of @SecRubio, Department of State charter ‚Äãflight and ground transportation operations are underway and will continue to ramp up with additional flights and ground transports taking place today," Assistant Secretary of State for Global Public ‚ÄãAffairs Dylan Johnson said in a post on X on Thursday.
It was ‚Äãunclear from which countries charter flights would be departing.
American citizens in Kuwait, Bahrain, the United ‚ÄåArab ‚ÅÝEmirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Israel should fill out a crisis intake form to receive information about upcoming flights and ground transportation options, Johnson said, adding that a new task force has helped more than 10,000 Americans with ‚Äãguidance since the crisis ‚Äãbegan.
The State Department ‚ÅÝhad only issued warnings to U.S. citizens in Israel and Lebanon as tensions rose, but Iran's response has targeted ‚ÄãU.S. missions and civilian infrastructure in many more places, ‚Äãincluding in ‚ÅÝGulf Arab nations that are major transport hubs.
U.S. security alerts for Americans in Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq, Qatar and Bahrain went out after the conflict ‚ÅÝbegan. ‚ÄãThe U.S. on Monday urged Americans across 14 ‚Äãcountries in the Middle East to immediately depart the region using "available commercial transportation" without offering any U.S.-government-vouched ‚Äãmeans, drawing the ire of U.S. lawmakers .

item.title: Ukraine, Russia exchange 200 POWs each in latest swap
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - Ukraine and Russia exchanged 200 prisoners of war each on Thursday in their latest swap, ‚Äãofficials on both sides said, despite a ‚Äådeadlock in U.S.-backed negotiations to end the four-year conflict .
Kyiv and Moscow have carried out regular POW exchanges throughout the ‚Äãwar, committing during recent talks in Geneva to ‚Äãa new round of such swaps even ‚ÅÝas negotiations remain stuck over the question of ‚Äãterritory.
"Every time our people come home, it proves ‚Äãthat Ukraine is working to bring everyone back. No one is forgotten," President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on X.
"We involve mediators. ‚ÄãI am grateful to everyone who helps Ukraine. ‚ÄãI thank the United States for its support in making ‚Äåthis ‚ÅÝexchange possible."
Some of the POWs freed on Thursday were captured as far back as Moscow's siege of Mariupol in early 2022, Zelenskiy added.
Tearful ‚Äãrelatives greeted loved ‚Äãones - many ‚ÅÝof them draped in Ukrainian flags - as they emerged from a ‚Äãbus that had arrived at an undisclosed ‚Äãlocation ‚ÅÝto chants of "Welcome!"
The two sides are expected to trade a further 300 POWs each in a ‚ÅÝsecond ‚Äãday of exchanges on Friday, ‚Äãofficials said.

item.title: Finland to lift full ban on hosting nuclear arms, government says
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 5 (Reuters) - Finland plans to lift a long-standing ban on having nuclear arms on its territory, the government said on Thursday, aligning with Nordic neighbours ‚Äãin a move that could open the door to deploying atomic ‚Äåbombs on Finnish soil during times of war.
Finland's Nuclear Energy Act, passed in 1987, prohibits the import, manufacture, possession and detonation of nuclear explosives on its soil, seen by some ‚ÄãFinns as a clause that would benefit only Russia if there ‚Äãever was a war.
While Finland maintained neutrality during the Cold War ‚ÅÝera, the country in 2023 joined the NATO military alliance in response ‚Äãto nuclear-armed Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine the preceding year.
"The amendment is necessary ‚Äãto enable Finland's military defense as part of the alliance and to take full advantage of NATO's deterrence and collective defence," Finnish Defence Minister Antti Hakkanen told a press conference.
The proposed ‚Äãchange will next go to parliament where the right-wing coalition government holds ‚Äãa majority.
Neighbours Sweden, Denmark and Norway have long-standing policies against nuclear weapons on their territories in ‚Äåpeacetime ‚ÅÝbut do not have legislative bans during war.
NATO allies France and Germany announced plans on Monday to deepen cooperation with European partners on nuclear deterrence, marking a policy shift as the continent faces rising threats from Russia and instability linked ‚Äãto the Iran conflict .
Sweden's ‚Äãdoctrine is to ‚ÅÝstation no permanent foreign troops or nuclear weapons on its soil in peacetime, Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson told reporters last ‚Äãweek, when asked about the possibility of his country hosting French ‚Äãnuclear arms .
"If ‚ÅÝwe were to find ourselves in a completely different situation, that particular formulation would not apply," Kristersson said.
Finland shares a 1,340-kilometre border with Russia and in 2024 ‚ÅÝsigned ‚Äãa defence pact with the United States, allowing ‚Äãit to use 15 of Finland's military facilities and zones.

item.title: Middle East chaos stems from erosion in international law, EU's Kallas says
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - The current turmoil in the Middle East is a direct ‚Äãresult of the erosion in international law, EU foreign ‚Äåpolicy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
In a speech at the University of Zurich, Kallas said the international order has been undermined by major powers ‚Äãacting unilaterally, pointing the finger particularly at Russia, but also ‚Äãdirecting criticism at China and the United States.
"Today, the ‚ÅÝchaos we see around us in the Middle East is ‚Äãa direct consequence of the erosion of international law," she said, ‚Äãarguing that Russia's invasion of Ukraine had encouraged others to act with impunity.
China, she said, was also taking advantage of the erosion of international rules to ‚Äãextend its influence in the Asia-Pacific region and put pressure ‚Äãon Europe's economies.
"Without restoring international law, together with accountability, we are doomed to ‚Äåsee ‚ÅÝrepeated violations of the law, disruption and chaos," Kallas argued.
Turning to the United States, she said Washington's foreign policy shift had "rocked the transatlantic relationship to its foundation, with aftershocks in other parts of ‚Äãthe world," and ‚Äãcalling its impact ‚ÅÝon the international order "seismic."
"The current direction is a new world order characterised by competition and coercive ‚Äãpower politics, a world order dominated by a handful ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝmilitary powers who aim to establish and secure spheres of influence," Kallas said.
Kallas was delivering the Churchill Special Lecture some 80 years after ‚ÅÝBritain's ‚Äãwartime leader Winston Churchill spoke at the ‚ÄãUniversity and called for the building of a "United States of Europe" following the devastation ‚Äãof World War Two.

item.title: Seafarers can refuse to sail through Mideast Gulf region, main union says
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters) - Seafarers have the right to refuse to sail on ships passing ‚Äãthrough the Middle East Gulf, including the Strait ‚Äåof Hormuz, after the threat level for the region was raised to its highest level, the leading labour union and shipping industry groups ‚Äãsaid on Thursday.
Around 300 ships are anchored on ‚Äãboth sides of the Strait while the U.S.-led war against ‚ÅÝIran escalates . Since February 28, nine ships have been ‚Äãdamaged and at least one seafarer has been killed.
As ‚Äãpart of negotiated arrangements reached on Thursday between mariners and commercial shipping companies, known as the International Bargaining Forum (IBF), seafarers can refuse to ‚Äãsail into the area, with repatriation at the company‚Äôs cost ‚Äãand compensation equal to two months‚Äô basic wage.
In addition, they will ‚Äåreceive ‚ÅÝhigher pay and compensation will be doubled in the event of death or disability, the International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said in a statement.
"Today‚Äôs designation ensures that seafarers on vessels ‚Äãcovered by IBF ‚Äãagreements have ‚ÅÝcritical protections if they operate in this dangerous region," ITF General Secretary Stephen Cotton said.
"The ‚Äãfact we have to take these measures at ‚Äãall ‚ÅÝis a damning indictment of the situation facing seafarers today. No worker should have to risk being killed or maimed simply ‚ÅÝfor ‚Äãdoing their job ‚Äì particularly when that ‚Äãjob is transporting the oil and goods that keep the world‚Äôs economies running."

item.title: Israel decided to kill Khamenei in November, defence minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 5 (Reuters) - Israel took the decision to kill Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in November and was planning to carry ‚Äãout the operation around six months later, Defence ‚ÄåMinister Israel Katz said on Thursday.
Khamenei was killed in the first hours of the U.S.-Israeli air campaign that began on Saturday ‚Äãin the first assassination of a country's top ‚Äãruler by an airstrike.
The joint air assault is nearing ‚ÅÝthe end of its first week after opening ‚Äãsalvos killed the country's leaders and set off a ‚Äãregional war, with Iranian attacks in Israel, the Gulf and Iraq, and Israeli attacks against Iran's ally Hezbollah in Lebanon .
"Already in November ‚Äãwe were convened with the prime minister in ‚Äãa very tight forum and the prime minister (Benjamin Netanyahu) set the ‚Äågoal ‚ÅÝof eliminating Khamenei," Katz told Israel's N12 TV news. The timing was set for mid-2026, he said.
The plan was eventually shared with the Washington and brought forward around ‚ÄãJanuary after ‚Äãprotests broke ‚ÅÝout Iran, when Israel was concerned its pressured clerical rulers might launch an attack ‚Äãagainst Israel and U.S. assets in the ‚ÄãMiddle ‚ÅÝEast, Katz said.
Israel has said its aim is to eliminate the existential threat it sees in Iran's nuclear ‚ÅÝprogramme and ballistic ‚Äãmissile project, and to bring ‚Äãabout regime change. Iran's rulers have so far shown no sign of ‚Äãrelinquishing power.

item.title: Europol warns Iran crisis raises threat of terror, extremism and cyberattacks
item.description:
MADRID, March 5 (Reuters) - The Middle East conflict will have "immediate repercussions" for European Union security with an increased threat of terrorism, serious and ‚Äãorganised crime as well as violent extremism and cyberattacks, ‚ÄåEuropean police body Europol told Spanish news agency EFE on Thursday.
Europol spokesman Jan Op Gen Oorth said he expected to see more cyberattacks against ‚ÄãEuropean infrastructure and an increase in online fraud using increasingly ‚Äãsophisticated Artificial Intelligence and exploiting the flurry of information ‚ÅÝswirling about the conflict online, EFE reported.
Groups linked to Iran ‚Äãcould seek to carry out "destabilising activities" within the EU, he added, ‚Äãreferring to groups linked to the so-called Axis of Resistance, the network of anti-American and Israeli Shi'ite militias in countries including Iraq, Lebanon and Yemen. ‚ÄãThese could include terrorist attacks, intimidation campaigns, terrorist financing and ‚Äãcybercrime.
"The level of terrorist threat and violent extremism in EU territory is considered ‚Äåhigh," ‚ÅÝhe told the news agency.
The terror threat could be heightened by individuals acting alone or small cells acting on their own initiative, he said.
"The rapid spread of polarising content on the internet ‚Äãcan accelerate short-term ‚Äãradicalisation processes among ‚ÅÝdiaspora communities within the EU and other individuals," he said.
Europol did not immediately return a Reuters ‚Äãrequest for comment on the reported statements.
Iran and ‚ÄãIsrael on ‚ÅÝThursday were exchanging fire on a sixth day of war after Israel and the United States launched joint air strikes on Iran ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãthe weekend. So far the attacks have ‚Äãkilled more than 1,000 people including Iran's Supreme Leader, prompted Iran to attack neighbours ‚Äãincluding Qatar and UAE along with energy shipments.

item.title: US, Qatar discuss acquiring Ukrainian drones to down Iran's Shaheds, source says
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Qatar are in discussions with Kyiv about acquiring Ukrainian interceptor drones as a cheap alternative to down Iranian Shahed UAVs amid the war in the Gulf, a source familiar with the ‚Äãmatter told Reuters.
The early-stage talks are taking place between government officials, not companies, and the technology being discussed includes systems to listen out for ‚Äåincoming enemy drones and disrupt their communication signals, the source said.
Qatar's International Media Office did not respond to a request for comment. The Pentagon declined to comment.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday evening the United States had asked Kyiv for help in downing Shaheds.
"I have instructed that the necessary resources be provided and that Ukrainian specialists be present to ensure ‚Äãthe necessary security," he said, without giving further details or mentioning Ukrainian interceptor drones.
Zelenskiy said earlier that Ukraine had also received similar requests from Middle ‚ÄãEastern countries and that he would only go ahead with deals if they did not weaken Ukraine's own defences against Russia's invasion.
He has ‚ÅÝalso said he would be open to exchanging drones for air defence missiles.
A second source - a Western diplomat in the Gulf - said a Ukrainian delegation had travelled to Doha ‚Äãthis week to meet with Qatari officials to share Ukraine's experience in drone defence. A delegation also travelled to Abu Dhabi, the diplomat said.
Tehran has fired hundreds of missiles ‚Äãand drones at Gulf countries after the U.S. and Israel launched a massive campaign of air strikes at Iran on Saturday and killed much of the Islamic Republic's senior leadership.
Gulf countries have managed to intercept most of those strikes, making use of the U.S.-made PAC-3 Patriot systems that Ukraine relies on to defend its own energy and military infrastructure from Russian missiles.
Ukraine, however, has developed ‚Äãfar cheaper methods of downing the Shahed kamikaze drone during its four-year conflict with Russia, which has been using the Iranian-designed UAVs for much of the war.
Russia has ‚Äãlaunched 19,000 long-range drones at Ukraine this winter, most of which it has downed, Kyiv said.
After the Iran war erupted, Ukraine's SBU security service warned Ukrainian companies not to sell weapons to ‚ÄåMiddle Eastern ‚ÅÝcountries without permission from Kyiv, the first source said.
The SBU and Ukraine's defence ministry did not respond to requests for comment.
A third source said Britain was supporting Ukraine in early-stage talks with Gulf states about the use of Ukrainian drones to intercept Shaheds.
That source said some drones could potentially be supplied by Project Octopus, an existing interceptor drone joint venture between London and Kyiv.
Britain's Ministry of Defence did not respond to questions. Ukrspecsystems, the Ukrainian company behind the Octopus drone factory in the UK , declined to comment.
Zelenskiy said on ‚ÄãWednesday he had spoken with leaders of ‚ÅÝthe UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Jordan and Kuwait, without providing details.
Ukraine's discussions with Washington were first reported by the Financial Times, but Doha's interest and Britain's involvement have not previously been reported.
The U.S. and its Gulf allies have expended hundreds of air defence missiles , costing ‚Äãmillions of dollars apiece, since the Iran conflict began.
Lockheed Martin produces around 600 PAC-3 missiles a year, although it plans ‚Äãto increase that to ‚ÅÝ2,000 under a seven-year deal with the Pentagon.
Kyiv has been running low on those missiles for months, raising concerns that it would be unable to down Russian ballistic missiles, against which the Patriot is the only effective weapon in its arsenal.
Zelenskiy has proposed swapping Ukrainian interceptor drones for missiles for Patriot systems.
Taras Tymochko from Come Back Alive, a Ukrainian foundation that ‚ÅÝhas bought ‚Äãtens of thousands of interceptor drones with donations, said it was unclear who, other than Ukrainian crews, ‚Äãcould operate them.
"It's rather difficult to remove our pilots from their operations and send them to the Middle East," Tymochko said. "There's a significant need to scale up existing training capacities in Ukraine to share experience with ‚Äãour partners."

item.title: Brazil rocked by probe of central bankers aiding failed Banco Master
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 5 (Reuters) - Evidence that two senior regulators at Brazil's central bank secretly advised embattled banker Daniel Vorcaro has sent shockwaves through the capital Brasilia, threatening to drag the institution deeper into a snowballing scandal.
The revelations add to a widening blast radius surrounding Vorcaro, owner of the liquidated Banco Master, whose downfall has exposed ‚Äãa network of influence and conflicts of interest shaking trust in some of Brazil's most powerful institutions.
Vorcaro was arrested on Wednesday in the latest phase of an ongoing criminal investigation, accused of bribing ‚Äåthe two central bank officials - in addition to plotting to attack and intimidate several people he perceived as acting against his interests with an associate he called "Sicario," named after the hit men employed by Mexican cartels.
Among his targets were former employees, domestic workers and journalists.
For months, Brazilians have watched as the probe - first focused on fraud in Master's loan portfolio - grew to implicate public pension funds, a state-owned bank and high-ranking officials with ties to Vorcaro.
Unusual interventions by a federal audit court and the Supreme Court questioning the lender's shutdown - despite neither body having banking supervisory authority - failed ‚Äãto derail the investigation but added to questions about the banker's outsized influence.
Amid all that, the central bank's decision in November to close Banco Master had reinforced views of the regulator as a bastion of hard-nosed public servants ‚Äãsteeled against Brazil's politics.
Federal police shattered those impressions on Wednesday, alleging that Vorcaro likely bribed former central bank director Paulo Sergio Neves de Souza and Belline Santana, ex-head of ‚ÅÝthe banking supervision department, for tips and advice, according to cellphone messages obtained through court-authorized access to communications records.
Reuters could not immediately reach Souza or Santana or identify their lawyers.
The central bank declined to comment on the implications for its ‚Äãreputation or regulatory decisions involving the officials.
In a public statement, the central bank said the federal police probe was key to clarifying the facts and any violations would receive the appropriate sanctions under the law.
A person with direct knowledge of the recent ‚Äãinvestigations said the conduct uncovered was "absolutely unacceptable, absurd and horrifying," even if the central bank's decision-making structure ultimately led the regulator to the right outcome.
Despite any dissent from Souza and Santana, the source said, the central bank blocked regional lender BRB's bid to acquire Banco Master before liquidating the troubled lender.
Still, the revelations have amplified criticism that the central bank may have taken too long to act, allowing Master's problems to deepen while technical assessments that passed through Souza and Santana may have downplayed the severity of the situation, undermining decisions that could have ‚Äãcome sooner.
"The central bank was slow to rein in Master and it was slow to liquidate it," said another source with knowledge of the regulator's decision-making.
Souza, who was directly responsible for overseeing banks as director of supervision, sat for nearly ‚Äãsix years on the rate-setting Copom committee, between 2017 and 2023 - a period when Banco Master was expanding aggressively.
A court decision authorizing Wednesday's police operation cited messages showing he and Santana helped Vorcaro with regulatory matters, including prior review of filings to the central ‚Äåbank.
The ruling cited ‚ÅÝevidence they may have received bribes while in their senior roles, including efforts by Vorcaro to formalize sham service contracts through consulting firms, which were then used to transfer funds to the two officials.
Both officials remained at the central bank in different banking supervisory roles until January, when they stepped down from their senior posts amid an internal probe. They remained career staff, now suspended by Wednesday's court order. A formal dismissal would require separate administrative proceedings.
"I was surprised and, above all, saddened. There is deep dismay," said one former director who worked with Souza. Three other sources who served alongside him voiced similar shock.
"It is very sad. But any wrongdoing by two officials must be separated from the institution and its staff," one source ‚Äãsaid, adding that the central bank's own internal reviews contributed ‚Äãto the investigation by federal police and prosecutors.
A federal ‚ÅÝpolice source, also speaking on condition of anonymity, agreed that findings so far pointed to corruption of individual civil servants rather than institutional failure.
A central bank public employee since 1998, Souza joined the bank's board under former President Michel Temer and remained through the administration of former President Jair Bolsonaro into the early months of President Luiz Inacio Lula ‚Äãda Silva's current term, leaving in July 2023.
His tenure overlapped with the rapid rise of Banco Master, built largely on the sale of high-yield debt marketed to retail investors ‚Äãas covered by the Credit ‚ÅÝGuarantee Fund (FGC).
When Banco Master was previously known as Banco Maxima before Vorcaro bought it in 2017, it was already on the central bank's confidential watchlist of troubled institutions, flagged for lending that ignored principles of "selectivity, liquidity and guarantees."
Approval for the young banker to take over the rebranded bank came in 2019, under then-governor Roberto Campos Neto. Two sources familiar with Vorcaro's influence said he long benefited from strong political ties in Brasilia that helped him cast a risky growth model as a boon for competition.
Although the lender held ‚ÅÝless than 1% ‚Äãof Brazil's banking assets, its collapse last November - amid a cash crunch and mismanagement cited by the central bank - has cost the FGC fund ‚Äãsome 40 billion reais ($7.7 billion), roughly a third of its available resources, a bill that later rose with the liquidation of other institutions under its umbrella.
The FGC is financed with mandatory contributions from banks, especially larger lenders, which will bear the brunt of new funding.
That process gained breathing room this week after ‚Äãthe central bank allowed 30 billion reais in reserve requirements that would otherwise have been deposited at the regulator to be redirected to the FGC this year.
($1 = 5.2312 reais)

item.title: US suspending operations at embassy in Kuwait
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄãStates has ‚Äåsuspended operations at ‚Äãits ‚Äãembassy in Kuwait ‚ÅÝCity, the ‚ÄãState ‚ÄãDepartment said in a ‚Äãstatement ‚Äãon Thursday, amid ‚ÄåU.S. ‚ÅÝand Israeli strikes ‚Äãagainst ‚ÄãIran.

item.title: Czech parliament votes to shield PM Babis from trial on EU subsidy fraud charges
item.description:
PRAGUE, March 5 (Reuters) - The lower house of the Czech parliament voted on Thursday to deny a court request for billionaire businessman and Prime Minister Andrej Babis ‚Äãto face trial in long-running prosecution over an alleged fraud in drawing ‚Äåa European Union subsidy.
Babis, head of the populist ANO party, returned to power after winning an election in October last year, despite charges in the case involving a 2 million ‚Äãeuro subsidy granted in 2008, before he entered politics, for building ‚Äãa hotel and conference centre outside Prague called Stork Nest.
Deputies for ‚ÅÝANO and ruling coalition partners, the far right, pro-Russian SPD party and the ‚Äãanti-Green Deal Motorists, voted on Thursday not to lift parliamentary immunity from Babis, ‚Äãvoting records showed. The vote means Babis is protected from prosecution in the case until the end of the parliament's four-year term in 2029.

item.title: Iceland to propose holding EU referendum during autumn, broadcaster RUV reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 5 (Reuters) - Iceland's government will propose to its parliament to ‚Äãhold a referendum during the autumn ‚Äåon whether to resume European Union accession talks abandoned more than a ‚Äãdecade ago, public broadcaster RUV ‚Äãreported on Thursday, citing sources.
RUV reported ‚ÅÝon its website that the government ‚Äãwould submit the bill to parliament ‚Äãnext week and that the vote, according to sources, looked most likely to take ‚Äãplace late September.
KEY CONTEXT

item.title: EU's Kallas says Iran is seeking to escalate conflict
item.description:
ZURICH, March 5 (Reuters) - Iran is seeking to escalate ‚Äãthe conflict in the Middle East by ‚Äåattacking other countries in the region indiscriminately, EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said on Thursday.
"Iran is ‚Äãan exporter of war," Kallas told ‚Äãreporters at a press conference with Swiss Foreign ‚ÅÝMinister Ignazio Cassis during a visit to ‚ÄãZurich. "Right now, the regime tries to drag as ‚Äãmany countries into this war as possible."
When asked whether she thought Iran was trying to suck NATO into ‚Äãthe conflict and whether the EU had ‚Äãa strategy to avoid getting involved, Kallas accused Tehran ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝtrying to "sow chaos" in the region and attacking other countries "indiscriminately".
Both NATO and the EU had mechanisms enabling members to decide on whether they ‚Äãneeded to ‚Äãcall for ‚ÅÝhelp, she said. This had not been the case so far, ‚Äãshe added.
Kallas said Iran had been ‚Äãconsiderably ‚ÅÝweakened, and that she saw a chance for the Iranian people to "determine their own future."
She ‚ÅÝalso ‚Äãsaid the EU was continuing ‚Äãto work through diplomatic channels to pursue de-escalation in the ‚Äãregion.

item.title: Germany says it has no plans to send extra forces to Middle East
item.description:
BERLIN, March 5 (Reuters) - Germany said on Thursday it had no plans to send additional military support ‚Äãto the Middle East, after European allies announced plans ‚Äåto move forces to the eastern Mediterranean following a drone strike at a British air base in Cyprus.
Italian Defence ‚ÄãMinister Guido Crosetto told parliament on Thursday ‚Äãthat Italy, Spain, France and the Netherlands ‚ÅÝwould send naval assets to protect Cyprus ‚Äãin the coming days.
Britain is also deploying a destroyer and ‚Äãadditional helicopters with anti-drone capabilities to the region and is joining France and Greece in boosting air defences in Cyprus.
Germany's ‚Äãnavy participates in the long-running United Nations ‚ÄãUNIFIL monitoring mission in Lebanon, but the defence ministry said ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝmilitary's focus remained on NATO defence operations in eastern Europe.
"Germany is currently not planning any additional military capabilities beyond its existing contributions to international ‚Äãmissions in the ‚ÄãMiddle East," ‚ÅÝthe ministry said in a statement.
The statement said the anti-aircraft frigate "Sachsen" was ‚Äãcurrently taking part in NATO's Cold ‚ÄãResponse ‚ÅÝexercise in the Arctic.
The statement from the ministry reiterated comments from Defence Minister Boris Pistorius, who declared ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãparliament: "Germany is not a party ‚Äãin this war. The Bundeswehr is not taking part in ‚Äãthis war."

item.title: US, others express concern over violations of ceasefires in eastern Congo
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United ‚ÄãStates, European ‚ÄåUnion and others said ‚Äãin ‚Äãa joint statement on ‚ÅÝThursday ‚Äãthey were ‚Äãprofoundly concerned about "continued and recent" ‚Äãviolations of ‚Äãceasefires in eastern ‚ÄåDemocratic ‚ÅÝRepublic of Congo and called ‚Äãon ‚Äãall ‚ÅÝparties to recommit ‚Äãto end ‚Äãfighting ‚ÅÝand return to ‚ÅÝnegotiations.

item.title: Flight to bring French nationals from UAE forced back, minister says
item.description:
PARIS, March 5 (Reuters) - An Air France flight chartered ‚Äãby the French government ‚Äåto bring French nationals back from the United ‚ÄãArab Emirates was ‚Äãforced to turn back ‚ÅÝon Thursday due to ‚Äãmissile fire in ‚Äãthe area, French Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said on X.
"This ‚Äãsituation reflects the ‚Äãinstability in the region and ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝcomplexity of repatriation operations," Tabarot added.
France started r epatriation flights from the ‚ÄãMiddle ‚ÄãEast ‚ÅÝon Wednesday as governments rush to ‚Äãbring home tens ‚Äãof ‚ÅÝthousands of citizens stranded by the intensifying ‚ÅÝU.S. ‚Äãand Israeli ‚Äãconflict with Iran.

item.title: Trump ousts Kristi Noem, names Oklahoma senator as homeland security nominee
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - Kristi Noem, who oversaw U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive immigration crackdown and faced bipartisan criticism in hearings this week, will leave her role as homeland security chief at the end ‚Äãof the month.
Trump will tap Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace her, he said on his Truth Social platform, a move that would require U.S. Senate ‚Äåconfirmation.
Noem, a former governor of South Dakota, became one of Trump's most high-profile Cabinet secretaries with social media posts that portrayed immigrants in harsh terms, highlighting cases of alleged criminal offenders and using vitriolic language.
She faced criticism in January when she quickly labeled two U.S. citizens fatally shot by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis as committing "domestic terrorism." Videos that emerged after the deaths undercut the assertion by Noem and other Trump officials that ‚Äãthe two deceased - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - were violent aggressors.
The public backlash for deaths led the Trump administration to move to a more targeted approach to immigration ‚Äãenforcement in Minnesota after months of sweeps through U.S. cities that led to violent clashes with residents opposing the crackdown.
Democrats in the U.S. House ‚ÅÝof Representatives moved to impeach Noem and at least two Republicans in Congress called for her to lose her job after the incidents. During congressional hearings in March, Democrats and some Republicans ‚Äãcriticized Noem for her approach to the immigration crackdown and management of DHS, including concern over a $220 million ad campaign that heavily featured Noem.
Trump told Reuters on Thursday that he did not ‚Äãsign off on the ad campaign.
"I never knew anything about it," he said in a phone interview.
The ads prominently featured Noem, including a scene of her on horseback at Mount Rushmore in her home state of South Dakota.
In one of the congressional hearings this week, U.S. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican from Louisiana, asked Noem if Trump had approved the commercials.
"The president approved ahead of time you spending $220 million running TV ‚Äãads across the country in which you are featured prominently?" Kennedy asked Noem.
"Yes, sir. We went through the legal processes, did it correctly," she replied.
The staffing change raises questions about whether the Trump administration could seek to intensify its mass deportation push or retreat to a more targeted approach. Under Noem's leadership, masked immigration agents surged into Los Angeles, ‚ÄåChicago and Washington, ‚ÅÝD.C. , scouring neighborhoods and Home Depot parking lots in search of possible immigration offenders.
Mullin, who spent a decade in the House of Representatives before becoming a senator in 2023, also supports Trump's hardline immigration agenda. To become DHS secretary, Mullin would require Senate confirmation.
The popularity of Trump's immigration approach fell as agents detained U.S. citizens and tear-gassed streets in an attempt to drive up deportations, which last year fell short of the administration's goal of 1 million per year.
While Noem, 54, served as a prominent proponent of Trump's agenda, White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen ‚ÄãMiller, a long-time Trump aide, controls Trump's immigration policy.
Noem ‚Äãwas quickly confirmed to lead the 260,000-employee ‚ÅÝDepartment of Homeland Security in January 2025 after Trump took office. On social media, she referred to immigrants convicted of crimes as "scumbags" even as the number of non-criminals arrested by immigration authorities rose under Trump.
She joined immigration enforcement operations on the ground in New York City and visited a ‚Äãmaximum-security prison in El Salvador where Venezuelan immigrants deported by the Trump administration were being held without charges or access to lawyers.
The ‚Äãnumber of migrants caught trying ‚ÅÝto illegally cross the U.S.-Mexico border plummeted under Trump's restrictive policies, a steep drop after high levels of illegal immigration under former President Joe Biden, a Democrat.
Noem, reflecting Trump's agenda, also took steps to cut legal immigration programs and increase vetting. She ended several Temporary Protected Status programs that provided work permits to hundreds of thousands of immigrants from Venezuela, Haiti and other nations, drawing ‚ÅÝlegal challenges.
After an ‚ÄãAfghan immigrant was accused of attacking National Guard members in Washington, D.C., Noem said she recommended that Trump ‚Äãplace "a full travel ban on every damn country that‚Äôs been flooding our nation with killers, leeches, and entitlement junkies."
Critics said Noem demonized immigrants and promoted an immigration enforcement strategy that targeted non-criminal, working immigrants and families.
During Noem's tenure, ‚Äãthe number of deaths in immigration detention rose to a two-decade high while staff in DHS oversight offices were slashed sharply.

item.title: US military says it has sunk over 30 Iranian ships so far
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. military said on ‚ÄãThursday it has ‚Äåsunk over 30 Iranian ships so far during ‚Äãthe U.S. ‚Äãand Israel's war against Iran, ‚ÅÝincluding an Iranian ‚Äãdrone ship that ‚Äãis on fire.
Ballistic missile attacks by Iran have decreased ‚Äãby 90% since ‚Äãthe first day of the ‚Äåwar, ‚ÅÝU.S. Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. forces in the ‚ÄãMiddle ‚ÄãEast ‚ÅÝas the head of Central ‚ÄãCommand, told reporters.

item.title: Syria opens Mediterranean-Aleppo air corridor that could help ease regional snarl
item.description:
DAMASCUS, March 5 (Reuters) - Syria has opened a newly reactivated air corridor from the northern city of Aleppo toward the Mediterranean Sea for use by ‚Äãforeign airlines as well as its national carrier, the head of ‚Äåthe country‚Äôs Civil Aviation Authority told Reuters on Thursday, as air traffic gradually resumes through Aleppo International Airport.
Civil Aviation Authority director Omar Hosari said the route is intended to provide a safe flight path for ‚Äãaircraft arriving at and departing from Aleppo and will be available to ‚Äãany airline as long as it ‚Äúmeets international safety standards.‚Äù
‚ÄúThe corridors are ‚ÅÝnot dedicated exclusively to Syrian Air,‚Äù said Hosari. ‚ÄúOther airlines can use them to transit ‚Äãor operate through Syrian airspace according to the usual regulatory procedures.‚Äù
In a Thursday ‚Äãstatement, the authority said it had also reopened air routes in the northern part of Syrian airspace toward Turkey after conducting operational and technical assessments and reviewing regional developments affecting ‚Äãaviation.
The authority said the first Syrian Air flight departed Aleppo for Istanbul ‚Äãon Thursday and arrived safely, marking the gradual resumption of operations at Aleppo International Airport. ‚ÄåA flight ‚ÅÝfrom Aleppo to the Saudi city of Jeddah is scheduled for Friday, with authorities studying the addition of other destinations including Riyadh.
Hosari said Royal Jordanian is expected to use the Mediterranean corridor on Friday for a flight from ‚ÄãAmman to Aleppo as ‚Äãpart of the ‚ÅÝphased return of air traffic.
Commercial air traffic remained largely absent across much of the Middle East, with major Gulf ‚Äãhubs - including Doha and Dubai, the world's busiest airport for ‚Äãinternational passengers - ‚ÅÝlargely shut for a sixth straight day after the U.S.-Israeli launched air strikes on Iran , which responded with retaliatory missile and drone strikes on the entire region.
A week ‚ÅÝinto ‚Äãthe regional conflict, Syria is emerging relatively sidelined, ‚Äãwith Iranian presence ceased after the fall of President Bashar al-Assad in late 2024, and U.S. military bases ‚Äãin the country evacuated last February.

item.title: Trump: want to finish Iran then Cuba 'question of time'
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump said ‚Äåon Thursday he wants to finish the war ‚Äãin Iran first, ‚Äãbut then it "will be ‚ÅÝjust a question ‚Äãof time before you ‚Äãand a lot of unbelievable people are going to ‚Äãbe going back ‚Äãto Cuba."
Trump, speaking at an event ‚Äåwith ‚ÅÝthe Inter Miami soccer team at the White House, said ‚ÄãCuba wants "to ‚Äãmake ‚ÅÝa deal so badly."
"We want ‚Äãto finish this ‚Äãone ‚ÅÝfirst," he said, referring to the conflict ‚ÅÝin ‚ÄãIran.

item.title: Oilfield in Iraqi Kurdistan halts production after drone strike, sources say
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - A drone attack struck an oilfield operated by U.S. firm HKN Energy in Iraq‚Äôs ‚ÄãKurdistan region on Thursday, causing a fire ‚Äåand halting production, security sources and an oilfield engineer said.
The Sarsang field produces about 30,000 barrels of oil per ‚Äãday and is operated by HKN Energy, ‚Äãwhich has a 62% stake. HKN is a ‚ÅÝprivately held U.S. oil and gas company owned ‚Äãby Hillwood Energy, part of the Hillwood group ‚Äãfounded by Ross Perot Jr.
No group claimed responsibility, but Kurdish officials accused Iraqi militias allied with Iran of carrying out ‚Äãthe attack.
If so, the attack would mean Iran‚Äëaligned ‚ÄãIraqi militias, who have vowed to retaliate for the killing ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝIran‚Äôs supreme leader, have expanded targets from U.S. military bases in Iraqi Kurdistan to U.S. energy interests.
Production at the field was halted as a precaution ‚Äãafter an ‚Äãexplosion at ‚ÅÝits power unit, the engineer told Reuters.
Kurdistan regional authorities confirmed the attack and ‚Äãsaid it was carried out by two ‚Äãdrones.
Some ‚ÅÝenergy companies operating in Iraqi Kurdistan shut oil and gas production at their fields as a precaution ‚ÅÝafter ‚Äãthe United States and Israel ‚Äãlaunched strikes on neighbouring Iran.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine will help US to counter Iranian drones
item.description:
KYIV, March 5 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Thursday that Ukraine would provide assistance to the U.S. in response to its request for help in dealing ‚Äãwith Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Tehran has fired hundreds of drones at ‚ÄåU.S. targets in neighboring countries after the U.S. and Israel began a massive campaign of air strikes against Iran on Saturday.
During its war with Russia, Ukraine has developed highly ‚Äãeffective means of downing the Iranian-designed Shahed kamikaze drone, which Moscow has ‚Äãbeen using for much of the four-year war.
"We received a ‚ÅÝrequest from the United States for specific support in protection against 'Shaheds' in ‚Äãthe Middle East region," Zelenskiy wrote in English on the X social media ‚Äãplatform.
"I gave instructions to provide the necessary means and ensure the presence of Ukrainian specialists who can guarantee the required security."
The U.S. had requested assistance in protecting its bases and ‚Äãsoldiers in certain countries in the region, according to a source familiar ‚Äãwith the matter, who said Zelenskiy had instructed his military to do so.
Ukrainians would start ‚Äåwork "in ‚ÅÝthe next few days", the source said.
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, U.S. President Donald Trump said that he would take assistance from any country, when asked about Zelenskiy's offer of support to help defend against Iranian drones.
Zelenskiy ‚Äãexpressed concern earlier ‚Äãthis week that ‚ÅÝthe war in Iran - if it proves long and intense - could diminish weapons supplies to Ukraine from its Western ‚Äãallies, in particular the air defence systems it needs to ‚Äãprotect against ‚ÅÝRussian missiles.
In recent months, Ukraine has already experienced shortages of the U.S.-made Patriot air defence systems - the only effective weapon in its arsenal for downing Russia's ‚ÅÝballistic missiles.
On ‚ÄãTuesday, Zelenskiy said Ukraine would be open to ‚Äãexchanging interceptor drones for air defence missiles from the Middle East allies, which have their own ‚Äãstocks of Patriots.

item.title: Canada, EU sign deal to modernize economic and trade agreement
item.description:
TORONTO, March 5 (Reuters) - Canada and the European Union ‚Äãagreed on Thursday ‚Äåto expand trade ties and ease trade ‚Äãrules under their ‚Äãexisting pact as countries ‚ÅÝacross the world ‚Äãstrive to increase trade ‚Äãand diversify away from the United States.
Canadian Trade ‚ÄãMinister Maninder Sidhu ‚Äãand European Union Commissioner for ‚ÄåTrade ‚ÅÝand Economic Security Maro≈° ≈Ýefƒçoviƒç signed an agreement in Toronto ‚Äãon ‚Äãsettling ‚ÅÝinvestment disputes and launched talks on ‚Äãa digital ‚Äãtrade ‚ÅÝdeal that would support data sharing, digital ‚ÅÝservices, ‚Äãcybersecurity cooperation ‚Äãand emerging technologies.

item.title: Iran calling US about a deal, says Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Thursday said Tehran was reaching out to ‚Äãthe United States about making a deal amid ‚ÄåU.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, adding that further action to reduce pressure on oil was imminent.
"They're calling, they're saying 'how do we ‚Äãmake a deal?' I said you're being ‚Äãa little bit late," said Trump, speaking at ‚ÅÝan event with the Inter Miami soccer team ‚Äãat the White House.
Trump touted the U.S. ‚Äãmilitary actions in Iran, saying they were destroying Tehran's missile and drone capability and that "their navy is gone - 24 ships ‚Äãin three days," as he called on Iranian ‚Äãdiplomats to request asylum and help shape a better country.
"We also ‚Äåurge ‚ÅÝIranian diplomats around the world to request asylum and to help us shape a new and better Iran," he said.
Iran's mission to the United Nations ‚Äãin New ‚ÄãYork declined to ‚ÅÝcomment.
While Trump said oil had "pretty much stabilized," he said further action ‚Äãto reduce pressure on it was imminent.
The U.S. ‚ÄãTreasury ‚ÅÝDepartment is expected to announce measures as soon as Thursday aimed at combating rising energy prices, including potential action involving the ‚ÅÝoil ‚Äãfutures market, a senior White ‚ÄãHouse official said earlier on Thursday.

item.title: US not expanding military objectives in Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
TAMPA, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said ‚Äãon Thursday the United States was ‚Äånot expanding its military objectives in Iran, after President Donald Trump told Reuters that the United States ‚Äãmust be involved in choosing the ‚Äãnext leader of Iran .
The Pentagon earlier this week ‚ÅÝsaid the military campaign, known as ‚ÄãOperation Epic Fury, is focused on destroying ‚ÄãIran's offensive missiles, missile production and Navy, while not allowing Tehran to have a nuclear weapon.
"There's no ‚Äãexpansion in our objectives. We know exactly ‚Äãwhat we're trying to achieve," Hegseth said.
He added that ‚ÄåTrump ‚ÅÝwas "having a heck of a say in who runs Iran given the ongoing operation."
In a telephone interview with Reuters on Thursday, ‚ÄãTrump said ‚Äãthe United ‚ÅÝStates would have to help pick the next person to ‚Äãlead the country.
The United States has ‚Äãhit ‚ÅÝmore than 2,000 targets in Iran over the past six days, including Iranian warships.

item.title: Ukraine says Ukrainian bank employees detained with cash shipment in Hungary
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha accused Hungary early on ‚ÄãFriday of detaining seven employees ‚Äåof Ukraine's state savings bank while they were transporting cash from Austria ‚Äãback to Ukraine.
Andrii Sybiha ‚Äãwas writing on the X social ‚ÅÝmedia platform after Hungarian ‚ÄãPrime Minister Viktor Orban said Budapest ‚Äãwould force Ukraine with "political and financial tools" to reopen the Druzhba pipeline carrying ‚ÄãRussian oil to Hungarian ‚Äãrefineries.
Sybiha said the employees were travelling in ‚Äåtwo ‚ÅÝcars through Hungary when they were detained. He said their whereabouts were unknown at the moment.
"In ‚Äãfact, we ‚Äãare ‚ÅÝtalking about Hungary taking hostages and stealing money," ‚ÄãSybiha wrote. "If this is ‚Äãthe 'force' ‚ÅÝannounced earlier today by Mr Orban, then this is a force ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãa criminal gang. ‚ÄãThis is state terrorism and racketeering."

item.title: US, Venezuela agree to re-establish diplomatic and consular relations
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The United States and interim ‚Äãauthorities in Venezuela have agreed ‚Äåto re-establish diplomatic and consular relations, the U.S. State Department said in a ‚Äãstatement on Thursday, adding that it ‚Äãwas focused on creating conditions ‚ÅÝfor a peaceful transition to a ‚Äãdemocratically elected government.
"This step will ‚Äãfacilitate our joint efforts to promote stability, support economic recovery, and advance political reconciliation ‚Äãin Venezuela," the State Department ‚Äãsaid.
"Our engagement is focused on helping the Venezuelan ‚Äåpeople ‚ÅÝmove forward through a phased process that creates the conditions for a peaceful transition to a democratically ‚Äãelected ‚Äãgovernment."
After months ‚ÅÝof heightened tensions, the U.S. captured Venezuela's President ‚ÄãNicolas Maduro in January, setting ‚Äãoff ‚ÅÝa chain of changes in the country, including the swearing-in of interim ‚ÅÝPresident ‚ÄãDelcy Rodriguez.
The two ‚Äãcountries have since gradually resumed bilateral relations.

item.title: Kosovo's parliament fails to elect president, risking new election
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 5 (Reuters) - Kosovo's parliament ‚Äãmissed the deadline to elect a new president ‚Äåon Thursday, plunging the country into a fresh political crisis that could trigger another snap election.
The 120-seat assembly had ‚Äãuntil midnight to vote for a head ‚Äãof state, but Prime Minister Albin Kurti's ‚ÅÝruling party, Vetevendosje, was unable to bring opposition ‚Äãlawmakers on board and vote for its candidate.
The ‚Äãopposition parties have asked for a consensual candidate, but Kurti has nominated his foreign minister, Glauk Konjufca. "There is no quorum ‚Äãto continue this session... we cannot proceed with ‚Äãthe vote," speaker of the parliament Albulena Haxhiu said, noting ‚Äåthat ‚ÅÝonly 66 lawmakers were present, well below the 80 required.
If a snap election is called, it would be the third parliamentary vote in just over ‚Äãa year. Kosovo ‚Äãalready held ‚ÅÝa snap election on December 28 after failing to form a government following ‚Äãthe February 2025 poll.
The two largest ‚Äãopposition ‚ÅÝparties, the Democratic Party of Kosovo and the Democratic League of Kosovo, have urged the president to call ‚ÅÝfresh ‚Äãelections.
Outgoing President Vjosa Osmani, who ‚Äãhas another month in office, is expected to outline the way ‚Äãforward on Friday.

item.title: US House rejects war powers resolution, backs Trump on Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. House of Representatives rejected an effort on Thursday to stop President Donald Trump's air war ‚Äåon Iran and require that any hostilities against Iran be authorized by Congress, backing the Republican president's military campaign on the sixth day of the expanding conflict .
The vote was 219 to 212, largely along party lines, in the House, where Trump's fellow Republicans control a narrow majority of seats. ‚ÄãTwo Republicans voted in favor of the resolution and four Democrats voted against it.
Opponents accused Democrats of taking the issue ‚Äãto a vote only because they oppose Trump, putting Americans at increased risk.
"We all know that we ‚ÅÝwouldn't be here today if the president's name wasn't Donald Trump," Representative Rick Crawford of Arizona, the Republican chairman of ‚Äãthe House Intelligence Committee, said during debate on Wednesday.
Sponsors of the resolution described it as a bid to take back Congress' ‚Äãresponsibility to authorize war, as spelled out in the U.S. Constitution.
The U.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran on Saturday, a conflict that has killed more than 1,000 people, including at least six U.S. service members, and caused damage and instability throughout the Middle East.
Supporters said the resolution, ‚Äãby requiring Trump to come to Congress for a war authorization, would force him to explain to Americans why the ‚ÄãU.S. is fighting and how it might end.
"This is a war of choice, launched by this administration without authorization, without clearly stated objectives or ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝdefined endgame, and without explaining how they intend to keep Americans safe," said Representative Gregory Meeks of New York, the top Democrat on the Foreign Affairs Committee.
Just before the resolution vote, House members from both parties overwhelmingly passed a measure "Reaffirming Iran remains the largest state sponsor of terrorism."
The vote would not have stopped the conflict even if ‚Äãthe House had voted yes.
To go ‚Äãinto effect, the resolution ‚ÅÝwould also have had to pass the Senate and garner the two-thirds majorities needed to override Trump's expected veto.
The Senate, also narrowly controlled by Trump's party, backed his military campaign against Iran ‚Äãin a vote on Wednesday, voting to block a bipartisan resolution similar to the measure passed ‚Äãby the House. The ‚ÅÝvotes this week are not the end of the matter. The War Powers Resolution of 1973, which provides for votes on the resolutions, says a president can only involve the military in an armed conflict when Congress has declared war or provided specific authority ‚ÅÝor in ‚Äãresponse to an attack.
Trump and his Republicans have argued that Iran posed an "imminent ‚Äãthreat" so that his actions were legal under that law.
However, the War Powers measure also requires unauthorized military actions to be terminated within 60 days, giving ‚Äãthe Trump administration a deadline at the end of April to seek Congress' approval.

item.title: Exclusive: US investigation points to likely US responsibility in Iran school strike, sources say
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - U.S. military investigators believe it is likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on Saturday but have not yet reached a final conclusion or completed ‚Äãtheir investigation, two U.S. officials told Reuters.
Reuters was unable to determine more details about the investigation, including what evidence ‚Äåcontributed to the tentative assessment, what type of munition was used, who was responsible or why the U.S. might have struck the school.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Wednesday acknowledged the U.S. military was investigating the incident.
The officials, who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive military matters, did not rule out ‚Äãthe possibility that new evidence could emerge that absolves the U.S. of responsibility and points to another responsible party in ‚Äãthe incident.
Reuters could not determine how much longer the investigation would last or what evidence U.S. investigators are ‚ÅÝseeking before the assessment can be completed.
The girls' school in Minab, in southern Iran, was hit on Saturday during the first day ‚Äãof U.S. and Israeli attacks on the country. Iran's ambassador to the U.N. in Geneva, Ali Bahreini, said the strike killed 150 students. Reuters ‚Äãcould not independently confirm the death toll.
The Pentagon referred questions from Reuters to the U.S. military‚Äôs Central Command, whose spokesperson, Captain Timothy Hawkins, said: ‚ÄúIt would be inappropriate to comment given the incident is under investigation.‚Äù
The White House did not directly comment on the investigation, but press secretary Karoline Leavitt said in a statement ‚Äãto Reuters, ‚ÄúWhile the Department of War is currently investigating this matter, the Iranian regime targets civilians and children, not the United States ‚Äãof America.‚Äù
Asked about the incident during a news briefing on Wednesday, Hegseth said: ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre investigating that. We, of course, never target civilian targets. But we‚Äôre taking a ‚Äålook and ‚ÅÝinvestigating that.‚Äù
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio told reporters on Monday that the United States would not deliberately target a school.
"The Department of War would be investigating that if that was our strike, and I would refer your question to them," Rubio said.
Israeli and U.S. forces have until now divided their attacks in Iran both geographically and by target type, a senior Israeli official and a source with direct ‚Äãknowledge of the joint planning said. ‚ÄãWhile Israel was striking missile ‚ÅÝlaunch sites in western Iran, the United States was attacking such targets, as well as naval ones, in the south.
The U.N. human rights office, without saying who it believed was responsible for the strike on ‚Äãthe school, called on Tuesday for an investigation.
"The onus is on the forces that carried out ‚Äãthe attack to investigate ‚ÅÝit," U.N. human rights office spokesperson Ravina Shamdasani told a press briefing in Geneva.
Images of the girls' funeral on Tuesday were shown on Iranian state television. Their small coffins were draped with Iranian flags and passed from a truck across a large crowd towards the grave site.
Deliberately attacking ‚ÅÝa school ‚Äãor hospital or any other civilian structure would likely be a war crime under ‚Äãinternational humanitarian law.
If a U.S. role were to be confirmed, the strike would rank among the worst cases of civilian casualties in decades of U.S. conflicts in the ‚ÄãMiddle East.

item.title: Hezbollah warns Israeli residents to evacuate towns near border
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Lebanon's Hezbollah warned Israeli residents to evacuate towns within ‚Äã5 km (3.11 miles) of the border between ‚Äåthe countries in a message posted on its Telegram channel in Hebrew early on ‚ÄãFriday.
Hezbollah's message came less than a ‚Äãday after Israel warned residents to ‚ÅÝleave Beirut's southern suburbs, prompting an ‚Äãexodus from a swathe of the capital ‚Äãknown as Dahiyeh.
Lebanon was pulled into the war in the Middle East on Monday, when ‚ÄãHezbollah opened fire, sparking Israeli airstrikes ‚Äãfocused on Beirut's southern suburbs and on southern and ‚Äåeastern ‚ÅÝLebanon.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign it ‚Äãis carrying ‚Äãout will ‚ÅÝnot go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
Israel has said it will not ‚Äãevacuate its border towns and ‚Äãhas ‚ÅÝsent more soldiers into Lebanon, saying this was a defensive measure meant to ‚ÅÝprotect ‚Äãits citizens who live ‚Äãnearby.

item.title: Partner in Malaysia‚Äôs ruling bloc says it is reviewing ties as rifts deepen over graft scandal
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 6 (Reuters) - A key partner in Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim‚Äôs ruling coalition is reconsidering ‚Äãits role in the pact ahead of the next elections, a senior leader said, as frustration mounts among the premier‚Äôs reformist allies over the government‚Äôs ‚Äåresponse to a scandal at the anti-graft agency.
