Over the last decade, Malaysia has been working to regain public and investor confidence after a multibillion-dollar fraud at state fund 1MDB left the country with years of debt and saw a former prime minister jailed.
While smaller in scale than 1MDB, allegations of widespread misconduct at the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) this year have renewed scrutiny on Anwar‚Äôs pledge to tackle graft, after the ‚Äãpremier resisted calls for a comprehensive probe.
The allegations, along with existing concerns over the slow progress of governance reforms , have pushed the Democratic Action Party (DAP) ‚Äì the largest party ‚Äãin the ruling bloc and a staunch supporter of Anwar through his more than two decades as a progressive opposition leader ‚Äì to ‚ÅÝrethink its ties.
‚ÄúI expect the party to evaluate its position in the relevant coalitions when the time comes for elections, to determine the best strategy going forward,‚Äù the DAP's ‚Äãdisciplinary chief Tony Pua told Reuters.
Pua, however, said the party will not withdraw support for the government this term as it does not want to cause political instability.
Anwar's office did ‚Äãnot respond to a request for comment. Anwar has said his administration is working hard to address governance issues and eliminate graft, while acknowledging the challenge of stamping out systemic corruption.
MACC chief Azam Baki has faced repeated calls to step down , after Bloomberg reported in separate articles last month that he may have breached shareholding rules for public officials, and that the agency has been helping ‚Äãa group of businessmen to seize control of companies. Both Azam and the MACC have denied wrongdoing.
The government has formed a committee to probe the allegations, with its findings expected ‚Äãthis week.
But Anwar has so far dismissed calls by DAP for a royal commission of inquiry (RCI) into the broader misconduct claims, prompting warnings from coalition partners and analysts that a weak response risked alienating ‚Äåvoters, deepening ‚ÅÝinternal rifts and threatening the government‚Äôs stability.
"If this issue is not contained or solved quickly, it will be a major issue until the next election," said Adib Zalkapli of political risks consultancy Viewfinder Global Affairs.
Malaysia's national elections are due by early 2028, but two government lawmakers said Anwar could call for snap polls as early as July.
One of them, a member of Anwar's People's Justice Party (PKR), said they and several others were considering contesting the election as independents, having lost confidence in the premier's leadership. The lawmakers declined to be ‚Äãidentified due to the sensitivity of the ‚Äãmatter.
Hassan Karim, another PKR lawmaker, said full-scale ‚ÅÝreforms were needed to address systemic corruption.
"The prime minister and his government must terminate the contract of the MACC chief immediately and promptly form the RCI," he said.
Anwar's premiership has been marked by internal disagreements among cabinet members, public discontent over rising living costs , ‚Äãand fears over a rollback in democratic freedoms.
While his administration has implemented some progressive reforms, such as abolishing the mandatory death penalty , ‚Äãothers have faltered.
The parliament ‚ÅÝthis week narrowly failed to pass a constitutional amendment that would limit the prime minister's term to 10 years, after several government-aligned lawmakers were absent for the vote.
Anwar's coalition was also trounced in a regional election in November, the first in several expected over the coming months that will test support for the premier ahead of national polls.
DAP's Pua described the November loss as a ‚ÅÝshock to ‚Äãthe party, spurring a renewed push among its leaders for speedier reforms.
The party, which currently holds 40 seats ‚Äãcompared with PKR's 31, plans to meet in July to decide whether its leaders should resign from all government positions, including the cabinet - a move that Pua said was necessary if promised reforms were not implemented.
"If ‚Äãwe don't withdraw, the party will lose all credibility and can expect a punishing outcome from the elections," Pua said.

item.title: Three Australians were aboard US submarine that sank Iranian ship, PM Albanese says
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 6 (Reuters) - Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Friday that three Australian defence personnel ‚Äãwere on board a United States submarine that ‚Äåsank an Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, but stressed they did not take part in the attack.
The ‚Äãstrike, which occured off the southern coast ‚Äãof Sri Lanka this week, marked the first ‚ÅÝtime since World War Two that the United States ‚Äãhas sunk an enemy vessel with a torpedo. Sri ‚ÄãLankan authorities recovered the bodies of 87 sailors.
(This story has been refiled to fix a typo in the headline)

item.title: Falling Ukrainian drone injures nine in Sevastopol in Russian-held Crimea, governor says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - A downed Ukrainian drone fell next ‚Äãto a five-storey apartment ‚Äåbuilding in the port of Sevastopol in Russian-held ‚ÄãCrimea, injuring nine ‚Äãpeople and causing considerable ‚ÅÝdamage, the Russia-appointed ‚Äãgovernor said early on ‚ÄãFriday.
Mikhail Razvozhayev said the drone was filled with metal ‚Äãpieces and explosives ‚Äãfell next to the building, badly damaging ‚Äåit.
Nine ‚ÅÝpeople were injured, including six requiring hospital treatment, three of ‚Äãthem children.
Sevastopol ‚Äãhosts ‚ÅÝthe Russian Black Sea Fleet ‚Äãin Crimea, annexed ‚Äãby ‚ÅÝRussia in 2014, though Western countries do ‚ÅÝnot ‚Äãrecognise Moscow's control ‚Äãover the peninsula.

item.title: Trump urges Iranian Kurds to attack Iran as war widens
item.description:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump encouraged Iranian Kurdish forces in Iraq to launch attacks against Iran as the Middle East conflict widened, with Azerbaijan warning it would retaliate for being targeted by Iranian missiles.
Israel on ‚ÄãFriday said it had started a "broad-scale" wave of attacks against infrastructure targets in Tehran, as Gulf cities came under renewed bombardment by Iran.
The seven-day war has now seen Iran ‚Äåtarget Israel, the Gulf states, Cyprus, Turkey and Azerbaijan, and spread to the Indian Ocean off Sri Lanka where a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian naval ship.
On the possibility of the Iranian Kurdish forces entering Iran, Trump told Reuters on Thursday: "I think it's wonderful that they want to do that, I'd be all for it."
Two Iranian drone attacks targeted an Iranian opposition camp in Iraqi Kurdistan on Thursday, security sources said.
Iranian Kurdish militias have consulted with the United States in recent days ‚Äãabout whether, and how, to attack Iran's security forces in the western part of the country, according to three sources with knowledge of the matter.
The Iranian Kurdish coalition of groups based ‚Äãon the Iran-Iraq border in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan has been training to mount such an attack in hopes of weakening the country's military, ‚ÅÝas the United States and Israel pound Iranian targets with bombs and missiles.
Trump, speaking with Reuters in a telephone interview, also said the United States must have a role in deciding who will be the next ‚Äãleader of Iran after airstrikes killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei last week.
"We're going to have to choose that person along with Iran. We're going to have to choose that person," he said.
U.S. Defense Secretary ‚ÄãPete Hegseth said on Thursday that the U.S. was not expanding its military objectives in Iran, despite what Trump said about choosing the country's next leader.
"There's no expansion in our objectives. We know exactly what we're trying to achieve," he said.
The attack on Iran is a major political gamble for the Republican president, with opinion polls showing little support and Americans concerned about the rise in gasoline prices caused by disruption to energy supplies. Trump dismissed that concern .
Shares on Wall Street fell on ‚ÄãThursday, weighed by surging oil prices, as the economic impact , opens new tab of the campaign intensified, with countries around the world cut off from a fifth of global supplies of oil and liquefied natural gas and air transport ‚Äãstill facing chaos and global logistics increasingly snarled .
Azerbaijan was preparing unspecified retaliatory measures on Thursday after it said four Iranian drones crossed its border and injured four people in the Nakhchivan exclave.
"We will not tolerate this unprovoked ‚Äåact of ‚ÅÝterror and aggression against Azerbaijan," President Ilham Aliyev told a meeting of his Security Council.
Iran, which has a significant Azeri minority, denied it targeted its neighbor.
Lebanon's Iran-backed Hezbollah militia warned Israeli residents to evacuate towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the border between the countries in a message posted on its Telegram channel in Hebrew early on Friday.
"Your military's aggression against Lebanese sovereignty and safe citizens, the destruction of civilian infrastructure and the expulsion campaign it is carrying out will not go unchallenged," Hezbollah said.
Hegseth and Admiral Brad Cooper, who leads U.S. forces in the Middle East, said during a briefing about operations that the U.S. ‚Äãhas enough munitions to continue its bombardment indefinitely.
"Iran ‚Äãis hoping that we cannot sustain this, which ‚ÅÝis a really bad miscalculation," Hegseth told reporters at Central Command headquarters in Florida. "Our munitions are full up and our will is ironclad."
The Pentagon earlier this week said the military campaign, known as Operation Epic Fury, is focused on destroying Iran's offensive missiles, missile production and navy, while not allowing Tehran to have ‚Äãa nuclear weapon.
Cooper said the U.S. had now hit at least 30 Iranian ships, including a large drone carrier that he said was the ‚Äãsize of a World War Two ‚ÅÝaircraft carrier.
He added that B-2 bombers had in the past few hours dropped dozens of 2,000 penetrator bombs targeting deeply buried ballistic missile launchers, and that bombings were also targeting Iran's missile production facilities.
Iran's ballistic missile attacks had decreased by 90% since the first day of the war, while drone attacks had decreased by 83% in that time frame, he said.
In Iran, at least 1,230 people have been killed, according to the Iranian Red ‚ÅÝCrescent Society, including ‚Äã175 schoolgirls and staff killed at a primary school in Minab in the country's south on the first day of the ‚Äãwar. Another 77 have been killed in Lebanon, its Health Ministry says. Thousands fled southern Beirut on Thursday after Israel warned residents to leave.

item.title: Nepal's early vote count puts ex-rapper's party ahead
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 6 (Reuters) - Rapper-turned-politician Balendra Shah's party gained leads in early counting in Nepal's general election , far outpacing his other rivals, including the country's former ‚Äãprime minister who was forced to resign after a historic ‚Äåyouth-led uprising last year.
Shah, 35, former mayor of the capital city of Kathmandu, has dominated the race to become Nepal's PM during the campaign, gaining popularity across the ‚Äãcountry.
Shah's centrist Rastriya Swatantra Party, which was founded three years ‚Äãago, was leading in 37 seats while former Prime Minister ‚ÅÝK P Sharma Oli's Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist-Leninist - UML) was ‚Äãleading in three, early counting trends from the election commission showed on ‚ÄãFriday morning.
The centrist Nepali Congress led by 49-year-old Gagan Thapa, the country's oldest party, was leading in five seats.
Results are likely to be clear by Friday evening or ‚ÄãSaturday, officials said.
Out of a total 275 seats in Nepal's lower ‚Äãhouse of parliament, counting was in progress for 165 seats that are elected ‚Äåby ‚ÅÝdirect election, while the remaining 110 will follow a system of proportional representation.
Shah drew large crowds during his election campaign, connecting with legions of young voters clamouring for change on the ground and online, even ‚Äãas he took ‚Äãon Oli, 74, ‚ÅÝon his home turf in the Jhapa 5 constituency along the Indian border.
Perched between China and India, ‚Äãthe country of 30 million people has been plagued ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝdecades by political instability, rippling a largely agrarian economy and worsening unemployment - structural issues compounded by rampant corruption.
The long-festering malaise erupted into street demonstrations last ‚ÅÝSeptember, ‚Äãtriggered by a social media ban, that ‚Äãbrought thousands on the streets, leading to clashes and fatalities that forced the resignation of ‚ÄãOli.

item.title: Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai will not appeal outcome in landmark security case
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Hong Kong media tycoon Jimmy Lai will not appeal against his conviction and 20-year jail term for collusion with ‚Äãforeign forces and sedition, his lawyer said on Friday, after a landmark case ‚Äåthat sparked international criticism.
The founder of the now-shuttered pro-democracy Apple Daily newspaper, Lai , 78, was one of the most outspoken critics of the ruling Chinese Communist Party.
A nearly five-year legal saga ended with his sentencing in ‚ÄãFebruary following a December conviction on two counts of conspiracy to collude with ‚Äãforeign forces and one count of publishing seditious materials.
A member of Lai's ‚ÅÝdomestic legal team told Reuters his client would not appeal the conviction and sentencing , which ‚Äãdrew criticism from countries such as Britain and the United States.
"We can confirm we have ‚Äãclear and definitive instructions not to lodge an appeal against conviction or sentence," he said, speaking on condition of anonymity, but gave no reason for the decision.
Separately, Fung Wai-kong, the former editor-in-chief of the ‚Äãpaper's English news section, launched an appeal on Monday against his 10-year jail term in ‚Äãthe same national security case.
Lai's son and daughter have warned he might die in prison, as his ‚Äåhealth ‚ÅÝis deteriorating after spending more than five years in solitary confinement. He has diabetes and health problems such as heart palpitations and high blood pressure.
Rights groups and numerous democratic countries have called for Lai's release.
U.S. President Donald Trump raised the matter with his Chinese counterpart, ‚ÄãXi Jinping, and is ‚Äãexpected to follow ‚ÅÝup in a closely anticipated visit to Beijing at the end of this month.
Hong Kong and Beijing, however, say Lai received a fair ‚Äãtrial and all are treated equally under the national security law ‚Äãthat has restored ‚ÅÝorder to the city since mass pro-democracy protests in 2019.
Last month, in a separate case, Hong Kong's Court of Appeal handed Lai a rare legal victory by overturning his conviction for fraud ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãquashing a 69-month sentence.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump on rising gas prices during Iran operation: 'If they rise, they rise'
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Thursday he was not concerned about rising U.S. gas prices driven by the widening Iran conflict , telling Reuters ‚Äãin an exclusive interview that the U.S. military operation was his priority.
"I don't have any concern about it," he said, when asked about the higher prices at ‚Äåthe pump. "They'll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit."
The comments mark a shift in tone for the president, who touted a drop in gas prices in his State of the Union address last month and at a Texas rally focused on energy that took place just hours before the U.S. launched its air strikes ‚Äãon Saturday.
Political analysts say a persistent rise in gas prices could hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of the U.S. Congress will be at stake. Voters ‚Äãare already unhappy about the high cost of living and Trump's stewardship of the economy.
Despite Trump's public efforts to play down the price rises, White House Chief ‚ÅÝof Staff Susie Wiles and Energy Secretary Chris Wright have both engaged with oil CEOs to gauge possible options on combating rising energy prices, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said ‚Äãon Thursday.
Another White House official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said there was a scramble across the White House energy and national security teams to develop measures aimed at bringing down gas prices.
The ‚Äãofficial said Wiles had warned in White House meetings that failure to act on price rises would be "catastrophic" for Republicans in the elections.
US weighs oil futures market action to combat price spikes, White House official says
Trump has outlined a four-to-five-week timeline for the military campaign against Iran, but political and military experts have questioned it, noting that the U.S. government has yet to articulate its end goal while the conflict continues to spread to the region and beyond.
In the interview, Trump said he was not looking to tap the Strategic ‚ÄãPetroleum Reserve , the largest emergency crude stockpile in the world, and that he was confident the Strait of Hormuz, the critical channel for oil shipping near Iran, will remain open because Iran's navy ‚Äãis at the "bottom of the sea."
Global oil prices have jumped 16% since the war started on Saturday, as the spreading conflict disrupted Middle East supplies.
The national average cost of gas has risen 27 cents since last week ‚Äåto $3.25 per ‚ÅÝgallon, according to AAA, a U.S. travel organization that tracks fuel prices. The current national average is 15 cents higher than a year ago.
Trump said the costs "haven't risen very much."
The White House is betting the conflict with Iran - and the resulting pain at the gas pump - will prove short-lived.
White House energy advisers have told Trump aides the initial sticker shock in fuel markets has been less severe than many feared and have urged patience, according to two people who were granted anonymity to describe internal deliberations.
The advisers warned that any intervention by the Trump administration ‚Äãthat fails to quickly bring down prices could ‚Äãrattle markets and prove counterproductive.
Secretary of State ‚ÅÝMarco Rubio said early this week that the administration was rolling out a package of measures to combat rising energy prices, but the only plan unveiled so far is U.S.-backed risk insurance for oil tankers and the promise of potential naval escorts through the Strait of Hormuz.
Three energy executives ‚Äãtold Reuters the White House has few great options to push down energy prices.
"When you look across the menu of policy options, domestically ‚Äãor within other countries, they ‚ÅÝcan be helpful, but they don't move the needle far," one energy executive said, speaking on condition of anonymity so they could speak candidly about the administration's policies. "I think the primary focus is ... to do whatever they can to restore transits through the Strait of Hormuz itself."
Has the Iran war changed the Gulf forever?
Officials are also discussing a wide array of other options, including a federal gasoline tax holiday and loosening environmental regulations around summer gasoline ‚ÅÝthat will allow ‚Äãhigher blends of ethanol, according to the two sources familiar with the internal deliberations.
Officials were also weighing a potential ‚Äãrelease from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, the sources said, but the president ruled out that option - at least for now - in his comments to Reuters.
Congressional Republican leaders, like House Speaker Mike Johnson , have also dismissed concerns about rising gas prices, even ‚Äãas the party plans to focus its midterm election strategy on economic successes.

item.title: Gulf carriers resume limited flights, but missile fire fuels uncertainty
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 6 (Reuters) - Emirates and Etihad Airways were resuming limited flight schedules to key global cities from their United Arab Emirates ‚Äãhubs on Friday, though the ongoing threat of missile fire piled pressure on airlines as they scramble to accommodate travellers.
With most airspace in ‚Äåthe Middle East still closed over missile and drone concerns since the start of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran , authorities have been arranging charter flights and securing seats on limited commercial services to evacuate tens of thousands of people.
A government-chartered Air France (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab flight to bring French nationals back from the United Arab Emirates was forced to turn back on Thursday due to missile fire in the ‚Äãarea, French Transport Minister Philippe Tabarot said.
"This situation reflects the instability in the region and the complexity of repatriation operations," he said.
Etihad said on Friday ‚Äãit would resume a limited flight schedule through March 19. The flights will operate to and from Abu Dhabi and 25 destinations ‚ÅÝincluding London, Paris, Frankfurt, Delhi, New York and Toronto.
As of Thursday, traffic at Dubai airport, normally the world's busiest, had almost doubled from Wednesday, but remained only about ‚Äã25% of normal levels, flight-tracking website Flightradar24 said.
Dubai-based Emirates, one of the UAE's two flag carriers, said late on Thursday it was operating a reduced flight schedule to ‚Äã82 destinations including London, Sydney, Singapore and New York until further notice, and customers transiting in Dubai would only be accepted if their connecting flight was operating.
The limited operations at Middle Eastern hubs have hit travellers on routes from Europe to the Asia-Pacific region particularly hard.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways normally fly about one-third of passengers from Europe to Asia and more ‚Äãthan half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific Islands, according to Cirium data.
Qatar's Doha hub remains shut, though it has been arranging ‚Äãa limited number of relief flights from Oman and Saudi Arabia.
Data from Cirium showed that from February 28 - when the conflict started - to March 5, there were more than 44,000 flights ‚Äåscheduled in ‚ÅÝand out of the Middle East, with more than 25,000 flights cancelled so far.
Higher oil prices have sent jet fuel costs soaring, with Singapore jet fuel reaching a record high of $225 a barrel this week, which traders attributed to concerns about supply shortages from Middle Eastern refiners.
The price eased slightly on Thursday to about $195 a barrel after some profit-taking but remained nearly double that of last week.
Shares of Qantas Airways (QAN.AX) , opens new tab fell more than 3% on Friday, Air New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab was down ‚Äãnearly 7%, Hong Kong's flagship carrier Cathay ‚ÄãPacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab dropped more than 2%, while ‚ÅÝSingapore Airlines (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab was down over 1%.
The Hong Kong-listed shares of major Chinese carriers including Air China , China Eastern Airlines , and China Southern Airlines were down between 2% and 4%.
Passengers have been forking out huge sums of ‚Äãmoney to get out of the Middle East, with some who managed to travel back by commercial flight on Thursday ‚Äãfrom Oman saying it ‚ÅÝhad been "absolute chaos" to find their way back home from Dubai.
"We paid 1,500 pounds ($2,005.05) to get across to Muscat (Oman) to get on the plane," said Ed Short after he arrived at London's Heathrow Airport on a British Airways flight.
"We'd spent about 20,000 pounds booking Emirates flight instead. So we're hoping we get those back."
With the conflict showing little sign of ‚ÅÝeasing, wider ‚Äãaviation and air-cargo disruption looked set to linger.
Saudi budget carrier flynas will run a limited number of flights ‚Äãbetween Saudi Arabia and Dubai starting on Friday.
($1 = 0.7481 pounds)

item.title: Oil spill disrupts livelihoods in Mexico's Veracruz state
item.description:
JICACAL, Mexico, March 5 (Reuters) - Coastal residents in the Mexican state of Veracruz are concerned about the recent appearance of oil on local beaches, which has ‚Äãdisrupted their livelihoods, fearing it could contaminate a nearby lagoon where ‚Äåcommunities farm fish and shrimp.
Residents of Jicacal, on the Gulf of Mexico, have organized themselves to remove as much of the hardened tar as possible from the beaches, while the viscous ‚Äãspill spreads along the coast. Authorities have not told residents what caused ‚Äãthe spill.
Veracruz is heavy on oil activity in Mexico, both on ‚ÅÝland and at sea. State oil company Pemex ruled out any leak or ‚Äãspill from its facilities this week.
‚ÄúAs usual, we went out fishing and cast ‚Äãour nets, and we realized that the net was full of oil,‚Äù said Aurora Apolonia Martinez, describing how she noticed the presence of the spilled crude, which rendered the fishing nets unusable.
Pemex ‚Äãdid not immediately respond to a request for comment about whether it had ‚Äãnew information about the spill following the surveillance efforts it said it would carry out ‚Äåin the ‚ÅÝarea.
Cemda, an environmental organization, said this week that since March 1, over a dozen spill sites have been identified along the beaches of Veracruz and neighboring Tabasco along the coast of the Gulf of Mexico.
Pemex has denied responsibility for the ‚Äãspill. But the company ‚Äãhas been involved ‚ÅÝin several such incidents in recent years. The most recent was in October in Veracruz, where 2.7 million liters were ‚Äãrecovered from the Pantepec River following a leak in an ‚Äãoil pipeline.
Both ‚ÅÝJicacal and the neighboring town of Las Barrillas, also in Veracruz, are fishing villages and local tourist destinations. The spill has also impacted the local tourism industry, discouraging ‚ÅÝvisits to ‚Äãthe coast.
‚ÄúWhat we are interested in is cleaning ‚Äãup the entire beach,‚Äù said Nicol√°s Vargas, a tourism service provider in Las Barrillas, who said that ‚Äãotherwise, tourists will not visit the area.

item.title: Taiwan defence minister rejects opposition's cut‚Äëprice budget and U.S. arms deadline
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 6 (Reuters) - Taiwan's Defence Minister Wellington Koo on Friday rebuffed the main opposition party, which ‚Äåhad proposed a defence budget roughly one-third of the amount sought by the government and set a deadline for U.S. arms purchases that he said was impossible.
Taiwan has faced pressure from the United States to sharply raise defence spending. ‚ÄãTaiwan President Lai Ching-te last year proposed extra defence spending of $40 billion to counter China, which ‚Äãhas ramped up military pressure to force the island to accept its claim ‚ÅÝof sovereignty.
But the main opposition Kuomintang, which together with a smaller party holds a majority in ‚Äãparliament, refused to review the proposal and instead this week advanced its own, less expensive proposals, which ‚Äãonly fund around 30% of the spending that Lai wants.
The move came after growing pressure from politicians in the United States, Taiwan's most important international backer and arms supplier, to not hold up spending.
In the KMT's counter-proposal, a copy of ‚Äãwhich was reviewed by Reuters, the party set a cap of T$380 billion ($11.96 billion) on the spending ‚Äãand a deadline for completion by the end of 2028.
Talking to reporters in Taipei, Koo said the government's proposal ‚Äåincluded ‚ÅÝprecision artillery and anti-armour unmanned systems.
"If everything is required to be delivered and fully implemented before that deadline, it would in effect shut down these projects, making their execution impossible," he said.
The KMT also said it backs U.S. arms deals handled between the two governments and opposes deals arranged through commercial ‚Äãchannels, which it views as ‚Äãvulnerable to irregularities ‚ÅÝand inadequate scrutiny.
In response, Koo said omitting any channel for acquiring weapons would "create a major gap in our overall defence and operational capabilities and significantly undermine ‚Äãthe improvement of our joint combat capabilities."
He added the government's proposal has ‚Äãthe backing ‚ÅÝof the U.S administration and congress.
The KMT has described Lai's plan as "sky‚Äëhigh" and vague, citing the need for clearer oversight.
KMT chairperson Cheng Li-wun has said her party has been in communication with the Chinese Communist Party ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãthat she hopes to visit China this year for a meeting ‚Äãwith Chinese President Xi Jinping.
China regularly stages military exercises around Taiwan, and refuses to talk to Lai, calling him a "separatist". Lai says ‚Äãonly Taiwan's people can decide their future.
($1 = 31.7700 Taiwan dollars)

item.title: Cuba confirms fifth casualty in speedboat attack
item.description:
HAVANA, March 5 (Reuters) - A fifth casualty from ‚Äãlast week's speedboat ‚Äãattack on Cuba has ‚ÅÝbeen confirmed, the ‚ÄãCuban interior ministry ‚Äãsaid on Thursday night.
Roberto Alvarez Avila died on ‚ÄãMarch 4 in ‚Äãthe hospital, the Cuban authorities ‚Äåsaid, ‚ÅÝwhere he was being held after being injured in ‚Äãan ‚Äãincident ‚ÅÝin which Cuban forces killed ‚Äãfour Cuban ‚Äãnationals ‚ÅÝand wounded six others aboard a speedboat ‚ÅÝthat ‚Äãentered Cuban ‚Äãwaters.
(This story has been corrected to fix the name to Alvarez Avila, not Avila Alvares, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Trading Day: War fuels stagflation fears
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 5 (Reuters) - Stocks sank on Thursday and another surge in oil prices sent bond yields shooting higher, as the spiraling conflict across the Middle East raised investor fears over energy supplies , higher inflation and slower growth.
More on that below. In my column today I offer a reminder that, amid the fog of ‚Äãwar, economic fundamentals can't be forgotten completely. Friday's U.S. payrolls data, and any hint of AI's impact on jobs, will avert eyes from the ‚ÄåMiddle East, at least temporarily.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Learning the "transitory" lesson
As energy prices soar, markets are betting that central bankers won't repeat the 2021-2022 playbook of looking through supply shocks and a subsequent surge in "transitory" inflation. They've learned that lesson , right?
That appears to be traders' bet - only ‚Äãone Fed rate cut this year is baked in now, and that's not until October; another BoE cut isn't fully priced at all; the ECB is more likely to ‚Äãhike than cut; and the RBA could even hike again this month.
* No room for complacency
There was a glimmer of hope on Wednesday that back-channel U.S.-Iran diplomacy might pave the ‚Äåway for ‚ÅÝpeace to break out in the Middle East. Traders seized upon it, bought back beaten-down stocks, and Europe and Wall Street rallied.
Predictions are dangerous at the best of times, but that looks like a false dawn. The war is spreading, messy, and getting more entrenched. Energy prices and bond yields are spiking, and risk assets are feeling the heat. Yet the Nasdaq is flat on the week. Justified calm, or complacency?
* A job lot
With investors' focus firmly on the market implications of events ‚Äãin the Middle East, economic fundamentals are ‚Äãunderstandably taking a back seat. They ‚ÅÝshould be a driving force, however, at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time on Friday, when the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics releases the February jobs data.
A strong report will let Fed officials breathe more easily, while signs of cracks in the labor ‚Äãmarket will be tricky to navigate - yields are spiking on the energy supply shock, yet the curve is the ‚Äãflattest this year. Stagflation ‚ÅÝon the horizon?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views ‚Äãof Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Lloyd Blankfein memoir dispels Goldman's mystique
item.description:
LONDON, March 5 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ‚ÄúIts mystery is its life. We must not let in daylight upon magic.‚Äù Walter Bagehot‚Äôs famous prescription for the British monarchy , opens new tab could have been written for Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab . For many years the Wall Street investment bank hid its inner workings, its clout in boardrooms and government departments, and its partners‚Äô wealth behind a thick veil of discretion. Under Lloyd Blankfein‚Äôs leadership it reluctantly became more transparent. His new memoir blows ‚Äãaway any remaining mystique.
Blankfein, who was chairman and CEO from 2006 to 2018, admits he is an outlier. A working-class kid from Brooklyn, his initial application to the firm was rejected. The Harvard alumnus eventually arrived at ‚ÄåGoldman through the back door in 1982 by joining J. Aron, a commodity trading outfit the investment bank had acquired the previous year.
Self-deprecating humour and frequent references to his humble background set Blankfein apart from the intense, highly competitive types who tend to populate Goldman‚Äôs upper ranks. While previous leaders like Robert Rubin and Hank Paulson moved smoothly into the role of Treasury Secretary in the administrations of Presidents Bill Clinton and George W. Bush, respectively, Blankfein has shown little interest in higher office.
This lack of pretension helps make ‚ÄúStreetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman Sachs‚Äù highly readable. Blankfein recounts his upbringing and career with amusing anecdotes ‚Äãas well as honest and at times brutal assessments of his fellow executives. His initial evaluation of Paulson, who preceded him as CEO, is typical: ‚Äúa driven and highly capable but often inarticulate Christian Scientist who drank milk and loved bird-watching.‚Äù He ‚Äãquotes a former colleague describing John Thain and John Thornton, the bank‚Äôs co-chief operating officers and rivals for the top job in the early 2000s, as acting like ‚Äúthe owner‚Äôs sons‚Äù.
Blankfein‚Äôs career spanned Goldman‚Äôs ‚ÅÝtransformation from a Wall Street private partnership to a publicly traded global investment bank. In 1998 it earned revenue of $8.5 billion. Last year it brought in seven times that sum. Since listing on the stock market in May 1999, Goldman shares have risen more than 1,000%, ‚Äãfar outpacing other big U.S. banks.
That transformation owed much to the expansion of financial activity and the breakneck growth of markets around the world after 1999. It also reflected a deliberate transition as Goldman ‚Äì and its rivals ‚Äì moved from acting as an adviser and broker to ‚Äãalso trading and investing more aggressively on its own account.
Blankfein exemplified the shift to a more aggressive risk-taking mentality. The change is often attributed to the J. Aron acquisition. Yet ‚ÄúStreetwise‚Äù makes clear the commodity broker was a risk-averse firm which only started making big bets after Goldman absorbed it. Notable trades included an audacious scheme conceived by Gary Cohn ‚Äì later President Donald Trump‚Äôs chief economic adviser ‚Äì to snap up cheap aluminium due to a Russian production glut and stockpile it in a Rotterdam warehouse until prices recovered.
These proprietary bets often clashed with the interests of Goldman clients, who expected bankers to provide them with unbiased ‚Äãadvice and traders to secure the best price for securities. Blankfein is unapologetic about those tensions, arguing that wearing multiple hats allowed Goldman to act like economist Adam Smith‚Äôs famous invisible hand. ‚ÄúWe weren‚Äôt just serving our clients better. We were playing a primary role in ‚Äãthe allocation of capital, to the benefit of the economy and society.‚Äù Many clients had a less charitable interpretation.
The defining period of Blankfein‚Äôs tenure was the global financial crisis that kicked off in 2007. His nose for risk and attention to detail served Goldman well as financial markets melted ‚Äådown. He recalls ‚ÅÝspotting early signs of liquidity drying up in mid-2007 while reading emails on his BlackBerry in the movie theatre.
Unlike many rivals, Goldman decided to hedge its exposure to U.S. subprime housing debt, in part by buying protection from American International Group (AIG.N) , opens new tab against defaults in mortgage-backed securities. When the U.S. government ‚Äì with Paulson as Treasury Secretary ‚Äì bailed out the insurance giant in September 2008, many on Wall Street suspected the rescue had indirectly saved Goldman. Blankfein insists that the firm, which had also taken the precaution of buying insurance against an AIG default, would have survived its counterparty‚Äôs collapse. Still, whether the banks that sold that protection could have honoured their obligations in such a meltdown remains an open question.
Yet if Blankfein nimbly guided Goldman through the storm, he stumbled in the aftermath. Intense public scrutiny and criticism from politicians came ‚Äãas a shock for a firm unused to being a household ‚Äãname. Blankfein offers a spirited defence against Goldman‚Äôs many critics. ‚ÅÝYet he acknowledges that bailouts helped polarise public opinion, clearing the way for Trump.
A more damaging accusation is that Blankfein was slow to recognise deeper changes in financial markets. Tighter regulation meant fewer bets using the firm‚Äôs own balance sheet, while extra capital requirements made Goldman‚Äôs bond-trading operation less profitable. Arch-rival Morgan Stanley (MS.N) , opens new tab , which barely survived 2008, made an early decision under post-crisis CEO James Gorman to double ‚Äãdown on its wealth management business instead. Its stock has comfortably outperformed Goldman‚Äôs over the past decade.
The final years of Blankfein‚Äôs tenure were marked by some damaging scandals, most notably the bank's ‚Äãwork for 1MDB. The Malaysian sovereign fund ‚ÅÝpaid Goldman suspiciously high fees for bond issues in 2012 and 2013 and then siphoned off the proceeds in an audacious fraud. The only plausible explanations are that Goldman‚Äôs fabled risk management systems failed, or that it turned a blind eye in return for a big payday. Neither reflects well on the firm. Blankfein offers the tepid excuse that Goldman‚Äôs size made it hard to be on top of everything, and that he expected the committees that vetted the transactions to make sure they were above board. The saga ended up costing Goldman more than $3 billion in regulatory ‚ÅÝfines .
Since replacing Blankfein ‚Äãin 2018, current Goldman boss David Solomon has focused on making the bank's earnings less volatile. The swashbuckling pursuit of lucrative trades has given way to more ‚Äãstable, technology-enabled operations like managing cash for corporations. Towards the end of the book, Blankfein observes that Goldman has become ‚Äúa little less special‚Äù. By choosing to shine a bright and uncompromising light on the firm‚Äôs inner workings, the bank‚Äôs former boss has done his bit to dispel the magic.
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: How South Korea's largest and deadliest wildfire spread
item.description:
Spread by strong winds in bone-dry conditions, the wildfires have killed scores of people and charred thousands of hectares in the southeastern part of the country.
While wildfires are common in South Korea at this time of year, the rapid spread in late March was unprecedented.
What started as two fires near Uiseong town over March 22 to 24, soon spread overnight leaving a trail of death and destruction.
On March 25, three bodies were found near a burnt-out car trying to escape the fire in Yeongyang, and a woman in her 60s was found burnt on a road in Cheongsong. Parts of Gounsa temple, a heritage site first built in 681, was also burned to the ground that day.
A day later, a pilot was killed after his firefighting helicopter crashed near a mountain in Uiseong county on March 26.¬ÝA monk at a Buddhist temple in Yeongyang was also discovered dead on the same day.
In the aftermath, more bodies were found. On March 27, a 90-year-old woman was found dead at her house in Cheongsong.
Days later, the wildfire was contained but the damage was done, with scores dead and thousands of hectares burned.
The wildfires in North Gyeongsang province began in Uiseong county before spreading 70 km (44 miles) east, to devastate an area equivalent to about two-thirds of the island of Singapore and much larger than the Los Angeles fires in January.
‚ÄúStrong, dry winds blew from the west. The wind had the biggest impact,‚Äù said Lim Sang-seop, the minister of the Korea Forest Service, adding that smoke and fog reduced visibility this week, presenting a bigger challenge to helicopters seeking to douse the flames.
Experts have said the spread of the Uiseong fire was extremely unusual in terms of scale and speed, while climate change is expected to make wildfires more frequent and deadly globally.
The wildfires have displaced more than 30,000, many of them elderly, as is typical in rural areas across fast-ageing South Korea. The flames consumed everything in their path, from historic temples¬Ýto homes , in mountainous North Gyeongsang.
Charred, black ruins at South Korea's Gounsa temple stood on Thursday in stark contrast to the kaleidoscope of colours that are a trademark of the country's historic palaces and Buddhist temples.
A massive, ornate bronze bell sat in the ruins of its tower, cracked from top to bottom. Two out of the three national treasures at the site burned, while a handful of other structures escaped the blaze.
A man takes a photograph of a damaged temple bell lying amidst debris at Gounsa temple. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
A damaged temple bell lies amidst debris at Gounsa temple. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
Firefighters work at the Gounsa temple on March 27, 2025. REUTERS/Kim Hong-Ji
Gounsa was one of at least 18 designated heritage sites or objects, including two national treasures, that had been destroyed or damaged as of Thursday, according to the Korea Heritage Service.
At the picturesque Hahoe Folk Village, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, authorities deployed retardant and other measures in a desperate attempt to save its thatch-roof buildings nestled on a bend in the Nakdong River.
While residents were urged to evacuate, as of Thursday the village had survived.
Lee Byung-doo, a forest disaster expert at the National Institute of Forest Science said on March 25 that the Uiseong fire was exacerbated by gusty winds and showed ‚Äúunimaginable scale and speed‚Äù
Even the Gounsa temple chief Deungwoon told Reuters that ‚Äúthere was a wind stronger than a typhoon, and flames whipped through the air like a tornado, burning the whole area in an instant.‚Äù
Embers
Spotting
Wind can carry burning embers and sparks downwind of fires, increasing the rate at which fires can spread.
An illustration explaining how wind can influence and speed up spread of fire.
Terrain can also play a part in it‚Äôs spread.
Rob Rogers, the New South Wales Rural Service commissioner said ‚Äúwhere there‚Äôs mountains, they‚Äôll obviously accelerate the speed of the fire when it‚Äôs going up the mountains, but also they can change. Mountain ranges can change the direction of the wind‚Äù
Topography
The angle of slope can also affect fire behaviour, especially if coupled with wind.
Wind
Indraft
Convection
Radiation
pre-heats fuels
Downward slope
Upward slope
Usually slower to burn. However, compressed air and down-slope winds may still contribute to wildfire spread.
Fires burn faster uphill than on level ground as energy is easily transferred to un-burnt fuel.
Less energy
transferred to fuels
An illustration explaining the role of topography on fire spread.
The rapid spread of the fire on March 25, 2025 was also caught via satellite imagery. It shows the winds pushing clouds from west to east, and a flash of red lights up that area overnight.
Satellite imagery from March 25 to March 26
The fire burned about 48,000 hectares (119,000 acres), destroyed an estimated 4,000 structures, and forced tens of thousands of people to evacuate.
A forest fire that broke out in Uljin in March 2022 is the most recent major incident to occur in South Korea. It burned over 17,000 hectares of land according to The Korea Times.
The 10-year average from 2015 - 2024 was 4,003 hectares burned annually. 2022 saw the most number of hectares burned at 24,797 while 2024 saw the lowest at 132 hectares.
While wildfires are common across South Korea, the March fires burned more than the combined hectares of land burned between 2015 and 2024.
2025

Pohang

Gwangju
More detailed surface area data is available from NASA Fire Information for Resource Managment System (FIRMS) showing how this compares to the past 10 years.

Small multiple maps of South Korea showing the burned areas as determined by satellite image analysis from NASA.
Note: The map for March 2025 shows active fire hotspots only. The previous yearly maps show burned areas.
Source: Korea Forest Service, NASA
Authorities believe the possible cause for the March wildfire is a man, in his 50s, who began the fire in southeastern Uiseong County when he performed an ancestral rite by a family grave on March 22, an official from Gyeongbuk Provincial Police said.
He was booked on Sunday, March 30 and evidence was still being processed according to officials. Yonhap news agency said the man had denied the allegations.
Sources
Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS); Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM); Sentinel-2; Global Land Analysis and Discover team at the University of Maryland (UMD GLAD); OpenStreetMap; Cooperative Institute for Research in the Atmosphere at Colorado State University (CSU/CIRA); Japan Meteorological Agency (JMA); Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA)
Additional reporting by
Heejung Jung and Vijdan Mohammad Kawoosa
Edited by
Rebecca Pazos, Simon Scarr and Raju Gopalakrishnan

item.title: Gaza‚Äôs garbage crisis
item.description:
After 15 months of war, Gaza is suffering from a destroyed waste system. As the cleanup begins, the impacts on its people‚Äôs health and land will be long felt.
More than a year of conflict has crippled the already limited waste collection infrastructure in Gaza. With access to designated landfills blocked and no transport due to a fuel blockade imposed by Israel, hundreds of thousands of tons of solid waste has piled up in the streets.
Even before the war, Gaza faced severe waste management challenges, generating 1,700 tons of waste daily, with only three operational but overcrowded landfills. One of the sites, Johr Edeek, operated beyond capacity, with waste piling up to 35 meters high, according to a report from the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP).
However, despite the strains, a structure was in place, with towns and cities sending waste to the three landfill sites.
Limited resources caused frequent fires and environmental risks. From 2017 to 2022, the number of waste collection vehicles dropped to 73 from 112, with ageing equipment struggling to meet the needs of the growing population. The war has worsened the crisis, destroying waste collection vehicles, facilities, and medical waste treatment centers, leaving municipalities overwhelmed.
The solid waste removal network previously relied on three main landfills, all closed during the latest conflict.
Deir Al Balah
Khan Yunis
Small cities
or towns
Refugee camps
Rafah
Jabalia
Gaza City
Beit Hanoun
Main cities
Beit Lahiya
Three operational
landfills
The distribution of Gaza‚Äôs population has also changed dramatically, with over 60% of buildings likely damaged or destroyed, forcing many to relocate closer to humanitarian zones. The closure of the three main landfills by the Israel Defense Forces has also forced local authorities to find temporary dumpsites.
At least 225 temporary waste dumps were operating across the Gaza strip, as of May 2024.
Humanitarian Zone
Deir Al Balah
Khan Yunis
UN designated
waste dump
Conflict linked
waste dump
Rafah
Jabalia
Beit Hanoun
Gaza City
Beit Lahiya
Closed
landfills
Currently operational
landfill
These waste dumps were identified through an analysis of open-source information including satellite imagery, social media posts and official reports. It is suspected that many more such dumps could exist.
The silent threat of accumulated and improperly handled waste is made worse in a conflict zone that is also plagued by high temperatures, destroyed sanitary infrastructure and a population weakened by food scarcity and lack of medical attention.
The United Nations has repeatedly warned of the public health and environmental crisis arising from widespread pollution caused by solid waste in Gaza.
A key issue is the leachate, a polluted liquid that results when water filters through layers of accumulated waste in improperly managed garbage piles, posing a risk when it infiltrates soil and contaminates groundwater.
Single-use plastics
Glass
Contaminated ground water
The risk of pollution of the
groundwater by the leachate from
landfill sites is considered to be the
most significant risk for the natural
environment and human health.
External water dependence
Due to the contamination of natural
water sources, the population becomes
overly reliant on external water sources.
Contaminated grazing lands
Heavy metals and toxins can harm
animals grazing in and around areas
causing health problems and reduced
fertility in animals.
Disruption of aquatic systems
Leachate can enter in aquatic body
through surface runoff, can be ingested
and accumulated in different tissues of
fish, and lead to perturbations in fish
biological systems.
Reduced crop yields
Heavy metals and acidic compounds
reduce crop yields and the viability of
agriculture land.
Contamination of food crops
Consuming contaminated crops can
cause chronic health issues, such as
neurological and developmental
damage in children, reproductive
issues and cancer.
Types of waste
Biodegradable waste such as paper and textiles can attract pests, emit methane, and cause respiratory illnesses if burned.
Non-biodegradable waste like single-use plastics can break down into microplastics and be inhaled or ingested.
Hazardous and toxic waste like batteries, metals and chemicals can leach toxins, bioaccumulate and increase cancer risk.
Plastic bottles and containers
Paper and cardboard
Attracting pests
Houseflies, mosquitoes, rats, cockroaches, and fleas spread diseases like malaria, typhoid, and cholera. They contaminate food, water, and air, trigger allergies, and thrive in waste, posing serious health risks.
Houseflies
Scrap metal

Leachate seeps from landfill, or trash piles, and is made up of various chemical substances.
Batteries and household chemical waste

Ground water level
Root system damage
Roots absorb water mixed with
toxins and heavy metals which can
accumulate in plant tissue.
Bioaccumulation of pollutants
Pollutants can bioaccumulate in the
meat and milk of animals, which adds
to the human risk while consuming.
Soil degradation
Leachate contamination depletes
soil fertility and disrupt nutrient
availability.
While cleanup efforts have been attempted at various points throughout the war, recent UNDP research warned that most of these efforts are localised in Gaza‚Äôs south, with only 600 to 700 tons of waste collected daily. This barely covers the estimated 2,000 tons of waste generated every day.
Most of Gaza‚Äôs displaced people ‚Äî communities sheltering in refugee camps or makeshift areas ‚Äî have been forced to live near the piles of rotting garbage. No early relief for their plight is in sight, despite a ceasefire signed by Israel and Hamas in January.
‚ÄúWe can‚Äôt sleep, we can‚Äôt eat, we can‚Äôt drink. The smell is killing us,‚Äù said Ahmed Shaloula, originally from Gaza City but now one of many displaced Palestinians living in Khan Younis.
Gaza‚Äôs oldest educational institution, Al-Aqsa University was first established in 1955, with its first campus in Gaza City. Its second campus, located in Khan Younis, was built in the past decade. It‚Äôs this campus that has now become the home of many displaced people, with the campus‚Äô surroundings converted into a makeshift waste field.
The waste dump stretches over 300 metres and is estimated to cover nearly 10,000 square metres.
Before - October 20, 2023
After - October 10, 2024

dump

space
Satellite images: Planet Labs
A landfill in a public area in Central Gaza, shown in the video below, has become part of the grim reality for many Palestinians residing nearby.
Abou Mohammed, a Palestinian, who lives about 100-150 meters away from the landfill, says he is worried about the health of his children. ‚ÄúThis waste here has brought diseases. Our children are suffering from various ailments that we have never seen before,‚Äù he said.
The video shows a landfill in Central Gaza in May 2024.
The video shows a landfill in Central Gaza in May 2024. The two photos are taken at other dump sites in Gaza. REUTERS
Youssef Hammad, a municipality worker, also explained that the area was not originally designated for waste disposal. However, the conflict has given them little choice but to dump the waste there.
‚ÄúThe municipality was compelled to urgently dispose of waste in this location due to the inability of municipal crews to access the official dumping grounds ... With the onset of the war, we were unable to reach these locations due to the lack of mechanisms of movement (for access) caused by road blockages,‚Äù said Hammad.
Hammad also warned of the environmental hazard associated with such landfills. ‚ÄúThese include stray dogs, the proliferation of insects, as well as environmental pollution and the degradation of the surrounding environment in this area,‚Äù he said.
Abu Mazen Khatab, a displaced man living near the waste dump with his family, including six-month-old daughter, says he worries sewage water will soon seep into his tent. ‚ÄúIf we don't die from the missiles; we will die from the sewage and the bad smells here and the waste.‚Äù
Sources
United Nations, Planet Labs
Edited by
Simon Scarr, Rebecca Pazos and William Maclean

item.title: LA's fast fires show a larger trend in fire speed
item.description:
Fires are growing faster in the western United States. The fastest of them ‚Äì termed ‚Äúfast fires‚Äù ‚Äì often erupt near towns and account for most structural wildfire damage according to researchers who analyzed over 60,000 fires from 2001 to 2020.
Fast fires grow about 4,000 acres or more on their fastest day, at least two football fields a minute. That single-day growth threshold for a ‚Äúfast fire‚Äù is even higher in California at almost four football fields a minute.
Humans and our infrastructure start nearly all of the fires that threaten our homes. Exactly why fires become fast is a developing area of research, but weather and fuel conditions can feed the speed.
Of the 20 fastest fires between 2001 and 2020, 16 destroyed structures and all but one fire came within 2.5 miles of a property.
The fastest LA fires and overall top 20 fastest-growing fires across the continental US from 2001 to 2020. A structure is considered exposed when it is within 2.5 miles of the fire perimeter even if it was not damaged.

Small multiple maps of the 20 fastest-growing fires in the continental US, the Palisades fire and Eaton fire. Each map shows the fire perimeter, the year the fire took place, the state the fire burned in, and the settlement area around the fire.
Although many fast fires start near populated areas, the location alone doesn't determine whether a fire will be fast.
‚ÄúProbably hundreds, maybe thousands of ignitions happen around the Hollywood Hills every year. Most of those fires are able to be put out,‚Äù said Maxwell Cook, a research assistant with the Cooperative Institute for Research in Environmental Sciences and geography PhD candidate at the University of Colorado Boulder.
A wildfire not only needs the initial spark but also the optimal fuel and weather. When an ignition overlaps with poor conditions like heat, drought and wind, fires can spread faster than firefighters can contain them.
In the case of the fires in Los Angeles, a three-month drought parched the vegetation and buildings, making for piles of kindling. The seasonal high-speed winds blew embers to nearby fuel and made the pace of fire growth impossible to match.
Remnants of a house structure burning from the Eaton Fire in Pasadena, California January 7, 2025. REUTERS
‚ÄúWhen fires are moving that fast, often times there‚Äôs not much to do in terms of stopping the fire. It really becomes a situation of getting people out,‚Äù Cook said.
Researchers have observed an increase in human-started fires and expect that pattern to continue as the population grows and more people move into wild areas. The conditions that lead to faster fires are also expected to worsen as the West becomes hotter and drier in line with climate projections.

A scatterplot of square markers representing fast-moving fires in California from 2001 to 2020. The size of each square reflects the number of structures damaged by each fire. The plot shows an increasing trend over time, with more fires causing greater structural damage.
Note: Data shows fast fires (maximum growth rate of at least 7,092 acres in a day) in California between 2001-2020.
The type of vegetation in an area can determine how quickly a wildfire spreads. The majority of fast fires burn through grasslands where the short, dry grass is easily ignitable. Broadleaf forests tend to burn more slowly in part due to higher moisture levels in the vegetation. The fires in LA were different, spreading by structure-to-structure ignition.
‚ÄúWhen a house burns, it produces several million embers and flames up to 5 meters (14 feet) high for an hour, contributing to other houses catching fire,‚Äù said Guillermo Rein, professor of Fire Science at Imperial College London.
Housing density can work for or against fire suppression and potential damage. Heavily populated areas can often act as a fuel break for a wildfire because they tend to have less flammable vegetation. However, they face the risk of one burning house threatening another. More remote areas don‚Äôt have the same house-to-house risk but can be difficult for firefighters to reach.
‚ÄúPretty much any fire that starts in California is near where a lot of people live,‚Äù Cook said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs its own beast.‚Äù
Top 20 fastest-growing fires with structure loss in California from 2001 to 2020

Note: Damage inspections are ongoing to determine the exact number of structures destroyed from the LA fires. As of January 22, the damage assessment team has verified 6,662 structures were destroyed in the Palisades fire and 9,418 in the Eaton fire.
The frequency and size of wildfires is well documented, but Cook and his team say measurements of fire speed deserve more attention and are integral to assessing human safety in fire-prone areas.
‚ÄúIncorporating understanding of fire speed into city planning and into fire management, that's gonna give you a lot more nuanced understanding of what risk you‚Äôre actually assuming.‚Äù
Note
Average maximum fire growth rate in the American West is derived from an updated FIRED dataset documenting fires recorded between 2000 and 2024. The Palisades, Eaton and Hughes fires maximum growth come from comparing the fire sizes noted in CalFire status reports.

item.title: Indonesia says will withdraw from Board of Peace if it does not benefit Palestinians
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 6 (Reuters) - Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto has reassured local Islamic groups that he will withdraw from U.S. President Donald Trump's 'Board of ‚ÄãPeace' if the platform does not benefit Palestinians, a government ‚Äåstatement said on Friday.
The participation of Indonesia, the world's largest Muslim-majority nation, on the board, and its decision to contribute troops for a Gaza stabilisation ‚Äãforce, has drawn criticism from experts and Muslim groups at ‚Äãhome, who say it compromises its long-standing support for the ‚ÅÝPalestinian cause.
Prabowo gathered the groups' leaders for a meeting on ‚ÄãThursday evening where he reiterated his reasoning behind joining the board, according ‚Äãto a statement put out by the government communication office.
Prabowo will withdraw from the board if it does not benefit Palestinian and Indonesian interests, the statement quoted ‚ÄãHanif Alatas of the Islamic Brotherhood Front as saying.
"The president ‚Äãsaid that if he sees that there is no longer any benefit for Palestine...and ‚Äåthat ‚ÅÝit is not in line with Indonesia's national interests, he will withdraw," Hanif said in the statement.
The Indonesian Ulema Council, a leading clerical body, had previously called for Indonesia to exit the board due ‚Äãto the U.S. ‚Äãrole in the ongoing ‚ÅÝIran war .
Nahdlatul Ulama, the country's largest Muslim group, said Indonesia's government could use the board to encourage ‚Äãde-escalation in the Middle East.
"Indonesia could declare that ‚Äãthe (board's) agenda ‚ÅÝis on hold until there are talks on de-escalation and peace from the American-Israeli war against Iran," its chief Yahya Cholil Staquf said ‚ÅÝin ‚Äãa statement issued by Prabowo's office late on Thursday.
All ‚ÄãBoard of Peace discussions have been halted due to the war, Indonesia Foreign Minister Sugiono ‚Äãhas previously said.

item.title: US weighs oil futures market action to combat price spikes, White House official says
item.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. Treasury Department is expected to announce measures ‚Äãas soon as Thursday aimed at combating rising energy prices in the wake of the Iran conflict , including potential action involving the ‚Äåoil futures market, a senior White House official said.
The potential move would mark an unusual attempt by Washington to influence energy prices through financial markets rather than physical oil supplies, as officials race to blunt the political and economic impact of rising fuel costs.
The details of the plan are unclear and the White House official, speaking on condition ‚Äãof anonymity to discuss internal matters, declined to provide specifics, saying they did not want to get ahead of the Treasury announcement.
U.S. crude futures ‚Äãhave jumped nearly 21% since the war with Iran started on Saturday, as the spreading conflict disrupted Middle East supplies. The ‚ÅÝnational average cost of gasoline, meanwhile, has risen 27 cents since last week to $3.25 per gallon, according to AAA, a U.S. travel organization that ‚Äãtracks fuel prices.
The idea of U.S. intervention in the futures market reflects the background of Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, a former hedge fund manager and ‚Äãglobal macro investor who spent decades trading currencies, bonds and commodities before joining the administration.
Bessent previously served as chief investment officer at Soros Fund Management and later founded the macro hedge fund Key Square Group.
A Treasury spokesperson could not be immediately reached for comment.
Energy analysts said the effectiveness of such a move would depend heavily on ‚Äãthe specifics.
"The devil is in the details‚Ä¶ we will have to see what the U.S. government‚Äôs plans are," said Ben Hoff, head of commodity ‚Äãquant research at Societe Generale, who called the potential step unprecedented.
He said financial tools can only go so far in influencing energy markets, which are driven primarily by ‚Äåphysical supply ‚ÅÝand demand.
The U.S Federal Reserve intervened to combat the financial crisis in 2008 by purchasing massive amounts of mortgage-backed securities and Treasury bonds in a policy called Quantitative Easing.
Treasury last October also used its Exchange Stabilization Fund to prop up Argentina's currency by buying pesos in the open market and backing a $20 billion swap line for Latin America's third-largest economy.
That fund, created during the Great Depression, had total assets of $220.85 billion as of January 31.
In recent ‚Äãyears, it has been used to ‚Äãback Federal Reserve lending facilities ‚ÅÝduring crises such as the 2008-2009 global financial crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2023 U.S. bank stability crisis.
There have been examples of government energy market interventions outside of the United States.
Mexico, for example, has for years executed ‚Äãa hedging program called the "Hacienda hedge" - once the world's largest financial oil deal - to protect the country's oil ‚Äãrevenues from price ‚ÅÝcrashes on the world market.
However, the Latin American country is hedging physical oil inventory rather than using purely financial instruments.
President Donald Trump said on Thursday he was not concerned about rising U.S. gas prices driven by the widening Iran conflict , telling Reuters in an exclusive interview that the U.S. military operation was his priority.
"I don't have ‚ÅÝany concern ‚Äãabout it," he said when asked about the higher prices at the pump. "They'll drop ‚Äãvery rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit."

item.title: Asia struggles to find fuel oil as Middle East exports plummet, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Fuel oil traders in Asia are struggling to secure alternative ‚Äãsupply as the Iran war curtails shipments from key Middle Eastern suppliers through the Strait of Hormuz , prompting them to look to ‚Äåthe West for replacement cargoes.
The shortage of Middle Eastern fuel oil volumes is set to dent the supply of bunker fuel for powering ships, with prices at key bunker ports such as Singapore set for further hikes in coming weeks, raising refuelling costs for vessel owners. Those higher costs will translate into increased prices for ‚Äãcompanies transporting goods.
Expectations for a growing shortage sparked a sharp rally in fuel oil markets this week, especially for high-sulphur fuel oil ‚Äãwhich typically comes from the Middle East.
Volumes of fuel oil exports transiting the Strait of Hormuz and bound ‚ÅÝfor Asia typically average 1.2 million metric tons per month, or about 246,000 barrels per day, Kpler data showed, with about 70% ending ‚Äãup in Southeast Asia.
Overall fuel oil exports via the Strait of Hormuz usually total about 3.7 million tons per month, the data showed.
Tanker transits ‚Äãare now about 90% lower than last week, Kpler's analysis of vessel activity showed.
"When such a large share of the global high-sulphur complex depends on a single chokepoint, even partial transit disruption can tighten balances quickly and amplify bunker volatility," said Sumit Ritolia, lead analyst for refining and supply modelling at Kpler.
Prices for ‚Äãhigh-sulphur bunker fuel delivered in Singapore, the world's largest ship refuelling hub, have risen more than 40% since the start of the war, ‚Äãwhile prices for delivered low-sulphur fuel oil have climbed more than 30%.
Some high-sulphur supply could come from Western refineries, though sky-high tanker rates make trading economics extremely ‚Äåchallenging, ‚ÅÝfuel oil traders said.
"Everyone is struggling to find oil for the second half of March. Tankers are too expensive and arbitrage to Singapore is closed," a trader based in Singapore said.
Possible supply sources include the United States and Mexico, traders said, but volumes are insufficient. Another possible source is Venezuela, but these cargoes have remained in the West so far this year.
"Obviously there is also Russia but these barrels remain sensitive for some ‚Äãbuyers," another trader said. Russian fuel ‚Äãremains under sanction because of the ‚ÅÝwar in Ukraine.
Iranian fuel oil is also under long-standing sanctions but China continues to purchase it. However, those shipments have also halted because of the conflict.
Any pullback in Iranian HSFO supply would prompt China's independent ‚Äãasphalt producers to pull more straight-run fuel oil from Russia, limiting availability in the Singapore Strait, said ‚Äãconsultancy FGE NexantECA.
Others are ‚ÅÝlooking towards regional Asian refiners but volumes are set to decline as they cut production amid a crude oil shortage caused by the Middle East war.
In the low-sulphur market, price hikes were less steep as some supply still comes from Brazil and Nigeria, though cargoes from Kuwait's al-Zour refinery are shut ‚ÅÝin the ‚ÄãGulf.
Costs of future replenishment are expected to jump due to wider tightness in the ‚Äãmarket, said traders.
While the market is coping with an existing large build in onshore inventories in Singapore as well as volumes stored on ships, stockpiles are set to draw sharply in ‚Äãthe coming weeks, traders said.

item.title: Wealthy Asians look to move Dubai assets closer to home on Iran war fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE/DUBAI, March 6 (Reuters) - Soon after the first Iranian missile and drone attacks on Dubai last week, two Indian entrepreneurs based there tried to move more than $100,000 each from their ‚Äãlocal bank accounts to Singapore to hedge risk.
Technological glitches in the aftermath of the Iranian attacks initially scuppered those plans, the entrepreneurs, who did not ‚Äåwish to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, told Reuters.
One of them said he managed to subsequently transfer the sum to his Singapore bank account via another Emirates-based bank.
Scores of other wealthy Asians are making enquiries or taking similar steps to move their Dubai-parked assets to the regional financial hubs of Singapore and Hong Kong, industry advisers and lawyers said, as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran clouds ‚Äãthe Gulf's safe-haven aura and rattles investors.
While the rich typically diversify their investments across regions and asset classes, they choose where to be based depending on tax, regulatory, ‚Äãprivacy and operational considerations.
Towards that end, Dubai has emerged in recent years as a preferred wealth hub for entrepreneurs and rich families in ‚ÅÝAsia, mainly from China, as they look to take advantage of its favourable policies. Moreover, with a property and infrastructure boom, the Gulf region has also become an investment destination.
The ‚Äãtrend is now under sharp scrutiny, as the attacks on Dubai and Abu Dhabi have thrown into doubt the United Arab Emirates' (UAE) reputation for stability .
Singapore-based private wealth lawyer Ryan Lin said ‚Äãsix or seven of his 20 Dubai-based clients, each holding an average of $50 million in assets, contacted him this week, with three planning immediate asset transfers to the city-state.
One client is "checking how quickly they can transfer everything to Singapore", Lin said.
Iris Xu, principal at global corporate and fund services provider Anderson Global, said 10 to 20 family offices have enquired this week with her firm about moving assets back ‚Äãto Singapore from the Middle East on worries the conflict might drag on.
Family offices are one-stop firms that manage the portfolios of the wealthy.
"Dubai was always about tax benefits but ‚Äãnow I think the tax benefits may not be the top priority for them," she said.
A wealth management adviser in Singapore, who did not wish to be identified because they were not authorised ‚Äåto talk ‚ÅÝto the media, said they had spoken to 13 UAE-based clients so far with more than half serious about moving assets to Singapore.
"Flying back and forth will be a challenge even if the conflict ends tomorrow. It is a confidence thing," said the adviser.
Grace Tang, CEO of Phillip Private Equity, said her predominantly Asian clients are skittish, with 10 to 20 asking about moving their wealth to Singapore and looking to preserve their capital.
Not all wealth managers, however, view the ongoing Middle East conflict as prompting immediate capital flight.
Dhruba Jyoti ‚ÄãSengupta, CEO of Dubai-based WRISE Private Middle ‚ÄãEast, a wealth management group, said the ‚ÅÝfirm has not seen "serious capital flight discussions", as clients were confident about the UAE's long-term resilience.
"They are sophisticated global investors, already diversified internationally, but deeply invested ... in the UAE's growth story," he said. "Despite the broader geopolitical turmoil in the region, clients are feeling safe and ‚Äãsecure."
The UAE's banking and financial sector was resilient , strong, stable, and well-positioned to navigate regional developments, its central bank governor Khaled Mohamed Balama ‚Äãsaid on Thursday, adding ‚ÅÝthat banks, financial firms, and insurers were operating normally and without disruption.
Leading Singapore-based wealth managers, Bank of Singapore and DBS Group (DBSM.SI) , opens new tab said their clients were closely observing the developments in the region and were taking a wait-and-watch approach, for now.
As the UAE scrambled to maintain its safe-haven status, some were going ahead with their expansion plans in the Emirates.
Jeremy Lim, co-founder of GrandWay Family Office, ‚ÅÝis in ‚Äãthe process of opening a family office in Abu Dhabi and said his plans have not changed - as ‚Äãlong as the UAE does not become directly involved in the conflict and barring any further escalation from Iran.
"The real deal-breaker for businesses would be if the UAE were to...become directly involved alongside one side in a ‚Äãconflict," said Lim.

item.title: Justice Department releases missing FBI interviews in Epstein files with woman who made claims against Trump
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 5 (Reuters) - The U.S. Justice Department released FBI records on Thursday that summarize interviews of an unidentified woman in ‚Äåwhich she made accusations against President Donald Trump related to an alleged sexual encounter.
FBI agents interviewed the woman four times in 2019 as part of their investigation into accused sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. The Justice Department had previously released a log confirming that the interviews took ‚Äãplace but released a summary of only one of those four meetings, in which she accused Epstein of ‚Äãmolesting her when she was a teenager.
The newly disclosed records , opens new tab , which were posted on the department's website on ‚ÅÝThursday, show she also claimed Trump attempted to force her to perform oral sex after Epstein introduced her to ‚Äãthe future president in New York or New Jersey in the 1980s when she was between 13 and 15 years old.
The ‚ÄãWhite House did not immediately respond to questions about the disclosures. Politico, which first reported the disclosures, said White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt called the woman's claims "completely baseless accusations, backed by zero credible evidence."
The Justice Department has cautioned that some of the documents include "untrue and ‚Äãsensationalist claims made against President Trump." Reuters could not independently confirm the accuracy of the woman's allegations, and FBI records ‚Äãsuggest agents stopped speaking with her in 2019.
The Justice Department said in a post on the social media platform X that the records ‚Äåit ‚ÅÝreleased Thursday were among 15 documents that it had ‚Äúincorrectly coded as duplicative‚Äù and not published as a result.
The disclosure comes as the Justice Department faces scrutiny in Congress over its handling of documents from the Epstein investigation, which it is required to make public. Democrats have accused Trump's administration of concealing records related to Trump, and a committee in the House of Representatives ‚Äãvoted to subpoena Attorney General ‚ÄãPam Bondi so lawmakers ‚ÅÝcan question her about how the government is handling the disclosures.
Trump has said his association with Epstein ended in the mid-2000s and that he was never aware of the financier's sexual ‚Äãabuse. Records previously released by the department show Trump flew several times on Epstein's plane in ‚Äãthe 1990s, ‚ÅÝwhich Trump has denied. After the financier was first accused of sexual misconduct, Trump called the police chief in Palm Beach to say that "everyone has known he's been doing this," according to an FBI interview record.
In the report of the woman's final , opens new tab interview, conducted in October 2019, ‚ÅÝduring Trump's ‚Äãfirst presidency, agents asked whether she would be willing to provide more ‚Äãinformation about Trump. In response, the agent wrote, she "asked what the point would be of providing the information at this point in her life when ‚Äãthere was a strong possibility nothing could be done about it."

item.title: India's tech state Karnataka bans social media for children under 16
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 6 (Reuters) - The southern Indian state ‚Äãof Karnataka, home to ‚Äåthe tech hub of Bengaluru, has banned ‚Äãthe use of ‚Äãsocial media by those under ‚ÅÝthe age of ‚Äã16, state Chief Minister ‚ÄãSiddaramaiah said on Friday, becoming the first in India ‚Äãto do so.
"With ‚Äãthe objective of preventing adverse ‚Äåeffects ‚ÅÝof increasing mobile usage on children, usage of social media ‚Äãwill be ‚Äãbanned ‚ÅÝfor children under the age ‚Äãof 16," Siddaramaiah, ‚Äãwho ‚ÅÝuses only one name, said in his ‚ÅÝbudget ‚Äãspeech.

item.title: Oil falls but set for steepest weekly gain since 2022
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 6 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell ‚Äãon Friday for the first time in six days as the U.S. government is weighing potentially ‚Äåintervening in the futures market to blunt rising prices and issued waivers for Russian oil purchases to ease supply constraints from the Middle East war.
Brent crude futures were down 95 cents, or 1.1%, to $84.46 per barrel and West Texas Intermediate was down $1.08, or 1.3%, ‚Äãto $79.93 as of 0440 GMT.
Still, Brent has surged 16.4% this week while the WTI jumped 19.2% on ‚Äãtrack for the steepest weekly gain since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ‚ÅÝFebruary 2022.
The gains followed the start on February 28 of the war between the U.S. and Israel, on one side, and Iran that has halted tankers ‚Äãmoving through the Strait of Hormuz , which typically carries roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply.
The conflict has since spread ‚Äãacross the key Middle East energy-producing region, causing disruptions to oil output and the shutdowns of refineries and liquefied natural gas plants.
"With every passing day, halted activities in Hormuz will have two major impacts on oil: the inability to store 20 million barrels per day and the ‚Äãlack of flow to the world, which could drive global energy prices higher," said Priyanka Sachdeva, senior market analyst ‚Äãat Phillip Nova.
In the face of the potentially higher gains, the U.S. Treasury Department is expected to announce measures to combat rising energy ‚Äåprices from ‚ÅÝthe Iran conflict, including potential action involving the oil futures market, a senior White House official said on Thursday, without providing any details.
Such a move would mark an unusual attempt by Washington to influence energy prices through financial markets rather than physical oil supplies.
The U.S. Treasury also on Thursday granted waivers for companies to start buying sanctioned Russian oil ‚Äãstored on tankers to ease ‚Äãsupply constraints which have ‚ÅÝpushed refineries in Asia to reduce their fuel processing.
The first waivers were given to Indian refiners , who have responded by buying millions of barrels of Russian crude oil cargoes, reversing months of ‚Äãpressure on them to halt the purchases.
