Slattery ‚ÅÝheaded ‚ÄãBHP's petroleum division before ‚Äãit was acquired by Woodside.

item.title: Two Kuwait fire officers killed 'while performing duties,' Fire Force says
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Kuwait's Fire Force ‚Äãsaid on ‚ÄåSunday that two of its ‚Äãofficers were ‚Äãkilled "while performing duties," according ‚ÅÝto a ‚Äãpost on ‚ÄãX.
It did not provide details on ‚Äãthe circumstances ‚Äãof their deaths, but ‚ÄåKuwait ‚ÅÝhas been intercepting drones and missiles launched ‚Äãfrom ‚ÄãIran ‚ÅÝfollowing U.S. and Israeli ‚Äãattacks ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝthe Islamic Republic.

item.title: Trump praises Italy PM Meloni's willingness to help in U.S.-Israel war with Iran
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni ‚Äãand said she was willing to ‚Äåhelp the United States and Israel in their war with Iran, Italy‚Äôs Corriere della ‚ÄãSera reported on Sunday.
Speaking to ‚Äãthe newspaper in a telephone call ‚ÅÝon Saturday, Trump described Meloni as ‚Äúa ‚Äãgreat leader‚Äù and said Italy was ‚Äãdoing what it could to assist.
* "I love Italy, I think she is a great leader," ‚ÄãTrump was quoted as saying of ‚ÄãMeloni.
* "She always tries to help, she is an ‚Äåexcellent ‚ÅÝleader and she is a friend of mine," Trump said, according to Corriere della Sera.
* Italy is planning to ‚Äãsend air-defence ‚Äãaid to ‚ÅÝGulf countries in the face of Iranian air strikes, ‚ÄãMeloni said on Thursday.
* An Italian ‚Äãnavy ‚ÅÝvessel is preparing to sail to Cyprus, a navy spokesperson said on Friday, ‚ÅÝas ‚Äãpart of a joint ‚ÄãEuropean mission to protect the island after it ‚Äãcame under Iranian fire.

item.title: Israeli military says it will pursue every successor of Iran's Khamenei
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli ‚Äãmilitary warned it would ‚Äåcontinue pursuing every successor of Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah ‚ÄãAli Khamenei, who was ‚Äãkilled in U.S. and ‚ÅÝIsraeli strikes on Iran.
In ‚Äãa post on ‚ÄãX in Farsi, the Israeli military also warned it would pursue every ‚Äãperson who seeks ‚Äãto appoint a successor for ‚ÄåKhamenei, ‚ÅÝreferring to the clerical body charged with choosing the Islamic Republic's ‚Äãsupreme ‚Äãleader.
The ‚ÅÝwarning comes as the clerical body ‚Äãis set to ‚Äãmeet ‚ÅÝto choose Iran's next supreme leader.

item.title: Explainer: Who might succeed in Iran's theocratic system of power?
item.description:
March 8 - Iranian clerics involved in choosing a successor to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after his killing a week ago in U.S.-Israeli strikes say they are close to naming the Islamic Republic's new supreme leader.
Iran's revolutionary theocracy has never been in greater jeopardy , and with the clerical body tasked with naming a new leader ready for an announcement as soon as Sunday, it is hard to predict what might happen next.
Israel and the United States ‚Äãhave vowed no let up in their war, promising to kill whoever replaces Khamenei and even those involved in selecting the new leader - a group that may include the clerics who formally make the choice ‚Äåand the Revolutionary Guardsmen and political insiders who influence them.
The following explains how power is meant to operate in the Islamic Republic, how a new supreme leader can be chosen, some of the main candidates, and how the U.S. and Israeli attacks have changed the equation:
Iran's theocratic system dates to the 1979 revolution that ousted the Shah. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the leader of the revolution, introduced a new system of rule: vilayat-e faqih, or guardianship of the Islamic jurist.
The theory holds that until the return of the Shi'ite Muslim 12th Imam, who disappeared in the ninth century, power on earth ‚Äãshould be wielded by a venerable cleric.
It means whoever takes over as supreme leader, empowered by the constitution as the ultimate authority guiding the elected president and parliament, will have to be a senior cleric.
Under Khomeini, who died ‚Äãin 1989, and Khamenei, who has ruled since then, the supreme leader has had the last say in all matters of state. But any new leader will have to assert his authority at ‚ÅÝa moment of enormous rupture - and may struggle to do so.
The constitution says a new leader must be chosen within three months. Until then President Masoud Pezeshkian, Guardians Council member Ayatollah Alireza Arafi and Judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein ‚ÄãMohseni-Ejei have taken charge as a temporary leadership council.
Choosing a new leader is meant to be the responsibility of the Assembly of Experts, a body of around 90 senior clerics who are elected every eight years, many of them very elderly.
With strikes continuing, they have been ‚Äãconducting their consultations online, Iranian officials have told Reuters, and appear close to naming a new leader.
Khamenei never publicly tapped a preferred successor and in practice the decision probably rests with the most senior figures in the Islamic Republic who have wielded power under Khamenei for many years. It is their preferred candidate who is most likely to be approved by the assembly.
Among the most important of those senior figures is Khamenei's veteran adviser Ali Larijani , often seen as Iran's foremost powerbroker. The Guards will also have a critical backroom voice in the process.
Khamenei's son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is widely seen as the most ‚Äãlikely candidate after surviving the first salvo of strikes, which killed his wife. Although Iran's ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary succession, he has a powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's still-influential office.
The grandson of the revolutionary founder Ruhollah ‚ÄãKhomeini is another possible choice. Hassan Khomeini is closely associated with the reformist faction that has for decades tried to moderate the Islamic Republic's stance and may be seen as better able to assuage Western enmity and calm the fury of an embittered population.
Arafi and Mohseni-Ejei are less ‚Äåprominent possibilities who ‚ÅÝwould likely continue Khamenei's hardline stance. Mohseni-Ejei was responsible for stamping out internal protests following a disputed election outcome in 2009 when he was intelligence minister.
Assembly of Experts members Ahmad Alamolhoda and Mohsen Araki are also hardline senior clerics with a close involvement in Iranian politics who might be considered. Former president Hassan Rouhani is a senior cleric but he was distrusted by some of the most powerful hardliners who would have great sway over the choice.
The assembly could theoretically pick an even lesser known ayatollah as leader. But the ruling system has been so fractured by the strikes that it would be much harder to buttress the position of a newcomer.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps had long been expected to play a central role behind the ‚Äãscenes in determining Khamenei's successor. Unlike the ordinary military, which ‚Äãcomes under the elected president, the guards answer only to ‚ÅÝthe supreme leader.
But its top echelons have been hollowed out by U.S. and Israeli strikes over recent years and it is far from clear how far it will still be able to influence the decision.
The most important Guards leader of recent times was Qassem Soleimani, head of the elite Quds Force unit that spearheaded Iran's regional strategy of exporting the revolution through affiliated Shi'ite militias ‚Äãin Arab countries. He was killed by a U.S. strike in 2020.
During the brief summer war last year Israeli strikes killed other top Guards commanders. And the latest strikes killed its ‚Äãlatest top commander Mohammed Pakpour, three ‚ÅÝsources familiar with the matter said.
The Basij militia, a part-time paramilitary force under Guards control, is often used to quell protests inside Iran, giving the corps a formidable role in internal control.
Since the early 2000s, the Guards' economic power has also grown as its contracting company Khatam al-Anbiya won projects worth billions of dollars in Iran's oil and gas sector. Protecting that empire may contribute to the Guards' decision on backing a new leader.
Iranians elect a president and a parliament to four-year terms. The president appoints a government ‚ÅÝthat handles daily policy ‚Äãwithin parameters permitted by the supreme leader.
During the early years of the Islamic Republic the votes drew mass participation. But far fewer Iranians retain faith ‚Äãin electoral politics.
While President Pezeshkian, a reputed moderate, is on the three-member interim leadership committee, it is far from clear he has had any say over how events pan out and he was forced into an embarrassing climbdown on Saturday over the conduct of the war.
And while the Assembly of Experts is elected, all its ‚Äãcandidates - like those running in all Iranian national elections - must be vetted by the clerical Guardians Council, meaning only those already aligned with the authorities can take part.

item.title: Lebanese man flees hometown, months after repairing home damaged in last war
item.description:
HAZMIEH, Lebanon, March 8 (Reuters) - Just days ago, Hussain Khrais was proudly showing off his newly restored home in south ‚ÄãLebanon, fixed up after being badly damaged in 2024 clashes between Israel and Hezbollah. But a new war has since erupted ‚Äåand his home is in the line of fire again.
Khrais fled his hometown of Khiyam, about five km (three miles) from the border with Israel, as Israel pounded Lebanon with heavy airstrikes last week in retaliation for Iran-backed group Hezbollah's rocket and drone fire into Israel.
"Is the house I worked so hard to build, or the business I started, ‚Äãstill there? Or is it all gone?" Khrais told Reuters from a relative's home near the capital Beirut where he and his ‚Äãfamily are now staying.
"The feeling is very, very upsetting, because we still don't know if we'll go back ‚ÅÝor not."
It wasn't Khrais' first time - or even his second. The 66-year-old has been displaced at least four times in ‚Äãthe last four decades by Israeli incursions and airstrikes, each time returning to a town in ruins and rebuilding patiently.
Last year, he spent months and ‚Äãaround $25,000 repairing the damage from the last war between Hezbollah and Israel, which ended 15 months ago. Hezbollah started firing at Israel after the United States and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran on February 28.
"It really bothers me to think this is the life I've lived," Khrais told Reuters. "Once again, displacement, return, rebuilding, restoration - then again displacement, return, ‚Äãrebuilding. What kind of life is that?"
With no support from the Lebanese state and little coming from Hezbollah's social welfare programme, most Lebanese whose ‚Äãhomes were damaged or destroyed in the 2024 war have used their own private funds to rebuild.
Reconstruction has placed a huge burden on affected Lebanese families, still struggling ‚Äåto access ‚ÅÝtheir savings in commercial banks after a financial collapse in 2019.
Two weeks ago, Khrais had told Reuters he was scared that a new war would start. "I'm at an age where I can't start all over again. That's it," he said.
The new war has dealt Lebanese another blow. About 300,000 people have been displaced over the last week by Israel's strikes and by the Israeli military's evacuation orders, which encompass around 8% of ‚ÄãLebanese territory.
Khrais is staying with around ‚Äã20 other displaced relatives, some displaced ‚ÅÝfrom Khiyam and others from Beirut's southern suburbs, which have been hit hard by Israeli strikes.
He is glued to the television, where news bulletins have reported on Israeli troops and tanks pushing deeper into his hometown.
"I've been ‚Äãin Beirut for four days now, and these four days feel like 400 years," Khrais said.
He misses ‚Äãhis house dearly.
"Maybe the ‚ÅÝthing I'm most attached to, is when I open the door to my children's bedrooms and see the pictures of their children hanging on the walls," he said.
"That sight is worth the world's treasures - to see my grandchildren's pictures in Khiyam."
Khrais has no news on the state of his home. He said ‚ÅÝhe remains ‚Äãhopeful but that if it has been destroyed, he'll still do what he's always done.
"The ‚Äãbig shock would be if I came back and didn't find it. But my feeling says no, God willing, it will remain. And like I said, even if we don't find ‚Äãthe house, we'll go back and rebuild," he said.

item.title: Iran war threatens prolonged hit to global energy markets
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers grapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics, and elevated risks to shipping.
The outlook poses a global economic threat and a political vulnerability for ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump leading into the midterm elections, with voters sensitive to energy bills and unfavorable to foreign entanglements .
"The market is shifting from pricing pure geopolitical risk to grappling with tangible operational disruption, as refinery shutdowns and export constraints ‚Äåbegin to impair crude processing and regional supply flows," JP Morgan analysts said in a research note on Friday.
The conflict has already led to the suspension of around a fifth of global crude and natural gas supply, as Tehran targets ships in the vital Strait of Hormuz between its shores and Oman, and attacks energy infrastructure across the region.
Global oil prices have surged more than 25% since the start of the war, driving up fuel prices for consumers worldwide.
A nearly complete shutdown of the Strait means the region's giant oil producers - Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq and Kuwait - have had to suspend shipments of as much as 140 million barrels of oil - ‚Äãequal to about 1.4 days of global demand - to global refiners.
As a result, oil and gas storage at facilities in the Middle East Gulf are rapidly filling, forcing oil fields in Iraq and Kuwait to cut oil production , with the United Arab Emirates likely ‚Äãto cut next , analysts, traders and sources said.
"At some point soon, everyone will also shut in if vessels do not come," said a source with a state oil company in the region, who asked not ‚ÅÝto be named.
Oilfields forced to shut in across the Middle East as a result of the shipping disruptions could take a while to return to normal, said Amir Zaman, head of the Americas commercial team at Rystad Energy.
"The conflict could be ended, but it could take days ‚Äãor weeks or months, depending on the types of fields, age of the field, the type of shut-in that they've had to do before you can get production back up to what it once was," he said.
Iranian forces, meanwhile, are targeting regional energy infrastructure - including refineries and terminals - ‚Äãforcing them to shut down too, with some of those operations badly damaged by attacks and in need of repairs.
Qatar declared force majeure on its huge volumes of gas exports on Wednesday after Iranian drone attacks and it may take at least a month to return to normal production levels, sources told Reuters. Qatar supplies 20% of global LNG.
Saudi Aramco‚Äôs mammoth Ras Tanura refinery and crude export terminal, meanwhile, has also closed due to attacks, with no details on damage.
The White House has justified the attack on Iran, saying the country posed an imminent threat to the United States , although it has not provided details. Trump has also said he was concerned about ‚ÄãIran's efforts to obtain a nuclear weapon.
A quick end to the war would soothe markets. But a return to pre-war supply and pricing could take weeks or months, depending on the extent of the damage to infrastructure and shipping.
"Considering physical damage due to Iranian ‚Äãstrikes, so far we have not seen anything that would be considered structural, although the risk remains as long as the war continues," said Joel Hancock, energy analyst, Natixis CIB.
The biggest question for energy supplies is how and when the Strait of Hormuz will become safe for shipping again. Trump has offered ‚Äånaval escorts to ‚ÅÝoil tankers and promised U.S. insurance support to vessels in the region.
But safety in the waterway may be elusive, as Iran has the capacity to sustain drone attacks on shipping for months , intelligence and military sources have said.
The conflict could also encourage countries to top up their strategic petroleum reserves in the weeks and months after the conflict ends, by exposing the dangers of thin inventories. That would increase demand for oil and support prices.
In the meantime, the disruption in energy shipments is reverberating through supply chains and economies in import-reliant Asia, which sources 60% of its crude oil from the Middle East.
In India, state-run Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab declared force majeure on gasoline export cargoes, sources said this week, joining a growing number of refineries in the region unable to fulfill sales contracts due to lack of supply.
At least two refineries in China have ‚Äãcut runs. China, a big supplier to the region, has asked ‚Äãrefineries to suspend fuel exports. Thailand has also suspended fuel ‚ÅÝexports, while Vietnam has suspended crude shipments.
Disruption has given Russia a boost. Prices for Russian crude cargoes have risen as the U.S. has given Indian refiners a 30-day waiver to buy Russian crude to substitute for lost Middle East supply. Washington had pressured India to cut Russian oil imports under the threat of tariffs.
In Japan, the No. 2 global LNG importer, baseload power futures for Tokyo for the fiscal year starting in April ‚Äãjumped more than a third this week on the EEX in anticipation of higher fuel prices. And in Seoul, drivers queued up at petrol stations in anticipation of rising pump prices.
For European consumers, the ‚Äãcrisis in gas supplies and the higher ‚ÅÝprices are a double whammy. The region was hit the hardest by the disruption to gas supplies due to sanctions on Russian energy imports after Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022.
Europe turned to LNG imports to substitute for Russian pipeline gas. And Europe now needs to buy 180 more LNG cargoes than it did last year to fill gas storage to the levels needed before next winter.
The supply risks to the United States are fewer, as the country has grown in recent years into the world‚Äôs largest oil and gas producer. But U.S. crude and fuel prices rise in tandem with international crude ‚ÅÝmarkets, so pump prices ‚Äãfor gasoline and diesel are affected even if domestic supply is plentiful.
U.S. average retail gasoline, for example, hit $3.32 a gallon nationally on Friday, up 34 cents over last ‚Äãweek, according to AAA. Diesel prices, meanwhile, hit $4.33 a gallon, up from $3.76 a gallon a week ago.
Higher prices at the pump mark a major risk for Trump and his fellow Republicans as they head into midterm elections in November.
"Gasoline prices are psychologically powerful," said Mark Malek, chief investment officer at Siebert Financial. "They are the inflation number that consumers ‚Äãsee every single day."

item.title: Slovakia PM Fico to meet EU's von der Leyen to push for restart of Druzhba oil flows
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico said on Sunday he ‚Äåwould meet with EU chief Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday, as Slovakia and Hungary ramp up pressure on Ukraine to restart Russian oil flows, warning Slovakia could block a major EU ‚Äãloan to Kyiv.
Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia ‚Äãvia Ukraine have been suspended since late January, after Kyiv said a ‚ÅÝRussian attack caused a fire that seriously damaged the line.
The outage has sparked one ‚Äãof the most heated disputes between the neighbours since Russia's 2022 full-scale invasion of ‚ÄãUkraine.
Ukraine says the pipeline cannot be repaired quickly .
Hungary and Slovakia, European Union members which still import Russian oil and maintain ties with Moscow, have accused Kyiv of deliberately delaying a restart for political ‚Äãreasons.
Hungary has vetoed new EU sanctions ‚Äãon Russia and a 90-billion-euro loan by the bloc for Ukraine. Fico reiterated his threat to ‚Äåblock the ‚ÅÝloan in a Facebook video on Sunday.
He said he would meet von der Leyen in Paris and push for experts to visit the Druzhba pipeline.
"But the most important message will be that Slovakia is ready to take over the baton ‚Äãfrom Hungary, if necessary," ‚ÄãFico said in ‚ÅÝreference to the loan.
"Blocking this huge military gift to Ukraine is a legitimate tool to achieve the restoration of oil ‚Äãsupplies."
The EU Commission said Friday it was looking into ways to ‚Äãrestore Druzhba ‚ÅÝoil flows, including potential financial assistance.
Slovakia has already halted emergency power supplies to Ukraine amid the dispute.
The dispute escalates as Hungary heads into a tight election next month, with Prime ‚ÅÝMinister Viktor ‚ÄãOrban, in power for 16 years, making the ‚Äãwar in Ukraine a central campaign theme.

item.title: Italy weighs fuel-tax cuts as firms warn of energy price surge from Middle East war
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - Italy is considering cutting fuel excise duties using higher-than-expected value-added tax revenues triggered by rising ‚Äãpump prices, the prime minister said, as businesses ‚Äåwarned the conflict in the Middle East could sharply raise energy costs.
Excise duties account for a large share of the pump ‚Äãprice in Italy and are levied per litre, ‚Äãnot as a percentage of the total price ‚ÅÝlike VAT.
($1 = ‚Äã0.8607 euros)

item.title: Tokyo told national oil reserve site to prepare for release amid Iran crisis, Japan lawmaker says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 8 (Reuters) - A senior Japanese parliament member said on Sunday that the government instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible release ‚Äãof crude, as the Iran crisis has cut supply from the Middle ‚ÄåEast.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its crude supplies, with roughly 70% shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been effectively closed following U.S. and Israeli ‚Äãattacks on Iran.
Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition ‚Äãparty, told Reuters that an official from the Japan Organization for ‚ÅÝMetals and Energy Security (JOGMEC) at the Shibushi national oil storage base said it ‚Äãhad received the directive from the Agency for Natural Resources and Energy (ANRE) on Friday.
Details ‚Äãsuch as the timing of the release remain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
It was not immediately clear if any other storage bases received the same instruction.
An official at ANRE, under the Ministry of ‚ÄãEconomy, Trade and Industry (METI), declined to comment. The Shibushi base was not immediately ‚Äãavailable for comment, while JOGMEC could not be reached outside office hours.
The Shibushi site in southern ‚ÄåJapan ‚ÅÝis one of the locations where the country's strategic oil stocks are stored.
Japan holds emergency oil reserves equivalent to 254 days of domestic consumption - among the world's largest - including government-owned stockpiles, private-sector inventories, and jointly held reserves with oil-producing countries.
Tokyo last ‚Äãdrew down oil from ‚Äãits reserves in ‚ÅÝ2022 as part of an International Energy Agency (IEA)-led coordinated release following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
Industry Minister Ryosei Akazawa said early last ‚Äãweek that there were no specific plans to release oil reserves, ‚Äãbut the ‚ÅÝministry would continue to monitor the crude oil supply situation closely, working in coordination with the IEA.
Kyodo news agency reported on Friday that the Japanese government is considering using ‚ÅÝpart ‚Äãof its national oil reserves amid the ongoing Iran ‚Äãcrisis that has disrupted global energy supplies and may do so in coordination with other countries or ‚Äãeven on its own.

item.title: Iran attacks breach international law, Swiss Defence Minister says
item.description:
ZURICH/FRANKFURT, March 8 (Reuters) - The United States and Israel have broken international law with their attacks on Iran , Swiss Defence Minister Martin Pfister said in an ‚Äãinterview published on Sunday, the latest European leader to raise ‚Äåconcerns about the conflict.
Legal experts have said many countries will consider the attacks unjustified under the United Nations Charter, under which member countries must refrain from using force ‚Äãor the threat of force without U.N authorisation or unless acting ‚Äãin self-defence.
"The Federal Council is of the opinion that the ‚ÅÝattack on Iran constitutes a violation of international law," Pfister told ‚ÄãSonntagsZeitung, referring to the Swiss cabinet.
"In our view it constitutes a violation ‚Äãon the prohibition of violence," he added, calling on all parties involved to halt the fighting to protect the civilian population.
Pfister said he was referring ‚Äãto all the countries not complying with the prohibition on violence, ‚Äãincluding the United States and Israel.
"The Americans and Israel have attacked Iran from the ‚Äåair. ‚ÅÝIn doing so, they, like Iran, violated international law," Pfister said.
The comments chime with those of German Vice Chancellor and Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil, who told the RND newspaper network that he had "serious doubts that this ‚Äãwar is legitimate under ‚Äãinternational law."
Klingbeil ‚ÅÝalso came out strongly against the idea of any potential German participation in the war: "I say clearly: this ‚Äãis not our war. We will not participate in ‚Äãthis war."
He ‚ÅÝsaid there was a "great danger that we are sliding ever deeper into a world where there are no longer any rules. We do not want ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãlive in a world where only the ‚Äãlaw of the strongest applies."
Spain has also denounced the U.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless ‚Äãand illegal.

item.title: Airstrikes, not occupation: Where Trump voters draw red lines on Iran
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A week into a war with Iran that is already unpopular with much of the American public, President Donald Trump has offered various explanations for the bombing campaign, estimated the strikes could last weeks , cautioned there will likely be more U.S. casualties , and dismissed concerns about surging oil and gas prices .
While that has troubled many Americans, recent interviews with several who voted for Trump show they are largely standing by the ‚Äãpresident and his war ‚Äì at least for now. Even his most ardent supporters, however, warned that a large deployment of U.S. ground troops in Iran would alarm them.
In the days since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, Reuters has spoken ‚Äåto eight Americans who voted for Trump in 2024, part of a group of 20 whom Reuters has interviewed monthly since February , to hear their thoughts on the rapidly escalating conflict.
All eight opposed the idea of the Trump administration sending substantial U.S. ground forces to Iran or getting involved in a protracted effort to install new leadership. But five said they fully supported the air and sea attacks as the only way to prevent Iran from stockpiling long-range and nuclear missiles. Three were less clear about why the administration started the conflict, saying they worried it was unduly damaging the U.S. economy and endangering U.S. citizens.
Their reactions to the war so far roughly reflect the results of a Reuters-Ipsos poll conducted last weekend ‚Äãthat surveyed 1,282 U.S. adults. Nearly two-thirds of respondents who voted for Trump in 2024 said they approved of the strikes, while 9% said they disapproved and 27% said they were not sure. Overall, just one in four respondents expressed support for the U.S. attack ‚Äãon Iran.
If energy prices keep surging and Trump's tactics against Iran start to alienate his own followers , the conflict could erode support for Republicans as the U.S. heads into all-important midterm elections in November that will determine ‚ÅÝwhether Congress stays in the party's control.
Although most of the eight voters Reuters interviewed reported that gasoline in their area had shot up between 20 and 50 cents per gallon, those who supported the strikes said they expected the higher prices to be short-lived.
Jon Webber, 45, a Walmart ‚Äãretail worker in Indiana, pointed to the struggle his parents had with volatile oil prices following the 1979 Iranian Revolution. ‚ÄúYeah, it's gonna suck for a little bit, but it'll go back,‚Äù he said.
After watching U.S. presidents invoke the threat posed by Iran for most of his life, Webber said it ‚Äãfelt good to see Trump cripple the regime: ‚ÄúIt should have been done a long time ago and we wouldn't have had to deal with it for this long.‚Äù
Near Houston, Texas, Loretta Torres, 38, said she trusted the president had acted judiciously. ‚ÄúTrump was trying to get ahead of the game and trying to be proactive with the threats,‚Äù she said.
But Torres, a mother of three, also said she feared the war could spin ‚Äúout of control‚Äù or inspire terrorist attacks on major metropolitan areas like hers. Like all the voters Reuters interviewed, she dreaded the prospect of the U.S. becoming enmeshed in the region for years if Trump sends in ground troops.
The voters ‚Äãwho supported the strikes were confident Trump had authorized them because they were necessary to thwart an imminent attack on the United States. Democrats and even prominent conservative commentators have expressed skepticism about this, citing the administration's varying explanations for the war.
Chad Hill, 50, a supervisor at a nuclear power ‚Äãplant near his home in northwestern Ohio, said he had been expecting some type of U.S. military action, despite U.S.-Iran negotiations that had been underway over Iran's nuclear program just days before the strikes: ‚ÄúUnfortunately, it seems like this was probably the only way because in the end they don't trust us and we don't trust ‚Äåthem.‚Äù
Trump might need ‚ÅÝto send a limited military detachment into the country to fully destroy Iran‚Äôs missile capabilities, Hill said, but any larger ground deployment would raise red flags for him. ‚ÄúNo nation-building, that doesn‚Äôt work,‚Äù he said.
The idea of U.S. boots on Iranian ground also made Gerald Dunn, 67, uneasy. ‚ÄúOnly if they‚Äôre invited‚Äù by a new Iranian government should Trump deploy ground troops, he said, and even then, ‚Äúthe scale should be limited.‚Äù
Like Hill, Dunn, a martial arts instructor in New York‚Äôs Hudson Valley, applauded Trump for taking action where prior administrations had simply ‚Äúkicked the can down the road.‚Äù
Near Savannah, Georgia, insurance firm employee Amanda Taylor, 52, said that while ‚Äúthere‚Äôs so much we don‚Äôt really know,‚Äù she would back any military action that made the U.S. safer.
‚ÄúOur intelligence is usually right in these things so I'm going to hope and trust that [Trump acted] because of that, and not just because of his own gut feeling,‚Äù ‚Äãshe said. At the same time, Taylor added, ‚Äúnobody wants a drawn-out, true ‚Äãwar ‚Äì I would hate to see that happen.‚Äù
CONFUSION OVER RATIONALE
The shifting ‚ÅÝreasons Trump administration officials gave for the strikes puzzled some voters.
On Monday, Herman Sims heard Secretary of State Marco Rubio say the U.S. learned Israel was planning to attack Iran and struck first to prevent retaliation ‚Äì but on Tuesday, he heard Trump claim responsibility for leading the charge based on the president's hunch that Iran would attack if the U.S. didn‚Äôt.
Sims, 66, a night operations manager for a trucking company in Dallas, Texas, said ‚Äãthe conflicting reports ‚Äúdidn‚Äôt make any sense,‚Äù but added that he supported the strikes if they were indeed necessary to protect U.S. lives. Still, he was alarmed by the spiking gas prices and by a report that ‚Äãan ex-Marine ‚Äôs arm was broken while he ‚ÅÝprotested that the U.S. should not ‚Äúfight for Israel‚Äù during a Senate hearing.
‚ÄúI agree 100%. We should not be fighting a war for someone else,‚Äù Sims said.
In Madison, Wisconsin, college student Will Brown, 20, said he was frustrated by the administration's "wishy-washy" explanations for why the U.S. attacked when it did.
"Bombing them to the extent that we have is fine, but Trump's talked about boots on the ground and troops dying, and that I simply can't approve of," he said. Trump told the New York Post on Monday that he has not ruled out sending U.S. ground troops into Iran.
Though he was glad to see Iran's leader dead ‚ÅÝand the country's nuclear ‚Äãcapacity reduced, Brown said he could "not fathom the amount of destruction and death" a ground invasion would bring.
Don Jernigan, 75, a retiree in Virginia Beach, said Trump had not ‚Äãjustified putting U.S. troops in harm‚Äôs way.
Assuming the Iranian threat was ‚Äúso imminent that our cities are in danger of being destroyed from a long distance, from the mainland of Iran, then perhaps we should be destroying people from a long distance, like the mainland of America,‚Äù Jernigan said.
Though he couldn‚Äôt know what kind of threat Trump saw, Jernigan added that ‚Äãthe U.S. strikes had raised the odds of terrorist attacks against Americans.
‚ÄúIf we kill their brothers and their dads and their uncles over there, they're going to come over here and try to kill ours,‚Äù he said.

item.title: Pope Leo urges end to bombing, calls for dialogue amid Iran, Middle East violence
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 8 (Reuters) - Pope Leo said on Sunday that ‚Äãdeeply troubling news continued ‚Äåto arrive from Iran and across the Middle East, urging ‚Äãan end to the ‚Äãviolence and renewed efforts to ‚ÅÝopen space for dialogue.
Speaking ‚Äãat the Angelus prayer ‚Äãin St. Peter‚Äôs Square, the pope said the conflict was fuelling ‚Äãfear and hatred and ‚Äãraised concerns that it could spread ‚Äåfurther, ‚ÅÝdragging other countries, including "dear Lebanon".
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that ‚Äãthe ‚Äãroar ‚ÅÝof bombs may cease, that weapons may ‚Äãfall silent, and that ‚Äãspace ‚ÅÝmay be opened for dialogue in which the voices ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãpeoples can be ‚Äãheard", Pope Leo said.

item.title: From 1776 to 2026: Adam Smith's lessons for the global economy
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters) - Tax the rich. Trash the tariffs. End monopolies.
Such are the rallying calls of many of today's most heated economic debates. They could also come straight from the pen of revered economist Adam Smith, hailed by some as the "father of capitalism" and others as an early progressive.
Smith knew nothing of Donald Trump ‚Äãor tech billionaires when he railed against trade protectionism and extreme affluence in "The Wealth of Nations", the best-read economics book in history, which celebrates its 250th birthday on Monday.
"It is the maxim of ‚Äåevery prudent master of a family never to attempt to make at home what it will cost him more to make than to buy," the Scot wrote in the seminal work, for example.
"Would it be a reasonable law to prohibit the importation of all foreign wines, merely to encourage the making of claret and burgundy in Scotland?" he adds.
The foundational text of classical economics was published on March 9, 1776, the same year as the U.S. Declaration of Independence, 13 years before the French Revolution, and amid the early convulsions of the Industrial Age.
Yet ‚Äãit's not hard to find parallels between the doctrines the book denounces - such as rival mercantilist empires seeking to minimise imports and maximise exports - and President Trump's trade tactics and "America First" credo today.
And while Smith is ‚Äãmost often cited for his free markets and free trade zeal, when it comes to distribution of wealth, he could almost be channelling Bernie Sanders or Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, leftist ‚ÅÝU.S. lawmakers.
"It is not very unreasonable that the rich should contribute to the public expense, not only in proportion to their revenue, but something more than in that proportion," Smith writes in the 1,000-page-plus tome, which draws on everything from ‚Äãgrape-growing to pin factories.
"No society can surely be flourishing and happy, of which the far greater part of the members are poor and miserable," he says in one of his best-known quotes .
Many scholars of the book - full ‚Äãname "An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations" - say it remains uncannily relevant to the economic issues of our time, though debates continue to rage about what Smith was actually saying.
Advocates of free-market economics have long claimed him as their spiritual father, while some more recent readings even peg him as a moderate progressive - something akin to a left-leaning, modern-day European social democrat.
"You can find a 'Smith' to support anything you want to say," King's College London research associate Leo Steeds said of the Scottish Enlightenment thinker.
Smith also accepted ‚Äãthat there were certain circumstances when tariffs were called for, either because the terms of trade were unfair or for reasons of security - arguments increasingly heard in the United States, Europe and other trading blocs.
"Smith did understand those arguments," said ‚ÄãEamonn Butler, director of the free market policy think tank the Adam Smith Institute in London. "But he thought these things (tariffs) really should be as temporary as possible. He thought the more trade you have, the better everybody is."
One of the most ‚Äåfamous metaphors ‚ÅÝfrom the "Wealth of Nations" is that of the "invisible hand", most often interpreted as meaning how free markets channel the self-interest of different participants to the best outcome for all.
"It is not from the benevolence of the butcher, the brewer, or the baker, that we expect our dinner, but from their regard to their own interest," Smith writes.
But other students of the work note the invisible hand metaphor was used just once in the book and must be taken alongside his wider arguments rather than being used to justify "laissez-faire" policies.
"This book ... is actually a critique of the way in which special interests, monopolists, powerful people, lobbies capture the state," said Pratap Bhanu Mehta, a leading Indian academic and public intellectual.
"He says: You fix that, ‚Äãthen free markets come."
U.S. economist Joseph Stiglitz, a professor at ‚ÄãColumbia University and Nobel Prize laureate, agreed.
"It was much ‚ÅÝmore of an enlightened self-interest looking at society more broadly," he said. "Modern economics is based on infinitely selfish people. And clearly, Adam Smith didn't believe that."
Indeed, Smith - who taught moral philosophy at the University of Glasgow - is explicit about his views on selfishness at the expense of others.
"All for ourselves, and nothing for other people, seems, in every age of the ‚Äãworld, to have been the vile maxim of the masters of mankind," he writes.
Events to mark the 250th anniversary of "The Wealth of Nations" are ‚Äãtaking place throughout the year in ‚ÅÝGlasgow, Edinburgh, London and Smith's birthplace of Kirkcaldy on the Scottish coast.
In a sign of his lasting imprint on the popular imagination, the ghost of Smith made an appearance last year as a character in a satirical musical staged during the Edinburgh festival about the 2008 collapse of Royal Bank of Scotland.
There are, however, limits to how much Smith can be re-branded a progressive or anything else in today's terms.
While he criticised the rich and argued the accumulation of wealth by the few led to the ‚ÅÝpoverty of many, ‚ÄãMehta argued that Smith, like many of his contemporary thinkers, would have been comfortable with levels of inequality not acceptable today.
Still others - including Karl ‚ÄãMarx decades later - have criticised Smith's production-enhancing ideas on the division of labour into small tasks as being deployed in factories to leave workers with morale-sapping, mind-numbing jobs.
Nonetheless, economic historian Richard van den Berg, professor at Goldsmiths, University of London, said the many questions and differing interpretations surrounding the ‚Äãbook had clearly not diminished its appeal to subsequent generations.
"It is a tool," he said. "A tool for producing ideas."

item.title: Two Israeli soldiers killed in southern Lebanon, Israel military says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli military said on Sunday that two ‚Äãof its soldiers were ‚Äãkilled in southern Lebanon, marking ‚ÅÝthe first fatalities ‚Äãamong its troops ‚Äãsince Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week as Israeli intensified air ‚Äãstrikes on ‚ÄãLebanon.
The military did not immediately ‚Äåprovide ‚ÅÝfurther details on the circumstances of the incident.
Clashes have continued ‚Äãalong ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝIsrael-Lebanon frontier amid heightened tensions ‚Äãbetween Israel and ‚ÄãHezbollah ‚ÅÝfollowing the U.S.-Israeli military campaign on Iran .

item.title: Swiss voters set to reject plan to cut funding for state broadcaster
item.description:
ZURICH, March 8 (Reuters) - Swiss voters looked set on Sunday to reject a referendum to slash public broadcaster SRG‚Äôs funding, with early ‚Äãprojections showing 62% opposing a plan to cut the annual ‚Äãlicence fee, a move critics warned would weaken media ‚ÅÝand fuel disinformation.
The campaign wanted to reduce the annual licence fee ‚Äãthat all Swiss households must pay from 335 Swiss francs ($431.87) to ‚Äã200 francs.
Supporters, mainly from right-wing groups including the Swiss People's Party (SVP), had argued that the charge ‚Äì the highest in the world ‚Äì was too expensive, and that ‚ÄãSRG, which runs 17 radio stations and seven TV channels ‚Äãin four languages had become too bloated.
They also said the SRG was not politically ‚Äåindependent, ‚ÅÝand had a left-wing bias in its coverage.
Opponents had said the move reflected pressure on public media organisations from the political right, which has accused national broadcasters ‚Äãglobally of being ‚Äãpolitically biased ‚ÅÝagainst them.
News, sports and cultural coverage would suffer, opponents said, while an SRG weakened by lower funding ‚Äãcould mean that disinformation would be easier to spread.
"A ‚Äãmajor ‚ÅÝdismantling of Switzerland's media infrastructure has been prevented," said Laura Zimmermann, leader of the campaign against the cuts. "Our access to reliable information remains ‚ÅÝprotected."
"We remain ‚Äãfully committed to accompanying the public ‚Äãin their everyday lives with a diverse and high-quality programme," said Susanne Wille, SRG ‚Äãdirector general.
($1 = 0.7757 Swiss francs)

item.title: Greece sees deal to buy Italian-made frigates by April, minister says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Greece hopes to wrap up talks and sign a deal ‚Äãfor the purchase of Bergamini class ‚Äåfrigates from Italy by April, Defence Minister Nikos Dendias said in an interview ‚Äãreleased on Sunday.
High-ranking officials from ‚ÄãGreece and Italy last year signed memorandums for ‚ÅÝnaval cooperation, opening the way ‚Äãfor the acquisition of two FREMM frigates ‚Äãby Athens with an option for two more.
"We hope that negotiations with Italy will ‚Äãbe concluded and that we ‚Äãwill have signed (a deal) for the acquisition of ‚Äå2+2 ‚ÅÝfrigates by April," Dendias told a Greek newspaper.
Dendias said Athens was working to ensure the frigates could also ‚Äãcarry ‚Äãthe new-generation ‚ÅÝELSA missile.
Greece plans to spend about 28 billion euros ‚Äãas part of a multi-year ‚Äãdefence ‚ÅÝplan that includes the purchase from France and ‚ÅÝnew ‚Äãsubmarines as it tries ‚Äãto keep up with historic rival Turkey.

item.title: Colombians vote in legislative contest, presidential primaries
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 8 (Reuters) - Colombians were heading to the polls on Sunday to elect a new Congress and choose three of the presidential candidates who ‚Äãwill contest elections this May, a vote that will shape the next ‚Äåpresident's ability to push through legislation and fulfill their agenda.
Voters will choose from over 3,000 candidates to fill 102 Senate seats and 182 House seats, in an election analysts predict will ‚Äãbe divided among some two dozen parties, likely forcing the next president ‚Äãto form a coalition government.
Some 41.2 million eligible voters will also ‚ÅÝbe able to participate in consultations through which some right-wing, centrist and left-wing ‚Äãparties will select presidential candidates. Several leading candidates - including leftist Ivan Cepeda, right-winger Abelardo ‚ÄãDe La Espriella and centrist Sergio Fajardo - will not participate in Sunday's primaries.
"It is very important to come and exercise the right to vote. The most important thing is for Colombia to ‚Äãdecide its future and for the results to be respected," said Federico Rodriguez, ‚Äãa 32-year-old business administrator, after voting in Bogota's north.
"It is a source of pride that we ‚Äåcan ‚ÅÝgo out and exercise our right to vote and to democracy, but I also have uncertainty about the results, about knowing what Colombia's future will be in the next four years," said university student Isabella Suarez, 21.
The polls are open from 8 ‚Äãa.m. local time (1300 ‚ÄãGMT) until 4 ‚ÅÝp.m. (2100 GMT).
President Gustavo Petro, whose term ends in August, has repeatedly questioned the software that will be used for the vote ‚Äãcount in the elections, alleging possible irregularities, while National Registrar ‚ÄãHernan Penagos ‚ÅÝhas guaranteed the transparency of the vote and said party observers can verify the software's results.
Some 246,000 members of the military forces and the national police have been placed ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãhigh alert to prevent attacks by illegal armed ‚Äãgroups seeking to disrupt the election or pressure voters to cast their ballots for certain candidates, Defense Minister ‚ÄãPedro Sanchez said this week.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine has unique drone experience, discusses joint production with Dutch PM
item.description:
KYIV, March 8 (Reuters) - Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he and Dutch Prime Minister Rob Jetten ‚Äãdiscussed joint arms production during his visit to Kyiv on ‚ÄåSunday, and he stressed Ukraine's unique experience in defending against Iranian-made drones used by Russia.
"It is important that we are producing weapons together with ‚Äãthe Netherlands ‚Äì and we will certainly continue and expand this ‚Äãjoint work," Zelenskiy said on X, adding they had ‚ÅÝdiscussed investments and possible production volumes in detail.
In recent months, Ukraine ‚Äãhas stepped up efforts to establish joint arms production with European ‚Äãcountries, opening several factories to manufacture drones.
As Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine ‚Äãis hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage ‚Äãwith allies by showing them that the expertise it has built during four ‚Äåyears ‚ÅÝof Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
Zelenskiy said that Kyiv has unique experience in combating drones, which Iran actively uses in its attacks on Middle Eastern countries.
He said ‚Äãthat Ukraine is ready ‚Äãto sell ‚ÅÝthe interceptor drones that are not used by the Ukrainian military, and the first group of Ukrainian ‚Äãmilitary instructors will head to the Middle East ‚Äãon Monday.
"They ‚ÅÝare coming with the means to help," Zelenskiy noted, adding that Ukraine would provide the expertise and experience in defending against "Shahed" model drones ‚ÅÝand cruise ‚Äãmissiles.
Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones ‚Äãdesigned to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles have said they have the capacity to export ‚Äãin large volumes.

item.title: Two killed, 12 injured after projectile fell on residential location in Saudi Arabia
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Two people were ‚Äãkilled and 12 ‚Äåinjured after a projectile fell on ‚Äãa residential ‚Äãlocation in Saudi Arabia's ‚ÅÝAl-Kharj city ‚Äãon Sunday, the ‚ÄãSaudi Civil Defense said.
The two people killed ‚Äãwere of ‚ÄãIndian and Bangladeshi nationalities, ‚Äåit ‚ÅÝadded in a post on X.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãearlier ‚ÅÝon Sunday that they ‚Äãtargeted radar ‚Äãsystems ‚ÅÝin locations including Saudi's Al-Kharj.

item.title: Israeli strike kills two Palestinians in a car in Gaza, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - An Israeli air strike killed at least two Palestinians in Gaza City on Sunday, local health officials ‚Äãsaid, the deadliest incident in Gaza since Israel and ‚Äåthe United States launched their war against Iran a week ago.
Medics said the two people were travelling in a car near Al-Azhar University ‚Äãin western Gaza City. Their identities were not immediately ‚Äãclear. Several other people in the area were ‚ÅÝwounded in the strike, the health officials added.
Such attacks have ‚Äãdeclined since the start of the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran, ‚Äãalthough Israeli forces have killed several Palestinians over the past week.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment about ‚ÄãSunday's strike.
Israel and Hamas agreed to a U.S.-brokered Gaza ‚Äãceasefire deal that kicked off last October, but violence has continued on ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝnear-daily basis. Both sides have blamed the other for the violation of the truce agreement.
The Gaza health ministry said at least 640 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli fire ‚Äãsince October. Israel ‚Äãsays four ‚ÅÝsoldiers have been killed by militants in Gaza over the same period.
Gaza has been devastated ‚Äãby more than two years of an ‚ÄãIsraeli onslaught ‚ÅÝthat killed over 72,000 Palestinians, according to local health authorities, and left much of the enclave in ruins.
The war was ‚ÅÝsparked ‚Äãby Hamas' October 7, 2023 attack ‚Äãon Israel, where the militants killed 1,200 people and took over 250 hostages, ‚Äãaccording to Israeli tallies.

item.title: Iran's hardline succession challenged by shrinking loyalist base
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's next leader faces a massive external assault and growing internal anger, and while previous leaders relied on a hardcore of diehard ideologues in the population, it is far from clear how far his ‚Äãsuccessor can do the same.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed by U.S. and Israeli strikes a week ago and his hardline son Mojtaba Khamenei is seen as a frontrunner ‚Äåto succeed him.
Yet whether loyalists remain as numerous or as committed as in earlier decades - and whether they would rally behind a figure like Mojtaba - is increasingly uncertain.
Reuters interviews with three Basij members, as well as ordinary Iranians, officials, insiders and political analysts, point to a much narrower support base than the Islamic Republic once enjoyed.
"The strategy in choosing a hardliner as the new leader would be to consolidate the base, but they're ending up with an ‚Äãincreasingly small circle of supporters," said Ali Ansari, a modern history professor at the University of St Andrews in the UK.
"And the longer this goes on, the more it will ‚Äãall fray at the edges," he said.
The Islamic Republic emerged from a 1979 revolution backed by millions of Iranians. But decades of rule marked by corruption, ‚ÅÝrepression and mismanagement have thinned that support, alienating many ordinary people.
Still, a core of loyalists remains ‚Äî people who repeatedly show up at the ballot box to back the Islamic system and who ‚Äãturn out on the streets to crush opposition protests.
Highly organised and able to mobilise quickly, they still pose a major obstacle to any U.S. or Israeli hopes of effecting regime change.
"We have given many ‚Äãmartyrs. They have sacrificed themselves for our leader. Now we must show that the path of the leader Khamenei continues. We will solve any problems and support whoever is chosen as leader. We will even give our lives for him," said Mahdi Rastegari, 32, a religion teacher and member of the Basij, an official volunteer militia.
In the last presidential election, the most hardline candidate, Saeed Jalili, won around 9 million votes in the first round ‚Äãand 13 million in the second, according to official results. Over 61 million of Iran's more than 85 million people were eligible to vote that year.
However, the hardliners' apparent status as a small ‚Äãminority in Iran does not give much hope to all those who hope for change, and the continued bombardment has given rise to fears of chaos.
"The Guards and the system are still powerful. They have tens ‚Äåof thousands of ‚ÅÝforces ready to fight to keep this regime in place. We, the people, have nothing," said Babak, 34, a businessman in Arak who asked to keep his family name secret.
With their leader killed on the first day of the war and cracks emerging within the country's hierarchy, the extent of continued hardline support for the Islamic Republic will now be tested like never before.
Men like the Basij member Rastegari represent a network of power extending from the supreme leader's now bombed-out office in central Tehran to every village and city neighbourhood that stands in the way of any internal ‚Äãopposition.
Every night since Khamenei's death, hardliners have held ‚Äãstate-backed mourning ceremonies for him despite the bombs ‚ÅÝraining down across the country.
Among their number are true believers ready to die as martyrs for their fervent belief in rule by a divinely guided cleric, and those with more mercenary motives who have benefited from their status as public supporters of the system.
Another Basij member, Ali Mohammad Hosseini, goes ‚Äãfrom work at his father's grocery shop in the Shi'ite Muslim seminary city of Qom to spend his evenings manning checkpoints to deter ‚Äãany flashes of public dissent.
"The ‚ÅÝmost important issue is preserving the regime, which is what the Americans are targeting," the 29-year-old said, saying he would support whichever cleric replaced Khamenei as a "religious duty" that he was prepared to die for.
That degree of commitment is not universal, however. Another Basij member, who asked to give only his first name Hassan, and his location in the Shi'ite shrine city of Mashhad, said he had doubts the Islamic Republic ‚ÅÝwould survive.
"We ‚Äãneed to be realistic," he said, pointing to continued U.S. pressure and the ruinous aftermath of pulverising airstrikes if a ‚Äãhardliner like Mojtaba Khamenei is named as the new leader.
Members of the Basij and others who demonstrate loyalty to the system have for decades enjoyed privileges including preferential places at university, job offers and subsidised bank loans - but a collapsing economy ‚Äãcould end such perks.
"We do not even have airports any more. No ports. How are they going to rebuild this economy?" Hassan, 29, said.

item.title: Syria's Kurds caution Iran's Kurds against aligning with US against Tehran
item.description:
QAMISHLI, Syria March 8 (Reuters) - Kurdish residents of northeast ‚ÄãSyria warned Iran's Kurds against aligning with the U.S. to fight the Iranian government, citing their own experience in Syria in recent ‚Äåmonths as evidence their Iranian counterparts would be "abandoned."
Iranian Kurdish militias based in northern Iraq have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether and how to attack security forces in western Iran, as the United States and Israel pound Iran with air strikes , Reuters has reported .
But Syria's Kurds warned their Iranian counterparts against partnering with Washington.
"I hope that the Kurds of ‚ÄãIran will not ally themselves with America, because they will abandon them," said Saad Ali, a 45-year-old resident of the northeastern Syrian Kurdish ‚Äãtown of Qamishli.
"Tomorrow, if an agreement is made between them (the U.S.) and the Iranians, they will eliminate you. Do not ‚ÅÝmake our mistakes," he told Reuters.
Syrian Kurdish fighters aligned with the U.S. more than a decade ago to fight the Islamic State group, setting up ‚Äãtheir own semi-autonomous zone in the territory they had seized from the ultraconservative Islamist fighters.
But in January, Syria's new army under President Ahmed al-Sharaa captured most of ‚Äãthe Kurdish-held areas in a sweeping offensive. Syria's Kurds called on the U.S. to intervene on their behalf, and felt betrayed when Washington instead urged them to merge with Sharaa's forces.
'A NEGATIVE EXPERIENCE' WITH THE U.S.
It remains a bitter experience for Syria's Kurds and a lesson they say should be heeded by Iranian Kurds.
"In my opinion, the Kurds in Iran should ‚Äãmaintain a firm stance: they will not engage in any wars within Iranian territory without firm, signed guarantees from the United States regarding the future of ‚Äãthese Kurdish regions in Iran," said Amjad Kardo, a 26-year-old Syrian Kurd in Qamishli.
"We Kurds here in Syria, in particular, have had a negative experience with the Americans in ‚ÄåSyria, and ‚ÅÝtheir abandonment of Kurdish resistance movements."
An Iranian Kurdish source said Kurdish leaders did have concerns about being "betrayed" like the Kurdish groups in northern Syria.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday it would be "wonderful" if Kurdish forces crossed the border from northern Iraq into Iran, but declined to answer a question on whether the U.S. would offer them air support if they did so.
On ‚ÄãSaturday, he appeared to switch positions, ‚Äãtelling reporters he doesn't want Kurdish ‚ÅÝfighters going into Iran.
Ahmed Barakat, head of the Kurdish Progressive Democratic Party in Syria, told Reuters that Iranian Kurdish forces should exercise "extreme caution".
Barakat said the decision was ultimately up to them, but he believed that "accepting the ‚Äãinvitation of the United States and being considered the spearhead in confronting or weakening the Iranian regime is ‚Äãnot, at present, in ‚ÅÝthe best interest of the Kurds of Iran."
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, Reuters reported last week .
The Kurds are an ethnic group which was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the ‚ÅÝcollapsing Ottoman ‚ÄãEmpire.
Mostly Sunni Muslims, they speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous ‚Äãregion straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey.
In Iraq, they inhabit three northern provinces led by their own regional government. But in other countries - Iran, Turkey and now Syria - ‚Äãtheir dreams of an autonomous region or state have remained out of reach.

item.title: Brazil President Lula's annual medical checkup test results are normal
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 8 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, ‚Äãknown as Lula, underwent ‚Äåhis annual medical checkup on Saturday morning at Sao Paulo‚Äôs Sirio-Libanes ‚ÄãHospital and all test ‚Äãresults were normal, the hospital ‚ÅÝsaid on Sunday.
Lula will receive ‚Äãroutine followup care with his ‚Äãmedical team, led by cardiologist Roberto Kalil Filho and physician Ana Helena ‚ÄãGermoglio, the hospital said ‚Äãin a statement, adding there are currently ‚Äåno ‚ÅÝplans for additional exams.
Lula, 80, said in 2025 that he plans to seek reelection later ‚Äãthis year. ‚ÄãIn ‚ÅÝ2024, he underwent two operations to relieve and ‚Äãprevent bleeding in his ‚Äãskull. ‚ÅÝHe has been Brazil's president two times - from 2003 to ‚ÅÝ2010, ‚Äãand again since ‚ÄãJanuary 2023.

item.title: Russia says units intercept 170 Ukrainian drones over four hours
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Russia's Defence Ministry said on Sunday ‚Äãits air defence units ‚Äåhad intercepted 170 drones over various parts ‚Äãof central and ‚Äãsouthern Russia over a ‚ÅÝfour-hour period, including two ‚Äãdrones headed for ‚ÄãMoscow.
No damage or casualties were reported.
The ministry figures, higher ‚Äãthan average, covered ‚Äãthe period of 2 p.m. ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝ6 p.m. (1100-1500 GMT). The highest number - 73 - was recorded in Bryansk ‚Äãregion ‚Äãbordering ‚ÅÝUkraine.
Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin, writing on ‚ÄãTelegram, also reported ‚Äãtwo ‚ÅÝdrones downed while heading for the capital, ‚ÅÝwith ‚Äãemergency crews ‚Äãexamining fragments at the point ‚Äãof impact.

item.title: US considers idea of special operation to seize Iran‚Äôs uranium, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald Trump ‚Äåis weighing the ‚Äãoption of ‚Äãdeploying special forces ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãthe ground ‚Äãto seize Iran‚Äôs near-bomb-grade ‚Äãuranium, ‚ÄãBloomberg News reported ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝSunday, citing three diplomatic officials ‚Äãbriefed ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝthe matter.
Reuters ‚Äãcould ‚Äãnot ‚ÅÝimmediately verify the ‚ÅÝreport.

item.title: German Greens lead Chancellor Merz's conservatives in state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 8 (Reuters) - Germany's environmental Greens are set to win a state election in the southern region of Baden-Wuerttemberg, leaving them poised to continue a ‚Äãcoalition with Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservatives, initial forecasts showed after polls ‚Äåclosed on Sunday.
The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, which has made strong gains in recent years, came third, confirming its position as Germany's main opposition party, even outside its heartland in ‚Äãthe former communist eastern states.
The first forecasts on public broadcaster ARD showed ‚Äãthe Greens in first place with 32% of the vote, followed ‚ÅÝby Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) on 29% and the AfD on 17.5%.
Merz's coalition partners ‚Äãin Berlin, the centre-left Social Democrats were on course for just 5.5% of ‚Äãthe vote, confirming the precipitous drop in support seen in recent years but just scraping past the minimum threshold to enter state parliament.
Baden-Wuerttemberg, home to Mercedes-Benz (MBGn.DE) , opens new tab , Porsche (P911_p.DE) , opens new tab , Bosch (ROBG.UL) and one of the ‚Äãtraditional strongholds of the German car industry, is one of Germany's most prosperous ‚Äãregions.
But its auto sector and wider economy have been hit hard by increasing competition from ‚ÄåChina's surging ‚ÅÝelectric vehicles makers.
After a decade of Green-CDU coalition government in Baden-Wurttemberg, the moderate Green candidate for state premier, Cem Ozdemir is unlikely to make life difficult for Merz in the Bundesrat, the upper house of parliament that represents the states ‚Äãin Germany's federal system.
But ‚Äãthe result underlines ‚ÅÝMerz's struggles to cut through with voters, as promised reforms have stalled and a sluggish economy comes back slowly from ‚Äãtwo years in recession.
Initially trailing in the race, the Greens ‚Äãovertook the ‚ÅÝChristian Democrats as the ballot approached and the result is likely to fuel discontent among conservatives already alarmed at the record low approval levels for Merz's government in ‚ÅÝBerlin.
The Baden-Wuerttemberg ‚Äãelection was the first of five state elections ‚Äãthis year, with voters in neighbouring Rhineland-Palatinate due to go to the polls on March 22, followed ‚Äãby Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenberg-Vorpommern in September.

item.title: Iraqi oil production collapses with Strait of Hormuz blocked by conflict, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 8 (Reuters) - Iraqi oil ‚Äåproduction from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to ‚Äãthe Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday.
Production from the fields ‚Äãstood at around 4.3 million bpd before the war.
"Crude storage has ‚ÅÝreached maximum capacity and the remaining output after the major cut will be ‚Äãused to supply the country‚Äôs refineries," said an official with the state‚Äërun Basra Oil ‚ÄãCompany (BOC), which manages production and export operations from the southern fields.
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow, strategically vital waterway between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is one of the ‚Äãworld's key oil transit chokepoints, carrying roughly a fifth of global oil and ‚Äãliquefied natural gas flows.
The OPEC member's exports also fell sharply to an average of around 800,000 ‚Äåbarrels ‚ÅÝper day on Sunday, with only two tankers loading because vessels cannot move freely through the strait to Iraq's southern terminals, the source said.
With no new tankers able to reach Iraq‚Äôs southern terminals, exports from there are expected to halt completely ‚Äãat around 8 p.m. ‚Äãlocal time (1700 GMT), ‚ÅÝsaid two oil officials with direct knowledge of terminal operations.
Iraq‚Äôs oil exports from the southern oilfields stood at 3.334 million ‚Äãbarrels per day in February, an oil ministry document showed.
A ‚Äãdrop in ‚ÅÝIraq's oil production and exports is set to strain the country‚Äôs already fragile finances, as the state relies on crude sales for nearly all public spending and more than ‚ÅÝ90% ‚Äãof its income.
"This is the most serious operational threat ‚ÄãIraq has faced in more than 20 years," a senior Iraqi oil ministry official said.

item.title: Iranian army says at least 104 killed in US attack on Iranian warship last week
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Iranian army said on ‚ÄãSunday that at ‚Äåleast 104 people were killed and 32 ‚Äãwere wounded ‚Äãin an attack by ‚ÅÝthe U.S. on ‚Äãan Iranian warship ‚Äãoff Sri Lanka's coast last week.
A U.S. submarine sank ‚Äãthe frigate Dena ‚Äãin the Indian Ocean ‚Äåabout ‚ÅÝ19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city ‚Äãof Galle ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝWednesday, killing dozens of ‚Äãsailors and dramatically ‚Äãwidening ‚ÅÝWashington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

item.title: New York City police identify device outside Mamdani's home as explosive
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - New York City Police said on Sunday a device that was ignited and thrown during protests outside Mayor Zohran Mamdani‚Äôs home over the weekend was an explosive that ‚Äãcould have caused serious injury or death.
The device, a jar filled with nuts, ‚Äåbolts and screws and wrapped in black tape with a fuse, was thrown by a counterprotester on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion, but it extinguished itself before any explosion, according to a statement from New York ‚ÄãCity Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch. Two people were in custody, Tisch said.
The device was ‚Äãone of two that were thrown during the protests, which were led ‚ÅÝby two opposing groups, according to police. The second device was still being examined, Tisch ‚Äãsaid.
Far-right activist Jake Lang led a protest on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion - where Mamdani lives ‚Äãwith his wife - against a purported Islamic ‚Äútakeover‚Äù of New York City and against public prayer by Muslims. Tisch said at a press conference on Saturday that she did not believe Mamdani and his wife were home ‚Äãat the time.
In a statement on Sunday, Mamdani condemned Lang‚Äôs protest but said the violence ‚Äãthat followed it was more disturbing.
‚ÄúViolence at a protest is never acceptable,‚Äù Mamdani said. ‚ÄúThe attempt to use ‚Äåan explosive ‚ÅÝdevice and hurt others is not only criminal, it is reprehensible and the antithesis of who we are.‚Äù
Lang‚Äôs protest, which consisted of about 20 people, was opposed by a far larger counterprotest of 125 people aimed at running ‚ÄúNazis‚Äù out of New York, Tisch said at a ‚Äãpress conference on Saturday. ‚ÄãPolice said Emir ‚ÅÝBalat, 18, was among the counterprotesters before he lit and threw the device.
The device rolled near police before it extinguished itself, Tisch said ‚Äãon Saturday. Balat ran after throwing it and eventually lit and dropped ‚Äãa second ‚ÅÝdevice in the street, according to Tisch.
Balat and another man, whom police on Sunday identified as Ibrahim Kayumi, were arrested at the scene, Tisch said. New York police are working with the ‚ÅÝFBI and ‚Äãthe U.S. Department of Justice on the investigation.
Tisch said ‚Äãon Saturday there was no immediate indication the incident was related to the U.S. attack on Iran , but that authorities ‚Äãwere still investigating.

item.title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's Assembly ‚Äãof Experts has named ‚ÄåMojtaba Khamenei to replace his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as ‚Äãthe country's new ‚Äãsupreme leader, state media reported ‚ÅÝon Sunday.
Mojtaba , a mid-ranking ‚Äãcleric with close ties ‚Äãto the powerful Revolutionary Guards, had long been viewed by elements ‚Äãof Iran's ruling ‚Äãestablishment as a potential successor to ‚Äåhis ‚ÅÝfather, who was killed after the United States and Israel launched strikes on ‚ÄãIran.
Although ‚ÄãIran's ‚ÅÝruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary ‚Äãsuccession, he has ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝpowerful following within the Guards and his dead father's ‚ÅÝstill-influential ‚Äãoffice.

item.title: Iran defies Trump, elevates Khamenei's son Mojtaba as successor
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran's clerical leadership chose confrontation over compromise in appointing Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father, Ali Khamenei, a move regional officials say is a direct rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared the son "unacceptable".
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was ‚Äãkilled in a U.S.-Israeli strike at the start of the conflict, now in its second week .
The appointment of Mojtaba as his successor by the Assembly of Experts locks hardliners firmly ‚Äåin control in Tehran - a gamble that could reshape Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel and reverberate far beyond the Middle East.
"Having Mojtaba take over is the same playbook," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute.
"It's a big humiliation for the United States to carry out an operation of this scale, risk so much, and end up killing an 86-year-old man, only to have him replaced by his hardline son.‚Äù
Under Iran's complex, theocratic system, the supreme ‚Äãleader is the ultimate authority, including over foreign policy and Iran‚Äôs nuclear programme, as well as guiding the elected president and parliament.
Analysts say the choice ‚Äãof Mojtaba, a deeply hardline cleric whose wife, mother and other family members were also killed in U.S.‚ÄìIsraeli strikes - sends an unequivocal message: Iran‚Äôs leadership ‚ÅÝhas rejected any prospect of compromise to preserve the system and sees no path forward except confrontation, revenge and endurance.
According to insiders, Mojtaba will face immense internal and external strain from a disaffected population and ‚Äãan escalating conflict, but is expected to move swiftly to consolidate power.
That will likely mean expanded authority for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, harsher domestic controls and sweeping repression to crush dissent.
"The world will miss ‚Äãthe era of his father," a regional official close to Tehran told Reuters. "Mojtaba will have no choice but to show an iron fist... even if the war ends, there will be severe internal repression."
That stance comes after months of deepening domestic unrest - the bloodiest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution - that had already weakened the Islamic Republic before the war began.
Iran was grappling with a battered economy, soaring inflation, currency collapse and widening poverty, alongside tightening repression that had fuelled public anger and ‚Äãprotests - pressures now likely to intensify under wartime rule.
Difficult days lie ahead under Mojtaba, with far tighter internal controls, intensified pressure at home and an even more aggressive, hostile posture abroad, said ‚Äãanother Iranian insider familiar with the situation on the ground.
Paul Salem, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said Mojtaba was not a figure positioned to strike a deal with the United States or pivot diplomatically.
‚ÄúNobody emerging now ‚Äåis going ‚ÅÝto be able to compromise,‚Äù Salem said. ‚ÄúThis is a hardline choice, made in a hardline moment.‚Äù
In the eyes of Iran‚Äôs clerics, many of whom famously label America the "Great Satan", the assassination of Khamenei, the Islamic Republic's highest religious authority, has elevated him to "martyrdom".
Clerics have cast the slain leader as a heroic figure, likening him to Imam Hussein - the Shi‚Äôite symbol of sacrifice and resistance against oppression.
"Mojtaba is even worse and more hardline than his father," said Alan Eyre, former U.S. diplomat and Iran specialist, adding that he was the preferred candidate of the Guards. "He's going to have a lot of revenge to exact."
That calculus carries risks. Israel ‚Äãhas warned that any successor to Khamenei would also ‚Äãbe a target, while Trump has said ‚ÅÝthe war may only end once Iran‚Äôs military leadership and ruling elite are eliminated .
A powerful mid‚Äëranking cleric, Mojtaba, 56, has long opposed reformist groups advocating engagement with the West. His close ties to senior clerics and the IRGC - which dominates Iran‚Äôs security forces and its economy - give him leverage ‚Äãacross the state‚Äôs political and coercive security institutions.
He amassed influence under his father as a key figure within the security apparatus and the vast business ‚Äãempire it controls, operating for ‚ÅÝyears as Ali Khamenei‚Äôs gatekeeper and, in practice, a ‚Äúmini-supreme leader‚Äù, analysts say.
His elevation comes as the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran intensifies, with joint strikes hitting fuel depots and other targets inside Iran, while Iranian missiles and drones have struck Gulf states, widening the conflict.
Mojtaba studied under conservative clerics in the seminaries of Qom, the heart of Shi‚Äôite theological learning, and holds the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the ‚ÅÝsupreme leader ‚Äãin an official capacity despite never holding elected or formal government office.
A Gulf source familiar with regional government thinking said of ‚ÄãMojtaba‚Äôs appointment: ‚ÄúThis tells Trump and Washington that Iran will not back down, they will fight on until the finish."
Salem, of the Middle East Institute, likened Iran‚Äôs trajectory to Iraq under Saddam Hussein after 1991 or Syria under Bashar al‚ÄëAssad after 2012 ‚Äî governments that ‚Äãsurvived years of war and isolation but steadily lost control.
‚ÄúThey‚Äôre doubling down on the hard line,‚Äù Salem said. ‚ÄúInternally, it‚Äôs terrible ‚Äî and deeply destabilising.‚Äù

item.title: Jailed Istanbul mayor faces trial amid crackdown on Turkey's opposition
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 9 (Reuters) - Istanbul's jailed mayor Ekrem Imamoglu goes on ‚Äåtrial on Monday in a sweeping corruption case that could extinguish the ambition of Tayyip Erdogan's main rival to become president at Turkey's next election.
Imamoglu, 55, is the chief suspect among more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul ‚Äãmunicipality accused of corruption and bribery. He and his main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) deny ‚Äãthe charges, which carry prison sentences adding up to hundreds of years.
The case ‚ÅÝis being held at a courthouse in the Silivri prison complex west of Istanbul, where Imamoglu ‚Äãhas been held for almost a year, and is the centrepiece of a judicial crackdown on the CHP ‚Äãthat is overshadowing politics ahead of elections that are expected to be held next year.
In comments to Reuters , Imamoglu struck a defiant tone, saying Erdogan should call elections right away, but his prospects of challenging the veteran leader look ‚Äãbleak amid a crackdown that rights groups and foreign observers say has undermined the democratic credentials of ‚ÄãNATO member Turkey.
"(I)t's hard to avoid the conclusion that prosecutors are trying to remove Imamoglu from politics and discredit ‚Äåhis ‚ÅÝparty in ways that undermine democracy," said Benjamin Ward, the deputy Europe and Central Asia director at Human Rights Watch, describing the trial as coming after more than a year of "weaponising" the criminal justice system against the CHP.
The government denies exerting influence over the judiciary, which it says is independent.
Newly appointed ‚ÄãJustice Minister Akin Gurlek , who ‚Äãas chief Istanbul ‚ÅÝprosecutor led investigations into Imamoglu, said he carried out his duties in the case without bias or consideration for the mayor's position.
"I simply did my duty ‚Äãas a public prosecutor. My conscience is clear," Gurlek told reporters in ‚Äãcomments published ‚ÅÝon Friday.
Imamoglu has already suffered a severe blow to his presidential ambitions, with a court in January rejecting his lawsuit challenging the cancellation of his university degree ‚Äì a qualification required of any presidential candidate.
Erdogan, 72, has led ‚ÅÝTurkey ‚Äãas prime minister or president since 2003. Presidential and parliamentary elections ‚Äãare not scheduled until 2028 but if he wishes to seek a third term as president he is obliged to hold ‚Äãthe vote early, unless the constitutional term limits are changed.

item.title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil ‚Äåand predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ‚Äãpurchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
"It's a 30-day pause to allow, which ‚Äãis just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of ‚ÅÝoil that are sitting out on ships to go to Indian refineries," Waltz said on NBC's "Meet the Press."
Wright ‚Äãtold CNN's "State of the Union" that the waiver can help "tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price ‚Äãspikes and the concerns we see in the marketplace."
With the war now in its second week and no end in sight, Americans are grappling with higher prices at the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.
As ‚Äãof Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, up 11% from the previous ‚Äãweek and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% from a ‚Äåweek ‚ÅÝago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.
"We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a world where energy prices are returned back to where they were," Wright said on the "Fox News Sunday" program.
There is no shortage of oil or natural gas, said Wright, who asserted that the price increases are based on "fear ‚Äãand perception" that the Iran ‚Äãoperation will be a ‚ÅÝdrawn-out affair.
"But it won't be," Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump 's prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.
Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices ‚Äãwill "drop very rapidly" when the war is over.
Senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, ‚Äãcriticized energy speculators.
"The ‚ÅÝoil prices have gone up because you've got a bunch of oil traders out there in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding up the price," Kennedy said on "Fox News Sunday."
