Political analysts say a persistent rise in ‚ÅÝgasoline prices ‚Äãcould hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of ‚Äãthe U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump's characterization of the economy as "booming."

item.title: Europe now world's biggest arms importer, think tank says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 9 (Reuters) - Europe became the world's biggest arms importer over the last five years as governments reacted to Russia's threat and waning confidence in ‚ÄãU.S. security commitments, data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute ‚Äåshowed on Monday.
European countries more than trebled their arms imports in 2021-25 versus 2016-2020 to supply Ukraine in its defence against Russian invasion and to rebuild their own militaries ‚Äãafter decades of under-investment.
"The sharp increase in arms flows to European ‚Äãstates pushed global arms transfers up almost 10 per cent," Mathew ‚ÅÝGeorge, Director of the SIPRI Arms Transfers Programme, said.
Europe accounted for 33% ‚Äãof global arms imports, up from 12% in the previous five-year period, the ‚Äãreport showed.
Despite boosting domestic production, European countries continued to increase purchases of U.S. weapons, especially combat aircraft and long-range air-defence systems.
Arms imports by Middle Eastern states fell 13%, though ‚ÄãSaudi Arabia and Qatar were among the top four individual buyers.
The decline ‚Äãmainly reflected large Saudi orders during the previous period that still needed to be integrated, ‚ÄåSIPRI ‚ÅÝsenior researcher Pieter Wezeman said.
Even before the latest U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran , states in the region had placed significant new orders that have yet to show up in the figures.
The current conflict is likely to lift procurement ‚Äãfurther, particularly of anti-missile ‚Äãand air-defence ‚ÅÝsystems.
"They will first replace what they have used, but also that they are going to look at buying more equipment ‚Äãto protect themselves even better than they can do ‚Äãnow," Wezeman ‚ÅÝsaid.
In terms of suppliers, the U.S. increased its global share of the arms export market to 42% from 36%, cementing its dominance. France was the second-largest supplier ‚ÅÝwith ‚Äã9.8% of global exports, while Russia's share dropped ‚Äãto 6.8% from 21% after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Europe's combined export share was 28% - four ‚Äãtimes Russia's and five times China's.

item.title: Oman's Muscat airport limits private jet flights, email says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Oman's Muscat International Airport has asked private jet operators to avoid using the site for "additional flights," giving priority to government ‚Äãand commercial flights as fresh airspace closures hit the region‚Äôs ‚Äåattempts to increase travel, according to an email seen by Reuters.
"Due to the current crisis management measures at Muscat International Airport, flight movements are restricted ‚Äãto approved seasonal scheduled services only," the airport authorities ‚Äãtold charter plane operators on Friday.
The airport said this measure ‚ÅÝwas necessary to manage congestion and ensure that airport capacity ‚Äãremains within acceptable limits.
The email, first reported by the Financial Times, ‚Äãasked that all airlines and operators cancel any slots falling outside the approved seasonal schedule and to refrain from submitting non-approved flight requests until further notice.
"The ‚Äãonly additional flights that may be considered during this period are ‚Äãembassy-sponsored repatriation flights, subject to prior approval through the applicable diplomatic channels and ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝthe condition that no commercial sale of seats is undertaken in connection with such operations," the email added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran has led to flight cancellations across the Middle East , leaving airlines and ‚Äãgovernments scrambling to ‚Äãsupport thousands of ‚ÅÝstranded passengers.
With most airspace in the region still closed over missile and drone concerns, some people ‚Äãhave turned to private jets while charter flights and ‚Äãlimited ‚ÅÝcommercial services struggle to evacuate tens of thousands of travelers.
Travelers have shelled out high prices to get out of the Middle East, rushing ‚ÅÝto airports ‚Äãor heading over land to quieter ‚Äãhubs, with fighter jets occasionally escorting passenger jets.

item.title: Australia tells families of diplomats to leave UAE as conflict escalates
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australia has asked the family members and dependants of diplomatic officials in the ‚ÄãUnited Arab Emirates to leave the country, ‚Äåfollowing an escalation in the conflict in the Middle East as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.
U.S. ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump told reporters on Air Force ‚ÄãOne that he was not seeking negotiations ‚ÅÝto end the U.S.-Israeli military strikes against Iran, ‚Äãwhich have driven up global energy prices, disrupted ‚Äãbusiness and snarled air travel.

item.title: As Fukushima memories fade, Japan embraces a nuclear-powered future
item.description:
IWAKI, Japan, March 9 (Reuters) - Takuma Hashimoto was three years old when a massive earthquake and tsunami struck on March 11, 2011, triggering nuclear meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi plant just an hour's drive from his home.
As the worst nuclear disaster since Chornobyl unfolded, ‚Äãhis terrified family was trapped, unable to flee like their neighbours because they couldn't find gas for their car.
Now 18, Hashimoto wants to become part of Japan's next generation of nuclear talent.
"I don't think nuclear power ‚Äåshould be treated as something that's automatically dangerous," said the engineering student at a technical college in Iwaki, where a monitoring station still keeps check on local radiation levels.
Reuters spoke with Hashimoto, as well as a former crisis management official and a nuclear industry veteran, ahead of the 15th anniversary of the disaster. Their stories illustrate how Japan is pivoting back to a power source it had all but shunned.
Resource-poor Japan was once one of the world's biggest proponents of nuclear power, which provided roughly 30% of the country's electricity from 54 reactors.
The Fukushima disaster saw public opinion swing dramatically against nuclear, ‚Äãand all reactors were ordered to be shut down for safety inspections and upgrades. In 2012, the government even decided to phase out nuclear energy. That decision was reversed two years later, but reactor restarts have been slow and ‚Äãmany have been shut permanently.
Now Tokyo's staunchly pro-nuclear leader Sanae Takaichi, buoyed by a thumping election win , is pushing to accelerate restarts and advance new nuclear technologies ‚ÅÝto wean the country off costly imported fossil fuels.
The restart of one of the seven reactors at the world's biggest nuclear plant, Kashiwazaki-Kariwa , in January was a milestone. That said, only 15 of 33 reactors in Japan that remain operable are back online.
Middle East ‚Äãchaos unleashed by U.S. President Donald Trump's attack on Iran - a region that accounts for 95% of Japan's oil supplies - and an anticipated surge in energy needs of power-hungry AI data centres promise to bolster a shift in public opinion.
A slim majority of people - 51% - ‚Äãare now in favour of the restarts, an Asahi newspaper survey last month found. That's up from 28% when it began polling on the issue in 2013. The most supportive are young people aged 18 to 29 - at 66%.
Hashimoto's school, which has sent him on visits to nuclear power plants, receives government funding to nurture workers in nuclear power, regulation and decommissioning.
But securing enough talent may be a bottleneck in Japan's nuclear renaissance.
There were just 177 students admitted to nuclear-related courses across Japan in 2024, versus 317 just before the Fukushima disaster and a peak of 673 in the early ‚Äã1990s.
Hashimoto knows not everyone agrees with him. Almost every week, he encounters a vocal band of anti-nuclear protesters at the train station on his commute to school.
But he says: "What matters is using nuclear power properly, having measures in place in case something happens, and ‚Äãdeveloping technology to make sure accidents don't occur."
Seiji Inada, 49, was part of the government's crisis response team in 2011. Tasked with tallying the dead, he hunkered down for days with hundreds of officials in an underground bunker beneath the prime minister's office in ‚ÄåTokyo.
Inada remembers being ‚ÅÝin the crisis room, watching aghast footage of a building that housed a reactor exploding.
Around 150,000 people living around the plant were evacuated, many of whom never returned, while the government assessed the risks that Tokyo, one of the world's biggest cities, might be blanketed in a radioactive plume.
"I remember during my short lunch break, I called my dad and I told him: 'Well, I can't tell you any details, but just prepare for the worst case scenario," recalled Inada, who now works for private consultancy FGS Global.
An inquiry published in 2012 blamed plant operator TEPCO (9501.T) , opens new tab , regulators and the government for failing to develop safety protocols that could have contained the damage, calling it a "man-made" disaster.
The prime minister at the time, Naoto Kan, quit after he was criticised for bungling the response.
"The lesson of 3/11 is ‚Äãhumility: low-probability shocks happen. What matters is governance," said Inada.
Back then, ‚ÄãInada, like most other Japanese, found it hard to ‚ÅÝimagine that the country would ever return to nuclear power, but "time heals trauma", he added.
"The shock of 2011 was profound and it shaped attitudes for years. Over time, the debate shifted from emotion to trade-offs."
Nuclear industry veteran Keiji Matsunaga, 59, is putting the lessons learned from Fukushima into practice by developing safer reactors at his company Toshiba.
Joining the firm straight from ‚Äãuniversity in 1991, five years after Chornobyl, he's always been conscious of the stigma attached to the industry.
Fukushima made that worse and some years after the disaster, his teenage daughter ended ‚Äãup in an argument with a ‚ÅÝteacher at her school who said nuclear power was dangerous and Japan shouldn't be using it.
But even as Toshiba's nuclear business faltered in the wake of Fukushima and the bankruptcy of its U.S. unit Westinghouse, Matsunaga says he has never wavered from the view that Japan needs nuclear for energy security.
And the gradual increase in restarts is helping business.
Though Toshiba, now a private company, does not release its nuclear power division earnings targets, another big player, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (7011.T) , opens new tab , told Reuters last week that sales for its nuclear power unit will hit a record 400 ‚ÅÝbillion yen ($2.5 billion) ‚Äãnext year, a target it didn't expect to reach until 2030.
Any new plants that might be built have been designed to be much safer, says Matsunaga. ‚ÄãFor example, buildings housing reactors would have roofs fortified with steel plate and concrete that can resist the impact of falling aircraft.
The plants would also have more failproof natural circulation systems, in addition to other systems, to keep the reactor cool. That would help avoid a situation like Fukushima when the loss of air-conditioner ‚Äãcooling functionality contributed to the meltdowns.
"By adopting these measures, we believe events like Fukushima can be prevented," he said.

item.title: Trump says ending Iran war will be 'mutual' decision with Netanyahu
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãsaid on Sunday ‚Äåthat when to end the war ‚Äãwith Iran will ‚Äãbe a "mutual" decision made ‚ÅÝwith Israeli ‚ÄãPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Speaking ‚Äãby phone with The Times of Israel, ‚ÄãTrump said Netanyahu ‚Äãwill have input on ‚Äåresolving ‚ÅÝthe conflict.
"I think it‚Äôs mutual ... a little bit. We‚Äôve ‚Äãbeen ‚Äãtalking. ‚ÅÝI‚Äôll make a decision at ‚Äãthe right ‚Äãtime, ‚ÅÝbut everything‚Äôs going to be taken ‚ÅÝinto ‚Äãaccount," said ‚ÄãTrump.

item.title: South Korea parliament due to finalise draft US investment bill under trade deal
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - A South Korean parliamentary committee is expected to finalise the wording of a special bill on Monday, ‚Äãpaving the way for a plenary vote this ‚Äåweek to enable $350 billion of U.S. investments under a bilateral trade deal between the countries.
The bill is expected to be put to a ‚Äãfull National Assembly vote on March 12, as ‚Äãthe U.S. ally responds to pressure from Washington over ‚ÅÝperceived delays in enacting the deal.
The "Special Act on Investment ‚Äãin the U.S." will set up an investment vehicle, as ‚Äãwell as a risk management committee to implement last year's agreement for South Korea to invest in sectors such as shipbuilding and chips in ‚Äãreturn for lower U.S. tariffs.
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened in January ‚Äãto hike tariffs on imports from South Korea, accusing the country's parliament ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝdelays in enacting the trade deal.
Top South Korean officials have said the U.S.-South Korea trade deal remains valid despite a U.S. Supreme Court decision in February that struck down a large ‚Äãswath of Trump's ‚Äãtariffs.
Officials in ‚ÅÝSeoul have, however, voiced concerns about the impact of U.S. investments on an already weak ‚Äãwon currency and said that projects would be ‚Äãbased ‚ÅÝon consideration of commercial feasibility and foreign exchange market conditions.
The special committee, which includes lawmakers from the ruling Democratic Party and ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãopposition People Power Party, is expected to ‚Äãreview the final bill at a subcommittee on Monday and approve it at a general ‚Äãmeeting in the afternoon.

item.title: Brothers of Epstein accuser Virginia Giuffre visit New Mexico ranch, demand unredacted documents
item.description:
STANLEY, N.M., March 8 (Reuters) - Two brothers of one of Jeffrey Epstein's most prominent accusers visited the sex offender's former New Mexico ranch on Sunday for ‚Äãthe first time to demand the Trump administration release unredacted documents to reveal ‚Äåthe identities of men their late sister alleged sexually abused her at the property.
With Epstein's hacienda-style mansion in the background, the brothers of Virginia Giuffre , who took her own life in April, ‚Äãjoined hundreds of protesters at a roadside rally to mark international ‚Äãwomen's day near the gate of the ranch located 30 miles (48 ‚ÅÝkm) south of state capital Santa Fe.
Giuffre's brother Sky Roberts, 37, called on ‚Äãthe U.S. Department of Justice to release documents showing, among other things, names of ‚Äãvisitors to Epstein's Zorro Ranch where he and his acquaintances are accused of sexually abusing women and girls.
"All those names are in the files and right now the government is covering ‚Äãthose up," said Roberts, flanked by Giuffre's older brother, Daniel Wilson, 47, and ‚Äãtheir families.
The release of millions of records on Epstein has exposed the financier's social connections with ‚Äåpoliticians, business ‚ÅÝpeople and scientists who he invited to the ranch.
The files have become a persistent political problem for U.S. President Donald Trump, who was named in FBI records released on Thursday in which an unidentified woman made accusations against him related to an ‚Äãalleged sexual encounter.
New Mexico ‚Äãin February became ‚ÅÝthe first U.S. state to launch a legislative "truth commission" into how Epstein was able to operate in secrecy at Zorro ‚ÄãRanch for 26 years.
‚ÄúNew Mexico is setting the example and we ‚Äãexpect other ‚ÅÝstates to follow behind,‚Äù said Amanda Roberts, 37, Sky Roberts' wife, citing New York and Florida where Epstein had residences where similar probes needed to occur.
Americans generally view ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãEpstein case as an example of wealthy and ‚Äãpowerful people rarely being held accountable and believe the U.S. government is still hiding information about Epstein's ‚Äãclients, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling .

item.title: Compounding errors and narrow self-interest threaten global fuel crisis
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 9 (Reuters) - Mounting miscalculations and a retreat to narrow self-interest by major countries, including the United States and China, are threatening to turn the current conflict in Iran into a global crisis for the supply of refined oil products.
Much of the media focus tends to be on the price of crude oil, with benchmark Brent crude futures jumping as much as 20% to $111.04 a barrel, the highest since July 2022, in early Asian trade on Monday.
But while the ‚Äãleap in crude is dramatic, of more concern is the even bigger surge in the cost of refined fuels such as gasoline, diesel and jet fuel, which are the products that consumers actually buy.
The explosive climb in refined ‚Äåproduct prices last week was led by jet fuel, with Singapore spot prices hitting a record high of $225.44 a barrel on March 4, before easing to end the week at $155.82.
However, that price is still 66.7% higher than the $93.45 a barrel that prevailed on February 27, the day prior to the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
Singapore gasoil , the building block for diesel and jet fuel, reached $123.39 a barrel on March 4, the highest since September 2023 and up 33.5% from the close on February 27.
In effect, product markets in Asia are starting to price in a shortage of supply of the key fuels that keep economies running.
The effective ‚Äãclosure of the Strait of Hormuz is cutting some 18 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude and products, roughly split into 14 million bpd of crude and 3 million bpd of products, according to three-month moving average data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
While ‚Äãthe administration of U.S. President Donald Trump claims the Strait remains open and has offered to insure vessels transiting the narrow waterway between the gulfs of Persia and Oman, the market has yet to ‚ÅÝbe convinced that Iran won't attack any ship attempting passage.
It is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that is the most important strategic error made by the Trump administration and Israel in their attack on Iran.
In common with the majority of analysts and presumably Gulf governments ‚Äãthe assumption was that this conflict would follow the same template as previous flare-ups in the region.
The assumption was that everybody would behave rationally and not target oil production and transportation infrastructure, as it is in nobody's interest to halt the flow of crude and products.
But it turns out ‚Äãif you tell a dictatorial religious government that your aim is regime change, then that government feels little compunction to play by the prior rules.
Iran's decision to target its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. military bases has upended all previous calculations about the conflict.
Gulf countries Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait and Iraq are highly dependent on their oil, fuel and liquefied natural gas exports, and right now their revenue is severely curtailed.
Dubai, one of the Emirates, is also increasingly dependent on its status as the region's financial and tourism hub, with both sectors now taking a massive hit from the ongoing conflict.
The compounding errors of ‚Äãthe Trump administration are now becoming more apparent.
It's clear that they did not expect the Strait of Hormuz to be effectively closed, and their stop-gap solution hasn't won market backing.
It's one thing to offer insurance and perhaps naval escorts, it's entirely another to ensure safe passage can happen ‚Äãfor hundreds of vessels each week.
All Iran has to do is hit one fully laden crude tanker with a ballistic missile and all bets will be off, with the situation then likely to be worse than it is currently.
The next U.S. step of offering a waiver to India to resume purchases ‚Äåof Russian crude is ‚ÅÝa bit like feeding cookies to an elephant, it's nice but largely ineffective.
What is already happening is that Asia's refiners are scrambling to source crude from other suppliers outside the Strait of Hormuz choke point.
What is the likely result of this?
Crude oil prices will rise, and the longer the close of the Strait continues the more short of crude, and thus refined products, the region will become.
The response to this looming shortage is that countries with refining capacity will focus on their domestic needs and cut exports of fuels, thereby exacerbating the shortage of refined products.
This can be seen by reports that China has ordered its major state-owned refiners to stop exports of refined products.
Some Indian refiners are also reported to be ending exports of fuels, while plants in some countries, such as South Korea, are cutting refinery processing.
The obvious question for the authorities in Beijing is ‚Äãhow long before the decision not to supply refined fuels to ‚Äãimporting nations blows back on the Chinese economy.
It is another example ‚ÅÝof a miscalculation that will prove more costly than the alternative of actually ramping up fuel exports to help meet demand.
China isn't a major exporter of refined products, with about 600,000 barrels per day.
But it is the only major producer with significant spare capacity and a massive crude oil stockpile, estimated at over 1 billion barrels.
This level of inventories means China could continue to refine oil at its current rate for about ‚Äãthree years, even if its imports dropped to zero.
In effect, China could use its huge stockpile to boost refinery runs, lift exports and ease the looming supply crisis.
This would not only be highly ‚Äãprofitable but would also win favour with ‚ÅÝimporting nations, who may otherwise run short of fuels.
Australia is Asia's biggest fuel importer, taking about 900,000 bpd, according to Kpler.
Imagine a situation when the country could no longer meet this demand, resulting in shortages.
The government would have to prioritise food production and delivery, and keeping as much of the economy going as possible.
But a brave Australian government could say to China that no more iron ore would be shipped because of diesel shortages unless Beijing supplied refined fuels.
China gets about two-thirds of its iron ore imports from Australia and without these flows its steel industry would be severely curtailed, which in ‚ÅÝturn would devastate manufacturing ‚Äãand construction.
Getting to this point would only happen if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed and countries follow narrow and short-term self-interest.
The problem is that political leaders ‚Äãso far seem unable to grasp the extent of the stress they are putting on the energy supply system, and their actions suggest they see only their own country's needs without realising that it is a global system that needs global solutions.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, ‚Äãdata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: What‚Äôs next for Iran?
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week ago I woke up to find out that the United States and Israel were attacking Iran . It‚Äôs the second time this year that President Donald Trump has targeted another country‚Äôs leader ‚Äì on a Saturday. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in your inbox every weekend.
‚ÄúIn Siena‚Äôs brutal Palio horse race, a steed can ‚Äãstill win after losing its rider. Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , headquartered in the same picturesque Tuscan town, is applying that tradition to hostile bank M&A.‚Äù
Read more here: Italy‚Äôs MPS adds governance risk to dicey ‚ÄåM&A .
‚ÄúNo operational plan can extend with any degree of certainty beyond the initial encounter with the main enemy force.‚Äù Field Marshal Helmuth von Moltke‚Äôs advice , opens new tab to military ‚Äãstrategists is also a warning for anyone drawing lasting conclusions from the first days of a war. A week after the United States and Israel attacked Iran and killed its Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, it would be ‚Äãfoolish to predict the outcome of the conflict, let alone its long-term repercussions. Instead, here are the five most pressing questions:
This key question is the hardest ‚ÅÝto answer. Many strategists expect it to be short, in part because the U.S. bombing campaign means Iran will eventually run out of missiles and launching sites it needs to retaliate. With tepid public support at home and the price of petrol rising, ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump may also want to avoid a drawn-out battle. But on Friday he demanded ‚Äú unconditional surrender ‚Äù. Financial markets are beginning to price in a longer conflict: the price of a barrel of oil ended the week above $90. Yet while the spot ‚Äãprice of gas in Europe has jumped, contracts for delivery in a few years have barely budged.
Trump could declare ‚Äúmission accomplished‚Äù and attempt to move on, as his predecessor George W. Bush did six weeks after the invasion of Iraq in 2003. But that historical precedent reminds us that an end to fighting depends on what happens on the ground. As George Hay argues , there are three broad scenarios for Iran: the current government clings on to power under a new leader; the country splits along factional or ethnic lines; or the population rises up ‚Äãand takes power. Only the third scenario is consistent with more enduring calm. Don‚Äôt forget that it‚Äôs less than nine months since Trump claimed to have destroyed Iran‚Äôs nuclear program.
Blocking the Strait of Hormuz was long seen ‚Äãas a move Iran would only make in desperation. About a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil and gas passes through the narrow waterway. Yet the outbreak of hostilities has produced two extraordinary outcomes. First, tanker traffic has stopped largely on the strength of Iranian threats : anxious shipping ‚Äåcompanies and jittery ‚ÅÝinsurers did the rest. Second, the blockage has not yet led to the $200-a-barrel oil price traders once predicted. Markets may expect a swift reopening, but as Yawen Chen points out , this is not entirely in Trump‚Äôs gift.
The United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and their neighbours have presented themselves as hyper-modern and impartial finance and technology hubs. They have attracted banks, tax exiles and tourists, while making splashy investments around the world. Iranian missiles and drones flying over gleaming towers shattered that glitzy image. Even though most of the projectiles have been shot down , George Hay and Afiq Fitri Alias make the case that the blow to the Gulf‚Äôs regional reputation is likely to last ‚Äì and may benefit Saudi Arabia. Meanwhile, extra spending on domestic defence ‚Äãmeans less cash for overseas investments.
The president revels in his unpredictability. After abducting Venezuelan ‚ÅÝPresident Nicolas Maduro and killing Khamenei he may feel emboldened to settle other scores. On Thursday, he hinted at intervention in Cuba as ‚Äújust a question of time‚Äù. Yet overseas military adventures clash with his domestic agenda. Meanwhile, increased military spending, higher interest rates and missing tariff revenue are increasing the risks of a sovereign debt spiral, Gabriel Rubin warns . Success in Iran could backfire ‚Äãat home.
Every financial meltdown brings obscure acronyms to the fore. The Enron scandal taught us about special purpose entities (SPEs). In 2008 collateralised debt obligations (CDOs) were on everybody‚Äôs ‚Äãlips. Today, BDCs are finding a ‚ÅÝwider audience. Business development companies are vehicles that allow asset managers to accumulate funds without a set expiration date. Now they‚Äôre under stress. Jonathan Guilford explains what‚Äôs going on .
If you‚Äôre looking for something a bit more optimistic, how about a fresh perspective on Africa? It‚Äôs easy to be gloomy about its prospects. But Joe Studwell, author of ‚ÄúHow Africa Works‚Äù, offers a glass-half-full perspective about the vast continent. He joined me on The Big View , opens new tab to discuss his research and conclusions.
Over on the Viewsroom , opens new tab , George Hay and Yawen Chen joined Aimee Donnellan ‚ÅÝand Jonathan Guilford ‚Äãto provide a download on the fast-moving Iran conflict, and the fallout for energy markets.
For years, Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab hid its inner workings behind a ‚Äãthick veil of discretion. But the firm has changed, and this week Lloyd Blankfein published ‚ÄúStreetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman Sachs‚Äù, an amusing and at times brutally frank account of his career at the bank he led between 2006 and 2018. It makes for a highly readable book ‚Äì and also reminds us ‚Äãthat Goldman is now a bit less special .
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Japan should use nuclear plants to offset Iran crisis, opposition party head says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - Japan should operate all of its nuclear power plants available to offset the impact of the ‚ÄãIran war on electricity bills, Yuichiro Tamaki, the ‚Äåleader of a Japanese opposition party, said on Monday.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its oil supplies ‚Äãand 11% of its liquefied natural gas imports ‚Äãwith around 70% and 6% respectively coming via ‚ÅÝthe Strait of Hormuz, which is effectively closed due ‚Äãto the war.
"Unless we make full use of nuclear power ‚Äãas a carbon-free power source with less dependence on overseas sources, electricity bills will inevitably rise," Tamaki, who heads the Democratic ‚ÄãParty for the People, said in a statement on ‚ÄãX.
Oil prices surged about 20% on Monday, hitting their highest since ‚ÄåJuly ‚ÅÝ2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran fuelled fears of tighter supply.
Japan shut down all of its 54 nuclear power reactors - which provided roughly 30% of the ‚Äãcountry's electricity - in ‚Äãthe wake ‚ÅÝof the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi plant nuclear meltdowns. Thirty-three remain operable but only 15 ‚Äãhave been restarted.
"A policy shift is needed ‚Äãto protect ‚ÅÝthe lives of the people," said Tamaki, who has consistently supported restarting and expanding nuclear power in Japan.
New Prime ‚ÅÝMinister ‚ÄãSanae Takaichi is also staunchly ‚Äãpro-nuclear and Japan's public is also gradually becoming more supportive of nuclear power.

item.title: VIEW Oil surges 20% as Iran war fuels supply fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged about 20% in early trade on Monday, hitting their highest since July 2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran led some major Middle East oil producers to cut supplies and on concerns of ‚Äãprolonged disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
COMMENTARY:
"I think prices have ‚Äårallied this morning on the reports that Middle East producers are now reducing output due to storage facilities filling up fast."
"I certainly think the spectre now of Middle Eastern producers curtailing output is going to keep those prices elevated. The next flag will be whether it eventually reaches a ‚Äãpoint where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only further impacts output but also delays a ‚Äãresponse once the conflict eases. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer."
"A sudden supply shock reverberates well beyond just who is a net energy exporter and ‚Äãimporter. There are acute supply chain effects beyond how just price eats into margins. Even for a place like Indonesia (where) it is ‚Äãnot unusual to see street protests if pump prices go up."
"Asia takes the brunt of the sharp escalation in oil prices and there are few places to run and hide. The dollar has to be the one outperforming, given Japan and Korea's exposures here and the sharp pain that ‚Äãcan be expected from Brent at $107."
"The market had been complacent about the scope ‚Äãand duration of the war and associated supply disruptions until last Friday.
"Its a case of the oil market who cried wolf. After three years ‚Äåof geopolitical ‚ÅÝrisk premia rising only to fail to translate to supply disruptions, the market became complacent about the current events.
"But this existential Iran war is the energy crisis scenario that has been wargamed for 50 years, finally coming to fore."
"Our baseline scenario is the conflict in Iran will be large but short-lived, though there is a clear risk of a prolonged ‚Äãwar. Among emerging markets, the ‚Äãeconomic impact will be felt ‚ÅÝmost clearly in the Gulf Cooperation Council, reflecting the shock's adverse impact on trade, logistics, tourism and investment."
"First, Pakistan and India are most vulnerable as they are energy importers with relatively high exposure ‚Äãto the Strait of Hormuz. Second, outside of physical trade disruption, Egypt and Turkiye are ‚Äãmost exposed. This ‚ÅÝis due to their high energy import bill, fragile external positions, large energy subsidies and unanchored inflation. Third, commodity producing economies in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America are least at risk, including Nigeria, Ghana and Peru."
"The threats to attack refineries ‚ÅÝare ‚Äãvery concerning. Because it threatens the worst of all economic worlds. Cutting off ‚Äã15%-20% of the world's oil supply not only slows down every economy globally, but it introduces an inflation impulse. And inflation plus slowing growth is stagflation, and ‚Äãthat's an economic disaster."

item.title: Australians reach for VPNs, find porn sites blocked as online age-restrictions take effect
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australians ‚Äãwere downloading virtual private networks (VPNs) in droves, while one of the world's largest pornography distributors said ‚Äåit was blocking users as the country rolled out sweeping online age restrictions on Monday.
Last December, Australia became the first country to impose a nationwide ban on teenagers using social media. A separate law now requires AI-powered chatbot services to keep certain content - including ‚Äãpornography, extreme violence and self-harm and eating disorder material - from minors or face fines of up to ‚ÄãA$49.5 million ($34.5 million).
The country also joined Britain, France and dozens of U.S. states requiring ‚ÅÝwebsites which disseminate pornography to verify users are over 18. App stores must also run age checks before ‚Äãallowing downloads of software labelled 18+.
The country's eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant said the measures aimed to afford children ‚Äãthe same protection online as the world expected offline.
"A child today can't walk into a bar and order a drink, they can't stroll into a strip club or browse an adult shop or sit down at a blackjack table in a casino," she told ‚Äãthe Australian Broadcasting Corp.
"This just really brings ... those protections that we put for kids in place to the ‚Äãdigital realm."
Three of the 15 most downloaded free smartphone apps on Monday were VPNs, a chart published by iPhone maker Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab ‚Äåshowed. The ‚ÅÝmost downloaded VPN, called VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy, ranked ahead of any social media platform, the chart showed.
VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment. All internet-connected devices carry an individual code which discloses their location, and VPNs hide the user's location by assigning a new code to the device.
Canada-based Aylo, ‚Äãowner of a large network ‚Äãof pornography websites, meanwhile ‚ÅÝblocked Australians from accessing the platforms RedTube and YouPorn, while presenting a version of Pornhub without explicit content.
All the websites carried a banner saying it was "not currently accepting ‚Äãnew account registrations in your region".
Aylo did not respond to a Reuters request for ‚Äãcomment. The Sydney ‚ÅÝMorning Herald quoted an Aylo spokesperson saying the company's Australian users would be "presented with a safe-for-work experience when they view our platforms".
Tom Sulston, deputy chair of advocacy group Digital Rights Watch, said it was unsurprising that people were reaching ‚ÅÝfor VPNs.
"My ‚Äãhope is that, not only will they discover that this works ‚Äãfor looking at spicier internet sites, but that it's just generally a good idea to use VPNs when you're traversing the internet, because they ‚Äãdo offer you some privacy protections."
($1 = 1.4351 Australian dollars)

item.title: Shots fired at Rihanna's house in Beverly Hills, no injuries reported, media say
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A woman fired numerous shots into the Beverly ‚ÄãHills home of pop music ‚Äåstar Rihanna on Sunday, and a round went through a wall ‚Äãof the house, local news ‚Äãoutlets reported Sunday.
The Los Angeles Times ‚ÅÝand NBC4, citing a ‚ÄãLos Angeles Police spokesperson, reported ‚Äãthat authorities responded to the shooting at 1:21 p.m. (2021 GMT) Sunday and detained ‚Äãa 30-year-old female suspect.
Los ‚ÄãAngeles police and a representative for Rihanna did ‚Äånot ‚ÅÝimmediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Rihanna was at home but no injuries were ‚Äãreported, the ‚ÄãLos ‚ÅÝAngeles Times said, citing a source. The ‚Äãnewspaper cited police radio ‚Äãtraffic ‚ÅÝthat "approximately 10 shots‚Äù had been fired from a vehicle across ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãstreet from the ‚Äãproperty‚Äôs gate.

item.title: Bangladesh shuts universities early to save power amid energy crisis
item.description:
DHAKA, March 9 (Reuters) - Bangladesh will close all universities from Monday, bringing forward the Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ‚Äãelectricity and fuel amid a worsening energy crisis linked to ‚Äåthe conflict in the Middle East.
Authorities said the decision applies to all public and private universities across the country, a move that officials said will not only ‚Äãreduce electricity consumption but also ease traffic congestion, which leads ‚Äãto fuel wastage.
Officials said university campuses consume large amounts of ‚ÅÝelectricity for residential halls, classrooms, laboratories and air conditioning, and the ‚Äãearly closure would help ease pressure on the country‚Äôs strained power ‚Äãsystem.
Government and private schools in Bangladesh are already closed for the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, meaning most educational institutions across the country will now remain shut ‚Äãduring the period.
The move comes as Bangladesh faces mounting uncertainty over ‚Äãfuel and gas supplies following disruptions to global energy markets caused by the ongoing ‚Äåconflict ‚ÅÝin the Middle East.
The country, which relies on imports for 95% of its energy needs, imposed daily limits on Friday on fuel sales after panic buying and stockpiling.
As part of broader austerity measures, the government has also ‚Äãasked all foreign‚Äëcurriculum schools and ‚Äãprivate coaching ‚ÅÝcentres to suspend operations during this period to limit electricity use.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt ‚Äãoperations at four of its five state‚Äërun fertiliser ‚Äãfactories, redirecting ‚ÅÝavailable gas to power plants to avoid widespread outages.
The country has also bought LNG from the spot market at sharply higher prices while seeking additional ‚ÅÝcargoes ‚Äãto bridge supply gaps.
‚ÄúWe are doing everything ‚Äãwe can to reduce consumption and ensure stability in power, fuel and import supplies,‚Äù a ‚Äãsenior energy ministry official said.

item.title: Ukraine sent drone experts to protect US bases in Jordan, Zelenskiy tells NYT
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine sent interceptor drones and ‚Äãa team of ‚Äådrone experts to protect U.S. military ‚Äãbases in ‚ÄãJordan, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚ÅÝsaid in ‚Äãan interview with The ‚ÄãNew York Times published on Monday.
The U.S. ‚Äãmade the ‚Äãrequest for help on Thursday, ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝthe Ukrainian team left the next day, Zelenskiy ‚Äãsaid ‚Äãadding ‚ÅÝthat it was expected to ‚Äãarrive in ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝMiddle East soon, according to ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãreport.

item.title: Indonesia searches for missing after landfill collapse kills four
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Rescuers are still searching for five missing people after a large stack of ‚Äãgarbage collapsed at Indonesia's biggest landfill ‚Äåsite over the weekend, killing at least four people, an official said on Monday.
The collapse took place ‚Äãat the Bantargebang landfill on Sunday, located ‚Äãin the region of Bekasi on the ‚ÅÝoutskirts of the capital Jakarta, said Desiana ‚ÄãKartika Bahari, head of the local rescue agency.
It ‚Äãwas likely triggered by the heavy rain that hit the area from Saturday evening, she told Reuters.
"It was ‚Äãraining all day even from (Saturday) evening and ‚Äãthe mountain of garbage was unstable," Bahari said.
Some trucks were ‚Äåunloading ‚ÅÝgarbage when the collapse took place, and there was also a food stall near the site.
"The missing people are the truck drivers and ‚Äãscavengers," she ‚Äãsaid, adding ‚ÅÝthat there there could be more people buried in the landfill.
Four ‚Äãpeople survived the accident.
More than 200 rescuers, ‚Äãincluding ‚ÅÝpolice and military personnel and 17 excavators, have been deployed since Monday morning to search ‚ÅÝfor ‚Äãthe missing.
Bantargebang covers around 110 ‚Äãhectares and receives around 6,500 tons-7,000 tons of garbage per ‚Äãday.

item.title: Lawyers for Bondi Beach shooting suspect seek gag order to protect family
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Lawyers for a man accused of carrying out a deadly shooting on Sydney's Bondi Beach sought a court order ‚Äãon Monday to stop media naming his family, saying their ‚Äåsafety was at risk.
Police allege 24-year-old Naveed Akram opened fire on a Jewish Hanukkah celebration on December 14, killing 15, in Australia's worst mass shooting in decades.
His ‚Äãfather, Sajid Akram, who police also accuse of carrying out ‚Äãthe shooting, was shot dead by police at the scene.
Police ‚ÅÝsay the men were inspired by the Islamic State militant group ‚Äãto carry out the attack using several high-powered weapons the elder man had legally ‚Äãacquired.
Lawyers for Naveed Akram on Monday requested a court in Sydney prohibit the publication of the names of his mother, brother and sister, as well as their ‚Äãhome address and places of work and schooling. Magistrate Greg Grogan ‚Äãextended an interim suppression order until next month, citing the global media interest in ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝcase.
Akram faces a total of 59 charges over the attack, including 15 counts of murder, 40 counts of wounding with intent to murder and a terror offence. He is yet to enter a plea.
The case ‚Äãis expected to ‚Äãbriefly return ‚ÅÝto court next week, before a longer hearing on April 8, where the prosecution will outline its brief ‚Äãof evidence.
The Bondi attack shocked a country with strict ‚Äãgun laws ‚ÅÝand fuelled calls for tougher controls and stronger action against antisemitism.
Australia last month launched a government-backed inquiry into antisemitism and social cohesion in the country ‚ÅÝwhich ‚Äãis expected to report its findings by ‚ÄãDecember this year.
The government has already responded by tightening gun laws and introducing new legislation ‚Äãagainst hate speech.

item.title: Governments' actions in response to oil price surge and the escalating Middle East conflict
item.description:
March 9 - Oil prices have soared while share markets have skidded on fears that the escalating U.S.-Israeli war on ‚ÄãIran will squeeze energy supplies and hamstring industries ‚Äåaround the world.
Following are actions that governments are taking or plan to take to reduce the impact of the war on ‚Äãtheir economies.
South Korean President ‚ÄãLee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities ‚ÅÝwould cap domestic fuel prices for the first time ‚Äãin nearly 30 years. The country will also look ‚Äãfor sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz, and a 100 trillion won ($67 billion) market-stabilisation programme should be ‚Äãexpanded if needed, he added.
The Japanese government instructed a national oil ‚Äåreserve ‚ÅÝstorage site to prepare for a possible release of crude, Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition party, told Reuters on Sunday.
Details such as ‚Äãthe timing of ‚Äãthe release ‚ÅÝremain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
Vietnam is planning to remove import tariffs ‚Äãon fuels to ensure supplies amid disruptions, ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝgovernment said, adding that the measure is expected to last until the end of April.
Bangladesh ‚ÅÝwill close ‚Äãall universities from Monday, bringing forward ‚Äãthe Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ‚Äãelectricity and fuel.

item.title: How Europe can play a bad hand over Iran
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - How many wake-up calls does Europe need? Donald Trump‚Äôs first administration did not do the trick, despite the U.S. president‚Äôs anti-European rhetoric. Nor did Russia‚Äôs full-scale invasion of Ukraine four years ago. Nor did Trump‚Äôs re-election in 2024. Now in quick succession this year, Washington has pummelled Venezuela, threatened Greenland and attacked Iran.
It is hard to see anything good for Europe from the ‚ÄãUnited States-Israeli attack on the Islamic Republic. It strengthens Russia and weakens Ukraine, threatening Europe‚Äôs own security. It undermines the global rule of law, which is valuable for the region given that it is ‚Äånot a geopolitical power.
The latest Middle Eastern conflict is also threatening Europe‚Äôs economy. Much, of course, will depend on how long the conflict goes on ‚Äî in particular whether Iran can keep up a blockade of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz. As a hydrocarbon importer, Europe is vulnerable to high energy prices. Its mostly highly indebted governments are not well placed to cushion a blow to either businesses or consumers. Meanwhile, a descent into civil war in Iran, or a further crackdown on internal dissent, could unleash a wave of immigration, with some of its 93 million ‚Äãcitizens heading towards Europe.
In the short run, there is little the region can do but suffer and squirm. A principled response would have been to condemn Trump‚Äôs attack as contrary to international law while at the same time ‚Äãdenouncing Iran‚Äôs nuclear weapons programme. But Europe is too dependent on U.S. military support to do that, especially given the Russian threat. It is also increasingly dependent on American energy ‚ÅÝsince its gas purchases from Moscow are down dramatically and now Qatar is offline .
Spain has criticised the attack on Iran. But Europe‚Äôs big three ‚Äî Germany, France and Britain ‚Äî have provided half-supportive , opens new tab statements , opens new tab that sit uneasily with their values. Not that that has earned them U.S. ‚Äãgratitude: Trump lambasted British Prime Minister Keir Starmer as ‚Äú not Winston Churchill ‚Äù.
The lesson is clear. Europe will be helpless in a world of sharks until it gets stronger and more united. The EU itself will need greater integration. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom requires ‚Äãa bolder partnership with the club it left as a result of Brexit, covering economic and energy security as well as defence.
Europe‚Äôs core vulnerability is that it needs U.S. protection because it cannot yet defend itself against Russia on its own. Its biggest worry therefore is that the Iran war is strengthening Moscow by relaxing the grip on the Kremlin‚Äôs financial windpipe.
True, Vladimir Putin has had to sit by idly as the United States has bombed an ally and killed its leader. This is bad for the Russian president‚Äôs prestige. What is more, Tehran will ‚Äãstruggle to supply Moscow with more attack drones, as it will need any it can produce for itself.
But what Russia gains from higher oil and gas prices probably more than compensates for this loss. At the start of the year Moscow ‚Äãwas feeling the squeeze from tighter sanctions and lower crude prices. Trump was strong-arming India, previously Russia‚Äôs largest customer after China, to stop buying its oil. Moscow had to offer purchasers deep discounts on the global price while some cargoes were piling up on the high ‚Äåseas unsold. ‚ÅÝThe government‚Äôs oil and gas revenue halved to $5.1 billion in January compared to the same month last year.
These dynamics are going into reverse. Brent crude prices are up 50% to around $90 a barrel since the start of the year, before the drumbeats of the Middle East war started in earnest. European gas prices have roughly doubled. With rival Gulf suppliers unable to export, Russia has bargaining power. Indian refiners have started buying its crude again, paying a $4-$5 a barrel premium over Brent on a delivered basis compared to a $13 discount for cargoes traded last month. The Kremlin‚Äôs war chest could soon fill up.
What is more, Ukraine will struggle to get more of the anti-missile defences it desperately needs from the United States, as Washington will keep these for itself, Israel ‚Äãand its Gulf allies. This will make Ukraine‚Äôs infrastructure vulnerable ‚Äãto Russian attack. Not that Kyiv is totally without ‚ÅÝcards. It has developed cheap anti-drone defences that could be of use to Washington and Gulf states ‚Äî and may be able to trade some of these for sophisticated weapons it does not produce itself.
Even so, the overall balance has swung in Moscow‚Äôs favour. And that is bad for Europe.
To be fair, Europe increasingly realises that it will only be able to advance its ‚Äãvalues and interests if it increases its autonomy. For example, France last week set out a plan to boost its nuclear arsenal and involve European allies in war games with ‚Äãits atomic weapons. Meanwhile, the EU ‚ÅÝunveiled proposals to work with trusted partners to boost investment in strategic economic sectors to cut dependence on the likes of China.
The EU is also making a renewed attempt to boost its competitiveness by breaking down barriers in services industries and unifying its capital markets. It seems increasingly determined to move ahead even if some of its 27 members disagree. Great Britain is also getting closer to the bloc, albeit with insufficient ambition or urgency.
But it will take a long time for these initiatives to bear fruit. In the meantime, ‚ÅÝmany Europeans will be ‚Äãin two minds about how they want Trump‚Äôs new war to pan out. If the U.S president triumphs he may feel emboldened to bully ‚ÄãEurope ‚Äî perhaps turning his attention back to Greenland, which is part of Denmark, an EU member. On the other hand, if the United States gets embroiled in a quagmire in Iran, the big winner may be China ‚Äî and Europe will not be happy if the People‚Äôs Republic throws its weight around more.
All ‚Äãthe more reason to heed the wake-up call and make itself stronger and more unified.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: New Delhi has weak hand in bank deal frenzy
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - A dealmaking boom in India's banking sector has an unlikely loser: the government. Canadian insurance holding firm Fairfax Financial (FFH.TO) , opens new tab leads , opens new tab the race to buy a ‚Äã61% stake from Indian state entities in $13 billion IDBI Bank (IDBI.NS) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported in February, citing sources. An $8 ‚Äåbillion transaction would be the largest-ever foreign direct investment in a local bank. But crystallising a premium valuation looks challenging.
A deal would complete a full circle for the lender hardest hit by an asset quality crisis: in 2018, bad loans comprised nearly one-third of its portfolio. Provisions for that ‚Äãsour pool eroded its capital base and prompted New Delhi, which then owned 86% of IDBI, to press state-backed ‚ÄãLife Insurance Corporation (LIFI.NS) , opens new tab to pump in 216 billion , opens new tab rupees, or $2.4 billion at current rates, to raise its 8% ‚ÅÝstake to 51% in 2019.
LIC now holds 49% of IDBI's shares and the government owns 45%. Selling a 30% stake ‚Äãto Fairfax at the latest market price would fetch the insurer a 136% return on its 2019 investment. New Delhi would be ‚Äãworse off, though: the lender's shares trade lower than they did 13 years ago.
Yet even current multiples may be difficult to fetch. IDBI's shares are trading at about 2 times forward book value, almost twice that of similar-sized rivals Yes Bank (YESB.NS) , opens new tab and IDFC First Bank (IDFB.NS) , opens new tab . Throwing in employee liabilities, restructuring ‚Äãcosts and the likely absence of indemnity clauses gives the buyer a strong case for a discount.
An abundance of takeover targets ‚Äãhas hurt New Delhi, too. Launched in 2022, the slow-moving sale process of IDBI prompted early potential bidders to look elsewhere: last year ‚ÄåSumitomo Mitsui ‚ÅÝBanking Corporation (8316.T) , opens new tab bought a 24% stake in Yes Bank.
With Emirates NBD (ENBD.DU) , opens new tab still in the reckoning with Fairfax, it's a two-horse race to own IDBI. Both bidders already have a foothold in India's credit market: the Dubai-headquartered lender is set to take control of the $2 billion RBL Bank (RATB.NS) , opens new tab and Fairfax owns $675 million CSB Bank (CSBB.NS) , opens new tab .
That chips away at any shred of bargaining power left with the sellers, ‚Äãwho can hardly demand a ‚Äãcontrol premium. Regulations cap voting ‚ÅÝrights of private bank shareholders at 26%. That puts the new owner effectively at par on voting decisions with LIC and the government, which will hold a combined 34% after the sale. ‚ÄãTo maximise takings, officials could ask the central bank to relax the voting rule. The ‚Äãother option is ‚ÅÝto reduce their total stake to well below 26%.
Otherwise, New Delhi risks catching the weak end of India's banking M&A wave.
Follow Shritama Bose on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: G7 to discuss joint release of emergency oil reserves, FT reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄãGroup of Seven (G7) finance ministers will ‚Äådiscuss on Monday a joint release of oil from emergency reserves coordinated by ‚Äãthe International Energy Agency, the ‚ÄãFinancial Times reported.
Three G7 countries, including ‚ÅÝthe U.S., have so far ‚Äãexpressed support for the idea, the ‚ÄãFT said citing sources, and added that the ministers and the IEA Executive Director ‚ÄãFatih Birol will hold a call ‚Äãto discuss the impact of the Iran ‚Äåwar.
The ‚ÅÝreport comes as oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday to their highest levels since mid-2022 as some ‚Äãmajor producers ‚Äãcut ‚ÅÝsupplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the ‚Äãmarket due to the expanding ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli ‚ÅÝwar with Iran.
The IEA and the G7 presidency did not respond to ‚ÅÝrequests ‚Äãfor comment outside regular ‚Äãbusiness hours.

item.title: Indian refiners tumble as crude surges on widening Iran war
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shares of Indian refiners slumped on Monday, as the widening U.S.-Israeli war with Iran propelled ‚ÄãBrent crude prices toward $120 per barrel - a near four-year high - threatening their ‚Äånear-term earnings.
State-run Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab dipped 6.6%, Hindustan Petroleum (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab slid 7.5% and Bharat Petroleum (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab dropped 7.1%, and were headed for their steepest falls in more than ‚Äãa year.
Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab lost 2%.
Brent crude soared as ‚Äãmuch as 26.4% to $117.16 a barrel and was ‚ÅÝlast up 23% at $114.08 by 9:15 a.m. IST.
UBS said ‚Äãoil marketing companies are "negatively leveraged" to the crude spike as ‚Äãthey sell more diesel and gasoline than they produce, estimating sales-to-production ratios at 1:2 for IOC and BPCL and 2:2 for ‚ÄãHPCL.
The brokerage cut IOC and BPCL to "neutral" and BPCL to "sell" from "buy".
The ‚Äãrout dragged the Nifty oil and gas index (.NIFOILGAS) , opens new tab down 2.7% and the ‚Äåenergy ‚ÅÝindex (.NIFTYENR) , opens new tab 2.1% lower, while the benchmark Nifty 50 (.NSEI) , opens new tab slid 2.8%. The oil and gas index has fallen 6.6% since the U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on February 27.
Citi warned that refiners' ‚Äãearnings will hinge ‚Äãon how long the ‚ÅÝgeopolitical shock lasts, flagging risks from a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz and ‚Äãany shutdown of Qatar's LNG output - each supplying ‚Äãabout ‚ÅÝhalf of India's crude and LNG imports.
Any disruption beyond the one month currently priced in could sharply tighten LNG ‚ÅÝmarkets, ‚Äãwith low European storage for October ‚Äã2026 raising the risk of "non-linear" price spikes, the brokerage added.

item.title: Azerbaijan reopens Iran border to cargo traffic after drone incident, TASS reports
item.description:
BAKU, March 9 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said on Monday it ‚Äãhad reopened its border ‚Äåcrossings with Iran for all cargo traffic, the ‚ÄãRussian state-run TASS ‚Äãnews agency reported.
The border ‚ÅÝcrossings, one of the ‚Äãshortest land routes ‚Äãconnecting Iran to its ally Russia, were closed last week ‚Äãafter what Baku ‚Äãdescribed as an Iranian drone attack ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe Nakhchivan exclave.
Late on Sunday, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian phoned his ‚ÄãAzerbaijani ‚Äãcounterpart ‚ÅÝIlham Aliyev, telling him Iran had ‚Äãnot been involved ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝthe Nakhchivan incident and that Tehran was ‚ÅÝinvestigating, Aliyev's ‚Äãoffice said.

item.title: Khamenei's hardline son Mojtaba appointed as Iran's new leader
item.description:
DUBAI/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader , signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge, while the week-old ‚ÄãU.S.-Israeli war with Iran sent oil prices surging and Asian stock markets into a nosedive.
Iranian institutions and politicians, from the foreign ministry to lawmakers, issued statements expressing their allegiance to the country's new supreme leader ‚Äåas the war entered its 10th day and fresh missile and drone strikes reverberated across the Middle East.
"We will obey the commander-in-chief until the last drop of our blood," a statement from the defence council said.
Senior cleric Ayatollah Sadeq Amoli-Larijani said Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment was "a balm for the spiritual suffering of our people and an emphasis on the need to continue the luminous path of the late Imam (Khamenei senior)."
Mojtaba's father, Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, was killed in one of the first strikes launched against Iran more than a week ago.
The show of solidarity for Mojtaba comes ‚Äãafter U.S. President Donald Trump earlier rejected him as a candidate to be Iran's new supreme leader, and Israel saying it would target whoever leads Iran.
Mojtaba, a cleric with influence inside Iran's security forces and vast ‚Äãbusiness networks under his father, had been viewed as a frontrunner in the lead-up to Sunday's vote by the Assembly of Experts, a body of 88 clerics tasked with choosing ‚ÅÝAli Khamenei's successor.
The position gives Mojtaba the final say in all matters of state in the Islamic Republic.
Trump said on Sunday that Washington should have a say in the selection. "If he doesn't get approval from us, he's not going to ‚Äãlast long," he told ABC News, adding that ending the war would be a "mutual" decision with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
In an interview with the Times of Israel after the new supreme leader was named, Trump declined to respond, saying only "We'll see ‚Äãwhat happens," according to the newspaper.
Pope Leo on Sunday warned of "a tragedy of enormous proportions" due to the "widespread climate of hatred and fear‚Äù in Iran and across the region.
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that the roar of bombs may cease, that weapons may fall silent, and that space may be opened for dialogue in which the voices of peoples can be heard", Leo said at the Angelus prayer in St. Peter‚Äôs Square.
The Israeli military said on Monday it had begun a wave of attacks in central Iran ‚Äãand had struck infrastructure belonging to Hezbollah militants in Beirut.
Iran and its proxies appeared to have launched attacks too, with rocket and drone strikes targeting a U.S. diplomatic facility near Baghdad's international airport that were intercepted by C-RAM defence system, ‚Äãsaid police sources.
A drone strike targeted a U.S. military base near Erbil airport in Iraqi Kurdistan, security sources said, while Saudi authorities reported intercepting a drone east of its northern Jawf region.
Thick smoke was seen rising from the direction of the Bapco oil refinery in ‚ÄåBahrain, according to ‚ÅÝa Reuters witness, after the government said an Iranian drone strike hit the area causing injuries and damage. Bapco Energies later declared force majeure on its group operations following the refinery attack, adding to fears about energy supplies from the region.
The U.S. military reported a seventh American has died from wounds sustained during Iran's initial counter-attack a week ago, a day after Trump presided over the return to the United States of the remains of the six others killed.
The Israeli military said on Sunday that two of its soldiers were killed in southern Lebanon, marking the first fatalities among its troops since Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week as Israel intensified air strikes on Lebanon.
The military did not immediately provide further details on the circumstances of the incident.
At ‚Äãleast four people were killed when an Israeli strike ‚Äãhit an apartment in the Ramada hotel building in ‚ÅÝcentral Beirut early on Sunday, with Israel saying it targeted Iranian commanders operating in the Lebanese capital.
The U.S.-Israeli attacks have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador.
Oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday to their highest levels since mid-2022, as major Middle Eastern oil producers cut supply because they cannot safely send ‚Äãshipments through the Strait of Hormuz to refiners worldwide.
Traffic through the Strait was largely after Iran attacked at least five ships, with a limited number of tankers transiting, choking off ‚Äãa key artery accounting for about ‚ÅÝ20% of global oil and LNG supply.
Brent crude futures were up $24.96 or 27% at $117.65 per barrel at 0451 GMT - on track for the biggest-ever jump in a single day, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures were up $25.72, or 28.3%, to $116.62.
The war has forced production stoppages by oil and gas exporters from Qatar to Iraq, with Kuwait announcing cuts over the weekend. Analysts predict the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will soon cut output as they run out of oil storage.
The G7 finance ministers will discuss ‚ÅÝon Monday a ‚Äãjoint release of oil from emergency reserves coordinated by the International Energy Agency, the Financial Times reported.
Three G7 countries, including the U.S., have so ‚Äãfar expressed support for the idea, the FT added.
A senior Japanese parliament member said on Sunday that the government instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible release of crude. Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its crude, with roughly 70% shipped ‚Äãthrough the Strait of Hormuz.
The spike in oil prices weighed on stock markets in Asia, with Japan's Nikkei (.N225) , opens new tab down 5.8% and import-reliant South Korea falling 6.5%.

item.title: Kenya says death toll from floods nearly doubles to 42
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 9 (Reuters) - The death toll from heavy rains and flooding in Kenya's capital Nairobi and elsewhere has nearly ‚Äãdoubled to 42, the government said in a ‚Äåstatement issued late on Sunday.
Intense rains on Friday unleashed heavy and widespread flooding, causing some people to drown, washing away vehicles ‚Äãand disrupting traffic at the country's largest airport.
The ‚Äãprevious death toll was 23.
Emergency workers from various ‚ÅÝagencies including the military were still conducting search ‚Äãand rescue operations across the country, Geoffrey Kiringa Ruku, ‚Äãminister for public service and human capital development said in the statement.
"Search mission is still in progress by the multi agency emergency ‚Äãresponse teams with the aim of ensuring that ‚Äãbodies of all the flood victims are found and retrieved," he ‚Äåsaid.
The ‚ÅÝfloods had also done extensive damage to infrastructure and livelihoods. But 172 vehicles that had been swept away by flood waters have been recovered.
On Saturday, President William ‚ÄãRuto said he ‚Äãhad ordered relief ‚ÅÝfood from the country's national strategic reserves be immediately released for distribution to families ‚Äãaffected by the floods.
Scientists say global warming ‚Äãis ‚ÅÝworsening floods and droughts across East Africa by concentrating rainfall into shorter, more intense bursts. A 2024 World Weather ‚ÅÝAttribution ‚Äãstudy found climate change had made ‚Äãdevastating rains in the region twice as likely as before.

item.title: Turkey to deploy six F-16 fighter jets to northern Cyprus, broadcaster NTV says
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey plans to deploy ‚Äãsix F-16 fighter jets ‚Äåto northern Cyprus on Monday to ‚Äãbolster the defences ‚Äãof the Turkish community ‚ÅÝthere, broadcaster NTV ‚Äãsaid, citing a ‚Äãcivil aviation official from the breakaway Turkish Republic ‚Äãof Northern Cyprus.
On ‚ÄãSaturday, a Turkish defence ministry ‚Äåsource ‚ÅÝsaid Turkey was considering the deployment of F-16 aircraft to ‚ÄãCyprus, ‚Äãamong ‚ÅÝother steps being taken to ‚Äãensure the ‚Äãsecurity ‚ÅÝof the Turkish Cypriot state as conflict ‚ÅÝspreads ‚Äãin the ‚Äãregion.

item.title: Allowing Iranian ship to dock was right thing to do, Indian foreign minister says
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Iran had sought India's permission ‚Äãfor three of ‚Äåits ships to dock at its ‚Äãports, which ‚Äãwas granted on March ‚ÅÝ1, India's ‚Äãforeign minister said ‚Äãon Monday, adding that the government believed that ‚Äãit was ‚Äãthe "right thing to do".
One ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthe three ships docked at India's southern port ‚Äãof Kochi ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝMarch 4 and its ‚Äãcrew was ‚Äãat ‚ÅÝIndian naval facilities, Subrahmanyam Jaishankar told ‚ÅÝparliament ‚Äãon ‚ÄãMonday.

item.title: Tracking LNG flows as key global gas prices go haywire
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The sudden stoppage of LNG exports from Qatar - the world's second-largest exporter in 2025 - has sent natural gas prices soaring by 50% from year-ago levels across Europe and Asia and has prompted panicked buyers to seek out replacement cargoes.
However, a shortage of available vessels and limited spare liquefaction capacity is restricting ‚Äãthe number of liquefied natural gas cargoes available for immediate delivery to either region, and is likely to keep gas prices elevated for some time.
That said, major LNG ‚Äåexporters outside the Middle East are re-routing whatever shipments they can to needy buyers, spurred on by bumper profit potential created by the widening premium of gas prices in Asia and Europe over local prices.
Here's a breakdown of the latest natural gas price action in key markets that is likely to drive further cargo diversions, as well as the current LNG export flows from key suppliers.
Exporters in the U.S. are likely best placed to capitalize on the redirection opportunities, as ‚Äãthe top global exporter likely has the greatest volumes of undeclared capacity available for spot shipment.
However, exporters in Australia, Russia, Malaysia and Nigeria - the next largest LNG shippers, according to Kpler - ‚Äãwill likely also adjust delivery schedules and locations after the steep run-up in natural gas prices in Asia and Europe.
LNG forward contracts for delivery to key ‚ÅÝmarkets in Asia currently average around $12.95 per million British thermal units (MMBtu) for 2026 as a whole, data from LSEG shows.
That marks a 53% rise compared to 2025's average levels, and would mark the highest ‚Äãannual average since 2022.
In Europe, benchmark TTF futures for 2026 currently average around $12.41 per MMBtu, which marks a 49% increase from 2025's average.
While those forward prices are bound to shift dramatically over the coming weeks in ‚Äãresponse to the ongoing market gyrations, it is instructive to take a snapshot of the current picture in order to assess potential arbitrage opportunities.
U.S. exporters face projected average natural gas costs of around $3.63 per MMBtu in 2026 - per current LSEG data - and so could score profits of more than 200% even after accounting for liquefaction and shipping costs to either Europe or Asia.
The profit potential is less clear for other exporters such as Australia and Russia, due to more ‚Äãopaque gas and liquefaction cost levels.
Nonetheless, as long as Europe's and Asia's gas costs remain close to current levels, it can be safely assumed that all LNG exporters face historic profit potential if they could ‚Äãeffectively divert cargoes to those markets.
LNG analysts are already closely scrutinizing shipment patterns from major exporters for signs of sudden diversions.
The U.S. shipped 68% of its LNG to Europe in 2025, and is expected to continue ‚Äåprioritising Europe ‚ÅÝover other regions due to the comparably low cost of shipping to Europe.
That said, loading schedules for the first three months of the year indicate that U.S. exporters raised shipments to Asian buyers by nearly 20% compared to the same period in 2025.
Asia-bound tonnage from the U.S. for the first quarter of 2026 is estimated at 3.75 million metric tons, compared to 3.16 million tons for the same period in 2025, data from Kpler shows.
U.S. shipments to Europe from January to March are estimated at 22.7 million tons, compared to 19.8 million tons for the same months last year.
With buyers in both Asia and Europe set to ‚Äãremain strong bidders for LNG over the near ‚Äãto medium term, further increases in U.S. sales ‚ÅÝto both regions can be expected.
In contrast to U.S. trade flows, an overwhelming majority of shipments from Australian LNG exporters have been bound for customers in Asia, with Asia accounting for over 95% of Australian exports last year.
Relatively short transit times to the likes of China and Japan compared to cargoes shipped ‚Äãfrom the United States means that Australia retains a cost advantage to Asia buyers, as well as more extensive relations with regional buyers.
However, Australian LNG ‚Äãsellers have already committed close ‚ÅÝto all their projected supplies to contracted buyers, and so may have limited scope to redirect any shipments to the spot market where potential buyers are prepared to pay up for deliveries.
That may leave openings for other exporters including Russia, Malaysia and Nigeria, which have already established global LNG trade routes and look well placed to offer further competition to their larger exporting peers going forward.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a ‚ÅÝcolumnist for Reuters.
Enjoying ‚Äãthis column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning ‚ÄãBid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.title: Governments scramble to limit fallout of Iran war as oil prices surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Governments in Asia are scrambling to limit the impact on ‚Äãeconomies and consumers from the widening Iran war, which fuelled a record surge in oil prices on Monday after key ‚Äåproducers cut output and Tehran signalled that hardliners will remain in charge.
In South Korea , which buys 70% of its oil from the Middle East, President Lee Jae Myung said Seoul would cap fuel prices for the first time in nearly 30 years and warned against panic buying.
Speaking at an emergency meeting, Lee called the crisis "a significant burden ‚Äãon our economy, which is highly dependent on global trade and energy imports from the Middle East."
A senior Japanese parliament member ‚Äãsaid on Sunday that the government had instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible ‚ÅÝcrude release, although the country's chief cabinet secretary said on Monday that no decision had been made to release stockpiles.
Japan imports around 95% of ‚Äãits oil from the Middle East. It has reserves to cover 354 days of consumption.
Elsewhere, Vietnam removed import tariffs on fuels and Bangladesh shut universities to ‚Äãconserve electricity and fuel, while China last week asked refiners to halt fuel exports and try to cancel shipments already committed.
U.S. President Donald Trump tried to tamp down concern about rising U.S. gasoline prices, which were up 11% for the week on Friday, while Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called on him to ‚Äãsell oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve .
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, ‚Äãis a very small price to pay for U.S.A., and World, Safety and Peace," Trump posted on Truth Social on Sunday night. "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Oil jumped 25% , with ‚ÄåBrent ‚ÅÝon track for a record one-day gain, after Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, while OPEC producers Kuwait and Iraq cut oil output during the weekend as the crucial Strait of Hormuz remained effectively shut.
Across Asia, which sources 60% of its oil from the Middle East, stock prices slid and the dollar rose, with officials looking to contain the impact ‚Äãof oil prices, including weighing the ‚Äãrelease of strategic reserves, as ‚ÅÝworries mounted that disruption in energy supplies could be prolonged.
Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, a move expected to draw the ire of Trump. Weekend attacks on Iranian ‚Äãoil storage facilities fueled fears of retaliatory strikes on energy facilities.
"Oil prices have now gathered all the ‚Äãingredients for a ‚ÅÝperfect storm - Middle East Gulf producers cutting output, the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz ... all compounded by a growing pessimism about a quick turnaround in the current situation," said Kpler senior oil analyst Muyu Xu.
Iraq cut oil production from its main southern oilfields by 70% to 1.3 million barrels per ‚ÅÝday, three ‚Äãindustry sources said on Sunday, while Kuwait Petroleum Corp began cutting oil output on Saturday ‚Äãand declared force majeure.
No.2 LNG exporter Qatar has already halted exports of the superchilled fuel, and analysts predict that the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will also have to ‚Äãcut output soon as they run out of oil storage due to the Strait of Hormuz closure.

item.title: Saudi Aramco offers crude in rare tenders as Iran conflict disrupts exports, traders say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco has offered ‚Äãmore than 2 ‚Äåmillion barrels of Saudi crude in ‚Äãrare tenders ‚Äãas the U.S.-Iran conflict ‚ÅÝdisrupted exports from ‚Äãthe Middle East, ‚Äãtrade sources said.
The state company offered 2 ‚Äãmillion barrels of ‚ÄãArab Heavy crude for ‚ÄåMarch ‚ÅÝloading in one of the tenders it issued last ‚Äãweek. ‚ÄãIt ‚ÅÝalso offered 650,000 barrels ‚Äãof Arab Light ‚Äãcrude ‚ÅÝin a separate tender, the ‚ÅÝpeople ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Bahrain's Bapco announces force majeure on operations
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Bapco Energies declared ‚Äãforce majeure ‚Äåon its group operations ‚Äãon Monday ‚Äãfollowing a recent ‚ÅÝattack on ‚Äãits refinery ‚Äãcomplex, the company said.
Bapco said ‚Äãthat all ‚Äãdomestic market needs ‚Äåremain ‚ÅÝfully secured and supplies will continue ‚Äãwithout ‚Äãdisruption, ‚ÅÝsupported by proactive ‚Äãplans ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝplace.

item.title: Oil pares gains but still on track for record surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices came off earlier highs on Monday but were still up more than 15% at levels not seen since ‚Äãmid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market due to the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Brent crude futures were up $15.51, ‚Äåor 16.7%, at $108.20 per barrel at 0642 GMT - on track for the biggest-ever jump in a single day, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures were up $14.23, or 15.7%, at $105.13.
Disruptions in tanker movements and rising security risks have already slowed shipping activity, and left Asian buyers reliant on Middle Eastern crude especially vulnerable because the crisis is unfolding around the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes.
WTI surged 31.4% ‚Äãto a session high of $119.48 a barrel earlier on Monday, while Brent rose as much as 29% to $119.50 a barrel. Before the surge on Monday, Brent had already climbed ‚Äã27% and WTI by 35.6% last week.
Prices pared gains after the Financial Times reported that the Group of Seven (G7) finance ministers and the International ‚ÅÝEnergy Agency will discuss on Monday a joint emergency oil reserves release , and Saudi Aramco offered prompt crude supply through a series of rare tenders .
""Unless oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz resume ‚Äãsoon and regional tensions ease, upward pressure on prices is likely to persist," said Vasu Menon, managing director for investment strategy at OCBC in Singapore.
Iraq and Kuwait have begun cutting oil output, adding ‚Äãto earlier liquefied natural gas reductions from Qatar, as the war blocked shipments from the Middle East. Analysts expect the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will have to also cut output soon as they run out of oil storage.
Refinery disruptions continued due to escalating tensions in the region, with Bahrain's BAPCO announcing a force majeure following a recent attack on its refinery complex.
Fujairah Media Office said a fire broke out in the UAE's Fujairah oil ‚Äãindustry zone resulting from debris falling, with no injuries reported. Saudi Arabia's Defence Ministry said on X it intercepted a drone heading to the Shaybah oilfield.
Also boosting prices is the appointment of Mojtaba Khamenei ‚Äãto succeed his father Ali Khamenei as Iran's supreme leader, signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge in Tehran a week into its conflict with the United States and Israel.
"With the appointment of the late leader's ‚Äåson as ‚ÅÝIran's new leader, U.S. President Donald Trump's goal of regime change in Iran has become more difficult," said Satoru Yoshida, a commodity analyst with Rakuten Securities.
"That view accelerated buying, as Iran is expected to continue its closure of the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on other oil-producing nations' facilities, as seen last week," he said, predicting WTI could rise to $120 and then $130 a barrel in a relatively short period.
